### Gagnrad — 2014-03-03T15:31:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/patrick-introducing-myself.9975/post-113166>

> [patgrif@hotmail.com said:](/goto/post?id=113003)
>
> Thanks Gracelind and Caroline. Yes I will get the picture sorted out fairly soon. I have read Nora Gedgaudas' book and really liked it. But personally I don't go for things like biofeedback not that I know much about it. Just in principle..............the idea of 'controlling' the mind in that way does not appeal to me.

I envy you growing up near the sea, Patrick. I've lived fairly near it, but not just a walk away or anything like that. My parents lived in North Devon at one time (my father's actually from there). I love that North Devon-North Cornwall coast. South Devon is pretty warm and bucolic with very good farmland. Up near places like Hartland and the Valley of the Rocks it's much bleaker but beautiful for those like me that like that sort of thing.

The neurofeedback might be interesting to read about, though, even if it's not appealing to do -- specially to someone like you with a background in psychology. One of the groups it seems to be helpful for is ex-soldiers. They can, for example, get stuck in a state of hyper-alertness, this having been brought on by always having to expect they might be attacked at any time. What the neurofeedback seems to do is show them what their own brains are doing: once the brain can see that, it's able to self-regulate and switch off an unusual state like that. There's a piece on that here, if it's of any interest:

[http://www.eeginfo.com/newsletter/?p=761#more-761](http://www.eeginfo.com/newsletter/?p=761#more-761)

I went for an assessment once, which was interesting enough. They gave me a map of what my brainwaves currently looked like. They were actually in fairly good shape -- the right places tended to be coming up, and on both sides, when I was doing tasks and so on. My brain was evidently communicating internally, too, which, apparently, some people's are not so good at. They said where that doesn't happen they suggest people take fish-oil for the omega-3s. Anyway, I went off with the map but didn't pursue it: maybe I could have got some benefit from sessions, but it was too expensive for me really.

Oh, I have to tell you this, which will make you laugh. When they were mapping what the parts of my brain were doing when on-task they gave me things to do, such as, listen to and repeat back a sequence of numbers, and I had to do it in reverse, I think. IIRC, that's a standard test in other appliactions and anyone with a university education should be able to get up to seven digits. I also had to do mental arithmetic. Now I've always thought I was fairly bad at this. It turned out I was actually good. It's irrelevant how good or bad you are at the tasks -- they merely want to see where and how you're "lighting up" as you do them. But it was interesting to be told that and to be told that children and young adults are often, according to the operator, completely inept at the mental artithmetic tests. Can't do them at all. They told me they think it's because the kids always used calculators and never really practised those skills.

> Caroline I will take my life in my hands now and try the journal thing and see what happens.

You'll gladden her heart. She loves participation.

### patgrif@hotmail.com — 2014-03-03T17:06:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/patrick-introducing-myself.9975/post-113170>

Well thanks a lot "Gagnrad" you make me feel very welcome here (still floundering a bit). I am not expierienced so much with 'social media' but I start to like playing with features here and am 'proud' of myself for what I have achieved so far! That's very interesting what you say about bio-feedback and I can imagine that could be quite helpful I mean the thing about how if one can realize or 'see' that one is acting from some old or previous threat then the brain might sort of automatically correct itself. I had sort of put it all down as about trying to 'control the mind' and you have shown me it is probably quite a bit more than that. On a somewhat related subject I may look into the 'sensory deprivation tank' that Evan spoke about last Tuesday they have one here for $40.00 a session and he says it helps him relax (shut of the cortisol or sympathetic nervous system for a while) which is interesting to me and if you think about it kind of the problem soldiers with PTSD have.

I wonder if the difference between North and South Devon might be down to the influence of the Gulf Stream. I am from Kerry in Ireland which is in the same corner (SW) of the country and I know there are places in South Kerry that are almost 'tropical' as you say. Where I lived was more like what you describe in North Devon, rather harsh actually. But I love being by the ocean but it's funny growing up there we never learned to swim and my Dad 'bragged' to me he was NEVER in eneough water to drown in (not even in a boat). The people there were afraid of the sea and I suppose with reason. Also I think because of the very puritanical attitudes they did not like to take their clothes off! We also did not eat much fish so in some ways I find Dr Kruse's ideas kind of go back to what I MIGHT have done as a child.

I saw an earlier post of yours (before I figured out how to reply!) about the Gaelic Christianity which was very interesting. You mentioned 'anam cara' and that word 'anam' is very evocative in Gaelic it means 'soul' and 'spirit' and what survives after death etc. But the word has a very STRONG connotation of like the essence of a person. Also what you said about the Irish invented 'private confession' I did not know that but I think the 'indigenous' Irish culture was in many many ways wonderful, wise and compassionate. Not to put down the English but they kind of did do a lot of damage to the country................I have just read a book about the Irish Famine and I did not really know this. The author says it was basically un-neccessary it was a function of the kind of politics and economics practiced at the time. But no need to worry I am not a fire breathing IRA guy or anything. Actually I have always more seen the English point of view and I am more balancing that out. Thanks again for making me feel welcome and I am sure we will have more to talk about

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-03T19:56:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/patrick-introducing-myself.9975/post-113190>

Thanks very much for the reply, Patrick. My actual name's Mike.

> [patgrif@hotmail.com said:](/goto/post?id=113170)
>
> I wonder if the difference between North and South Devon might be down to the influence of the Gulf Stream. I am from Kerry in Ireland which is in the same corner (SW) of the country and I know there are places in South Kerry that are almost 'tropical' as you say. Where I lived was more like what you describe in North Devon, rather harsh actually. But I love being by the ocean but it's funny growing up there we never learned to swim and my Dad 'bragged' to me he was NEVER in eneough water to drown in (not even in a boat). The people there were afraid of the sea and I suppose with reason. Also I think because of the very puritanical attitudes they did not like to take their clothes off! We also did not eat much fish so in some ways I find Dr Kruse's ideas kind of go back to what I MIGHT have done as a child.

I expect you're right about the Gulf Stream. It's funny in a way to think of people growing tropical plants in parts of Cornwall and yet the part that adjoins North Devon can be fairly bleak and bitter. I've read that that part of Cornwall was so poor in the 19th century that it was said that there wasn't even a set of wheels in the area: they were still using packhorses. I think many of the people cleared out to Prince Edward Island in Canada.

My father's mother's family were descended from a carpenter from County Cork. I think the dates suggest that he probably came over to Devon during the Famine. People seem to have had to leave home a lot back then, don't they? My father's father worked as a cowboy in Canada, staying with relatives who were already out there, though he did come back to Devon. His brother went to Australia.

> I saw an earlier post of yours (before I figured out how to reply!) about the Gaelic Christianity which was very interesting. You mentioned 'anam cara' and that word 'anam' is very evocative in Gaelic it means 'soul' and 'spirit' and what survives after death etc. But the word has a very STRONG connotation of like the essence of a person. Also what you said about the Irish invented 'private confession' I did not know that but I think the 'indigenous' Irish culture was in many many ways wonderful, wise and compassionate.

Thanks for that. The Celtic Christianity does interest me. Those wandering monks who got to Iceland even before the vikings! I'm sure you're right to say it was compassionate, too. The priest from New Mexico says that for the Old Irish penance was about reconciling a person to his community and not punitive in intent. That doesn't seem to me to be typical of the later medieval view. The historian R. W. Southern, remarking on the "harsh medieval system of penance" in place later, said that a system that could have Henry II walking through the streets in his underpants had a powerful grip on men's minds, and he meant through fear.

I think it's also fascinating that there were Irish and Welsh hermits who were celebrating the natural beauty of their surroundings. It staggered me when I found out -- because, of course, this was supposed to have to wait on Wordsworth. Actually, Thomas Traherne, an Anglican clergyman in the Welsh Borders, was doing it in the seventeenth century:

> The corn was orient and immortal wheat, which never should be reaped, nor was ever sown. I thought it had stood from everlasting to everlasting. The dust and stones of the street were as precious as gold: the gates were at first the end of the world.

But centuries before him … there's an entire section of poetry entitled "Nature" in this book:

[http://www.amazon.com/Celtic-Miscellany-Translated-Translations-Literatures/dp/0140442472/](http://www.amazon.com/Celtic-Miscellany-Translated-Translations-Literatures/dp/0140442472/)

Of course, I can only read the translations.

Some of the stuff from the _Camina Gadelica_ from the Gaelic-speaking areas of Scotland appeal to me, too. But that's much later, of course. Do you think that there can be some continuities, though? I'm something of a religious sceptic but there's something appealing to me in these people singing their prayers in undertones as they went about their daily tasks. So, for example, there's a prayer a woman might use when kindling her fire in the morning while she asks to be:

> Without malice, without jealousy, without envy,
> Without fear, without terror of any one under the sun

… which is really charming.

[http://www.smo.uhi.ac.uk/gaidhlig/corpus/Carmina/B82.html](http://www.smo.uhi.ac.uk/gaidhlig/corpus/Carmina/B82.html)

> Not to put down the English but they kind of did do a lot of damage to the country............….

Yes, it's a painful thing to read about, isn't it? So much suffering. I don't know Irish History well, but I know enough to know that the Crown behaved appalling there for centuries. I remember C. S. Lewis -- himself an Irishman, of course -- remarking when writing about Edmund Spenser's poetry that Spenser had been "the instrument of a detestable policy in Ireland" (i.e. Elizabeth's). Lewis said he thought there are signs in _The Faerie Queene_ that it had corrupted Spenser's imagination … and yet Spenser was a great poet in spite of it.

### patgrif@hotmail.com — 2014-03-03T23:33:55+0200

Dr Kruse, I can assure you the feeling is mutual it is an absolute thrill for me to finally talk to you. I have only just got on the Forum but I have 'followed' you very closely for well over 2 years and it's weird I almost felt you were talking just to me. Of course I don't mean that literally but it was a very 'private' pleasure of mine but I must say going on the Forum adds another dimension I am really excited about it all

About T2D I had a VERY stressful life in work, running my own business which became quite large and successful but at a great cost to my health. I ate badly, slept badly everything badly really so I am not so surprised. ### Gagnrad — 2014-03-04T08:53:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/patrick-introducing-myself.9975/post-113297>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=113216)
>
> Mike ....... I love participation???? I guess I do! - hadn't really thought about that.... I guess you have my number - pretty transparent eh?

LOL

> I was so worried that the forum was going to die last year after everyone went so "nuts" over EMF and ran off a cliff .... that I just started talking to try to keep everyone engaged - until it righted itself ..... and, unfortunately, for all of you ... I still haven't shut up!

Here's another thing that came up in Ranulph Fiennes new book I'm reading at the moment -- EMF strangely enough. I only just read that by-the-way account in an anthropology book of Australian radar operators shooting down crows with their equipment from the 60s or 70s, and now here's another unexpected reference.

So Fiennes' wife was operating the radio at the base for them. She nearly died of CO poisoning when the fumes from the generator powering it blew back under the door. Accordingly, an RAF expert, very concerned, contacted her and said something along the lines of "You do know this equipment can kill you in several different ways?" There follows a list of the hazards, including RF burns and Beryllium in some of the components. Later on, she gets a static shock, which she describes to Ranulph as being like "an explosion inside her lungs".

I find it fascinating how you read _old_ material in a different way and find things strike you in ways they wouldn't have before you'd known something else. I'm sure after reading Becker, there's lots of things I'd come across like that I wouldn't have thought about in the same way before.

Of course, because our radio sets have been so much less powerful and purely receivers all this has passed us by for years. It's been off _our_ radar. I guess everyone who has a current smartphone has something that transmits (mobile network _and_ wireless internet) in his or her bag or, worse, pocket now, though … I shan't forget Devra Davies telling Sean Croxton not to carry his mobile phone in his trouser pocket, hence next to his private parts. I shan't forget Jack saying that Apple's new iPhone switches wireless off via an infra-red sensor if it senses it's actually touching your body. As Jack said, Apple knows.

### caroline — 2014-03-04T09:06:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/patrick-introducing-myself.9975/post-113298>

Mike .... we truly are seeing everything with new eyes!

I once sat beside a young man on a train for 90 minutes .......he was beside himself with what to do with his phone. He didn't have a back pack or anything ..... it was like he was holding a time bomb!

But why did it take a strange woman \[me\] on a train to tell him that?

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-04T10:24:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/patrick-introducing-myself.9975/post-113302>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=113298)
>
> Mike .... we truly are seeing everything with new eyes!
>
> I always find it interesting when I ask a guy where he keeps his iphone .... he instantly gets it!
>
> I once sat beside a young man on a train for 90 minutes .......he was beside himself with what to do with his phone. He didn't have a back pack or anything ..... it was like he was holding a time bomb!

But why not just switch it off? This I don't understand. I used to have it off when carrying it and turn it on and put it on a table not too near when I got where I was going. Now I've gone back to only turning it on now and then, like I used to do when I first got a mobile. I think it was getting a BlackBerry and enabling email that made me have it on more -- email might arrive. I decided it's too expensive -- phone contracts are a licence to print money for the telcomms companies -- I've deleted the email account and I'm converting back to pay-as-you-go. The phone will now be for the odd occasions when I want to send a text or make a call, and there's no landline handy. If someone texts or phones me and it's off ... well, it's going to be something that can wait till I happen to switch it on. Nobody needs me urgently.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/patrick-introducing-myself.9975/post-113400>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=113364)
>
> Caroline posting in a thread does not hurt me..........The only things that hurt us are the things we let hurt us. We are in control of our emotions at all times when we are healthy

This brings to mind several quotes from Epictetus:

> If evil be spoken of you and it be true, correct yourself, if it be a lie, laugh at it.

> There is only one way to happiness and that is to cease worrying about things which are beyond the power of our will.

Many people have said that if you _can't_ choose not to react to something then you aren't free: you're at the mercy of an external stimulus. I think I must be a very a long way from that. I'm sure most of us are. I think this is one of the things meditation can help with, but it takes regular application ...

### patgrif@hotmail.com — 2014-03-05T19:23:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/patrick-introducing-myself.9975/post-113618>

I ordered the magneteco today, I got the 20 guass one based on Dr K's advice of 'dude you need about 1,000 Gauss' so.................we'll see. I am one of those people who seem resistant to the effects of ANYTHING, like I talked about losing my stomack. Well first off I read "Wheat Belly" and was convinced that would do it................waited and waited...........NO...........the Paleo and epi-Paleo.............still NO.............drink lots of water............................do CT 'like a madman' well maybe not like a madman maybe that's my problem............anyway still NO..........what's left, I've decided I need exerecise hike up and down a hill once a week, ride my bike everywhere .............still NO.............what's left oh yeah eat less I don't really do that I have to admit so now I am going to do tennis once a week and the magneteco...............we will see.....................I have a funny feeling it will STILL be "NO"
I am T2D and Dr K somewhere said the ONLY way for those people to lose their belly fat is CT.................nothing else will do it...............is it I am not doing CT intensivly eneough(not eneogh like a madman)...............any suggestions gratefully received and considered. I am off to the beach now for some CT (but the stomack is there as ever)..................

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-05T21:41:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/patrick-introducing-myself.9975/post-113640>

> [patgrif@hotmail.com said:](/goto/post?id=113618)
>
> I am T2D and Dr K somewhere said the ONLY way for those people to lose their belly fat is CT.................nothing else will do it...............

Well, I think he was probably using hyperbole. AFAIK, people like Wolfgang Lutz and Ron Rosedale have had success with diabetic patients using low-carb diets, and Jack himself says of someone who would be showing a tendency towards pre-diabetes:

> They need a dietary intervention to save their life.

[http://jackkruse.com/what-is-biologic-concrete/](http://jackkruse.com/what-is-biologic-concrete/)

He's also said that one drug intervention -- cycloset -- does work (and that the fact that it does work tells us something). However, googling "jack kruse cyclist" I can only find this:

[https://www.facebook.com/drjackkruse/posts/426307837433576](https://www.facebook.com/drjackkruse/posts/426307837433576)

What I'd guess he meant is that CT is _the_ most powerful tool in the box for T2D.

The only thing you don't mention is sleep and "hygiene" as regards light intensity and colour. I'm guessing that you probably know all about that, but if not that's important, too. IIRC, one experiment showed that it was possible to make a group of young healthy students pre-diabetic simply by depriving them of sleep. They ate exactly the same as the controls who got proper sleep.

I expect your guess is in the right direction -- it's the CT that's the important factor. Exercise is generally good, of course, but it's not a very effective weight-loss strategy.

### nonchalant — 2014-03-06T03:48:22+0200

Hi Patrick, welcome!
One suggestion for the tummy would be sunshine. It may be strong enough already at your latitude. It is a gradual process, but I think it might help. Measurements would be helpful to track progress.

I'm not saying that the fat won't redeposit itself elsewhere, since your body, if it is inefficient, needs the mass. I have been exposing my upper arms to sunshine most of this winter, and my shoulders look awesome now, if I do say so myself... I'm now lifting my arms to expose the underarms to more sunshine, and I've already seen some improvement in the last month.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-06T10:52:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/patrick-introducing-myself.9975/post-113739>

> [patgrif@hotmail.com said:](/goto/post?id=113668)
>
> Funny thing today I walk into a bookshop and one of the first books I see is one called "Anam Cara" so I got it. Looks very interesting now if only I can chill out eneough to actually read it! It's all about Celtic philosophies of life etc

That sounds good.

I just remembered there's a book I've been meaning to get hold of for some time and not got round to, Helen Waddell's _Beasts and Saints:_

[http://www.amazon.com/Beasts-And-Saints-Helen-Waddell/dp/1445505797/](http://www.amazon.com/Beasts-And-Saints-Helen-Waddell/dp/1445505797/)

This seems to be another interesting aspect of Celtic Christianity -- their attitude to animals. The stories have some of the hermits and saints having easy relations with animals or having animal-helpers. I think this could easily tip over into sentimentality, and there seems to be an incipient vegetarianism in some of this. And much of the material sounds highly legendary. But maybe it's another aspect of their seemingly being more at home in the natural world.

### sooperb — 2014-03-06T11:01:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/patrick-introducing-myself.9975/post-113740>

It's interesting what you say about your being reluctant to CT. I talk to myself about it too  for me, though, I find that while I'm still debating it, I'm turning on the bath tap and testing the water. Then without any more ado I just get in, it's as though there is a part of my brain that over-rides my reluctance. It's odd, I often find this operates with other less attractive tasks. I guess with you it works the other way, you know you should but you are able to rationalise why you shouldn't. Maybe an avoidance technique?

I sympathise with the ever present abdominal protrusion, I have a similar situation. I will persevere because, I have a year's head start on you chronologically and I don't want to be stuck with a half grown papoose forever fixed to my front.

### patgrif@hotmail.com — 2014-03-07T05:23:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/patrick-introducing-myself.9975/post-113910>

Mike, about the Celtic love of animals. I just read a book about the Irish Famine and he says in that the pig in particular was like 'part of the family' would come in sort of eat with them, they would drop stuff on the floor for the pig and he even says at some point the pig might sleep between the parents and children in the bed. Now that's love! Of course I am not sure about these kinds of stories but I CAN imagine it.................we were 'close' with our dogs and cats we never had pigs so I don't know it is hard to imagine that but it COULD happen!

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-07T09:00:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/patrick-introducing-myself.9975/post-113948>

> [patgrif@hotmail.com said:](/goto/post?id=113910)
>
> Mike, about the Celtic love of animals. I just read a book about the Irish Famine and he says in that the pig in particular was like 'part of the family' would come in sort of eat with them, they would drop stuff on the floor for the pig and he even says at some point the pig might sleep between the parents and children in the bed. Now that's love! Of course I am not sure about these kinds of stories but I CAN imagine it.................we were 'close' with our dogs and cats we never had pigs so I don't know it is hard to imagine that but it COULD happen!

I should think that's pretty accurate, although I'd doubt many people actually allowed the pig in bed with them. Over here you can still find some older houses that are fairly long and low: at one time people lived in one end of the building and had the shippon -- the area for animals -- in the other.

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dartmoor\_longhouse](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dartmoor_longhouse)

To us living in close proximity to livestock seems strange, but people in agricultural societies seem to have taken it for granted.

And I can see how a pig could easily become something of a pet -- they're quite intelligent, aren't they? You can train them to hunt truffles like you can a dog, for example.

I suppose what's surprising is that people could make a pet of an animal and still feel comfortable with slaughtering and eating it. Maybe people used to be much more realistic about life and death?

### patgrif@hotmail.com — 2014-03-07T17:10:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/patrick-introducing-myself.9975/post-113999>

Mike, you are correct, that picture of the house is very reminiscient of the houses around where I grew up. We were 'upper class' (kidding) our house was separate from the animal quarters. That whole thing of killing animals that you 'know' is a very tricky and weird situation. That's why I suppose it was treated as a 'sacrament' or all kinds of rituals surrounded it. But wer'nt they more in touch than we are today where we take meat as just so much 'material' (myself included) I remember my Mom bleeding the chickens to death but though I did not enjoy that it was all forgotten with the wonderful roast chicken later. We used to eat the heart, liver, kidneys etc actually it was my favorite parts.

Sooperb I have not seen that movie. Is is good? I imagine it would be very funny. (I don't know why my words get underlined, not my doing they are not that important lol)

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-07T17:35:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/patrick-introducing-myself.9975/post-114005>

Wow, I bet home-raised chicken tasted good.

I didn't know chickens were bled. Pigs, of course, because the blood would be used for black puddings. Chickens I didn't know about. I wonder why people did that. Would the carcase keep longer perhaps?

Funny thing -- someone just happened to say to me today that she used to visit her aunt in Ireland and ate fresh-caught fish "almost morning, noon, and night". She mentioned a place called Rabbit Island? She said they caught mackeral and cooked it over an open fire on a slab of slate, adding what everyone I've ever heard of who's caught fish and then cooked it immediately says: that as fresh as that it tastes like so out of this world it's like nothing you could ever buy even from the best fishmonger.

### patgrif@hotmail.com — 2014-03-07T18:08:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/patrick-introducing-myself.9975/post-114008>

Wow that sounds amazing! Can you imagine what brain power they had though maybe they did not know what use to put it to. Yes my Mom would kind of bend the top of the chicken's head and then slowly carve into it and hold it while she bled it into a can. We used the blood for 'black pudding' but what I remember was we felt the chicken was much 'better' if the blood was drained out. When I came to the US and heard they killed turkeys by twisting their necks that seems so groos. On a side note my sister used to help my Mom and she later became a top surgeon in Ireland and she credited her 'training' with my Mom as being influential in terms of becoming a surgeon! Yes the aroma of the freshly roasted chicken was just amazing, filled the whole house it seemed.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-08T13:05:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/patrick-introducing-myself.9975/post-114125>

> [patgrif@hotmail.com said:](/goto/post?id=114008)
>
> When I came to the US and heard they killed turkeys by twisting their necks that seems so groos.

I think country people often did that with chickens. I'm sure it was done in England. Presumably Germany, too -- because didn't Hitler say that he'd wring England's neck like a chicken? Churchill, using a U.S. idiom, commented, "Some chicken, some neck."

I recall someone talking about slaughtering his geese and saying that people used to strangle them with a broomstick but lopping the head off seemed kinder to him. Maybe they wanted to keep the blood in with some of this poultry? I know there was, probably still is, a Paris restaurant that specialised in "pressed duck" where the duck is put through a mangle or something and cooked in its own blood. All the celebrities used to go there, including the Beatles in Paul McCartney's pre-vegetarian days.

One thing I like about Jack's approach is that it doesn't cut out all the things you'd want to eat. You could call it gourmet dieting.  It's kind of comical that people cut all the best stuff out in order to lose weight and it doesn't work anyway.

Are you going to celebrate Monday week with a glass of something?

# Thread: Part 2 of my Journal - Margo :) WOW!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/part-2-of-my-journal-margo-wow.9976/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### margor_j — 2014-03-02T02:16:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/part-2-of-my-journal-margo-wow.9976/post-112970>

So,
as per part one - fat, miserable, depressed and all the rest. Im still based at my parents place- which wasnt meant to be for long, but when contracts fall through, then things change. but what did that failing contract allow? Me to go swimming! daily, in the ocean! Its not freezing, we're in summer in nz (officially autum now) but if i dont move around a lot, and stay in the water for an hour or more, trust me, i get cold! so i do that daily, and then sit in the sun to dry, and walk home.

I have just gotten back from ten days work in australia. Pre departure, I had one of my 'leanest' feelings ever! I couldnt believe it, even my parents noticed - face and neck thinner, but the fat rolls that come and go (at will) on my back were barely noticeable (hello, inflammation! finally i get some answers re those!) I really kicked up the fats - to the point where i had to cut back later in the day - I was lying awake at nights and didnt know why - but even the fact that i noticed higher energy from having coconut oil is amaze balls - usually nothing i take makes any change..

I sometimes, not all the tim,e will sit on the lawn in the evenings and read. And weve been turning wifi off at night - though sometimes we forget. Sleep in a black room, drinking a lot of fluids per day, and doing my TJ protocol - off dairy, working on gut and digestive health etc (up to 3200mg of HCI per meal and still no burning feeling!  this really has me wondering now, the more i learn about leaky gut etc... so, really pleased i did my consult with TJ for that fact alone!)

So off i went to aus for work. And I was pre menstrual for most of it. (gonna use some detail here, sorry guys but trust me, periods tell you a LOT about whats going on!) First period in a while that didnt puff me up out of control, bloat me to looking 9 months preggis, make me cry, shitty etc!! I did feel bigger, but not like before, about 90% less! Coffee - yes, i had some. the black heroin, its a bitch for me, but so are changing time zones and working, heat etc - all of which i had in queensland. My whole life, pre period is always the coffee time - i can literally go from one a day to up to 7, dont notice any changes, get my period and cant stand it again.. freaky right?! and there was one day there where i fell for some carb cravings, that i think were part imaginery, and also because i was tired - I kept waking up at 4am the whole time i was away :-/ BUT, in general, i felt so fine the next day so... high fat? and the carbs were NOT worth it. even a work colleague noticed i had 'glazed over' - boy i felt like shite! lesson learnt - im so not into sugar now its not worth it! and its reallly easy to say no!

Im now not on any supplements for mood - been over a month. Id say that i do still get some dark thoughts around my period, but its not for 3 weeks a month, and i do eft when they come up.

Period was a bit hit and miss for two days, but now - no back pain, no nausea, no headaches, no bloating. and i didnt get the hideous gurgling gut for a day before it - that used to do my head in. iv read it was to do with estrogen but... I didnt get it this month!

the other big change for me is getting a sex drive back!  OMG! thats been dormant for far too long now and i cannot help but hi five myself for that! i didnt want to live out my days with sex as a far away memory!!! And yup, took advantage of that while i was away (bit of lovin between friends, whats not to love, right?!)  lets remember tho, my road of hell even took me to breast reduction land, and so much self loathing / hatred and disgust over the past few years, that, while sex may be a daily occurrence for many, well yah, celebrate that!!! I wouldnt say im fully confident of my body, yet, but to have male interest again, to feel more self confident, to be getting complimented on how young i look all the time.... those 'little things' are a big deal on the road to recovery!

While i was away, i was on two meals a day. Some days, im doing 8 massages in the afternoon - so id have my BAB and then eat before i started massages. Usually along the lines of seafood and avocado. Hungry at nite when iv finished work? not even close!! its heavenly!! the boys all go for dinner around 6.30pm - so i know they wont eat til nearer 7.30pm.. too late for me! Oh and to be at a race and not feel hungry is also so so so nice! solid energy = great mood, higher concentration etc..

woohoo me!

oh, but on the not so woohoo side - iv learnt for me that travel / time zones / and pmt (even mild) are my markers for getting me onto the black heroin (coffee) again.. im not sure how else to get thru - i was doing cold showers to wake me up in the morning.. I know i can get bulletproof coffee in aus, and i go back to aus in about a week, so i may get some decaf BPC to try... to shut me up!! first morning in aus i went to a huge park near the hotel and grounded myself on the wet grass / did some breathing / hugged a tree

Looks like Il be in sydney for a few days - staying with a friend in kings cross (ie in town!?) so if any of you are about, pm me, would be awesome to meet up - NOT in a cafe but in a park, where we can earth!

still waiting on 23andme tests to come back.

But hugs and kudos to you Jack Kruse, again (one day i hope to meet and thank you, in person!) - to think a year ago, all i wanted was to not be alive, i was constantly thinking of dying and just so miserable - and to all of you here on the site, sharing ideas / knowledge / advice, wow! thanks !! theres nothing more powerful than feeling change when youve been stuck in shitsville!

hugs to all xx

### Alex97232 — 2014-03-02T23:01:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/miracle90402.9978/post-113094>

> [fitness@home said:](/goto/post?id=112992)
>
> Tell us more...that was a very interesting lead in.

Hi and thanks for your reply. I do want to tell you more. It's hard to know where to begin. There is a lot to my story that will likely be familiar to a lot os us. Maybe I should just start with the miracle and what immediately led up to it.
On March 29, 2013, from my place in Santa Monica, I had a phone meeting that I had set with my Oregon MD-Naturepath: I wanted to discuss with him my research and the recent discovery of the 5+ milligaus EMF readings centered in my bedroom (recently confirmed by So. Cal Edison, and by the consultant I had hired). Dr. Newman said, "You need to get out of that bedroom." That night, I did. I moved to sleep in the living room-dining room where the readings were much lower (about .5 milligaus). Within about 3 days of sleeping in the low EMF environment, my once relentless gut issues (alternating diarrhea-constipation, bloating, cramping, etc.) began to shift. My brain fog began to clear, headaches as well. The night sweats and palpitations stopped. I began to have real bowel movements for the first time in I can't remember. And I began to have hope that I would be able to be around awhile longer to spend time with my kids and my extra beloved 3 year old granddaughter.
That was the beginning of this journey to getting clear--sleeping clean of high EMF's. I know that none of the other stuff matters if you don't have a cleared dark space to allow your body to do it's thing without interference through the night.

### Alex97232 — 2014-03-09T01:53:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/miracle90402.9978/post-114236>

> [nicld said:](/goto/post?id=113121)
>
> Did Dr. Newman come in with his own meter to do the testing or did you rent one yourself?

Hi,

No, Dr. Newman didn't see my place in CA. I have my own meter (Trifield from Less EMF.com). You can get or rent a meter and check your space yourself for starters--it's easy, and will possibly save your life.
Dr. Newman is up here in the Pacific NW where I am right now--we had a phone consult appt. last March 29, 2013, when I was down at the place in Santa Monica. I was continuing to get sicker. I had been doing a lot of reading (Devra Davis, Robert Becker, AnnLouise Gittleman, etc. and a lot of things online. I have no background in biology or physics, just a determination to discover why I was so sick (brain fog, bloating, cramping, alternating constipation-diarrhea, headaches, palpitations and night sweats) and getting sicker by the day.
Doctors in L.A. had no clue--focused only on possible Celiac and certain SIBO--I had been off of gluten for about 3 years (per Dr. Newman). I had lost about 20 more pounds over the 2 years in CA (5'6" and 108 lb). They wanted to put me on a special 3 week liquid diet (the kind they put in feeding tubes). I said nothankyou--sounded like a death sentence to me.

I hadn't seen Dr. Newman since the Summer of 2013. My health was continuing to decline (I had acquired the Santa Monica place at the start of 2011 thinking that favorable weather and closeness to family would be good for my health). By mid March. 2013, I had read a lot about body electricity and electricity in general trying to understand what was happening to me. I wanted to ask him if he thought that the high bedroom EMF readings that I and So. Cal Edison's rep. found were possibly at the root of my health issues and my slammed immune system. I told him that I had a hunch that my health issues were related to something electrical in my body since acupuncture helped me so much--the puzzle for me was that the acupuncture treatment was so short lived--no wonder now, considering where I was sleeping.

When I told Dr. Newman of the 4-5 milligaus readings over the center of my bed, he was not puzzled--he told me to immediately get out of sleeping in that bedroom. I did, and for me, then, a healing miracle began to take place.

Dr. Kruse SO gets it. We have to examine our environment if we are sick--even if we are not yet really sick, but just wanting to optimize. We don't stand a chance of getting well or staying well if our environment, PARTICULARLY WHERE WE SLEEP, is heavy with EMFs--whether it's from electromagnetic fields (measured in milligaus), electric fields (measured by body voltage) , or microwaves/WIFI (measured in microwatts/centimeter squared).

Good places to start and learn are anything Dr. Kruse has written on EMFs as well as Michael Neuert's web site and work with Dr. Kruse.

hi hi
well, i moved back to my parents place in early january. # Thread: Patrick's journal
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/patricks-journal.9979/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 25
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### patgrif@hotmail.com — 2014-03-02T05:31:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/patricks-journal.9979/post-113006>

_Hi everybody, below is a 'journal' I wrote for the primal therapy blog and it seemed like it would be ok to just put that on here. Maybe even 'interesting'. Just so people know when I talk there about 'primal' I mean primal therapy the psychological movement and I use that word in contrast to 'paleo'. However I do understand all these words are 'fungible' and what we are doing here could be called 'primal' just as much as 'paleo'_

\--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

People who read the blog will know that diet has been a somewhat recurrent theme of mine . Diet and it’s importance are only one part of the equation as I also believe in a focus on appropriate exercise and living closer to the light and temperature cycles found in nature. I am aware this seems to be more important to me than the average person, or even the typical person in Primal therapy and since G. told me to make this a personal issue I have seriously tried to examine what is it about this topic that I always want to stress and can’t seem to leave alone.

It is certainly accurate to say I have had many issues revolving around food and stomach health in the most general sense. I had my appendix removed when at age 17 it quite literally ‘exploded’ . The pain was so awful, the kind of pain I KNEW would kill me if I didn’t have surgery. I did have surgery and they removed the appendix and I seemed to make a good recovery with no known lasting effects. On and off over the years I suffered stomach pains, was given antacids and various pills without the problem really clearing up. It started to get dramatically worse around 1996, with frequent vomiting, a lot of stomach pain culminating in a perforated ulcer in that year. Again I woke in the middle of the night with a pain that again I KNEW would kill me, I managed to crawl to a phone and a friend drove me to the hospital at 3 in the morning and before I knew it I was about to have a major operation. Again the surgery was a success, and though this was a slower and more difficult recovery than the appendix I did recover and seemed to stabilize. The next ‘disaster’ to occur was that I was diagnosed as Type 2 Diabetic in 2003. This was kind of a watershed moment for me and thus began the journey of reading endless ‘biology’ and ‘health’ related books. I wanted to understand what Diabetes was and what if anything could be done about it.

I eventually hired a ‘personal trainer’ and ‘improved’ my diet. I DID lose weight and my blood sugar numbers DID improve but it began to feel like a ‘treadmill’ in both a literal and figurative sense. What I mean is that it seemed to demand constant vigilance, ‘will power’ combined with both a treadmill and jogging time were all designed to maintain what was still a very unsatisfactory situation. The more I thought about it the more clear it became that this approach could not be right. As a Primal person’ (if a bit ‘lapsed’) it seemed awfully like some kind of standard psycho-therapeutic approach versus the Primal one where the possibility did exist of actually solving some of these problems and not just forever managing or handling them. I mentioned elsewhere I first became aware of the so-called ‘caveman diet’ from my sister who is a doctor in Ireland. Not to overdo the similarities to Primal but it did ring a similar bell with me much like when I first heard of Primal Therapy through John Lennon’s Rolling Stone interview. At that time my reaction was “Well that makes all the sense in the world” . Obviously it’s a long way from Lennon’s throw away comments to actually investigating the therapy , reading the books and eventually deciding that this is the way for me. I would say I went through a similar experience re the ‘caveman diet’ except that there were many MORE books, MORE biology and MORE authors to explore. . The more I read and researched the more convincing and thorough I found their view point to be. This is a large topic and I can give a list of 10 to 20 books and websites that anyone can delve into. The amount of work and research that is being done in this area is impressive to say the least and I very much admire the independence of these various authors while remaining held together by a common idea.

### patgrif@hotmail.com — 2014-03-02T05:32:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/patricks-journal.9979/post-113007>

(continued)................
To me the case for health is over-whelming (not a small matter, I don’t need to point out some of the health challenges primal people have encountered ) but since this is a therapy blog, speaking for myself only , it is fair to say it is ONLY since I have reformed my diet and lived as close as possible to the principles of ‘ancestral living’ that I CAN even do therapy. I like to think of it like this … just as we can usually only feel how unloved we have been in the presence of love now, similarly if we feed our body the foods it evolved and thrived on the body is ‘loved’ in a basic biological sense. That feeling of well being can be an enormous aid in journeying through some of the very difficult terrain we have to confront in the course of therapy. It seems to me that feeling of well being is not only important but is, in fact, a ‘wormhole’ back to feelings. There is a notion in Primal Therapy that I have heard put this way ‘that you can’t feel truly good feelings until you have plumb the depths of the ‘bad feelings’ . This could be quite problematic in that it sets people on a quest for ‘bad feelings, and as you know if you go on a quest for something you usually find it!. Simpler I would say is to look for ‘well being’ with the aid of proper nutrition and exercise. From my experience, this is more authentic and more immediate while at the same time providing the basis for feeling ALL kinds of feelings rather the ‘one track mind’ of digging for ‘bad’ feelings in order that we can feel ‘good’ feelings. Another way of looking at this is the Primal project of ‘feeling pain’ can be a double edged sword and can and does, for some, create a kind of ‘hair shirt’ approach to life.
The bottom line is that to feel our pain we must take the best possible care of ourselves and in my view we must do that in the most natural way possible. Obviously this is an enormous topic and really difficult to encapsulate in a short essay. We can examine one aspect of conventional dietary advice which claims that fats are bad, fats will make you fat and fats will clog your arteries. The Paleo people say this is completely wrong. They point out humans and specifically infants have a great need for fat for their developing brains and that human infants are born with rolls of fat as opposed to say chimpanzees and further that a human mother’s milk is mostly composed of fat. In that way I see the anti fat mindset as a form of acting out our earliest and most damaging deprivations. Instead of nourishing fat initially in the form of mother’s milk and later from real food sources like fish, infants are often given the ‘fake boost’ provided by sugar, a ‘boost’ that is then sought with great and damaging results for the rest of our lives. Unfortunately this does not seem to be a problem that is getting better as the prevailing ideology is so ‘anti fat’ (Of course I am simplifying here as there are ‘bad’ fats, fats that have been altered by heat, light and oxygen, trans-fats, hydrogenated fats and so on, ‘sugar’ is also complicated but in general Paleo people say the sugar problem is not limited to ‘refined’ sugars like sodas but also includes our excessive reliance on grains, pasta, rice etc).
Interestingly Jean Liedloff touches on another aspect of this in “The Continuum Concept” when she says that infants EXPECT certain survival needs be met so strongly that they reach the status of “certainty”. What she is saying I think is that when an infant is born it’ has certain ‘expectations’. One example would be skin to skin contact with its mother, it has been inside the womb and now has a big change to deal with, so it’s important to KEEP the old connection, skin to skin as it was inside the womb, now even clothes feel ‘foreign’ or ‘alien’ to the infant, he can’t make sense of these foreign things . Similarly, in regards to nutrition certain foods, and ‘non foods’ are foreign or alien to us. So in that sense I suppose it is an analogy but just an an infant is ‘programmed’ to expect skin to skin contact etc………our bodies on a biological level expect certain foods. The Paleolithic Diet attempts to give the body what it ‘expects’ , what it evolved on, what it already ‘knows’. Just as the infant does not ‘know ‘ clothing for instance, our bodies do not ‘know’ non foods, pizza, processed foods etc etc. It is not what the body ‘expects’ .

Properly understood I see a great congruence and synergy between the Primal and Paleo worlds. There is no need to see any kind of ‘them or us’ I believe if Primal was infused and informed by the Paleo ideas it could really broaden and deepen it, make it more relevant to the actual world we live in and kind of get us out of what I call the ‘mental health ghetto’. Similarly for the Paleo people I do think they sometimes make the mistake of ‘trying to do everything through food’ possibly missing the life long lasting effects of childhood trauma and are often a bit mystified having done everything perfectly re food that they still feel bad. They wonder why and continue trying to tweak their diet. At this point in my life I feel lucky and privileged to operate on BOTH fronts, and it is a beautiful combination, I am never bored and there is always something to work on. I no longer feel I am trying to ‘squeeze out’ feelings but that I am operating on health in the true and full sense of the word.

This as I say is an enormous topic but I would like to close by saying thankfully as far as I know ALL my intestinal issues have cleared up, my blood sugar numbers (diabetes) are quite a bit better than the ‘normal’, for the first time (and this is not the only reason) I can participate in Primal therapy in a way that at least to me is quite satisfactory and I feel a joy and love of life that I never had before. Oh and I can’t remember the last time I had to go to the dentist which was all too common before. So I may have ‘arguments’ from others about this but I have no argument with myself. Patrick

### sooperb — 2014-03-02T17:54:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/patricks-journal.9979/post-113068>

Ok Patrick. At the top left of the header you will see your forum name. If you click on that it brings up a box, in the box is "signature". If you click that it will open a dialogue box where you can enter your details. It is limited but I think it's reasonably generous. Your photo you can add where it says Avatar (in the same box). This allows you to load directly from your computer. You might have to reduce it first, you will need to experiment.

I didn't mean swimming against the tide in here, I think most of us are swimming in the same direction and going with the flow. I meant outside in life itself. I have a huge problem trying to restrain myself from blathering on about the good things I've found and I suspect I would have made a good missionary, I'm always keen to share when I find something that I just know is right. I imagine my nearest and dearest are both used and resigned to it now but strangers can get a bit of a shock when they make a passing comment on something and get a full blown Soo lecture about why and how and when they should or shouldn't use it, eat it, sit on it etc.

I once overheard a chap in the health food shop asking the woman assistant some questions. Now much as I like most of what they sell, I always hold this mantra in the back of my mind, "don't ask them anything" because they are in business for just one reason, to make sure you don't leave their premises without a bag full of bottles and lotions lol. So, once she'd finished talking her mostly nonsense, I felt myself pulled in his direction and entered into conversation with him because he'd been talking about diabetes about which I know a thing or two. We had a lovely conversation, he had been recently diagnosed Type ll and was information gathering and I was able to point him in a sensible direction about where to gather the best information that would help him. He was pleased, I felt happy that I'd been able to save another human. Oh alright, maybe save is too strong a word, perhaps enlightened is better.

Ah Preston North End. My son is a huge fan. He was at the funeral despite never having met Sir Tom, a gentleman and an exceptional footballer. Preston itself is, erm, um, horrid. I live outside fortunately, not rural but not really urban. It's a compromise lol.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/patricks-journal.9979/post-150923>

LOL. If you are not an exception, try to be the example. Everything starts with today Pat. This is where it all begins. New hope is the point of departure with a lack of explanations. You can't change where you come from, but you can change where you go from here, right now. Just like a lyric in a song, if you don't like the ending, you can make up a new one. Change comes easy in Christmas cheer.

### patgrif@hotmail.com — 2014-12-24T17:16:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/patricks-journal.9979/post-150925>

Thanks Dr Kruse I DO need to 'remind' myself the future and present is more important than the past. I have spent much of my life 'regretting' the past and even spent a lifetime in 'therapy' doing it

I tried to 'send' the whole results as much as I could but couldn't so decided instead to concentrate on the ones Dr Kruse mentioned as of being important plus some I am particularly interested in.

As a 'diabetic type 2' these are interesting, Keep in mind I was 'diagnosed' 11 years ago and I take NO medications, supplements etc of any kind. I pretty much follow a strict 'ketogenic' diet at least at this time of the year (winter in LA). I don't think those numbers are 'too bad' I accept of course 'too bad' in one thing , optimal another and I would like to be optimal. Dr K has said Hbaic should be at 5 but again I believe a few years ago I was around 7.
Fasting Glucose............122
HbAic............................5.7
Uric Acid.......................7.5

Dr Kruse has stressed the meaningfulness of BUN/creatinine as a measure of de-hydration. I believe he said somewhere it should be under 10 so 15 is 'not so good' if not 'terrible either the way I understand it. Interestingly I am thirsty a lot and I drink a lot of water about 7 or 8 gallons a week of Sparkletts water (delivered)
BUN.............................15.0
Creatinine......................1.01
BUN/Creatinine.............15.0

Dr K has stressed on VitD and I put it in with Calcium as there is a connection there. I started on this 'health' journey right around 3 years ago when a test showed VitD at 20 and the doctor recommended supplementation. I am 'allergic' to supplements and pills so I decided to go to the beach instead. I was one who had bought into this whole 'the sun is dangerous for skin cancer' and AVOIDED the sun for many years. Being Irish in LA I was told fair skin gave me a greater risk of skin cancer. What was interesting to me at the time was after 2 days at the beach my MOOD was so much better. I tend towards 'depression' and 'pessimism' as a person and typically was/am concerned with 'global warming' 'ozone depletion' or just about anything I suppose. I was amazed on as I say the effect on my 'mood' and in a way kind of have not looked back since. Shortly after I discovered Dr K and instantly felt he was on the right track. So I am gratified to see my VitD much better
Calcium............................9.3
Magnesium......................1.8
VitD................................50.3

Dr K has stressed 'inflammation' and here I don't do so well it seems
Homocysteine..........................12.1
CRP-HS....................................1.7

In spite of a quite 'high fat' diet I don't think there is too much concere on the cholesterol numbers not that I think high fat is a problem or necessarily attack so much value to these numbers anyway here they are
Total Cholesterol.....................172
Triglycerides............................63
LDL.........................................100
HDL.........................................59
VLDL.......................................13
Chol/HDL ration...................2.9

The doctor I saw raised some concerns about the Thyroid numbers. He said the TSH was 'low' and the T4 'high' and wondered where the T4 was coming from. Raised the possibility of Hashimoto's

TSH...........................0.872
Thyroxine..................11
T3 Uptake..................30
Free Tyroxine Index....3.3
T3............................89
T3 free serum.........2.8
Reverse T3.................20.2
T4 free....................1.62
Tyroid Peroxide.........8
Tyroidglobulin Anti..........<1

Some blood numbers
WBC................5.3
RBC.................4.94
Hemoglobin..........158
Hematocrit...........465
Iron Binding.........300
VIBC....................200
Iron Serum...........100
Iron Saturation....33
Ferritin...................94

The doctor I saw raised a bunch of issues. Said I likely had a gut infection he speculated I had low hydrocloric acid as a result. Said there was evidence of 'tissue distruction' or maybe the liver was overtaxed with too much protein. Said I should eat more vegetables. Said the conversion from T4 to T3 was not good. Speculated I had 'adrenal fatigue' also speculated I had low B12 and low folate. 'speculated' quite a lot wanted to put me on some herbs for 'gut infection' but I am not convinced I have that or that his 'cure' would work

I wish I could send the whole file if people here think it's worth it I can painstakingly write it all down and send it. I appreciate ANY feedback including 'speculations' and suggestions or comparisons etc. Thanks for your patience

### Jack Kruse — 2014-12-25T05:32:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/patricks-journal.9979/post-150959>

there is a lesson here for the young............a young person filled with wellness should break their mirror early. Sometimes we can’t see what’s right in front of us, because it’s behind us, and we’re looking in a mirror.

### patgrif@hotmail.com — 2014-12-25T08:02:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/patricks-journal.9979/post-150967>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=150959)
>
> there is a lesson here for the young............a young person filled with wellness should break their mirror early. Sometimes we can’t see what’s right in front of us, because it’s behind us, and we’re looking in a mirror.

Funny you should say that. This is a (true I believe) event written by somebody I know "Not long ago B. and I were visiting our daughter E. in Boston when something rather odd happened. The three of us were sitting outside E. apartment when we noticed a middle aged woman walking directly towards us. When she approached the area where we were sitting I saw she was holding a rather cumbersome mirror precisely and steadily to the front of her face. We watched her as she walked up and down the street in an almost deliberate manner never once moving that mirror from its exact location . E. explained that this had gone on for years and that she was known as “The Mirror Lady” in and around Boston. Apparently not much has been established about her background but day after day,week after week, relentlessly she would walk up and down the same general area always holding tightly to that rather large mirror, carefully adjusting it until it was in just the right position. Over dinner that night we discussed what might be the meaning behind the behavior. Clearly this was a tragic situation and a highly disturbed individual, but why the mirror? We talked about the possibility that she felt less alone with this image of herself staring back at her or possibly there was something she was searching for or trying to see as she examined herself in that mirror. At some point and with further thought E. proposed another theory altogether ” I really don’t think it is any of those things, I believe the mirror allows her to see what is behind her” she said. Somehow this rang true for B. and I, it just felt right, but at the same time we knew we could never really know with any real certainty. Periodically I would find myself thinking about the Mirror Lady and I believe I now know why. Maybe in some way it was not so odd after all. Was it possible that we all walk through life with our own version of that mirror? That we are all afraid of what is behind us and in our own unique way we too search to find the ability to trust . "

And my reply "
In a funny way though I feel that Primal Therapy itself can become a form of looking in the mirror. Sometimes I think of primal therapy as like the rear view mirror , or the reverse gear in a car – useful at times but not something that should be used all the time especially if you want to drive the car. Something that is there and can be quite vital and necessary at times but better to use you front windscreen and the other gears if you want to move forward.

Just a cautionary notion before a lot of people come on here saying how tragic the Mirror Lady is and how sorry they feel for her. Sadly I feel for a lot of people (including myself) in primal therapy have relied on the rear view mirror too much. We have also been driving in reverse a lot of the time. One of the philosophers (Kierkegaard I think) said something like life can only be understood backwards but can only be lived forwards. In my own case I find myself constantly ‘reviewing’ my life backwards and it was this very tendency that made primal therapy so appealing. It sort of fitted perfectly with my ‘neurosis’ you might say. So then primal therapy becomes at once the solution but is also the problem in that it reinforces the very tendency that is problematic and that it is trying to ‘solve’.

Anyway maybe getting in the weeds a bit there but Kierkegaard also talked about if you doubt the mind as something that can know reality you can never get out of that loop because the very thing (the mind) that could as it were get you out of it is already in doubt. He was writing in the 1800’s when there was a lot of concern about things like that but it can be applied to a more modern situation or in psychology. Can we heal ourselves since our system is already distorted and all out attempts at healing will be run through that distorted program?. You can see this more easily with say drug addicts where the very attempt at healing themselves leads them further astray and after the drugs they are more depleted and desolate than ever. I feel ‘primal people’ should be a bit cautious about this and some aspects of Primal have ‘drug like’ aspects to it. Years of driving in reverse gear and looking in the rear view mirror can leave results that dont look too good

### patgrif@hotmail.com — 2014-12-25T22:13:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/patricks-journal.9979/post-150998>

Dr K said the website was undergoing a big revamp. My 'suggestion' would be on the Forum not to give a 'like' the same status as an actual post. To me it clutters up the blog too much and adds to the feeling of 'over-whelm' when actually I think it would feel more manageable if there were less posts and that can be achieved by NOT making a 'like' the same as a new post. Also I tend to think of this 'like business' tends to encourage a kind of 'people pleasing' atmosphere that I am not sure is needed. We are all here to learn and express ourselves not necessary to be 'flattered'. Just my take maybe others disagree.

### patgrif@hotmail.com — 2015-01-01T04:30:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/patricks-journal.9979/post-151284>

I had a Skype session with Dr Kruse on Monday and I see now why people go on about the benefits of 'live events'. This was not 'live' just Skype after all but I have to say it inspired me, I got a very good feeling from Dr K not that I would expect anything different but well I know of course about the brilliance of his intellect but well I can feel intimidated by someone like him. But I have to say he was down to earth, accessible in every which way. The last 3 days I have been CT'ing 'like a madman' it is cold out here in LA these days and between whipping along the PCH on my bike and jumping in the ocean..........well I feel a new day dawning. Up to now I kind of did all the stuff I was 'supposed' to but it's like something was missing. The thing is I am by myself out here and I think it is hard sometimes to be 'inspired' all on your own, talking to Dr K gave me a glimpse of what 'community' might mean how the power of that can mean so much.

For that reason I feel Yew is on the right track with what he is saying and I look forward to what he comes up with. For myself I have 'some' science I have taken here courses in Chemistry, Physiology, Biology, Anatomy etc, this is all years ago but it means I am not totally at sea even if some of it is difficult for me. But I am learning too and have really enjoyed catching up on the "Electric Universe" ideas and now some of the quantum biology stuff. But my background is really 'psychology' specifically 'primal therapy' and I find the congruence and differences I tease out (in my own mind) fascinating. I feel Dr K may well have the potential to totally revamp psychology as is mostly do away with it. But to me it could be worth going into this a bit more, after all 'psychology' is a big business and lots and lots of 'smart' people have put their minds to it. And mental health is not a small matter it is all part of overall 'health'

Anyway and I don't mean to be presumptuous or pushy here but if Yew gets his thing going I could imagine going into this in more depth. Maybe it is an example of what the 'community' might be able to contribute and I can imagine there would be many other people with different interests and specialties. I can imagine 'editing' it all could be tough it can't be some kind of 'free for all' or it can be but that's kind of what we have on the Forum already. Anyway just to say I feel supportive of what Yew is proposing and am ready to contribute if needed and if I can get all my thoughts sorted out about psychology and Dr K's stuff. Once I REALLY understand 'quantum entanglement' I might really have something to say........

### Inger — 2015-01-01T11:07:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/patricks-journal.9979/post-151299>

I love it, Patrick............ I just love your post above
it does is so hard at times to inspire one self totally alone..... and it feels so blessed to get inspired and "dragged up" and pushed a bit...lol.. by others......
community sure is magic stuff. It always interested me too, since many years.... psychologically.. what potential it has. Truly amazing!

### patgrif@hotmail.com — 2015-01-02T03:33:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/patricks-journal.9979/post-151347>

Thanks Inger, yes it seemed to make a big difference. Then over the weekend I did CT a lot ocean and also riding my bike. I like that kind of CT also the wind is very cooling like a radiator effect you could say. Anyway the Skype happened on Monday and I am still all 'charged up' and doing much CT and eating much fish. Once I REALLY understand 'quantum entanglement' I might really have something to say........

Now this is for Patrick to understand entanglement better:
Coral has no eyes, no ears, and no brain, Patrick. Coral's do something rather amazing to survive.
Their life cycles use some quantum communication. You Pat, might describe it like a mystical marine riddles. So you have to ask what is the secret behind coral’s “telepathic” communication. Every year on the same day, at the same hour, usually within the same minute, corals of the same species, although separated by massive distances will suddenly spawn in perfect synchronicity (think circum-annual cycles). The dates and times vary from year to year for reasons that only the coral knows when this will happen. Stranger still, while one species of coral spawns during one hour, another species right next to it waits for a different hour, or a different day, or a different week before spawning in synchronicity with its own species. So how do they know how to do this Pat? In the studies of coral, distance seems to have no effect; In fact, if you broke off a chunk of coral and placed it in a bucket beneath a sink in LA or Ireland, that chunk would, in most cases, spawn at the same time as other coral of the same species around the world in the oceans!!!

The answer? Entanglement. They are all connected. None of them need fancy sensory systems to pull it off. They just need to be connected in a deep subatomic way.

The synchronous spawn is essential for corals’ survival, Pat. Coral colonies must continuously expand outward to thrive. To remain healthy and strong, they must breed outside of their gene pool with neighboring colonies. This means they must communicate among and within their environment in a deeply complex fashion. Once released to the surface, the coral sperm and eggs have only about thirty minutes to fuse. Any longer, and the coral eggs and sperm will either dissipate or die off in the ocean. Researchers have found that if the spawning is just fifteen minutes out of sync, coral colonies’ chances of survival are massively reduced. It seems corals pull it off. Today, the Great Barrier Reef is dying at an astounding pace. 50% dead in 50 years since we began using non native EMF in the photic zone of the ocean. Why do I pay attention to the Great Barrier reef Pat? It is the largest living creature on planet Earth. So when it dies it is mankind's canary in the coal mine. Coral is the largest biological structure on the planet and covers some 175,000 square miles of the seafloor. Its reproduction ability is intimately tied to its ability to communicate via entanglement. If it can't entangle its electrons and protons or photons it dies...........It can communicate in a way far more sophisticated than anyone ever thought. And when you understand it.......there is a great lesson for man here in its story. I will now remind you of something more shocking Pat, coral is one of the most primitive animals on Earth, yet it still has the ability to harness the spark of life to communicate. I will remind you again, coral has no eyes, no ears, and no brain. Entanglement allows for amazing natural abilities.............so it is critical you get how primordial its ability is and how it can lend all living things a huge boost.

### Josh — 2014-03-03T01:38:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/antihistamine-alternatives.9980/post-113126>

Worms, I am 95% improved so far over last year with nothing except when I get really tired a little extra Pregnenolone seems to help with the mild nasal symptoms. Makes sense as I probably steal all of the Pregnenolone in my body to make cortisol to deal with external and internal stress which does include the worms themselves. It is a dynamic system with limited resources; so if I mismanage them, I figure things may go out of wack until I settle down and rest and hopefully restore a little... That usually means a few extra hours rest and meditation.

# Thread: Japanese Ama
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/japanese-ama.9981/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-03-02T10:26:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/japanese-ama.9981/post-113029>

This from Ranulph Fiennes' new book _Cold_:

> But although in theory humans, like other animals, may acclimatize to cold on an individual level, proof of such an adaptation process is lacking, except in the case of Japanese _ama_, women pearl divers who have a unique tolerance of cold water. Their bodies show a marked ability to shut down their surface blood vessels so that the blood flow to the skin, and even to the underlying muscles, is limited. This reduces heat loss.

Interesting. I can't recall hearing of these women before.

He continues:

> ... growing up in a cold climate will also have a direct influence on cold-protective mechanisms. Studies of laboratory piglets raised in cold rooms have shown that their legs will be shorter than the norm, due to less blood circulating in their limb growing points. A similar effect may underlie the general stature of the Eskimo.

I recalled at this point that the Mongolians (who, incidentally have a fairly paleo diet) also tend to be stocky and strong but not particularly tall. They may also have limbs that are relatively short in proportion to their size: that I don't know.

One sometimes hears people in Paleo with very schematic notions making large claims about food and stature. Contrary to what they claim, populations that have cereal grains in the diet havee not always been short -- I'll forbear to give examples: I'm sure everyone here already knows that -- so long as the rest of the diet is good.

(I'm not, of course, recommending that anyone eat cereal grains -- I'd recommend the contrary! -- merely pointing to a physiological fact in the interests of truth and accuracy.)

Now it seems that cold may affect stature and build, too.

These things are complex. Everything is not always about the food.

I thought that maybe, as we almost certainly would notice the difference if I switched the heater off completely, what if I left it on during the day and switched it off during the night? Our body heat should be enough to keep it warmish while we sleep, enough so that my OH doesn't notice, but this would be pointless if the EMF lingered in any way.
