No resolvins and protectins from DHA to blunt the pain........O6's magnify the the pain response because their breakdown products are all tied to excessive creation of inflammatory prostaglandins in the face of no resolvins and protectins from DHA in cell membranes. [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/8397297](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/8397297) "

"You also need DHA in those circuits to capture electrons and build up working memory to move electrons to and fro. This is why so many people complain of a cognitive haze with AF, menopause, and **CFS.** Then you need to be able to move those electrons in that neural pathway to create consciousness. Consciousness is not all or none.......it is a continuum tied to electron collection and flow in these circuits."

"Why does cortisol help AI folks for s short time? Cortisol removes electrons from the mix so it stops immune activation of the T regulator cells in the local immune reactions. This is the fundamental physical break in AI disease as a common tie. Get your microbiome tested I bet you see it simplified and altered. I'd be surprised if you don't have dysbiosis. **The cause is likely Calcium efflux** from your environment. Most cases are. Every study on Autoimmune disease **(including fibro)** that has investigated dysbiosis and leaky gut has found it."

"@Christy I cant practice medicine on here but the treatment hack I use for **Fibromyalgia** is among the most extensive I do and it requires 18-36 months to reverse. It is a complete system failure and dosing is all custom done to blood work."

**"Let’s talk about chronic fatigue patients.** Do they suffer from the same condition but on a more subacute level? Yes they do. Patients with **ME/CFS** patients usually have dramatic increases than normal levels of ventricular lactate in the CSF, and 36% less GSH in the cortical regions of the brain. The cortex is bathed in surface CSF that usually is not able to “proton superconduct” for many reasons. Fluoride is a dielectric blocker in CSF. The industrial omega 6 PUFA fats in the surface of their cell membranes in their cortical neurons are the most common reasons for avalanche collapse of proton super conduction in “CSF water” by displacing iodine and causing an up regulation of lactate production to use as a fuel when ketones are not present for energy production to make maximum ATP. When we have an energy inefficiency in the brain, we get neurodegenerative disorders soon following. This is why Alzhemer’s disease and metabolic syndrome are bedfellows. It is also why so many **fibromyalgia** and fatigue patients also have similar phenotypes.

Neurons in the CNS and PNS **love lactate,** especially when they are suboptimal in ATP production or suffering from a neurodegenerative disease. Neurons become inefficient because of poor mitochondrial function due to lowered ATP production. This poor ATP recycling is the signal to the neurons and the brain mitochondria that they are trying to increase proton superconduction in CSF (density of CSF water) to improve CMRO2 and the current in the brain’s other semiconductor, water. The higher CMRO2 is, the less the brain relies on ATP and the more it uses water superconduction. It is like a high octane fuel for neurons. Patients with **ME/CFS** are very energy inefficient and subsequently they have high lactate levels in their CSF because they ARE energy inefficient and poor ATP recyclers. The lactate supply in the brain is made by up-regulation of MCT1 which is called mono carboxylic acid transporter 1. People with **ME/CFS generally generate a lot of superoxide in the brain** as the cause of their disease and not being able to make a lot of ATP and this is why they eat way to many carbs to fast recycle ATP. This never allows them to open their proteins fully to open the maximum amounts of water binding sites to foster water/proton super conduction.

Neurons in the brain try to avoid superoxide generation at all costs and neurons usually attempt to increase lactate production as a consequence to offset superoxide generation to use lactate as a secondary fuel source to ketones. Most **people with ME/CFS do not use ketones** for electrons along their inner mitochondrial membrane for ECT; **instead they use glucose** for fuels because they are no longer metabolically flexible due to the defects of protein folding at cytochrome 1 (NADH) because of what I wrote about in [EMF 4](http://www.jackkruse.com/emf-4-why-might-you-need-carbs-for-performance/). They **use too much complex one** because of glucose and glutamate fuels, and they try to run their mitochondria on NADH complex one. It can lead to mis-folding if they cannot make enough ATP to complete the task. When this fails, you activate apoptosis pathway and some of the things I spoke about in the recent [webinar](http://www.jackkruse.com/april-webinar-2013/) I did.

When you have metabolic syndrome you really want to access ECT at FADH2, or complex two in electron chain transport on the inner mitochondrial membrane instead using FFA or the ketogenic diet to bypass this issue in these diseases. When one fuels their diet with glucose/carbs, you are increasing ROS and superoxide as a consequence. The more you use these fuels, the more you increase ROS and superoxide and limit your ability to make ATP. I covered this in big detail in [EMF 4](http://www.jackkruse.com/emf-4-why-might-you-need-carbs-for-performance/).

_**(note to self: check out EMF 4)**_

**Electron dense foodstuffs** supply the best possible neuronal FADH2:NADH (F/N) ratio of 0.2 to minimize ROS and superoxide generation. Moreover, this is why this pathway of electrons gives one FADH2 for 5 NADHs when you do this to minimize superoxide maximally. I talked about this very issue in the [quantum electron blog post](http://www.jackkruse.com/do-food-electrons-impart-a-quantum-effect/) over two years ago. The more NADH you make the higher lactate will be made to offset the glucose in the brain. This shows you why the brain really **does not like nor need a lot of glucose** as a first line fuel contrary to what most believe or regurgitate. Ketones and lactate support fat metabolism optimally in neuronal use from foods/fuels to protect the brain."

### Martin — 2014-11-26T19:47:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-chronic-fatigue-syndrome-cfs-myalgic-encephalomyelitis-me-thread.10052/post-148578>

**Onset**

_"The majority of CFS cases **start suddenly**,\[34\] usually accompanied by a "[**flu-like illness**](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Flu-like_illness)"\[5\] while a significant proportion of cases begin within several months of severe adverse stress.\[34\]\[35\]\[36\] An Australian prospective study found that after infection by viral and non-viral [pathogens](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pathogen), a sub-set of individuals met the criteria for CFS, with the researchers concluding that "post-infective fatigue syndrome is a valid illness model for investigating one pathophysiological pathway to CFS".\[37\] However, accurate prevalence and exact roles of infection and stress in the development of CFS are currently unknown.."_

I'm a little embarrassed.

I missed the connection as to why my problems came on _suddenly_.

When I crashed over five years ago, it started me on a research journey that brought me to know so many fundamental things about health and this site is far and away the most valuable. I knew one of the keys was the fact that one week I was doing very well, and the next, very poorly.

How did I miss it? Poor search terms? Assumptions about "chronic fatigue" and "fibro" that stopped me searching because those are such popular catch-all terms?

I went in another direction at first, thinking it was Lyme's, then discovered the mold connection, and began to think that biotoxin illness was at the root of my sickness.

I mentioned that "sudden" event it to all my docs, but it raised no discussion about it having precipitated my CFS/ME/MCS. I had to start somewhere so I went going after mold after the HLA DR results came back and some of Shoemaker's tests showing elevated results that indicated mold, but some of the markers were negative. Remediated my home at great expense. Changed my habits. Took CSM for five months with no change in symptoms. Went down the methylation trail. Not much difference, but some.

It's all good, but it still _nagged_ at me that that viral event caused such a huge drop in the quality of my life. While searching one day last week I came across "post-exertional malaise" and "80% of CFS patients had flu-like illness", and I was stunned at how a simple wikipedia entry described my experience almost perfectly. I'm beginning to think that mold, methylation, SNP's, et al, are _aggravated_ by this fall off the cliff, not the cause, per se.

My curiosity about how things fail has been with me since I was a child, and I love to learn how things work and how to fix them. I'm looking for a pathophysiological reason why a virus would hammer someone **_so suddenly_**. Huge methylation crash? I'd welcome any thoughts on this.

NSH

I've been thinking "huge methylation "crash myself ,caused by huge ROS accumulation. Insane amount of chronic cardio and a 80% carb Pritikin Diet+ a huge number of Methylation related SNPs(23 & me).

Mainly viral diseases such as eppstein-baar virus etc, obviously chronic stage issues like CFS and fibro. My doctor is now using it for chronic lyme s well. Some really bad cases and the response is there and the change of direction slowly follows the response. Youcan do it at home at almost no costs and longterm. # Thread: Reductive science?  Reductio ad absurdum
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/reductive-science-reductio-ad-absurdum.10053/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-03-07T08:38:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/reductive-science-reductio-ad-absurdum.10053/post-113944>

Here's an example of the scientific method gone mad.

You start from the reasonable premise that it's best to try to limit variables; you pursue that to the point of absurdity; you end up with a totally artificial result that tells you nothing useful.

I got this one from Peter's blog:

[http://high-fat-nutrition.blogspot.co.uk/2014/03/would-you-like-soya-oil-poured-over.html](http://high-fat-nutrition.blogspot.co.uk/2014/03/would-you-like-soya-oil-poured-over.html)

As he puts it:

> ... the same Aussie pile of wallanga pertaining to report science

I think we've got a malapropism there, and by "pertaining" he means "purporting". But never mind, I haven't Peter's skills: I can spot that kind of thing, but I can't do something as complex as operate on a cat with appendicitis. And we get the drift.

Why is it a pile of codswallop? Well the the media story he links:

[http://mobile.news.com.au/national/...s-bad-as-smoking/story-e6frfku9-1226845436762](http://mobile.news.com.au/national/breaking-news/protein-diets-nearly-as-bad-as-smoking/story-e6frfku9-1226845436762)

says:

> They tested 25 different diet combinations on 900 mice to see what happened to their appetite, metabolic health, ageing and lifespan.

Uh, uh. It _wasn't_ just "mice". Peter looked it up and it turns out they used:

> the standard crippled C57BL/6 mouse strain.

I seem to recall the notorious T. Colin Campbell has pulled the trick of swapping out the mice strains when he doesn't get the result he wants. Here they've cut straight to the chase and started with a crippled strain. Not only is this mice not humans, _it's not even normal mice_.

So what did they do. In Peter's words:

> They fed umpteen macronutrient ratios to umpteen mice and looked at the longevity.

Here's where it gets interesting -- and here's what the Australian news report _doesn't_ tell you. This special crippled strain of mice can't eat saturated fat: it "breaks their brains". No problem …

> A brief trip to supplementary data gives what the diets were made of:
>
> "Diets varied in content of P (casein and methionine), C (sucrose, wheatstarch and dextrinized cornstarch) and F (soya bean oil)".

In fine, the study tells us nothing about humans; it wasn't even done with normal mice; they weren't even fed a normal diet. Who among us breakfasts on a mixture of casein, sucrose, and soya oil?

OK, they tested 25 different macronutrient ratios in their _reductio ad absurdum_ experiment, but they might as well have not bothered.

Final quote from Peter:

> DO NOT CONSUME BULK CALORIES AS PUFA, ESPECIALLY OMEGA-6 PUFA.

Final quote from the news source:

> "\[This study\] is an enormous leap in our understanding of the impact of diet quality and diet balance on food intake, health, ageing and longevity," Prof Simpson says.

An enormous leap? What utter nonsense! I think people in the biological sciences really must be out to make themselves a laughing stock among real scientists.

# Thread: Suicidal children
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/suicidal-children.10055/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-03-07T11:01:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/suicidal-children.10055/post-113957>

There was a poster in the train this morning. It was a charity appeal, as many of the posters in trains are. The idea is that you text a particular word to the number given and the charity gets money. (This shows, by the way, shows how ubiquitous playing with your mobile is on the train is. But that's another story.)

The poster said:

> The NSPCC's Childline is receiving 33% more calls from suicidal children.

I paraphrase. That was the gist of it, the pitch if you like.

This does sound ominous to me, but perhaps it's worth stating the _caveats_ first. First, a number like that means very little on its own: they didn't give the period over which the increase had occurred; they didn't give the numbers but just the percentage. So there can be blips in anything from month to month, whereas an increase on a monthly average from one year to another would be significant. And a 33% increase on a small number is still a small number. Secondly, this doesn't necessarily mean that there are more suicidal children around: maybe it's just that this service is now better known. Perhaps previously they rang another service, or perhaps they told their parents or a teacher or their doctor or a friend instead.

I should think this probably does represent a significant increase in calls, however, and I'd not discount the possibility that there are now more children having suicidal thoughts. (I wonder if there are some more reliable statistics on that.) If so, this is concerning. We generally think of children as very resilient, both physically and mentally, and I think they are up to a point. Look at the rubbish people feed them without breaking their metabolism! How often do you see an outsize mum leading a little slim fairy-like girl by the hand? But maybe the limits of their resilience are being reached.

There's this worry lurking at the back of my mind that we may have got to the point where all the "environmental mismatches" that Jack talks about may be frying children's brains. Some children have always had to put up with difficult social circumstances, bullying at school, violence at home, and all the rest of it. But this kind of burden will now be coming to them on top of a biological being that's been compromised by poor food choices, and specifically fat-denuded food, sugar-laden sodas, lack of sleep, TV, light-pollution ... and now ubiquitous wi-fi signals, mobile phones (the cynical exploitation of children by mobile-phone companies is a story in itself) ... and all the rest of it.

### sooperb — 2014-03-07T11:07:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/suicidal-children.10055/post-113959>

In line with your thinking, there is the increased pressure from social media. While they are frying their brains with all the gadgets, the keyboard bullies seem also to be driving mental stress via all the sites that have been specifically targeting youngsters. It's hard to know how to apply the brakes.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-08T13:29:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/suicidal-children.10055/post-114129>

> [sooperb said:](/goto/post?id=113959)
>
> While they are frying their brains with all the gadgets, the keyboard bullies seem also to be driving mental stress via all the sites that have been specifically targeting youngsters.

That's an aspect I had forgotten. I saw a newspaper hoarding this morning with the headline: "12 year old girl tried to commit suicide after being targeted by internet bullies".

I guess whether there's any more bullying going on now it's partially shifted to the virtual realm or not is another imponderable. But possibly: it seems possible that the anonymity of the internet and the "remoteness" of the victim could encourage that. The thought that tends to come up in my mind is: "Why try to kill yourself (or make a "cry for help" if that's what it is) rather than unplugging?" I guess it shows that if they're not online some kids have little else to do. That seems ominous.

I think the whole upbringing of children in the UK is misguided, though. It's quite another topic, but I think it's ludicrous that they're sent to school before five and begin immediately on what's known as "forced learning". AFAIK, they don't start till seven in much Scandinavia or some other parts of Europe. They shouldn't be sitting for protracted periods and doing tasks like reading and writing for which many of them are not yet ready -- not even at a basic physiological level. Politicians keep offering earlier admittance for more children and also all kinds of academic "testing" younger and younger. The former seems more about providing daycare for children whose parents don't want to look after them (and who possibly do need two incomes with the way our economy currently works). The latter -- the testing -- seems to me to be competitiveness gone mad, and feeds some of the worst instincts in the parents. Scandinavian children aren't "behind" as the term is by the time they leave school at 18 are they? Get off the kids' backs and let them be children.

At five they should be exploring the world, seeing what the sand and water do, learning rhymes and songs, finding out how to catch and throw and climb and so on -- and, of course, learning about friendships and how to relate to others to take turns. Even coordinating the eye-movement you need for reading can be a problem for small ones, and there they are trying to use fine motor skills with pens when they haven't mastered gross motor skills. (In fact, IIRC, it's not even possible to use scissors till the bones in wrist are ready.) The politicians are totally unaware of all this. They also like to curry favour with the electorate by directing occasional abuse towards teachers -- failing teachers will be sacked and so on. But most of these people work pretty hard and have my sympathy. It's the way the whole system is set up that's the failure.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-08T16:28:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/suicidal-children.10055/post-114152>

What is the implication of this? Think EMF 5 and 7. Or EE 3. MHC1 gene had two major effects on primates......gave them cell mediated immunity and created explosive neurogenesis. This is where humanness lies. SO where will humanness begin to go away in an extinction? Last in evolution is first diseases out.......Now think AI's, ADHD, PTSD, Depression, Bipolar, Schizophrenia.......etc. That is what is up. If these kids live longer they get other neuroimmune diseases we call neuro degenerative disease and they will begin to show up earlier and earlier as the process speeds up epigenetically. GO look at a medical book from the 1940's on PD or AD. They tell you it is a disease of the elderly. Today we have people in their 30's getting them. This all has happened in less than 100 years.

### RobH — 2014-03-09T19:11:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/suicidal-children.10055/post-114336>

^^^^^ he's right. again. Shift last evening was emblematic of that. Of 23 patients seen, 4 were suicidal, 3 kids on ADHD drugs (rarely ever see kids in the ER who aren't.) Most patients carried some psychiatric diagnosis (usually bipolar or depression). 3 were intoxicated. 1 guy was on chronic disability from intermittent explosive disorder (IED) -- also had DM, HTN, obesity, and chronic pain -- he is 29. Another 37 yo with DM, HTN, obesity and pneumonia. Saw 6 elderly people 3 of which had severe dementia. Only 1 was really sharp, and she had COPD (I guess the smoking got her lungs before her brain)

4 people had already had cholecystectomies at some point. 2 of them were coming in for "pain the same as before I had my gallbladder out"

One more had irritable bowel syndrome, 1 with fibromyalgia.

And that's just what I can remember off the top of my head. I'm sure when I go back and sign all my dictations the rest will come back into focus.

The kids on adderall / ritalin / other psych drugs make me really sad. What a lousy start to life -- you can't imagine it will be getting much better for them.

I think during my entire shift I had one person who didn't carry multiple diagnoses of neolithic disease. She was only 25 and had a kidney stone which is a hydration issue, at least on the macro scale.

Many band-aids -- literal and figurative, were placed.

It is staggering.

RobH

### caroline — 2014-03-10T06:23:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/suicidal-children.10055/post-114468>

Rob - how do you keep all this from getting to you? Do you ever feel like you can make a difference for some of these folks? Other than pain relief or whatever?

Then you have to go home and put on a "happy" face for your wife and kids....

When you decided to go to med school ....you probably didn't have all the above in mind?

No wonder my doc likes to see me - I am never sick! I just go in to talk and get labs.

The last time I was in the ER .... The staff was just about as unhealthy ...Well - no one had dementia - I don't think!

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ketone-testing.10058/post-113979>

Hi
I have started testing my Ketone levels first thing in the morning but I keep getting negative results? I have tried various times of the day still negative? Does anyone have any idea why I am not going into ketosis? I hardly eat any carbs and have fat for breakfast & dinner and seafood for lunch?

### nicld — 2014-03-07T20:52:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ketone-testing.10058/post-114032>

Good question, I have the same issue.

From some of the research that I have done, you hydration level has some effect. If you drink as much water as Dr. K recommends, one might now see it. There are some times when I get it to register small on the sticks but that is rare.

I did see in my GI tests that I have poor fat absorption so I am wondering if that is having an effect on the results too and have methylation issues too.

Hope other people chime in.

Nicole

### cantweight — 2014-03-08T04:00:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ketone-testing.10058/post-114093>

Are you using the keto urine stix? I find them really unreliable. I have the opposite issue. Shows me high in ketones after just a day or two and even a high carb mishap day does not knock me out of ketosis according to the strips.
Ketone blood test would be more accurate...but the test stick things are pricey!

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnet-therapy-applied-to-stomach.10059/post-113980>

Hi guys,

I have had some success with using the magnetico sleep pad for my issues. In particular I have noticed better sleep, increased energy and lower heart palpitations. This has confirmed for me that the use of a magnetic field to the body can be beneficial.

The same people that make the magnetico also are looking into using real strong magnets to parts of the body to increase healing ([http://www.amri-intl.com/](http://www.amri-intl.com/)). I think this works by vastly increasing the DC current in the specific area.

I'm wondering if anyone knows more information about this. I'm hopeful that maybe if this real strong magnet is applied to my stomach then it will allow my body to heal itself. This treatment sounds unavailable and probably real expensive however.

Do you think it will work, what's your opinion on it?

I was suffering from a lot of structural damage at that time and could not walk. I was doing many other things, but I relied mostly on magnets to control my pain. That was the time before I learned about earthing. I noticed that magnetico was making it somewhat easier for me during the night, but it was still not enough. So, I started placing individual magnets on all of my injured areas and found a significant pain relief that way. Made a believer out of me, for sure.

### Josh — 2014-03-08T18:19:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnet-therapy-applied-to-stomach.10059/post-114162>

I will attempt not to shoot myself in the foot here...but it will probably happen anyway...

1.  Magnetico is a broad treatment for moving and balancing qi/energy. Something like needling many points on the body...It is sophisticated in how it does this, but subject to the same limitations and issues that challenge one with all broad treatments. It is all in the physics. Essentially when you move qi/energy, you need energy...think redox potential. So if one has a functioning water battery with near 100% efficiency that will supply the energy, everything may go "well". This will mean different things for different people based on how they have framed the experiment/treatment...all treatment is an "experiment" because as we know, N=1. In addition, the Magnetico seems to facilitate the functioning of people's "water batteries" to a point...
2.  There are issues with broadly moving qi/energy; such as running out of qi/energy, depleting it in the weaker organ systems, blocked areas-think collapsed matted collagen that will not conduct piezoelectric current or support nerve conduction or worse, and there is the perennial question of why isn't the body doing this itself??????
3.  So why would one have hypofunction in an organ system in the digestive tract? Vagal tone perhaps? Cerebellar degeneration due to a factor such as GAD65 antibodies that are triggered by gluten and things that cross react with gluten such as caesin and others? Cerebellar degeneration=decreased vagal tone due to lower Purkinje cell generation of piezo electricity for vagal tone/stimulation that triggers peristalsis in the stomach fro example? This is not a complete model as I am not a neurosurgeon...
4.  There are no generalizations about treatment in Chinese/Oriental Medicine because it is an N=1 system. I cringe every time I hear a Western trained scientist/doctor or anyone else make general statements about treatments because the most interesting thing about the system is its N=1 approach within a sophisticated conceptual framework (s). One might say that "generally a magnet of such and such strength applied to an individual with the following characteristics in such and such an environment at such and such time of year might be governed by the following parameters..." I will not even get into the possible politics and cultural issues about why a Chinese practitioner at a conference in Europe may or may not have made the statement the Dr. Bonlie quoted...
5.  Practically, Oriental Medicine has a lot to offer people with issues such as these. Typically points distal to the area affected are treated. There are also subsystems on the hands, face, head, feet and ears that map the whole body and can treat with varying efficacy...think the holographic stuff in the 2014 Q&A if you have heard it...Magnets would work to some extent in all of these systems and I have used them with varying efficacy along with all of the other ways of "stimulating" a point which is probably a vortex at a junction in the semiconducting collagen matrix that Dr. Kruse is illuminating so nicely for all of us...I am learning right along with everyone else...I have so much "phenomena" to explain...it is not funny...
6.  The famous example that is similar to this issue that is taught about the history of what we might call "modern" Oriental Medicine comes from the 1950's when a condition was presented to the allopathic and oriental practitioners in China for treatment. The story goes (I will call it a "story" because I was not there) that uterine prolapse was the condition and each group presented their treatment and results. The allopathic community returned with surgical approaches that bound the prolapsed organ up in more or less the proper place. This has longterm negative repercussion for health and does not fix the root of the problem which is "why can't the body just hold the organ in the right place and how does it do that anyway?" The oriental practitioners said we strengthen and balance the systems that support the function of the organ and hold it in place and the body pulls it back up by itself. Their demonstration of this was so effective that many allopathic doctors reputedly switched over and or at least embraced traditional treatment. We can now translate this as they restored the redox potential and the ability to maintain it so that the body could activate the muscles and possibly it is really the hydrated 3D collagen matrix to restore the position and function of the uterus. Doctors are still sewing uterus's and bladders back in place (think Da Vinci Robot) like crazy and oriental and functional medicine practitiners are still having decent results with compliant patients getting them to go back on their own. In comparison, improving peristalsis is easier.

I have probably said too much already, but the wind is blowing with a warm sun and it is spring and the yang is rising disturbing my shen and overacting on my stomach making it harder for me to descend my food in the proper manner (this model is a few thousand year old...) and making me a little "hutu" so take what you like and leave the rest.....

### Tanya — 2014-03-08T23:08:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnet-therapy-applied-to-stomach.10059/post-114209>

As I told Sean in my previous post, I do have Magnetico and will continue using it. As a matter of fact, I just recently upgraded it to the super level..... but the individual magnets would never leave my personal "first aid kit".

If I were to plan a trip right now, the 3 most important things for me to pack would be my toothbrush, coconut oil and my magnets - not necessiraly in that order...

### seanb4 — 2014-03-09T10:35:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnet-therapy-applied-to-stomach.10059/post-114286>

Yeah I think I'm going to concentrate on improving my "environment" by upgrading sleep pad, and getting more light for now. Then keep my eye on this therapy to see how it progresses and maybe try spot magnets later on.

### caroline — 2014-03-09T11:45:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnet-therapy-applied-to-stomach.10059/post-114299>

> [seanb4 said:](/goto/post?id=114164)
>
> Okay so the essence of what you are saying, Josh, is that you can't make the blanket statement that using individual magnets is bad for more than an hour per week because for some people it might be good, you need to try it for yourself.
>
> Whilst I do agree with this on some level, it also concerns me that there is a logical reason / theory why return field might be bad and you can explain people get good results as short term results.
>
> Whilst it is possible that Bonlie could be "making it up" or wrong to sell his sleep pads over cheaper versions, why would Jack back him up on this. Jack could be wrong too but he's been right on most things so far.
>
> I just don't want to spend my money on something that has a decent chance of hurting me in the long run.

As far as I can remember - Dr. K. had an issue with something and it was discussed here somewhere.

### caroline — 2014-03-09T11:48:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnet-therapy-applied-to-stomach.10059/post-114300>

> [Tanya said:](/goto/post?id=114167)
>
> 1\. No shooting in the foot - that absolutely did not happen...
> 2\. Information that you provided is very accurate and thorough
> 3\. I have a lot of confidence in the magnet therapy from my own experience and that is the only thing that matters to me. Just as you said n=1 here.

Tanya - can you talk more about the magnets you use .... there is a magnet thread somewhere that nonchalant started. It is all so very interesting!

### Tanya — 2014-03-09T19:27:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnet-therapy-applied-to-stomach.10059/post-114338>

Audrey, I don’t know how much more is there really to say about it, but I will try.

First of all, I should emphasize that the magnet therapy was only a part, albeit a significant part of my entire treatment process.

As you already know, there was a time when I could not even walk anymore because of all the irreparable damage due to my RA and I already had a magnetic pad

My luck began to change when someone mentioned to me about one chinese man who was able to help some people with very serious health problems. Actually, at first, I was rather skeptical about it, because I have already tried acupuncture several times before from a few different practitioners (2 of them also being chinese) and it was not helping me. But after we were told that he and his family had plans to move to CA, I decided that I should give it a try before it is too late.

It was not your typical health provider office (he had a massage table right in the middle of his living room), this older man and his wife were one of the nicest and genuine people you would ever hope to meet.

Even though he did not speak much English, I could tell right away how experienced and knowledgeable he was. I was getting so much from him while his fee was so little (he would not take any extra money when I tried to give it to him). I probably would still be in that wheelchair, if it would not have been for him and I was so upset when it came time for them to move - I was afraid that I will loose all that progress that I have made. Well, before they left, he had a present for me - a set of magnets. He showed me the points where I needed to place them and told me to continue massaging and exercising as he already taught me to do so.

That is exactly what I’ve been doing since then. I have purchased some more magnets and books about the magnet therapy since then. These magnets are such a small investment to make when you are getting so much more back from them and the books will describe the magnet therapy way better then I ever could do it justice....

### seanb4 — 2014-03-09T21:22:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnet-therapy-applied-to-stomach.10059/post-114363>

How many months did you use the spot magnets for?

How long before you were able to comfortably walk again?

### Tanya — 2014-03-09T22:00:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnet-therapy-applied-to-stomach.10059/post-114373>

> [seanb4 said:](/goto/post?id=114363)
>
> How many months did you use the spot magnets for?
>
> How long before you were able to comfortably walk again?

Sean, I am still not walking as comfortably as I would have liked - slight limp still remains, but I continue working on it.

I was in a wheelchair for several long month before I started improving and moved to using a walker and later a cane. Now all of that equipment is in the basement...

I have a structural damage in both of my ankles with no hope for it being repaired, I was told. They say that the body cannot repair the damaged cartilage. I might need to get another MRI done (have not had one since the first ones showed all the damage) to see what is happening there to explain that I am walking so well. Knowing now what I do about Robert O. Becker’s experiments, I really wonder how accurate all these doctors’ assessments have been.....

### Josh — 2014-03-09T23:21:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnet-therapy-applied-to-stomach.10059/post-114384>

Inspiring story, thank you.

### seanb4 — 2014-03-10T09:06:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnet-therapy-applied-to-stomach.10059/post-114484>

Cool, I hope that the MRI shows it is growing back!

### caroline — 2014-03-10T09:43:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnet-therapy-applied-to-stomach.10059/post-114489>

Inspiring indeed ...thanks Tanya for sharing that. I suppose because he didn't speak much English - he couldn't really explain much? where did he go?

You are one determined women .... but there really is no other option is there.....

I love what Mr. Pinkies says \[Kate} Impossible is just an option! Have you read her journal?

### caroline — 2014-03-10T09:28:49+0200

but he isn't smiling!! what's up with that? maybe his budgie smugglers are too tight?

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-10T11:34:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-is-cool.10060/post-114506>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=114487)
>
> but he isn't smiling!! what's up with that?

Looking into the sun, isn't he? It's making him screw his eyes up, and I guess that affects his expression. Since he wants the background ...

But you can get nice portraits taken into the sun, if it's not too bright and you use fill-in flash.

### caroline — 2014-03-10T11:50:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-is-cool.10060/post-114507>

you seem to know a little about everything..... how the heck do you do that?????

I gotta go to bed!

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-10T12:26:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-is-cool.10060/post-114512>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=114507)
>
> you seem to know a little about everything..... how the heck do you do that?????

The occasional photography book -- and just noticing things. Just seeing the eyes. AFAIK, photographers would often take a model and put her the other way round with her back to the sun. Now she won't screw her eyes up, but her face might be rather in shadow, so you throw a little light back the other way. Either fill-in flash, or an assistant holding up a reflector -- even a white sheet or a newspaper.

Talking of noticing things, this should amuse you. I'm just reading Eric Newby's _Love and War in the Apennines_ about when he was hiding from the Germans in Italy during WWII. (Very enjoyable, and said to be his masterpiece.) Newby said it was very much harder to escape in Italy than in Germany. This wasn't because the Italians were keen on the War -- far from it, many helped Allied troops stranded behind the lines. It was because Italians were very observant and very interested in clothes and fashions. A POW could saunter past Germans dressed in a "civilian coat" he'd made from a blanket but you'd never be able to get away with that with Italians.

> I gotta go to bed!

You've been up all night? You'd best do that!

### caroline — 2014-03-10T23:52:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-is-cool.10060/post-114613>

That is very funny about Italians and clothing! I always love all your observations........

# Thread: OMG my Hair!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/omg-my-hair.10063/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 38
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Shijin13 — 2014-03-07T21:46:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/omg-my-hair.10063/post-114037>

I'm sure many of you have a history of dull lifeless hair. I know I do! Add to it, I always had horrible cystic acne on my scalp. Fast forward to last fall when I went searching through a great thread (For some reason I can't find it!) but it was on optimal beauty products. I believe Fitness@home mentioned using Morroco method products. I'd used them in the past w/o great success...

we'll I've been using them for the past 8mths. my cystic acne is gone. I can get away with washing my baby fine hair once a week.

Yesterday instead of washing my hair I decided to do a hair mask, I added some lavender and tea tree oil to the zen detox last night instead of my usual. put it on, covered my hair w/plastic wrap, then wrapped a towel around my head. rinsed it out an hour later.... very few tangles (I get tangles in my hair no matter how often I wash/condition regardless of the product I use) there are times I want to take clippers to my hair and shave it all off...

today I woke up and my hair had this amazing wave in it. The henna I use to color my greys - is rich and deep. OMG what Happen? I have sex kitten hair!!!! WOOT!

### Tanya — 2014-03-07T22:22:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/omg-my-hair.10063/post-114040>

Gretchen, that is a great news - I am happy for you. I went to Morroco's website and there is, of course a choice of products... Is there a particular one that you liked better then the other? As far as coloring your greys - hmmmm, you don't look old enough to have any

### Shijin13 — 2014-03-07T22:45:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/omg-my-hair.10063/post-114042>

> [Tanya said:](/goto/post?id=114040)
>
> Gretchen, that is a great news - I am happy for you. I went to Morroco's website and there is, of course a choice of products... Is there a particular one that you liked better then the other? As far as coloring your greys - hmmmm, you don't look old enough to have any

Tanya I bought the healthy hair starter pack. One thing I did was mix the shampoos/conditioners with RO water to stretch it further. [https://www.morroccomethod.com/raw-store/packages/healthy-hair-starter-package-detail](https://www.morroccomethod.com/raw-store/packages/healthy-hair-starter-package-detail)

the first time around these products didn't work all that well for me... but then I wasn't paleo either... Its took my scalp about 6 wks to fully transition to the products and get to the point where I could easily wash my hair only 1x/week. I have found that the longer I use the products the more I don't use the conditioner. I will tell you that my favorite shampoo is different from Chaos's based upon the smell....

Since my hair is now past shoulder length, many times I just put it up to keep it out of my way.

I have grey around my entire face - I've been dying it for years. But I went to henna back in the fall and Haven't looked back... beautiful rich color.
here's where I buy it... [http://www.hennaforhair.com/](http://www.hennaforhair.com/)

### Inger — 2014-03-09T09:33:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/omg-my-hair.10063/post-114272>

I use Ghassul.. it is from Marocco too I think. It is meant for washing, hair and body. It is kind of slippery, like soap a little bit. Use it since many years and I love it. My hair gets better and better! I am growing it out now, it already have reached my shoulder and even a bit longer than that. If I feel my hair is too "dry" after washing and the curls are not too organized, I just brush a bit to straighten and spray with rain water. I so love how it makes my hair!

Also a mask once in a while with Silica gel, raw egg yolk, lemon juice and some EVOO makes my hair feel great. I put it on my face too. Raw egg yolk for face is pretty amazing

I wash my hair every other day.

### caroline — 2014-03-09T09:43:39+0200

### Inger — 2014-03-11T09:11:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/omg-my-hair.10063/post-114754>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=114621)
>
> lovely Inger .......I wish I could get my hair longer too but every once in a while it gets so sun damaged and we have to give it the snip!!
> what does the mud do for your hair? what products would you suggest I start with?

I would suggest, just start washing with ghassul and then do a mask with silica, egg yolk, EVOO and lemon juice before washing once in a while... and spray your hair with rainwater often and rinse it in rainwater or soft water from a river or lake or even well/tap if it is soft (hard water does not work..) and then just let it grow

You will need some transition time to get used to the ghassul I think. I will send you some to try, just PM or mail me your address, ok! It is very cheap so I would gladly send you some! Sure you will find it is Australia too if you search around, it can be spelled Rhassoul too I have seen.

The mud makes my hair feel thicker, and it just have made it healthier over all. I used to use loads of products and sprays to keep my hair looking "normal"... that is how thin it is.
If you feel the sun damages your hair just do masks more often in summer  You might try out different kind of home made masks that suits your hair the best but the mask mentioned above is great for me and I think we have pretty same kind of hair....
You could also use a at to cover your hair from the sun when it is strongest/at the beach. But I do not do that I like how the sun bleaches my hair

Funny, I still have days when my hair looks better or worse... must be the hormones or hydration level?

### Lahelada — 2014-03-11T11:35:21+0200

### kathylu — 2014-03-13T05:57:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/omg-my-hair.10063/post-115123>

Wow...I am still using baking soda in RO water to wash and an AC vinegar rinse. I wash once a week and every other week I use about a teaspoon of baby shampoo before the baking soda. I admit to using about a 1/2 teaspoon conditioner on the lower 2 inches of my hair (I have long hair). That takes care of any tangles. I use Avalon Organics Rosemary conditioner. My hair is better than it has ever been. I really like only needing to shampoo once per week!

# Thread: Just Starting Out
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-starting-out.10064/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Scott M. — 2014-03-07T23:50:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-starting-out.10064/post-114049>

Greetings,

I am brand new to this forum and excited to be here! Thank you all for having me! I'm beginning to understand and implement many of the topics mentioned here, but could use a little help getting started. I have a long list of questions that no one has really been able to answer, which includes things like the following:

-   Why would a person's blood calcium be high, if their PTH is normal and there are no parathyroid tumors? Lack of calcitonin? Compensation for high levels of acidity? Low Na/K ratio?
-   Why would exposure to sunlight, vitamin D, or vitamin A (found in almost every vegetable) cause painful trigger points in a person's connective tissue (upper body)? Inability to redox properly? Poor hydration?
-   Why would taking small amounts of magnesium or D-ribose result in anxiety attacks? Mitochondrial dysfunction?
-   Why would consumption of small amounts of vitamin B6 cause pins and needles (symptom of vitamin B6 toxicity)? Methylation problem?

It seems like more context might be helpful, but I wasn't sure if I should post my whole story here, or if anyone would be willing to do an educational consult to help explain some of these phenomena? I would be most grateful! We've done a number of labs, but I wasn't sure if I should post them here, or if there were a better forum for that? Thank you all in advance for your guidance and direction!

Thanks again!
Scott

# Thread: Girls in dresses....
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/girls-in-dresses.10065/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 345
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### caroline — 2014-03-08T00:27:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/girls-in-dresses.10065/post-114056>

I guess this is a slightly frivolous question .... but we don't seem to have a new blog yet......

Do guys like girls in dresses?????

I have noticed since wearing dresses lately ...... things have changed. I probably haven't worn a dress in 20 years.

I seem to feel quite different about myself - way more feminine..

I do look okay in jeans and t-shirt because I have long legs and am pretty narrow .... it is something more - much more.

Do guys really love a girl in a dress?
if so - why?

How do the girls feel in a dress?

and then there is the question of high heels..............stilettos or otherwise????? How high do you like to go????

Do you feel differently in a dress?

I find there is a huge difference that I wouldn't have expected..........

### sjoshua — 2014-03-08T00:53:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/girls-in-dresses.10065/post-114060>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=114056)
>
> Do guys really love a girl in a dress?
> if so - why?

Depends on the girl, and depends on the dress  Assuming both of these criteria are proper given the environment, yes:

\-a dress will typically allow more skin exposure, vs. something like a pants suit. Visible (healthy) skin can be alluring
\-a dress will accentuate a proper walk - its like allowing your hips to extend/flow to the bottom seam
\-a dress typically goes along with someone who has put some time/effort into their looks that day (otherwise they would have just put on pants)

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=114056)
>
> and then there is the question of high heels..............stilettos or otherwise????? How high do you like to go????

Heels are OK for special occasions, but to me heels are an anologous for being less in touch with nature... distancing from the ground in an impractical way. Makes me think that we would not have much in common, since I strive to be barefoot when society can't dictate otherwise (can you run down a beach with me wearing those stillettos!?  ). Also, being 5'9" male, much height in heels and a female can quickly become taller than me. This is notsomuch an issue for me since I know their real height when they take them off (reading this a second time, I realize this is irrelevant without knowing that I am mostly attracted to shorter females..), and I do not particularily care what others think. But I have dated some females that didn't wear heels because they like the idea of the man being taller.

### Martin — 2014-03-08T01:07:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/girls-in-dresses.10065/post-114062>

Hell_ yeah.

Helps to celebrate/accentuate/demarcate what's different between men and women. For instance, if _I_ wore one, somehow it just would _not_ be all that attractive. You, on the other hand...

Heels, meh.

NSH

### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-08T01:57:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/girls-in-dresses.10065/post-114068>

Funny you brought this up.........I just told my wife and one of her friends that women are crazy not wearing dresses..........they differentiate a women's assets and they really turn me on.........the clothing fashions for the modern women are horrendous. Part of the reason I like the south is the girls here still know how to rock a dress.

### Lahelada — 2014-03-08T02:05:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/girls-in-dresses.10065/post-114069>

Love to wear a dress but the shoes have to be dainty and slightly high as well. As the silhouette of a dress is flirty they cannot be clodhoppers but do not need to be stilettos. Stilettos are limousine shoes for when you are exactly aware of how many steps you will be taking. For some reason flats only work on young girls with dresses, older women need at least a little heel . Older women I am talking 30 and older which is not to say old ,just not 19 anymore. For older women the sexiness is in the cut of the shoe, cute thin little straps etc to offset lack of heel height.
For the guys , we do not only dress for you but also for our peers. For example we do not wear Crocs with a sheath dress so as not to invite the comment from our girlfriend "what's with the clodhoppers,they ruin a sensational dress "
Ya see dresses are multifaceted and complicated so appreciate them when we wear them.

but - in the work place? ........... guys would like the girl in the tight,short skirt and even tighter sweater?

nothing has changed .....sigh

But I am really, really glad that I have discovered dresses .... oh yeah!

When I met my Canadian friends in Sydney awhile ago - I was sliding out of a bench seat and looked up to see Brian \[a Doc\] looking at my legs - I loved it!
He is so used to seeing me in chef"s whites and probably didn't know I had a body let alone legs! He has probably been so worried about me for a very long time......

I guess it is an occupational hazard with Docs ...... they need to make sure our breasts and legs are okay and we don't fall over and hurt ourself ???

Would we have it any other way? I sure wouldn't ....... It is very nice to get an appreciative glance now and then ...... from both men and women.....

I do like a guy in a kilt

It is pretty nice to appreciate one another when we have taken care with our appearance.

I just realised something ..... Jack put that in the sex webinar! holey moley - can't I go anywhere without him in my head??? I may need an exorcist ....

I cannot believe how I start out in one direction and always end up back here at - the quilt!

and He thinks I am not paying attention..........sheesh...

### Inger — 2014-03-11T08:21:30+0200

### caroline — 2014-03-11T09:18:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/girls-in-dresses.10065/post-114757>

This thread is pretty amazing .... we start out talking about dresses - but look where we end up!

So when a man or woman is comfortable in their own skin ... and confident ...and own it and have a proud posture - we attract some attention .... nothing to do with the dress - not much anyway.

It all melts together .... what could possibly be sexier.....

I am not sure that a man truly understands ....when we are having trouble with our weight ...none of the above happens. We aren't the least bit confident and quite uncomfortable in our own skin - it doesn't matter what we have on - how much money we have spent on a spectacular dress.

I know our partners aren't usually concerned about the extra pounds and love us just the same - no matter what size we are. Dr. K. said once that a caring husband will help his partner to feel good about herself...... but therein lies the problem ... we have to feel good about ourself first and foremost. It doesn't work otherwise.

I mean to say .... It is wonderful when you are alone with your partner and he celebrates your curves and loves you to bits .... but putting on a dress to go to a party and it is tight and pulling in various assorted places and you feel like the side of a barn and a stuffed sausage - that makes us feel inadequate on so many levels.

Does a man feel like this at all - ever? Dr. K. - when you were a big man ...how did you feel? Maybe a man just gets on with it?

### prAna303 — 2014-03-11T10:57:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/girls-in-dresses.10065/post-114771>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=114757)
>
> I am not sure that a man truly understands ....when we are having trouble with our weight ...none of the above happens. We aren't the least bit confident and quite uncomfortable in our own skin - it doesn't matter what we have on - how much money we have spent on a spectacular dress.
>
> I know our partners aren't usually concerned about the extra pounds and love us just the same - no matter what size we are. Dr. K. said once that a caring husband will help his partner to feel good about herself...... but therein lies the problem ... we have to feel good about ourself first and foremost. It doesn't work otherwise.
>
> I mean to say .... It is wonderful when you are alone with your partner and he celebrates your curves and loves you to bits .... but putting on a dress to go to a party and it is tight and pulling in various assorted places and you feel like the side of a barn and a stuffed sausage - that makes us feel inadequate on so many levels.
>
> Does a man feel like this at all - ever? Dr. K. - when you were a big man ...how did you feel? Maybe a man just gets on with it?

I strongly think that men also have the same problem BUT often do some things to cover it, not just physical. We tend to gather up and make it a power sign, i felt it many times in my former business days. A little extra around the middle line, you can say that you live a good life and compensate with your career. And in this crowd you start to live and act like your brothers.

Men, today have big identity problems, not all but many i know of. We live lifes where the phone is king and camera, the queen. The weird world of "looks" have slowly crept over from female to male. So many guys do not know what they are, what they want and where they are going.

The problem today is that we have lost the sync between body, mind and the world we live in. We think we are so smart now but in the end we are screaming for help. We go to gym to make up and show up.

Can only relate to myself and my world, i do not have the looks of a model, not by far. The biggest thing i have had from day one is the whole me, face in me is just something that does not make heads turn. Instead i try to be everything else, i can not change my face but everything else is more flexible. I like to melt in BUT stand out.

Or like a female athlete said to me. I can not wear everything, my legs is to muscular for dresses, my back is to big for a dress shirt, weighting in at 56kg and would have to buy XXL in jeans to even get them over the calves, i had to stop to care. I am my body and as i know what it can do i choose to see it as a whole.

So yes, many men feels like women today, just not saying it loud.

### sooperb — 2014-03-11T12:26:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/girls-in-dresses.10065/post-114782>

Dressing for me has to be simple, I simply don't want to spend hours creating an alter ego, that's what I see the way women doing. Paint your face, style your hair, hide behind it all, wear clothing that isn't comfortable, shoes that show the only thing in your head must be air lol. I like simple, functional clothes that fit. That's the sticking point for me, nothing fits. They tend not to make clothes that can accommodate having large lumps of matter unless they can label it "baby" and produce clothing to cloak it like some dark secret. I don't really want to be wearing maternity dresses etc.

I love dresses, just slip one on and you're, well, dressed. The right dress in the right fabric, cut well is just heaven. "Gagnrad in England I could never find a nice dress unless it was for an occasion." Sums it all up does that, the designers here are all focused on the current fashion to be androgynous. I'm not and never will be, too much frontage for one thing. So it's stretchy kecks and tops for moi in the colder months and stretchy shorts and tops when it's warm enough.

A little extra around the middle line, you can say that you live a good life and compensate with your career. And in this crowd you start to live and act like your brothers.
>
> Men, today have big identity problems, not all but many i know of. We live lifes where the phone is king and camera, the queen. The weird world of "looks" have slowly crept over from female to male. So many guys do not know what they are, what they want and where they are going.
>
> The problem today is that we have lost the sync between body, mind and the world we live in. We think we are so smart now but in the end we are screaming for help. We go to gym to make up and show up.
>
> Can only relate to myself and my world, i do not have the looks of a model, not by far. The biggest thing i have had from day one is the whole me, face in me is just something that does not make heads turn. Instead i try to be everything else, i can not change my face but everything else is more flexible. I like to melt in BUT stand out.
>
> Or like a female athlete said to me. I can not wear everything, my legs is to muscular for dresses, my back is to big for a dress shirt, weighting in at 56kg and would have to buy XXL in jeans to even get them over the calves, i had to stop to care. I am my body and as i know what it can do i choose to see it as a whole.
>
> So yes, many men feels like women today, just not saying it loud.

My lover had acne scars... and I must have fell in love with them (the scars, yeah) without even knowing it before later

I think it is not the outer look that matters in the end. It is something else

maybe it is the imperfect things that makes it all so perfect? That is what makes someone so loveable?

### SeaHorse — 2014-03-11T23:40:27+0200

### caroline — 2014-03-11T23:59:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/girls-in-dresses.10065/post-114865>

Sarah ..... I wrote the above - but forgot to post - before you said the above^^^

Point very well made . I do apologise for the way that sounded.

From my perspective .... I just meant, for me, at this point in my life - someone would have to be on the same wave length and be caring for themselves .... mind, body and spirit .... and that would have outward manifestations. I shouldn't have been so glib. My apologies....

Last time I was travelling there was a very large man- probably the size that Jack used to be - and I would think how sad for him. It turns out he was the loveliest man you could imagine and treated his wife with such love and care and kindness and respect. It also turns out he had many serious health issues ..... and wouldn't give up his cake and icecream and wine etc. etc. etc. I even asked Jack about him - and Jack knew what the problem was.
He would not even really listen to what Jack said .....He had been very ill for about 10 years and still thought his Docs new best....

### SeaHorse — 2014-03-12T00:07:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/girls-in-dresses.10065/post-114866>

### sooperb — 2014-03-12T11:44:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/girls-in-dresses.10065/post-114978>

"remember there are a lot of people with pot bellies and saggy arses who need to love themselves first before they can get better."

Words of wisdom

I've watched so many programmes about dieting and bodies etc. and the big people in it seem to always have become that way because they have been made to feel bad about themselves, poor relationships with parents, badly treated by their peers, bullied at school. I wonder what the connection is between emotional upset and over-eating? I've also known some very large people who are lovely, kind, caring and funny. Those that do slim down seem to need a Damascene experience though before they make changes. Being unable to get on fairground rides seems to be a common one, or seeing themselves in holiday or wedding snaps.

I remember a girl I worked with, obese but pretty, well spoken and she was married, to a quite large bloke. She was talking to another colleague of mine, a qualified counsellor and she was saying of course she didn't overeat, nor did she have any issues, she'd had a happy childhood being raised by her GRANDMA! The counsellor's face was a picture. The girl unexpectedly got pregnant and we were all hoping that at last she would recognise that she needed to lose weight. It never happened, she was even larger afterwards.

I think people are like houses, you can be put off by the outside but often the inside is amazing.

### sooperb — 2014-03-15T11:33:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/girls-in-dresses.10065/post-115552>

I'm banned from sports stores, they see me heading their way and the doors snap shut, they don't want a fat lass getting in the way of the racing snakes that throng their aisles , it's not good for business. (Just kidding)

I went commando a few years back and I haven't worn pantyhose probably for nearly a decade apart from the odd posh dress function where I have dug around and brought out a slightly tatty pair, saved for just such an occasion. I no longer wear socks either, I've gone right through the winter without any, don't miss them.

### Inger — 2014-03-15T20:30:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/girls-in-dresses.10065/post-115654>

My thighs rubbed together too when I was in my late teens. I weighed 65 kg... the most I have ever weighed. I am 166 cm but it is way too much for me as my bones are smallish. I hated when the thighs rubbed together in hot summer days and the next winter I just fasted 3 weeks and lost 10 kg... and never weighed that much anymore. Sure I gained something back and I had other health issues because of my bad life style. And I am still thinking what harm that fasting might have done to me...  But the thighs rubbing together thing was not present anymore... Oh man Nicole I so hope for you soon your thighs will not rub together anymore!

### fitness@home — 2014-03-16T01:22:07+0200

Caroline, if I wear a dress to work there are times I use a slip. If the dress is lined, I don't need it. If not - I use a half slip. My drawer is full of all lengths from short to maxi length.

I'm also the odd one out...still use pantyhose and/or stockings garter (the latter is for DH's benefit). There are some women at work, both younger & older, that should _never_ wear dresses without hosiery - - trust me when I say that it is not a pretty site. Doesn't matter if fashion dictates you don't have to wear them any longer...look in a mirror for Pete's sake!

### Stella — 2014-03-23T00:16:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/girls-in-dresses.10065/post-117266>

This thread is such a hoot.

Personally after feeling soooo good and believe me it's been way too long since I've felt this well, I finally pulled out some of my dresses from the wardrobe. To say that I turned heads is an understatement. Since I'm in the land of eternal summers, my dresses are typically sleeveless and light. I prefer the A line dress as it is most flattering. Forget the hose and slip. I guess sales of my products can only go up from here!
I went to see a customer yesterday and he complemented me on my nice tan, which of course was not hidden under the typical pants and blouse he was accustomed to seeing me in. He said he gave my tan an A, but he wasn't giving me an A+ because he wasn't doing a physical exam (he is a doc).

Funny when you feel great inside it shows through your skin and those around me have taken notice!!

As for the other conversation about what turns me on in men's clothing - a nice, crisp white guayabera, preferably smoking a Cuban cigar and drinking a nice aged rum! Something about those smells that i find extremely sexy!

### prAna303 — 2014-03-23T20:50:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/girls-in-dresses.10065/post-117426>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=117365)
>
> I say a girl last night in a black dress that was stunning............she walked into the restaurant and it was as if every person, man and women just could not stop staring at her dress. I have never seen that with a pantsuit or jump suit.

Quite the same for me this week, not a dress but the whole package...

Slowly walking with a gait of a dancer, proud and alive. Eyes where looking around, probing and seeing the world, boy that prescence where breathtaking... At the café where i hang and write she where like a wild creature just moving, ready to attack. My first thought was, wow, i must talk to her. My second thought was, since she resonated directly to me, why be so direct... Sending signals that i liked what i saw would be enough.

After an hour of small glimses at her table, all of a sudden she presented herself. We talked, shared stories, she was like i thought, a dancer or actor. Actor it was, when she later asked for my number she looked quite puzzled when i told her i do not work that way, no phones for me... She then asked if i was online, i answered, i live analog and not digital. If she would like to meet me again, look me up at the café or find me where i spend most of my days, in the wild.

So to break it down... A dress, a girl, together it can be magic but it most resonate with the one who is reading the signals AND the one sending them.

### nicld — 2014-03-24T21:09:19+0200

> [prAna303 said:](/goto/post?id=117426)
>

>
> Slowly walking with a gait of a dancer, proud and alive.

In a book that I am reading it talks about gait and how it has been changed due to the invention of shoes. When walking barefoot one walks more on the pads of the feet and slower as to not injure the feet. I can see a dancer walking this way as I believe that they use the ball of their feet more then the heel. My goal this summer is to go barefoot as much as possible and when I do wear shoes go with the lightest ones I can find, oh and get some Juil shoes for work (sandals are a no go at work). Will be doing more walking in the moccasins too, no padding in those.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-24T21:28:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/girls-in-dresses.10065/post-117676>

> [nicld said:](/goto/post?id=117669)
>
> In a book that I am reading it talks about gait and how it has been changed due to the invention of shoes.

Sure. It turns out that there are a number of quite complex reasons for that inherent in the design of most shoes -- and to some extent in all shoes:

[https://nwfootankle.com/files/rossiWhyShoesMakeNormalGaitImpossible.pdf](https://nwfootankle.com/files/rossiWhyShoesMakeNormalGaitImpossible.pdf)

> When walking barefoot one walks more on the pads of the feet and slower as to not injure the feet.

Depends what they mean by that …

Some people who've got enthusiastic about shoelessness seem to have confused the running gait and the walking gait. One should, of course, land on the ball of the foot when running, but when walking the heel makes contact first, and then the foot kind of rolls. That's just normal walking gait: what was the earliest footprints of all to show bipedalism, those at Laetoli, show hominids walking like that -- you can see the heel in the print is somewhat deeper than the rest of the footprint:

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Laetoli](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Laetoli)

However, if they mean that the toes tend to dig in and actually grip the ground a bit when someone is barefoot, then, sure, they have more of a role there. That can't happen even with very light shoes -- although I guess it can with the Vibram five fingers.

### nicld — 2014-03-25T03:42:38+0200

### prAna303 — 2014-03-25T09:51:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/girls-in-dresses.10065/post-117808>

> [sooperb said:](/goto/post?id=117723)
>
> I think this why you seldom see gorgeous women with ugly men, unless of course they have fat wallets or they're famous or both. There can be no other explanation for Bernie Ecclestone lol.

Ah, now we are talking. This is not just a female subject, us men do have some issues also...

I promise you this... Show the picture of me and most girls think i am not a beauty, many would say ugly also but here is the paradox. Thru my whole life i had a lot of girlfriends, why?

First of all, since i do not like to look at mirrors where i can see my face, look at pictures taken of me i do not focus on looks. I have always lived my life by my own rules, in sync with the laws of nature.

Second, this makes me totally unaware of how i look but instead 100% aware of how i move, explore and read the environment. I never bother myself with thoughts on how do this or that look (on or about me).

I have dated, models, actors, dancers, surfers, skiers and the list goes on... All of them with their own sublime details, and well most of them extremely beautiful.

Money, once i had it... Girls, some liked it and so did i at that time. Today, i have less than most in Sweden, i live below the bottom line of income here. Guess what? Girls is drawn even more to me these days, why? I am a living human, having the time to live 100% all of the time. One thing that so many likes is that i am always there for them BUT i do not make them the number one reason for living.

So in the weird modern world of today... Start with being at least as much human you can and you attract others, if you do, 99% will not.

### Albert83BCN — 2014-03-25T10:22:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/girls-in-dresses.10065/post-117814>

Interesting points by prAna303. As sooperb said, I think that an ugly man with a gorgeous woman is perfectly possible and we see this quite often if the man has the fat wallet, or I'd say it shows some sign of power in general. I think that fulfils the need for protection that a woman might seek in order to provide her children with the best chances to develop with success and to protect the nucleus of the family. That would be her mission.

You won't see the reverse situation happen quite often because in general, men we don't care at all about the wealth of a woman, I'd say we are totally driven by physical (at first sight) and intellectual (in second place) characteristics of a woman to rapidly assess if it might make for a suitable partner to distribute our genes or not. Remember, a man can reproduce very frequently if he wants to, so a more promiscuous behaviour seems to make sense, while a woman will spend 9 months of her life in the process and will find future opportunities more difficult since then. It's pretty normal that our mechanisms to seek for reproductive partners are quite different.

Now, many men don't fit in very well in the picture of wealth and security that we were talking about, yet they show some other characteristics that might get the attention of woman, that quite relate to what prana said: self-confidence, an ability to show empathy for them, giving some expectatives then showing that you are not tied to anyone... I think these are things that create a challenge to a woman that drives them to put their attention on that kind of men because of the feelings they can make them feel and the sense that these men have some strong inner world. They are seen more like the 'bad guy' than 'providers'... and are the ones that make girls crazy when they are young and wealth expectations haven't kicked in yet in their world, don't you think? The proof is that once life goes on and we mature, the interest seems to change more and more to the provider pattern and the bad guy stops looking appealing, it might even be interpreted as ridiculous by that time.

Things here in spain are getting quite boring... I doubt it will be very different from the US or Australia right now, specially in big cities where everything seems to fall into the same place, same with love and relationships. Of course you can always find nice stories... but the common scenario is something like this: boys and girls start engaging in relationships at 13, have lots of mates by their 20's, but they don't feel comfortable enough with any, then they get caught by the university, work and all the chaotic lifestyle here and by their 30's they both get alarmed and then they hurry up to find the first mate that will bear with them, they might find it by their late 30's with unexpected results

### caroline — 2014-03-26T01:26:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/girls-in-dresses.10065/post-117947>

so Albert ..... are you saying that life has changed dramatically there? because of the economy I suppose?

But I always thought you guys go out for late dinners and drinking and dancing....... I always pictured it being much more orientated to family and friends and much more social.....

Do you love to dance?

### Albert83BCN — 2014-03-26T01:37:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/girls-in-dresses.10065/post-117950>

Well, most of them still do! going out party, drinking and dancing... it's quite a social must here, yep. And I did also, until my 27's or so... then I started losing interest on it. In fact, it's not that I liked it a lot before... but since that is what your friends do, you do the same. But since I ever started questioning things... nowadays I don't do anything just because the others do. I even got to the point to tell many of my friends that if they want to spend some nice time, call me at noon, not past midnight. I used to be a night owl but I have successfully changed to an early bird and I don't want to mess again with my circadian biology. I love now being able to get up refreshed early on Sundays and make it a profitable day. And I like music a lot, but not so much dancing. Or at least not dancing the kind of music that is the most danceable! hehe. I bet if I were still single, I'd go out partying more often. In fact, it's what almost everyone does here. Once they get a partner, they forget about friends, hehe.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-31T11:31:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/girls-in-dresses.10065/post-119013>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=119009)
>
> how do you find a post by the #?
>
> see why I have problems!
>
> How did you do that????

You see at the bottom of any post there's a dashed line in blue?

Under that there's the autosignature, if someone's set one -- right?

To the right of the autosig. you'll see a number in pale grey preceded by the hash sign (used in the U.S. instead of "No." to indicate number). That's the post number. I just looked back through the thread till I found the post I wanted and then cited its number.

Something else. Hover the cursor over it with the mouse. You'll see a little window come up with "permalink" in it. That the link to that particular post. So if you ever want to cite a particular post -- say in order to direct a questioner to it -- hover the mouse over, right-click for a context menu, and choose "open in new tab". Now you can go to te new tab and copy the exact right URL for that post from the address bar.

Thus I can say: "See what Audrey says here":

[http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/girls-in-dresses.10065/page-12#post-118972](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/girls-in-dresses.10065/page-12#post-118972)

I need to take a bit of a detour here.........I stopped at the liquor store tonite on the way home. I had bought this great Malbec the other day .....I got talking to this lovely man from Brazil - he knew a lot about red wines - we talked for ages.

The point of this little story is ..... He smelled so darn good!  WOW

it is really hard to resist a man that smells that good ..... yikes!

I was walking home and I walked down the wrong street for crying out loud!!! It took me for ever to get home!
However - I did have a HUGE grin on my face.

Ladies - do you like your gentlemen to smell great? what is your favorite?

funny thing is ..... I don't much care for perfume on women ...

But - why do some men smell so freakin awesome????

Does this heat things up for you?

So - you have a woman in a sexy dress .... and a man that smells awesome.......

Dos this rock your world?
