Brazilian men, hell anything Brazilian... handsome man with an intoxicating scent... a woman so intoxicated by the man that she wanders around lost in a spell for hours... huge grin... rocking your world...

### Albert83BCN — 2014-04-08T02:13:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/girls-in-dresses.10065/post-120306>

Yeah, I get that, and in fact I consider the possibility that I might learn something from it. You can always learn something useful almost from the most unexpected thing. I've read some erotic literature in the past in fact... and even a great book about seduction science by a 'guru' here in Spain called Mario Luna. The book is titled 'Sex Code' and gives some kind of 'framework' for men so we can understand, learn and counter how a woman thinks and take benefit from it.

In fact, it's not intended to be sexist or anything like that, on the contrary, is more like a self-help book that teaches us how to be more self-confident, be consistent with our ideas, learn to interpret woman's feelings but also learn how to play a little bit nasty so we can take them out of their comfort zone and surprise them, to avoid some behaviours that would reflect negative things, some neurolinguistic programming (NLP), etc... He also explains that woman is always trying to disprove us in a way to assess the quality of our genes,to see if we're real 'alpha males', and explains how to be aware of that and not let be caught. He also explains that when you keep in touch with other woman that can be a shortcut for the woman you're interested in to get her attention beucase she can infere a lot of things just based on the fact that other woman has choosen you. Pretty cool and interesting stuff. It also gives quite a few explanations about our diferences from a evolutionary perspective. I once heard that this guy was also into the Paleo Diet. You see, everything's tied.

But, honestly, I was more interested in these kind of things when I was 19 to 25. Today I'm way more into unleashing the keys to optimal health and performance, trying to get my IT startup up and running and pass my BS in Nutrition Science, heck that does not leave a lot of time for the 50 shades hehe

**Too LATE AUDREY!!!!** OMG that book was horrible. just horrible. (see following [review](https://www.goodreads.com/review/show/340987215) which highlights the entire book IMO) DH Lawrence's The Rainbow was better.

Though I have to say Sommerset Maughman's _The Razors Edge_ is a better book about great love and kindness. So for that matter is _For Whom the Bell Tolls_ by Hemmingway. Even _Lolita_ is a great love story. _Atlas Shrugged_ and the _Fountainhead_ are probably the two greatest love stories of all time - the love of self. which in this day and age many of us don't know how to love ourselves completely to the point where we can't love other fully until we learn how to love ourselves

Now if you're looking for erotica & romance that highlights love and kindness - check out anything by Mya Banks. some of her stuff is way beyond what's in 50 Shades and far better writing..

He buys so many and uses so much...and only the most expensive brands... lol Sometimes when he had got him a new one I could not like and he put a lot on and we drove somewhere together in the car I was about to DIE  it was seriously a nightmare! later I have had him to let me smell the stuff he buys and tell him if it makes me feel bad and good. Thanks god

### caroline — 2014-04-09T02:39:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/girls-in-dresses.10065/post-120482>

### SeaHorse — 2014-04-09T02:49:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/girls-in-dresses.10065/post-120485>

They are not attracted to chemical smells…but many people do aromatherapy with essential oils and have a lot of success. When they like an oil they usually try to eat the bottle! Horses like garlic and bitter herbs to eat, so they must not mind the smell. I love the smell of horses breath after they've been eating spring grass…very outdoorsy and fresh…and the smell of a barn full of animals is just about the best thing ever. My one and only contribution to this thread….it's been a great source of entertainment and laughs…thanks everyone!

### cinnamon — 2014-05-18T04:06:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/girls-in-dresses.10065/post-127423>

So, I have a dress story.

I was shopping last weekend in a thrift store - and I saw this dress. I really liked it, but it looked like it was for a twenty-something to wear to a cocktail party. I decided to try it on anyway. Of course, it fit perfectly! It had these tiers of fabric that floated out and moved like peacock feathers. Amazing! I agonized for a few minutes thinking I have no idea where I would wear this and then decided to go ahead. It looked fabulous and at $7.50, a steal!

Both the clerk and the woman in line behind me literally gasped as I put the dress on the counter. I said I didn't know when I was going to actually wear it, but that I liked it. They both agreed that it was gorgeous. And the woman behind me said, "Why wouldn't you wear it? You should wear it for a hot date tonight!" I laughed and blushed and protested that it was probably not age-appropriate, but that I really liked it. I don't think they believed me when I told them I was 51.

I know Audrey is going to say she needs pics. So here they are. On seeing them, my mom wanted to know when I was going out dancing...

### fitness@home — 2014-05-18T17:15:28+0300

### caroline — 2014-05-18T23:34:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/girls-in-dresses.10065/post-127485>

I was thinking last nite ...as I was falling asleep ....I wonder what stories that dress could tell? I wonder where that dress has been? I wonder what body it caressed? What amazing parties it has been to .... or did it hang in someone's closet unused and unloved.......

### cinnamon — 2014-05-21T03:47:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/girls-in-dresses.10065/post-127747>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=127430)
>
> It would be interesting to know - would you ever have bought that dress if it wasn't for this thread???

I'm not sure about the impact of the thread. Last summer (so before this thread was started) I bought a dress one day and wore it out of the shop! LOL The day had started out cool and cloudy but had gotten much too warm for the blue jeans I was wearing. The shop was one that sold Indian cottons and the dress was a lovely bright red one from the sale rack out front. When I put it on, I knew it was for me.

I would like to say that this thread may not have been the direct inspiration, but being a member of the community here is definitely part of the story. I have this glow, especially in the summer. And my skin looks great. And well, even being modest, I have somehow developed this "hot" body. LOL I caught a glimpse of myself reflected in a glass door as I was walking up the stairs of my back deck wearing a bikini the other day. Hey, when did I start channeling Bo Derek in "10"?  Well, maybe Bo Derek with an A cup...

### cinnamon — 2014-05-21T03:53:24+0300

### cinnamon — 2014-05-21T04:04:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/girls-in-dresses.10065/post-127753>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=127485)
>
> I was thinking last nite ...as I was falling asleep ....I wonder what stories that dress could tell? I wonder where that dress has been? I wonder what body it caressed? What amazing parties it has been to .... or did it hang in someone's closet unused and unloved.......

> I do want y'all to know I generally have other things I think about before falling asleep ..... a little more interesting things .......

Audrey, I often find myself thinking about the person who first bought an item that I see in a thrift store. It is surprising to me how many of the items are in excellent condition. Did they like it in the store and then decide that it didn't look great on them when they got it home? Are they so well off they can afford to get all new clothes every season? Or did they give it as a gift to someone who had to pretend for a while that they liked it or that it fit? LOL Oh and there are always some items where you can NOT imagine how anyone actually could have decided to buy them in the first place...

### caroline — 2014-05-21T04:43:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/girls-in-dresses.10065/post-127758>

I wish I could shop in a thrift store ..... clearly I need to shop with you!

everything always seems so icky to me and the shops smell - but I continually talked to random people and will compliment them on something they are wearing and they will tell me they bought it at a thrift shop for $5.00...
and I go WTH - what is wrong with me.....

Cin ....I love how you are loving yourself ......that is a pretty great takeaway from jackkruse.com!!!

### Stella — 2014-05-21T17:02:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/girls-in-dresses.10065/post-127828>

I have to admit that this thread was one of the inspirations behind me hitting the stores for dresses. Also I've lost 15 lbs so went down a size. My shopping is seasonal so in anticipation of the summer heat, I hit the outlet stores and ended up paying an average of $25 per dress. I have bought mainly sleeveless knit dresses that can be washed at home. BTW I share Audrey's sentiments about thrift stores.

Cinnamon, I too have seen changes in my skin, hair and nails. Also changes in body composition. I've always worn bikinis but I'm looking better than ever. And feeling great and most importantly my brain is working again. Must be all the oysters and the BPC.

### caroline — 2014-05-22T02:04:37+0300

### HoneyChild — 2014-06-06T20:40:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/girls-in-dresses.10065/post-130005>

The other week I was at my psychology tutorial which was mainly about behaviourism/positive reinforcement (ie use reward to increase the frequency of a particular behaviour). Our tutor told a story about a guy (I think he's a psychology author) who did a guest lecture for some psychology students. The students decided to play a little trick on him. They had a young attractive women wearing a low cut dress sit in the middle of the front row. Every time the guy would step to the right she would lean forward to reveal her cleavage (as a "reward"). By the end of the lecture the guy was way over the right-hand side of the stage up against the wall... and had no clue as to how he ended up there!

### shoalie — 2014-06-07T08:57:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/girls-in-dresses.10065/post-130079>

Up late, reading through all of these posts!! Love Them, compelled to jump in.. Just recently a guest at the Biltmore, and granted access to Mrs. Vanderbilt's private stash of dresses. A whole Room
of Steamer trunks dedicated to transporting one dress each. Just kept thinking.. "I'm glad we're not doing this anymore!" Made me feel like a light traveler with my one humble suitcase!
Feeling, "all girl, all the time".. Mann.... I love nice dresses & clothes ~ Knowing its all frequency ~ we are all naked..
Like to keep a balance of knowing that it wasn't so long ago, that my gender was not even allowed to vote! LOL
Was also very saddened to see the news story of the woman who was recently stoned to death for loving the wrong man outside of family and society expectations. Why wan't the man stoned to death?
and her crime? She loved.. That takes me to my knees..
On a lighter note, also read the story of the couple who just gave birth to twins.. They were born holding hands.. Isn't that cool!
xo for tonight everyone!!

### shiran — 2017-07-30T14:27:37+0300

### Sheddie — 2017-07-30T17:51:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/girls-in-dresses.10065/post-220448>

> [Shijin13 said:](/goto/post?id=114083)
>
> I love wearing a nice dress. I especially love my Power dresses/skirts that I'll wear for business meetings. As one of very few women in the world of Logistics and Transportation with my experience. I've found that most people ignored me when I wore pant suits. but If I wore a dress or skirt with a nice top and a killer pair of 3" heels. 1. 2. 3. all eyes on me for my presentations. And people remembered me, and my talks.
>
> The lengthiest job I ever had was 16 years with 53 male PhDs (and only 2 female PhDs, myself not one) and I thoroughly enjoyed the workplace 'camaraderie!' Early on someone advised me not to adopt sweaters as that was the secretarial, not administrator, class uniform; so it was skirt & jacket suits, heels & hose. Hated nylon hose, and the style then mostly was slightly below-the-knee dress lengths. Three seasons of the year I wore knee-high Italian, leather-lined dress boots (available in Montreal, not the US South) with socks no one saw -- solved the hose discomfort! Loved those boots! Maybe 1.5 inch heels but I could MOVE in the 'uniform' I put together. And, get effective attention to my work-related utterances! Now, it's mostly cotton bike shorts, T-shirts and flip-flops around the house (barefoot more & more to hit the grass outside faster), nicer rayon shorts & tops for errands out, quality leggings in winters... Ninety-eight per cent of the time I wouldn't care for male attention/interrruption now, but, I do keep a slimming black dress for socials, and sadly, funerals. Thanks for that reminiscence treat! Those were the days...

**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 5

# Thread: Piezoluminescent CO???
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/piezoluminescent-co.10073/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### RobH — 2014-03-09T03:16:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/piezoluminescent-co.10073/post-114241>

While digging into my coconut oil jar to brush my teeth last night... in total darkness ... the sun was down after all -- I noticed that mechanically agitating the relatively solid CO (was about 55F in the room) with my toothbrush actually caused a brief brief whitish blue flash.

Did it 5 different times. Very repeatable.

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Piezoluminescence](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Piezoluminescence)

It doesn't mention CO ... but I swear it happened. Will try again tonight. I was not intoxicated!

All those electron clouds in the shells of the MCT / Fatty acids actually conduct with mechanical stress, like a piezoelectric crytals, and give of photons perhaps? I'm kind of a QED / QM novice, but it sort of makes sense to me that way.

Anybody else noticed this?

RobH

### nonchalant — 2014-03-11T01:02:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/piezoluminescent-co.10073/post-114631>

Neat, Rob! No, I haven't noticed this, but kudos for not using regular toothpaste, being dark after sunset, and having your room at 55F! ;-)

### Shijin13 — 2014-03-11T01:04:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/piezoluminescent-co.10073/post-114633>

sweet... Now I'm going to have to try to duplicate this... and share it with the kids... they'd totally dig it...

### Jude — 2014-03-11T01:32:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/piezoluminescent-co.10073/post-114641>

I tried it lots last night, in the dark as well, but my co was most probably not firm enough so didn't get that reaction...but will still keep on trying as the days get a little cooler.....

### sjoshua — 2014-03-12T20:27:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/piezoluminescent-co.10073/post-115048>

> [RobH said:](/goto/post?id=114241)
>
> While digging into my coconut oil jar to brush my teeth last night... in total darkness ... the sun was down after all -- I noticed that mechanically agitating the relatively solid CO (was about 55F in the room) with my toothbrush actually caused a brief brief whitish blue flash.
>
> Did it 5 different times. Very repeatable.
>
> [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Piezoluminescence](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Piezoluminescence)
>
> It doesn't mention CO ... but I swear it happened. Will try again tonight. I was not intoxicated!
>
> All those electron clouds in the shells of the MCT / Fatty acids actually conduct with mechanical stress, like a piezoelectric crytals, and give of photons perhaps? I'm kind of a QED / QM novice, but it sort of makes sense to me that way.
>
> Anybody else noticed this?
>
> RobH

Curious of your brand? I picked up avocado oil instead of CO last time, but this is a fun sounding experiment!

### sjoshua — 2014-03-12T23:06:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/piezoluminescent-co.10073/post-115064>

Cool, I'll have to pick some up @costco and give this a whirl...

### persistence — 2014-03-09T05:59:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ask-jack-forum.10074/post-114251>

What is required to post questions in the Ask Jack forum? Does it require some kind of commercial membership?

### caroline — 2014-03-09T08:02:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ask-jack-forum.10074/post-114262>

Allow me Dr. K. ....

To post in "Ask Jack". You need to be a silver or Gold member. I am not sure that means a "commercial" membership.

To me it means that Dr. kruse gives us unlimited support in way too many ways to mention. If you have been here from the beginning - like a lot of us have....his generosity and patience is nothing short of amazing given his time constraints.

So, yes, if you want to ask Dr. K. a direct question and you want him to answer you ....it seems only fair that we help support his blog and forum.

The membership rules are very clear on the home page......

If you would like .... You can start a journal in the journal section and give Dr. kruse some history about your health concerns ....some context - he often pops in to help out.

Become a Bronze member .... It is free!

Right now - there is a free 90 day optimal reset and Dr. Kruse has given all of us a huge amount of his time ...plus lots of expert guests....

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-11T18:02:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ee-13-quantum-water-chemistry.10075/post-114812>

> This is why summer can be thought of as the season of illness
> reversals.

> Do we really eat sunshine? The only thing better than electrons for health is photons.

He's been hinting at this recently.

I'll tell you something that came to me recently. I was reading a book about journeys around the Mediterranean and when the writer got to St. Tropez he mentioned that it fell out of fashion in the 1920s. Why? Sunbathing came in -- popularised by friends of F. Scott Fitzgerald's -- and the beach there is stony. I just googled to find a link on that:

> Further up the coast, at the Hotel du Cap in Antibes, the modern sun and sand holiday was born at the end of the Twenties, when for the first time the likes of Scott Fitzgerald and Hemingway came to the Riviera in the summer and, famously, Coco Chanel went home to Paris with a suntan -previously only manual labourers sported tans.

[http://www.dailymail.co.uk/travel/holidaytypeshub/article-586817/And-God-created-idyll-Riviera.html](http://www.dailymail.co.uk/travel/holidaytypeshub/article-586817/And-God-created-idyll-Riviera.html)

Reading that a few weeks ago, I thought of Jack's hints, and thought ... I wonder: is there a historical significance here? End of the twenties? Was the birth of the sun-and-sand beach holiday, because people were unconsciously looking for ways to mitigate environmental mismatches that were already under way by then?

> Between 1924 and the outbreak of the Second World War, the [Phoebus cartel](http://forum.jackkruse.com/wiki/Phoebus_cartel) attempted to fix prices and sales quotas for bulb manufacturers outside of North America

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Incandescent\_light\_bulb](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Incandescent_light_bulb)

### NeilBB — 2014-03-09T19:51:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/notes-from-a-hospital-something-in-the-air.10078/post-114342>

Without practicing medicine day-in-and-day-out in the insane environment that we have today in America, you would not fully know just how bad things have gotten and how much worse it's going to be soon, in every way. It's indescribably discouraging from my vantage point. Everyone's only real hope is to absorb what Jack is saying and then to fully take your health into your own hands and to do everything possible to avoid becoming dependent on the medical system, for as long as you possibly can. It doesn't matter how hard it is, how much it costs, or how weird other people think you look. It's what you've absolutely got to do if you want to have a chance. Got this video on in the background, looking for a spark of life somewhere. Seems appropriate...

### prAna303 — 2014-03-09T20:04:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/notes-from-a-hospital-something-in-the-air.10078/post-114348>

I would say that the situation looks very much the same here in Sweden.

When i do my "santas little helper", giving advice on gait and posture related problem, i tend to meet the ones that modern medicin has given up upon. Many times they are broken by a system that is more like shipping gods thru a warehouse aka logistics. Not that i think doctors and their staff is bad, they have such weird work situation today. First stressed and then teached a method that is not working, can you not stress?

The weird things is that i have helped quite a lot of people that is coming back saying i changed their life. Many ask how i see things when i observe their gait for example when doctors do not, i say it is partly because i do not have a doctors education. Nowdays, thanks to Jack and others on this forum i can "cure" many problem related to tendons and other stuff, payback is great.

I do think that many more can help others, it would be great for all parts. Doctors could do what they perhaps should do, not struggling with paperworks and bad science. Everyone must become more willing to learn and try it live.

The funny things is when people ask about my education, they think i joke... Semiconductors, electronics and digital technology.

All of you doctors here, keep on fighting, that is what makes changes.

### Gracelind — 2014-03-09T22:16:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/notes-from-a-hospital-something-in-the-air.10078/post-114378>

Hi Neil

My DIL is a PA in an urban ER in a very rough city. What she sees daily is horrendous. Now with more insurance folks come in to ER with the most minute issues such as corns. They use ambulances like taxies.
They eat SAD with a vengeance. Then they want to be fixed by an ER visit.

She is a gifted healer who I'm gradually bringing around toKruse world. There's a lot of conventional med residue from med school to overcome. Epipaleo eating seems to be key.

We need the enlightened docs and med folk to hang in there and help us survive the current madness.

### Josh — 2014-03-09T23:51:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/notes-from-a-hospital-something-in-the-air.10078/post-114388>

For my 50th birthday, I was just informed by Blue Cross of AZ, I will be blessed with a 25% rate increase, just because....for a policy that gives me no access to basic healthcare. Sometimes I feel that if another healthcare practitioner alternative or allopathic tells me they do not have the time to review Functional Medicine and it is not their responsibility to know this stuff anyway, I will gouge their eyes out.....Traditionally being a healthcare provider/doctor was a lifelong commitment to learning, experimentation and self-cultivation with rewards and frustrations. Two of my heroes are depicted in "4th Uncle in the Mountain". Both he and his father embody this. He lives in Manchester, VT now....

### NeilBB — 2014-03-10T00:42:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/notes-from-a-hospital-something-in-the-air.10078/post-114404>

People need to realize that from the perspective of the modern "healthcare system" (and modern society at large really), we are all considered to be fungible, that is, easily replaced by an "equivalent unit," and therefore not very important as individuals. We are all (doctors and patients alike) cogs in a machine. An enormous, all powerful, malfunctioning, irresponsible machine.

Bottom line is this. If you consider yourself to be important, you have to learn to extricate yourself from the machine whenever and to whatever extent you are able.

Yeah, I've had a pretty rough week at work. But nonetheless, it's true.

### caroline — 2014-03-10T01:45:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/notes-from-a-hospital-something-in-the-air.10078/post-114422>

I have been very lucky to have many caring, wonderful Docs as friends over the years. At least 20 years ago I was talking about this with one of them and asking what to do ....and he told me to make sure I stay well and out of the hospital at all costs! he got it all those years ago! He also said to never leave a loved one alone in a hospital.
I never left my DH's side - I slept in a chair or on the floor or what ever ....they knew I wouldn't leave and so they cared for me too!
I think almost all of you professionals are amazing ....and now that you know better - you are doing better!

### Vinje — 2014-03-09T20:10:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/top-cities-areas-to-live-in.10079/post-114349>

Hey guys, I posted this on the Facebook page but I think I can get more recommendations here.
What would be your guys top ten places to live in the US as far as low emf, quality food, good farming practices, good people ect. Looking to make a move out of my city and into a more biology friendly city. Open to any suggestions. Thanks guys in advance, you all kick ass!

### nicld — 2014-03-10T16:04:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/top-cities-areas-to-live-in.10079/post-114532>

Jack would say where he lives is the best, close to fresh seafood and the whole crater things (by New Orleans). I think he has also said that Colorado is not too bad either (I vote for there). There are places you definitely DON'T want to live (large cities like San Fran, New York City on the 15th floor). Living in the country surrounded by pine trees with great well or spring fed water would be good but somewhere by the ocean for grounding CT would be better. I have been watching a bunch of shows about people who live in the remote areas and that just looks so appealing to me. I would prefer the mountains over the swamps though.

Hope this helps a bit.

Nicole

Lifestyle -eating mainly epi paleo, no processed foods, no gluten, mainly protein, nuts, fresh /frozen fruits and veggies. Taking all the recommended supplements. Sleep is excellent. Cortisol was a bit high in the AM so have taken supplements to fix that. On BHRT, menopausal. Have done leptin reset and avoided night shades to fix leaky gut.
Sans gall bladder for 20 years.
Working on thyroid issues as low T3.
Trying to figure out what is causing the inflammation that is preventing weight loss.
Environment - small town living but grew up on a farm so I have some concerns about pesticides and detox.

# Thread: Kids and technology
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-and-technology.10088/>
**Subforum:** The EMF Rx
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### NeilBB — 2014-03-10T04:30:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-and-technology.10088/post-114452>

[http://m.huffpost.com/us/entry/4899218](http://m.huffpost.com/us/entry/4899218)

Mainstream article on getting tech devices away from kids. But note that the backlash from moms in the comments is massive.

### NeilBB — 2014-03-10T04:35:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-and-technology.10088/post-114455>

> [NeilBB said:](/goto/post?id=114452)
>
> [http://m.huffpost.com/us/entry/4899218](http://m.huffpost.com/us/entry/4899218)
>
> Mainstream article on getting tech devices away from kids. But note that the backlash from moms in the comments is massive.

The comments were actually on a Facebook page and didn't post here. But trust me, those mom's were furious about kids being separated from their gadgets.

### prAna303 — 2014-03-10T08:16:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-and-technology.10088/post-114476>

Once again i chime in...

I observe technology usage, mostly because i come from a business where i was part of manufacturing and later on selling, to today, quite hostile agains the trends.

I have friends who give their 1 year old baby iPhone entertainment BECAUSE they can not stand having babies play around and make noices. In many cases the parents are high on medicin for ADHD and other problems, they can not stand the chaos. Also friends that make kids to show that they are perfect couples, trust me, they even confess to this. Often hear, but I want a silent moment, it will not harm the baby, look how smart he/she/it is.

My point to many is that because the parents are so low on energy they go the easy way, do not take the discussion when their kids want gadgets, they look at me and say that they have it all but you (me), what do i have. They can be really rude but i do not care, i take a stand and do not change my opinion.

For ten years i have seen the gear i worked with, yes WORKED with, transformed into entertainment. Everything must be rotating GIFS and effects. Everything is simplified into easy BUT stupid functions. I do not understand the reasons why schools use iPads, the books is transfered into light version with multitasking build in. When you read science you also should update Facebook, Twitter about that strange kid without iPhones, listen to Lady Gaga and look at cats on skateboards on youtube. Is this a great solution?

I remember even to this day some tests i did in a lab in 1989. At this place, a semiconductor factory in Sweden we tried some things that changed my life forever. In many of these, ESD (electrostatic discharge) was the main issue. For example, take a roll of regular office tape, pull a strip, just about 10 centimeters. The discharge would make changes to components 10 meters away, not completely kill it BUT make it just a little bit disturbed, weird, yes make it do weird things. Guess why humans is not allowed in a spot where they make components advanced like this? Yes you are right, we are trouble in a world of electronics BECAUSE we have tons of electronics in us also, easy described. We did tests with the tape and measured US, yes our skin surface react to that same roll of tape, we could even see changes in others parts in body also.

These tests makes me feel really bad when we come to children of today, the things that would slowly harm a grown up will change everything for kids in an instant, guess you guys, the doctors see this every day and me as a amateur see it in kids without ability to move in the real world...

### caroline — 2014-03-10T08:22:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-and-technology.10088/post-114478>

If this doesn't give you pause .... I don't know what would. Thanks Peter.

### cantweight — 2014-03-10T14:54:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-and-technology.10088/post-114518>

Wow the parents comments are just so typical....I'm surprised no one said they are not worried because a new drug will be developed to treat the kids.

I'm not even going to pretend to be the parent that did/does everything right, years ago when I was quite ill the tv was my surrogate babysitter while I laid on the floor with my toddlers, barely able to move from migraine, pain, and fever. But when you know better you do better right?
**
One thing I've learned in all of this is to never underestimate the power of telling kids the truth.**

Kids dont want to be ill, they dont want to be medicated, they dont want anxiety, they dont want their reproductive organs to fail, they dont want brain fog, they dont want depression, they dont want cancer.....more than anything they dont want these things at the hands of those that are to love and protect them. Kids trust that their parents will always do their best by them. Parents need to step up. Myself included.

Kids are smarter than we give them credit for, tell them what is in processed food and watch them stop eating it, tell them how playing video games affects their mood and energy for sports and watch them walk away from it, tell your son that keeping his iphone in his pocket is going to render his penis useless and watch that phone live on the charging dock, tell your daughter that using her phone at night destroys her sleep and causes acne and that phone ends up right next to her brothers....

Better yet...tell them the benefits of sunlight, water, magnetism, seafood, CT, and sleep and watch them blossom

Childrens instincts are still strong, they are resilient, and can still thrive when given the tools.....they have the power to change the future if we help them....now.

### Shijin13 — 2014-03-10T15:07:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-and-technology.10088/post-114520>

Yesterday when running around, Chaos asked me for iphone/ipod/ipad. to which I told her no. She asked why? I told her why. yet she was upset b/c all the other kids have this stuff. when I asked her if she wanted to be sick she said no. I told her these devises are like wheat. it doesn't seem bad initially, but in the long run they're not good for us.

My kids may want to have this stuff but they won't. They'll be better off w/o them. They understand we do things differently. they get that. At some point I anticipate they will rebel against this. but as we keep talking to them they'll continue to absorb this and internalize it.

I have several friends who have similar positions as we do regarding these devises/tv/computer etc for kids. and then I have those on the other side of the coin who've signed their kids up for computer programming classes at 6.

### nicld — 2014-03-10T15:49:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-and-technology.10088/post-114527>

I was a "mean" parent and never allowed video games in my house nor cable in the DD's room. The only computer she had to use with in the living room. She also did not get a cell phone until she was driving. Now she is 22 and I keep telling her how bad blue light is for you and what sleeping with the cell phone right by her bed is bad but she won't listen to me now. I just wish I could get her to at least look at some of this stuff since she is getting her RN degree in May and will soon be seeing sick people all the time.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-10T15:49:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-and-technology.10088/post-114528>

> [Shijin13 said:](/goto/post?id=114520)
>
> My kids may want to have this stuff but they won't. They'll be better off w/o them. They understand we do things differently. they get that. At some point I anticipate they will rebel against this. but as we keep talking to them they'll continue to absorb this and internalize it.

They're lucky to have you as a mother. Most people, of course, have no idea. Hard-hitting recent stuff from Devra Davis here:

> In South Korea where there is an extremely high use of wireless technology, a syndrome has been observed which they call “digital dementia.” Also, South Korea has one of the highest rates of dementia in the world – one out of thirty eight. ...
>
>
> WHO has classified
> electromagnetic radiation as a class 2 B carcinogen. This is the same category as lead, engine exhaust and DDT. ...
>
>
> The results are even more severe for our young. The brain triples in the first two
> years of life, and the faster cells are growing, the greater damage Unborn fetuses are
> at the greatest risk followed by infants and young children. The young child’s brain
> continues to develop until adulthood, and damage of the early years can accumulate
> and magnify. iPads over bouncy seats or potties pose uncharted risks to future
> brains and bodies. The Health Ministry of Israel—a nation sophisticated in radar
> and microwave radiation—advises limited exposures to wireless for young children.

[www.svhi.com/wp-content/uploads/2014/02/SLF\_2014Feb20.pdf](http://www.svhi.com/wp-content/uploads/2014/02/SLF_2014Feb20.pdf)

I also took a peek at her twitter account. She's just linked a long article on a baseball player who's died young from brain cancer. Areas like snakes across the back of his head. He's not the only one. Why baseball -- it's not a sport involving a lot of head trauma.

Here's probably why:

> What most people don’t realize is that part of the action behind the plate and elsewhere involves radar. The radar is aimed at the pitcher from behind home plate and at times, in center field aimed at the catchers to get the velocities off of the bat. It is used on almost every ball that is pitched and caught, whether it be at practice, spring training or during regular season, a radar gun is in operation aimed directly at the pitcher. The catcher is also smack dab in the middle of the radar beam

[http://thefullertoninformer.com/the...t-we-can-learn-from-what-happened-to-the-kid/](http://thefullertoninformer.com/the-untimely-death-of-gary-carter-and-what-we-can-learn-from-what-happened-to-the-kid/)

Deeply serious stuff. Can this go on? 'Tis at the moment, anyway ...

She has also recently linked something of direct relevance to this thread:

[http://movingtolearn.ca/2014/ten-re...ld-be-banned-for-children-under-the-age-of-12](http://movingtolearn.ca/2014/ten-reasons-why-hand-held-devices-should-be-banned-for-children-under-the-age-of-12)

It says a lot for Jack that recently challenged in Twitter by one of these "can't be anything in it" people he just calmly said go and have a look at this and at that and then come back and talk. It could be irritating to know just how serious all this is and to have people who haven't made the effort and taken the time to research it pooh-pooh it.

### caroline — 2014-03-11T06:35:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-and-technology.10088/post-114745>

I had no idea about the radar gun in baseball .... must be the same for hockey?

# Thread: "Superbug" Hmmm???

**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Josh — 2014-03-10T04:37:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/superbug-hmmm.10089/post-114456>

[http://www.bbc.com/news/health-26466674](http://www.bbc.com/news/health-26466674)

> Dr Rossolini said deaths from blood infections were running at more than 40%, but for immune-compromised patients they could be as high as 80%.
>
> Although KPC is still susceptible to an old and quite toxic antibiotic, colistin, in Florence this year more than 50% of KPC cases proved resistant to it.
>
> "Although present in the UK, the problem seems to be still much more limited as compared to Italy and Greece," he said.
>
> Professor Laura Piddock, of Birmingham University, said: "It's clear that what has gone on in Italy is our tomorrow. We have got to start preserving what we have got and use it wisely.
>
> **_"If we are really serious about tackling this problem, we have to start viewing this in the same way as high-income countries viewed the Aids epidemic in the 90s._**


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Hi Christian here from South Aus, 19 years old, currently studying science at Uni.
So let's begin.
I started the CT protocol I while back only to realize head dunking in 10 degree water gave me terrible brain freeze. This meant at least two things, I was severely dehydrated or had high tissue omega 6. However an AA/EPA ratio of 5.3/1 (and hence under 10/1) meant high tissue omega 6 was not the problem. So today I realized that urea on my Australian blood test is the same as BUN just in different units, and hence I worked out my BUN/creatinine ratio, it is 35/1!!!! Thus I have severe celluar dehydration, and hence why I'm getting brain freeze doing CT.

The cause of this is I think, due to a yearly dose of accutane two years ago. Due to the fact that the drug has caused me to have temperature issues (facial flushing/ hot body temperature), it must have caused leptin resistance at the hypothalamus (which regulates body temperature). This I think was caused by the drug specifically altering my photoperiod (due to a massive retinol dose) hence causing leptin resistance at the hypothalamus. This has then caused a lack of coherent water (from CPC 6 post on pseudotumor cerebri). Don't know enough about coherent water yet but perhaps it has something to do with the phosolipids not aligning correctly in the lipid bilayer of membranes (just read this from my BIO text).

Anyway so in order to lower my BUN/creatinine ratio I need to avoid dielectric blockers like the plague. Which brings me to my first question, chloride (comes from chlorine) and fluoride (comes from fluorine), are both halogens and hence dielectric blockers?

What water are people using in Aus to combat the donut whole (I'm doing spring ATM)?

Should I be taking any supplements to help lower BUN/creatinine ratio, magnesium perhaps? I know I'm low in Mg because I sweat like crazy easy.

Beside

Rest of Labs:
Vitamin D 49.6ng/mL
HS-CRP 0.74

Fasting Blood Glucose 90mg/dL
Fasting HDL Cholestrol 1.9mmol/L
Fasting LDL Cholestrol 8mmol/L
Fasting Triglycerides 0.7mmol/L

FBG shows that I'm leptin resistant at the liver, but HS-CRP isn't that high. Just thought I'd post all this up as my first post, any comments appreciated.

# Thread: Eating Lots of Protein at Breakfast
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-lots-of-protein-at-breakfast.10091/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### persistence — 2014-03-10T10:34:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-lots-of-protein-at-breakfast.10091/post-114495>

I have not read Jack's book yet, but can someone summarize why the recommendation is there to eat a large protein meal at breakfast?

I have noticed my glucose rises when I eat a large protein meal, and I read today that meals that are heavy on protein tend to increase glucagon (which is the chemical signaling the liver to produce more glucose). To contrast, meals that balance protein, fats, carbs tend to only raise insulin. This of course begs the most important question: what are the appropriate relative contributions of each macronutrient! They conveniently left that fact out of the text.

Just wondering if eating that protein meal together with some fat at the same time might temper the glucagon response, while still preserving whatever benefit is trying to be achieved here....

### caroline — 2014-03-10T11:11:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-lots-of-protein-at-breakfast.10091/post-114501>

Hi Pone .... nice Pic! Thanks!

Have you read the easy start guides on the home page? Lots of fat is a good thing! Do you have a BPC?

Have a read of Ricardo's posts ......lipids and Ketones

Are you doing the leptin reset? are you doing C/T?

Lots of reading my friend.........

Have you listened to Jack's podcasts?

### persistence — 2014-03-10T11:20:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-lots-of-protein-at-breakfast.10091/post-114503>

Reading one of Jack's blogs is an entire afternoon, particularly if I want to cross search ideas on Google. So there are only so many ideas I can pursue in my first two days here.

I'm following a low-carb Perfect Health Diet. I'm typically getting maybe 80 grams of starchy carbs. About 15% protein. The rest is fat. I am eating a lot of monounsaturated macadamia oil (my donation to the Australian economy . I am starting to use MCT Oil whenever my glucose gets out of control with the starchy carbs, since there is research suggesting it stops glucose oxidation (insulin resistance) but allows storage of glycogen. No idea if that is true but still experimenting. I get plenty of saturated fats from animal meats too. I have bone broth at breakfast and that is loaded with marrow.

I was on a traditional Paleo diet and was chugging dairy cream and coconut milk like crazy. My LDL particle counts went to obscene levels (very high risk category) so I backed off that and switched to the monounsaturated fats. I rejected olive oil and went for macadamia oil because it has by far the lowest polyunsaturated counts of any monounsaturated oil.

Now I am reading that the high LDL particle count may just be a side effect of high insulin levels, reflecting insulin resistance. I have not repeated the lipid test since going on monounsaturated. If the numbers stay horrible, then I guess it is insulin I need to focus on.

In any case, I'm focused on the topic here: really wanted to understand the reason to eat lots of protein with breakfast.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-10T11:21:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-lots-of-protein-at-breakfast.10091/post-114504>

> [Pone said:](/goto/post?id=114495)
>
> I have not read Jack's book yet, but can someone summarize why the recommendation is there to eat a large protein meal at breakfast?

Here's what Jack says:

> I use as a general rule 50-75 grams of protein with most patients. Some patients can use less and some need more. The key point of knowing how much is right for you is your hunger later in the day. If you remain ravenous throughout the day, you need to eat more protein in the morning.

[http://jackkruse.com/my-leptin-prescription/](http://jackkruse.com/my-leptin-prescription/)

There's also:

> Why did I choose to use protein and fat to retrain the brain? Because carbohydrates are coded for in the brainstem by a neuropeptide called neuropeptide Y (NPY). Altering NPY is easy via the diet and by timing. It appears our biology is adapted to realizing that excess carbs in our environment tend to show up only when the light cycles are long as well. Carb intake is also tied to the controller (dopamine) of the releasing hormones of the pituitary and to the light cycle. So the choice was pretty easy ...

[http://jackkruse.com/how-does-the-leptin-rx-work/](http://jackkruse.com/how-does-the-leptin-rx-work/)

The second passage seems to indicate that he feels that so long as carbs are out that re-training can take place.

Interestingly, Gary Taubes mentions in his book that if you make rodents diabetic and then allow them access to all kinds of food they will only touch protein and fat until their blood-sugar control returns to normal. Rats are evidently more sensible than most doctors ... which is remarkable when you think about it.

I suppose the first passage I quoted shows why Jack recommends quite a lot of protein at breakfast for the Leptin Reset. It seems to be helpful in killing hunger and cravings. He starts that first post that I linked by saying that you want first to work out if you need the Leptin Reset Rx, though. Others might want something like half what's recommended for that. And, of ocurse, tuning the amount is implicit in saying:

> The key point of knowing how much is right for you is your hunger later in the day.

I'm lean and I don't think I am overeating. I'll post my case in detail later in the Meet and Greet, but I'm having problems holding weight (probably related to absorption problems because of serious food intolerance issues) combined with loss of glucose control. I exercise to control the glucose (which works) but then this drains my glycogen and I have trouble getting that back. I'll post much more detail in another post.

Amazing about the rats. That is fascinating that the body intuitively knows to reject carbohydrate, yet humans in the same situation appear to have lost a basic intuition that animals still have.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-10T12:06:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-lots-of-protein-at-breakfast.10091/post-114508>

> [Pone said:](/goto/post?id=114505)
>
> Amazing about the rats. That is fascinating that the body intuitively knows to reject carbohydrate, yet humans in the same situation appear to have lost a basic intuition that animals still have.

The traditional response, I suppose, would be that animals just follow their feelings. _Their_ problem would be that where those are severely disrupted and providing inaccurate feedback, they're stuffed -- since they can't dissociate themselves from them.

It's hard enough for us! I think anyone doing anything like, say, chiropractic would be familiar with things like: "You feel that you're leaning over to the left now, don't you? Look in the mirror; you're not."

And, of course, it's not to say that our responses are rational in any elevated sense. I'm sure they are mostly down to feelings. With something like this it will be worries about "artery-clogging saturated fat" from countless determined, but scientifically groundless, press articles; trust in the doctor and his qualifications; trust, by proxy, in the pharmaceutical salesman (who convinced the doctor there was a pill for that) ... and so on. That's really mostly feelings. But I guess all those feelings come to us through our conceptions and are mediated through language, so that the rat is mercifully free from them.

### caroline — 2014-03-10T12:08:09+0200

your day should look like this...

get up - go outside and look at the sun - barefoot and on the grass would be great

Have a BAB \[big ass breakfast\] start within 30 minutes of rising ...at least 50gr of protein - you may need more
go by how you feel later in the day

Leave at least four hours between meals and at least that before going to bed. These details are hugely important - please don't underestimate that. Dr. kruse is very, very specific

absolutely no snacking

drink spring water or R/O water - lots

no fake lites after sunset. do you have blublocker glasses?

get everything out of your bedroom ... no clock radio, no TV, no ipad, no iphone etc.

walk barefoot and absolutely get to the ocean and walk in the surf - as often as possible.

### persistence — 2014-03-10T22:57:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-lots-of-protein-at-breakfast.10091/post-114606>

> [Gagnrad said:](/goto/post?id=114508)
>
> The traditional response, I suppose, would be that animals just follow their feelings. _Their_ problem would be that where those are severely disrupted and providing inaccurate feedback, they're stuffed -- since they can't dissociate themselves from them.
>
> It's hard enough for us! I think anyone doing anything like, say, chiropractic would be familiar with things like: "You feel that you're leaning over to the left now, don't you? Look in the mirror; you're not."
>
> And, of course, it's not to say that our responses are rational in any elevated sense. I'm sure they are mostly down to feelings. With something like this it will be worries about "artery-clogging saturated fat" from countless determined, but scientifically groundless, press articles; trust in the doctor and his qualifications; trust, by proxy, in the pharmaceutical salesman (who convinced the doctor there was a pill for that) ... and so on. That's really mostly feelings. But I guess all those feelings come to us through our conceptions and are mediated through language, so that the rat is mercifully free from them.

I have a different theory. The rats had simple choices: carb, fat, protein. Humans unfortunately don't get those three bowls. They get complex processed foods, that contain hideous - and well-hidden - combinations of macronutrients and toxins. The body if given three bowls of food will figure out by intuition which food gives the best sense of well being. But if your choices are 10 boxes of "commercial food stuff" there are so many macronutrient combinations sending out so many conflicting messages that the body - and mind - are completely overwhelmed.

The best gift of Paleo - and the diets it is inspiring - is to take humans back to simple foods and basic understandings of macronutrients. Once we master the parts, we can start to assemble a diet that makes us feel better, and which is also healthy.

### Martin — 2014-03-10T14:56:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/re-artificial-means-of-grounding.10092/post-114519>

Hi Matty, without testing your home for what levels of magnetic, electrical, and radio frequencies you have, you don't know if your becoming an antenna for all those fields to come through you when you use a grounding/earthing electrical circuit-based product. It may be fine, but in this modern world, probably not. When you are on a computer, for instance, any EMF fields coming from it will pass THROUGH you if your feet/hands are on a grounding mat plugged into the wall. You need to ground the computer's metal frame directly into the earth with a wire and metal rod, according to many emf experts. Look up Michael Neuert's site and search around for a way to make a simple USB ground device for your computer. The office building? Probably loaded with EMF, but unless you have ways of testing, you can't be sure. If there are flourescent lights in it, more than likely you have fields. Every structure is different. Hope this helps.

NSH

### av8r — 2014-03-12T11:43:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/re-artificial-means-of-grounding.10092/post-114977>

> [Matty said:](/goto/post?id=114884)
>
> I found an inexpensive multimeter, and have started testing everything as Jack recommends.
>
> I wonder this about using the multimeter. The black wire connects to CON plug and to the ground, and then you hold the probe of the red lead. They say the CON is your reference, the earth. Should that read 0.00? I get the expected readings, similar to what I've seen in the how to videos. But no one in the vids mention the number illustrated on the reference. Anybody?
>
> The multimeter shows a major drop in body voltage when I connect to my electric based ground, darn near zero. So should I still be wary of this set up over a dedicated ground rod system?

Hi Matty,
Yes, COM is the common lead to ground and measure it in VAC, volts AC. The closer to zero reading you get the better.

When it comes to grounding you, you’re much better off grounding to the earth, bare foot on the ground is the best and second to that is a ground rod to a grounding mat separate from your house ground… there are too many unknowns with house grounds, i.e. other currents caused by ground loops and dirty connections etc.

Don’t run your grounding wire from the grounding rod to your mat in close proximity to any house wiring too or other sources of EMF. If you can’t avoid it then run your grounding wire perpendicular over any house wiring paths you need to cross. Also, at the end of your grounding wire where it attaches to your grounding mat put in an RF choke to suppress any EMF that get on your grounding wire… a grounding wire is an antenna in theory! An RF choke is a simple toroid bead that the wire wraps a few turns around… radio shack or any electronics store sells them. [http://sigma.octopart.com/490833/image/RadioShack-RF-CHOKE.jpg](http://sigma.octopart.com/490833/image/RadioShack-RF-CHOKE.jpg)
Bruce

### Matty — 2014-03-12T12:29:49+0200

Denise Minger is in my opinion one of the few paleo super studs. She is not into bro science and deconstructs things into bites people can understand. I told many of you that the study was horribly flawed and not worth the paper it is written upon (Valter Longo—the director of the University of Southern California’s Longevity Institute, was the lead author)

.......I should have written a detailed account why but I don't have the time to focus in on trash when there is a lot of biophysics to teach............but maybe I should have. Denise uncovers some of the things I been telling you about with respect to amino acids...........soon you all find out why glycine is important. It is one of 3 AA in collagen. And I have already told many of you in webinars and in the blogs why collagen is so important from a physics perspective. Based upon Denise's blog........maybe there is hope for the paleo's who love their bro science yet?

And most of you all know what I have said about IGF-1. When your redox potential is low......its not the time to go get GH injections.......but when it gets better........GH has a deep helpful role in healthy aging. Again, here you see the same key open the door to heaven and hell.

**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-am-so-frustrated-with-my-computer-today-still-have-not-solved-the-problem.10100/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Tanya — 2014-03-10T20:43:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-am-so-frustrated-with-my-computer-today-still-have-not-solved-the-problem.10100/post-114582>

Here is my issue. When copying content from a web page that contains a flash video and pasting it into Mail, in place of the video there is a message saying "Blocked plug-in".

This was not happening before I installed the OS X Mavericks update on my mac.

I reinstalled the latest Adobe Flash player, but it is still not working. It works fine in Safari, just not in my Mail App.

Did anyone else have the same problem, by any chance?

### Shijin13 — 2014-03-10T21:29:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-am-so-frustrated-with-my-computer-today-still-have-not-solved-the-problem.10100/post-114591>

So, will try to load up on my seafood today before I attempt to play with it again...

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-11T16:13:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-am-so-frustrated-with-my-computer-today-still-have-not-solved-the-problem.10100/post-114793>

Same as Gretchen. I've never tried to imbed a flash movie in an email message either.

"Blocked plug-in" _sounds_ like a security message. Maybe Apple have tightened up the security in the latest iteration, so you can only send plain text or HTML. Or maybe you can send flash, but not run it, unless you specifically allow it in the settings. I don't know, though.

But I do know where there would be people who doubtless would know what's going on. You could ask here:

[http://arstechnica.com/civis/viewforum.php?f=19](http://arstechnica.com/civis/viewforum.php?f=19)

Lots of industry people there.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-11T20:20:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-am-so-frustrated-with-my-computer-today-still-have-not-solved-the-problem.10100/post-114829>

Having a quick look round … it's kind of interesting that the upgrade apparently uninstalls Flash (and Java):

> Mavericks doesn't come packed with two pieces of software you'll probably need at some point, Flash and Java. In fact, if you had either installed in OS X Mountain Lion, Apple went ahead and deleted it when you upgraded to Mavericks. ...

[http://lifehacker.com/how-to-fix-os-x-mavericks-biggest-annoyances-1450220339](http://lifehacker.com/how-to-fix-os-x-mavericks-biggest-annoyances-1450220339)

Presumably, you re-installed Flash, or you'd say it wasn't working in the browser, and you say it is. And the dialog box say "Plug-in blocked" NOT "Plug-in missing".

Basically, they don't want it there in the OS at all. It's not good software; historically, it's been notoriously flaky, slow and inefficient on OS X; it's also been a frequently exploited security hole. Better really if we all went over to doing videos via HTML5. And there's a further reason … but a short digression first:

You may already have the ClickToFlash Safari extension installed, which prevents Flash content auto-running (think ads, for example) and replaces it with HTML H.264 video where available:

> ClickToFlash prevents the Flash plug-in from running. It replaces any Flash element on a webpage with a placeholder; simply click the placeholder to load the Flash content. ClickToFlash also lets you replace Flash videos from select websites (such as YouTube) with H.264 video. Other optional features include advanced whitelisting.

[https://extensions.apple.com](https://extensions.apple.com)

If not you might want that.

(I think Adobe have got a bit worried and they've put a little more effort into the Mac version of Flash these days. It's updated so often, that in itself has become a pain in the neck.)

Anyway, the further reason is this: Flash was absolute garbage on mobile versions. A total resource hog. You probably remember how, until a year or two back, Flash content could get the fan going and play merry hell on a Mac. It was really no-go on handhelds. Allowing it on iOS would not only have ruined Apple's nice new toy (the iPhone) but have handed Adobe a barrel to put Apple over. Apple would then be dependent on Adobe to update Flash for iPhone. No way Steve Jobs would ever allow that. The battle was already lost on the desktop, but they weren't going to sell the pass on a new platform.

In short: _they don't want it there_. They have to put up with it on OS X, but they'd really rather run OS X and iOS under similar policies. If you think of yourself imbedding Flash content in a mail message and sending it to someone with an iPhone … well, they'd not be able to see it (because there's no Flash for iOS, for good reasons).

I'm not surprised the latest iteration of the OS strips out Flash -- and Java, to which many of the same problems apply.

I expect it's policy not to allow Flash to run in email messages -- by default at least -- under Mavericks. But I don't know, and I'm still running 10.7 here myself.

But the guys at Ars Technica will know what's going on.

### Tanya — 2014-03-11T20:58:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-am-so-frustrated-with-my-computer-today-still-have-not-solved-the-problem.10100/post-114832>

Thank you so very much, Mike.  I followed your advice and with ClicktoFlash was finally able to get it done. I suspect that you are correct in your estimate of what was happening with it... I was so reluctant to switch to Mavericks to begin with, and now that I looked at the list of "mavericks' biggest annoyances" in the link that you provided, I wonder if I should have done it at all.... Thanks again, Mike.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-12T19:45:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/statistics.10103/post-115035>

The Amazon URLs are much the same for the different countries.

Here's what fetches the reviews for the UK:

[http://www.amazon.co.uk/review/B00BIUAZUQ/](http://www.amazon.co.uk/review/B00BIUAZUQ/)

**B00BIUAZUQ** designates this product (Dr. Kruse's book) and is the same for any Amazon store anywhere in the world where there is one.

Substitute the domains for different countries to see what they have. So take:

Code:

```
.co.uk
```

out and replace it with:

Code:

```
.fr
.it

or

.de
```

There's one review on the German site:

> Dr. Kruse ist auf dem Paleo Sektor bekannt und seine Programme z.B. Cold Thermogenesis viel diskutiert und umstritten. Dennoch empfinde ich sein Buch als Bereicherung, selbst wenn es teilweise kompliziert zu verstehen ist. Und zwar nicht deshalb, weil er sich so kompliziert ausdrückt sondern wegen der Tiefe seiner Argumentation, da geht es richtig ans biologisch "Eingemachte". ...

[http://www.amazon.de/review/B00BIUAZUQ/](http://www.amazon.de/review/B00BIUAZUQ/)

My German is very poor. With the help of Google translate, something like:

> Dr. Kruse is well-known in the Paleo sector, and his programs such as Cold Thermogenesis are much discussed but controversial. However, I feel his book is enriching, even if it is in part difficult to understand. And this is not because he expresses himself in a complicated way, but because of the depth of his argument -- because it goes right to the biological "nitty-gritty". ...

# Thread: Uncontrolled Weight Loss, High Glucose, High Lipid Particle Counts
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uncontrolled-weight-loss-high-glucose-high-lipid-particle-counts.10106/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 86
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### persistence — 2014-03-11T09:17:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uncontrolled-weight-loss-high-glucose-high-lipid-particle-counts.10106/post-114755>

My problems may be too serious at this point for it to make sense posting this on a health forum. But Jack is different than others who pursue holistic health education, and I guess I am hoping for a unique insight that might positively affect my outcome. This is shaping up to be the first really serious health crisis of my life. It may end up being something quite bad.

Sorry for the length of this post, but since this is supposed to be a journal, I just want to be somewhat complete.

Summary

The quick summary: I have been losing weight and have had trouble regaining it. Some symptoms around this:

\* I was tested with high fasting glucose / prediabetes, and high LDL particle counts, in September 2013. I am over 40, so I am at the age where metabolism stops being optimal. I’ll detail numbers below, but – for purposes of this summary - understand that exercise appears to reduce the glucose numbers dramatically.

\* When I exercise, I immediately drop about five pounds of weight, and I then have problems getting that weight back. Most of this weight loss is glycogen and water weight, but for some reason no amount of caloric intake seems to regain that weight. After that exercise I get unusually thirsty, dehydrated, and seem to have to drink a lot more water for the next few days than I can remember doing in the past. I am 6 ft tall, and I went from 170 pounds to 158 pounds in a month, and I have been struggling now by forcing calories to keep the weight around 162. I eat past the point of hunger but still do not increase weight. I feel weak, and especially so the day after exercise. I seem to lose my aerobic capacity pretty quickly. This is new. I haven’t been this way after exercise before.

\* Greatly complicating my case is that I have long-standing issues with wheat intolerance. It was never formally diagnosed as celiac, but the symptoms below should make clear it is some kind of immune response to wheat. I have recently discovered that people with wheat intolerances can cross-react to many other foods, including rice and dairy. I have removed obvious candidates for cross reaction from the diet, but improvement has not been substantial. I would have to guess that after decades of eating many offending foods, my gut is somewhat compromised. Lack of nutrient absorption may have some role to play in the failure to gain weight.

\* I do not feel well in general, and every time I lay off the exercise to regain glycogen / muscle strength, I lose control on the glucose. The numbers are not diabetic numbers, but they are above 100 well after two hour mark of meals with small amounts of glucose. I’ll detail below.

\* General practitioner did not find anything remarkable on the blood panels in September, but this is a person who wanted to treat my high fasting glucose with statins. (Say what?) So I don’t feel that the case has gotten any kind of thorough review, particularly not from a holistic practitioner who might look for real causes of things instead of symptoms.

\* I have scheduled visits to specialists locally at a celiac clinic, as well as with an endocrinologist. But it takes three months to get seen. I am trying to accelerate that but in the meantime I get worse.

\* Last month I have developed a high frequency pitch sound that seems present all the time, most noticeable at night. Not sure if that has any relationship to current condition, just mentioning it.

So, I am one sick puppy. I would say at this point I am in a crisis, and it is not very far from the point where I just check into an emergency room and hope they can figure out something. I apologize in advance that I have in other forums tried to tackle questions head on and directly, and tried to get to an answer quickly. I just at this point feel weak enough, and feel like time is precious, and I probably am not in the place where I can read 40 blogs posts and absorb 200 new concepts in order to ask a question. If I were in a different “healthspace” I would take six months to absorb all of the new ideas here and then ease my way into questions.

Relative to my condition I am in over my head here. What I think I would really benefit from is having someone like Jack take on my case and at least point the other doctors in directions worth examining in more detail. Probably he would test more things than they would, and this might catch something to give a clue about the right direction to go.

Diet

Macronutrients:

I follow a low carb Paleo diet, on the low end of carb intake recommended in Jaminet’s Perfect Health Diet. I am typically eating about 80 grams of starchy carbs a day. At this point I eat either yam or taro for the starchy carb. I’m keeping it very conservative to avoid my food intolerance issues to all grains.

I get about 15% of calories from protein, typically high quality salmon/fish, grassfed beef, and some organ meats.

I eat about two servings of vegetables with each meal. I doubt these contribute many calories, but they do give plenty of nutrition.

I get the remaining calories from fat. I use a lot of monounsaturated macadamia oil (better than olive oil because it has much lower Omega-6 fraction). I use MCT Oil typically together with starchy carbs. I seem to get better glucose control with MCT so that is why I use it with carbs. I get worse glucose control using saturated fats like butter so I avoid those, plus dairy is a potential cross reactor for my food intolerance.

I have been making 100% cacao chocolates, mixing in one part stevia to four parts sugar alcohol erythritol. This creates a flavor similar to sucrose with zero calories, and the sugar alcohol causes me no intestinal distress. The chocolate is saturated fat with lots of stearic acid and I seem to tolerate it well. I will take a lot of these with me when out exercising and use it if I feel weak, to support the workout with some fat-based fuel. I will eat these between meals if I have hunger. If I see that I am losing too much weight I stuff my face with chocolate.  It seems to help maintain the weight.

I strictly avoid Omega-6 oils, but of course you get that indirectly through any animal meats that are fed with grains (e.g., chicken). I get Omega-3 from a slice of salmon in morning and some salmon eggs with salad.

Micronutrients:

In Morning:

\* Choline 150 mg
\* Green Pasture Butter Oil / Fermented Cod Liver Oil, two capsules for Vitamins A, D, and (maybe) K2. I will soon buy the pure oil and take up to two teaspoons each morning.
\* PLAN TO ADD SOON: Life Extension Vitamin K2, in addition to the Green Pasture product
\* DHEA 200 mg
\* Green coffee bean extract, 800 mg

In Morning and Again at Night (so double these doses):

\* Resveratrol complex 500 mg
\* Vitamin C 250 mg
\* Magnesium 200 mg
\* Prescript Assist Probiotic

When blood sugar is getting high:

\* Alpha Lipoic Acid, 200 to 400 mg
\* Cinnamon, two teaspoons
\* WILL ADD SOON: Vinegar, one tablespoon per meal
\* Chromium picolinate 200 mcg (once a day only)

Before Bed:

\* GABA, 2 grams

With each meal:

\* Small amount of sodium to taste
\* Slightly larger amount of potassium chloride to taste

Starting Soon but Not Currently Taking:

\* Vitamin K2
\* Selenium
\* Iodide (but I have a concern that this could make immune reactions to food worse)

Take occasionally:

\* Creatine
\* Nitric Oxide precursor

Typical Daily Meals:

10:30am Rise. I use eye shades while sleeping and often get fairly deep sleep, although lately that has been disturbed by my condition. After heavy exercise, that night I will be waking up to pee and drink a lot of water each time, trying to rehydrate
11:30am Eat slice of salmon. Eat two slices of frittata, loaded with veggies.
11:40am One cup of bone broth
12:15pm Eat 30 grams of tubers, usually yam, covered in MCT Oil (three tablespoons)
12:30pm Some cacao if I have hunger
02:00pm Light salad, some salmon eggs, maybe one sardine, covered in macadamia oil
05:00pm Lunch, consisting of meat and two servings of veggies, around 25 grams of starchy carbs, with two to three tablespoons of macadamia oil or MCT oil
10:00pm Dinner, similar composition to Lunch
02:30am Bedtime

<END OF PART ONE OF TWO>

### persistence — 2014-03-11T09:17:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uncontrolled-weight-loss-high-glucose-high-lipid-particle-counts.10106/post-114756>

<PART TWO OF TWO>

Wheat Intolerance

I have long-standing issues with wheat intolerance, from about the age of 18. I had horrible symptoms, including bad neurological symptoms. I would occasionally feel like someone fed me rat poison, unable to move or think. I would have difficulty gaining muscle, and felt constantly tired.

Both father and grandfather had similar symptoms but they never figured out it was wheat. My father had and survived non-Hodgkin’s lymphoma. My grandfather died of intestinal cancer. I only just learned both of those diseases closely correlate to celiac. Whether we all had celiac or not, there was clearly some intestinal food intolerance issues at work that seem to behave in ways similar to celiac.

Around age 30 a gastroenterologist (this was about the 10th doctor I had seen about these symptoms) suggested a food elimination diet. That made clear wheat caused the worst symptoms and I removed wheat from my diet. The gastro said to confirm celiac I would have to go back on wheat, but he indicated that might not be necessary since it was clear that I had some kind of immune response to wheat and celiac was just one form of this. I elected to not go back on wheat.

After removing wheat the truly horrible symptoms went away, but I still failed to thrive. Gaining muscle was still tough for me. Jump forward to a few months ago, and I find out that now they are discovering that people who have immune responses to wheat also “cross react” with other types of foods. One study is here:

[http://www.scirp.org/journal/PaperInformation.aspx?paperID=26626#.UxwXtj9dX-Y](http://www.scirp.org/journal/PaperInformation.aspx?paperID=26626#.UxwXtj9dX-Y)

Based on that study, the rice and dairy just came out of diet a few months ago. While I feel better without them, I still don’t feel good.

Summary of foods I have removed from my diet:

\* Wheat and all grains, including rice and corn
\* Dairy, including cream
\* Coffee (that was a tough one to remove)
\* Lactose (I don’t absorb the sugar in addition to cross reaction on dairy proteins)
\* Fruit / Fructose (I discovered that one out of three Europeans do not absorb fructose properly, and it does appear to cause me some strange feelings after eating it)

I understand that people with wheat intolerance can have malabsorption of one or more FODMAP sugars. I definitely have lactose malabsorption. I may have fructose malabsorption (not confirmed by hydrogen breath test yet). I need a good dietician who understands celiac and FODMAP issues to work through this with me, because I definitely eat other FODMAP foods.

Prediabetes

Two years ago I tested with high LDL cholesterol and higher than normal fasting glucose. I resolved to fix this, and I went on the “Paleo” diet. Unfortunately, I was tested again in September 2013 and my LDL cholesterol had really exploded up, and my fasting glucose was also up substantially, to around 115 mg/dL, prediabetic. I had the NMR Lipoprofile test for LDL, and it showed my LDL particle counts around 2300, where above 1500 is considered highest risk.

I started using a glucometer and I have a pretty good handle now on my glucose pattern. I wake with fasting glucose around 90 to 95. If I take GABA at night, this for whatever reason appears to lower fasting glucose to low 80s. An hour after rising, the glucose starts to rise to around 100-105. So something there looks broken. With a large protein meal that goes to 105.

After the breakfast carbs, glucose goes to the 120s and then slowly comes down to around 105. After maybe three to four hours I might get a reading in the 90s again.

At lunch I see a similar pattern, where glucose goes into 120s and then slowly tapers back to low 100s. After three or four hours it is under 100 again.

I can change these numbers dramatically with exercise. After intense exercise, glucose is pegged in the low 80s and after a meal to the 90s. I guess the muscles bypass normal insulin metabolism and just grab whatever glucose they can find. The problem - as described earlier - is that exercise causes me to lose a lot of weight quickly, and I have problems getting it back.

It seems to me that exercise presents a very narrow time window in which the muscle could be replenished by "cheating" outside of normal insulin / glucose metabolism. I guess I could superload carbs at this moment but have not tried this. Jack's article on EMF4 talks about using an alternate pathway with D-Ribose that can restore glycogen. I'm still reading that and I don't understand the details. Is there anything I can \*eat\* after intense exercise to speed up the glycogen replacement?

After much reading, my best guess is that I have insulin resistance and something is causing me to have constant high insulin levels. These high insulin levels are probably what drives my hyperlipidemia results. If I could get the insulin under control, the LDL particles would probably resolve. Unfortunately, I don’t have a way to measure my insulin day to day. I’m sure I could make constant adjustments to diet until I had insulin fixed \*if\* I could measure insulin constantly after meals, but that is not an option for a patient at home. If the endocrinologist orders a glucose tolerance test, I will request glucose insulin tolerance test, but that is just a one-time test. It doesn’t give me the tools for constant adjustments.

Summary

In the bigger picture, I wish I had the luxury of just worrying about how to fix my glucose / insulin / LDL problems. I am now confronting something much more disabling and scary. I find myself between a rock and a hard place. I can control the glucose with exercise, but with exercise I rapidly lose weight I cannot gain back. I stop the exercise to regain the weight and then lose control of the glucose. Even when I regain enough weight to exercise again, I feel weak and substantially impaired.

In general, it feels like I am working too hard to keep on weight, and getting too little result for the calories I eat. I certainly will see specialist doctors, but that is a crap shoot. If the problem is something horrible like a tumor, they probably will catch that. If the problem is something subtle like a particular food intolerance, that is a 50/50 that it will be caught. If the problem is something that can only be corrected in the context of an analysis of the entire diet and lifestyle, then I am totally on my own. I don’t think a normal medical doctor will begin to understand the latter issues. I can’t find anyone in my area south of San Francisco who practices medicine holistically, who I would trust with my case.

I am open to all ideas and suggestions.
