### Shijin13 — 2014-03-11T21:57:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uncontrolled-weight-loss-high-glucose-high-lipid-particle-counts.10106/post-114846>

Pone welcome to the forum. You've got some great advice so far. I'm gonna break this down for you, and it may not be easy to swallow at first- based upon what I'm seeing in your posts.

**First** your circadian cycle is OFF. Inger is spot on with her comment about your sleep. **Second** you're eating too frequently & you're eating too many carbs, which is sending any redox potential you may have down the toilet. **Third** your blood sugar spikes appear to be reactive hypoglycemia, due to the fact you're a sugar burner not a fat burner. FWIW 80g carbs isn't low carb in the paleo world. IF you weren't sick, you might easily be able to handle 80g carbs w/o issue. but right now carbs are your enemy.

So, now that I've touched on this. Here's what you need to do to fix it. START THE LEPTIN RESET ASAP. **BIG ASS BREAKFAST (BAB)** within in 30mins of WAKING.

Your BAB should have **50 grams of protein and the remainder of the meal should be FAT**. Your next meal will be lunch, and should be btwn 4-5hrs after you've eaten your BAB. If you can't make it to lunch then you need to UP your Protein in your BAB. If you're going to have carbs, leave them to be the leafy greens, salad greens, broccoli, bok choy, cabbage, sauerkraut. **drop your starchy carbs (sweet potatoes, yams, squash etc)** There are folks here who were eating upwards of 90grams of protein before they could make it to lunch w/o snacking. Next will be dinner. You want dinner to occur btwn 3-4hrs after lunch and AT LEAST 3hrs prior to when you should go to bed. So If you're trying to go to bed at 10pm, your dinner needs to be eat NLT 7pm. when it gets dark, you need to start wearing your blue blocking orange glasses. you also need to put down your electronic devises. If you want to read after dark, get a headlamp with a red light. the LR takes a minimum of 8 weeks to help reset your internal clock. One last thing about the Leptin Reset: **STOP EXERCISING!** The reason you lose weight when you exercise is b/c you're losing energy to your environment... and that's not going to help you get better.

Now about that Stevia. **DROP IT**. Some people have a negative reaction to it, and the result can have an impact on FBG and post glucose levels. If you're looking for something to sweeten things up. Consider D-Ribose - as its the building block for ATP and its sweet like sugar. But here's the cool thing it can do - it can help lower FBG.

Now for your food. Grass fed meats and organs are great. but they're not where the good stuff is at. **SEAFOOD**. you need **MORE SEAFOOD**. and by SEAFOOD I'm not talking Salmon or Tuna. I'm talking: **OYSTER, CLAMS, MUSSELS, SQUID, OCTOPUS, SARDINES, MACKEREL, WHITING, SHRIMP, CRABS, LOBSTERS. you basically want any seafood that isn't an APEX predator.** If you're going to splurge and have some salmon or tuna - do it sparingly. You can get this stuff fresh/frozen/canned at your local international market - and usually cheaper than grass fed beef/pork.

Now for your doctors and Labs. Post your labs here. We can hack them and help point you in the right direction. Also you want to find a doc who will run the Genova GI Effects [http://www.gdx.net/product/10150](http://www.gdx.net/product/10150) Call Genova and see if they can point you to a doctor who can help you get this lab run. Based upon your issues you have serious work you need to do with your gut. As you work the Leptin Reset - follow the EPCOTx Rx [http://jackkruse.com/the-epcotx-rx/](http://jackkruse.com/the-epcotx-rx/) to help you heal your gut. As for 23&me - you want the raw data, not the probability stuff they were displaying before the FDA told them to stop. You want the SNPs. Applications like Sterling Hills app, and Genetic Genie -are helpful but Audrey is right you need someone like Dr Tim Jackson to help you interpret them and make them actionable to help you recover your health.

As for Doctors. sometimes you find good doctors who may not know much about functional medicine, but who will run labs for you and begin to learn what these tests mean. Sometimes you end up in the office of a tool, who won't consider things have changed since he went to medical school 30 yrs ago. Your doctor is your subject matter expert, a consultant if you will on your journey to optimal health. I've built a resources page, along with e-courses to help you navigate the challenges that are the current health care system. [http://www.yourpaleohealthcare.com/resources/](http://www.yourpaleohealthcare.com/resources/)

I'll let you chew on this. make sure you ask questions, and listen to the webinars.

Gretchen

### Josh — 2014-03-12T01:12:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uncontrolled-weight-loss-high-glucose-high-lipid-particle-counts.10106/post-114891>

A little more clarity, typically issues such as yours are due to more inflammation than your body can handle from stressors: exercise, food, emf, infections, injuries, environmental allergies, toxins. Just adding more or different fuel to the system only goes so far.....a key is that when the cumulative stress exceeds your ability to handle it, you never go into repair and regeneration/autophagy. One remains insulin and leptin resistant, typically with the add-on sex hormone and thyroid hormone resistance. Things just do not work so well and one does not catch up and slides into adrenal exhaustion....Repair is usually a year or longer with some good gains in the first 90 days if one is meticulous...slow and steady....

Assuming I agree with all of the above, and assuming I also agree that one should not be one's own doctor, then who do you think should oversee the diagnosis and cure of the above symptoms / disease? Is it the gastroenterologist who specializes in similar conditions like celiac? My experience with those has been in past they focus on finding evidence of some immediate disease, and if they don't they simply have nothing to offer. Just finding one who specializes in celiac is incredibly tough. If you live someplace remote, forget it.

Is it a holistic doctor? Frankly a lot of those are chiropractors who don't have sufficient science training to tie all the complexity together. People like Jack do not grow on trees. And - unlike Jack - 90% of the people who do this on the blogosphere don't take patients and don't have much interest in helping individuals. They want to write books, give lectures, and do things that make a lot of money for minimal time spent. So the issue is where do you find this person? I've visited most of the online resources for such people and you end up pretty underwhelmed by credentials.

The path to health is not easy for this kind of condition.

### Rylee — 2014-03-12T05:30:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uncontrolled-weight-loss-high-glucose-high-lipid-particle-counts.10106/post-114952>

Personally, I don't think there's anything wrong with being your own doctor. I've been ordering (and paying cash for) my own lab tests for 3 years, and finding the medications/supplements I need one way or another. I don't have good insurance and I can't afford $400-$500/hour doctors. The nurse practitioners and PAs I've been to weren't worth my time.

You're certainly smart enough to figure things out given the right support, and no one cares more than you. The only advice I have on the topic is research all drugs/supplements thoroughly. When I've gotten myself in trouble it was because I listened to charismatic people who sounded like they knew what they were talking about. They didn't - at least not in my case. My only other concern about being your own doctor is the fact that you seem to be in a health crisis, so maybe there's not so much time for learning.

Just wanted to offer you some encouragement. It can be scary when your body isn't behaving the way it should.

### yewwei.tan — 2014-03-12T06:06:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uncontrolled-weight-loss-high-glucose-high-lipid-particle-counts.10106/post-114955>

> [Pone said:](/goto/post?id=114951)
>
> I found about 15 threads that had the word Ribose in title. Sure, I can go read all 15 of those.

Jack goes into D-ribose in his Jan 2013 Webinar, 'The Mitochondrial Rx', which you need to be a member to access.

But anyway, D-ribose is a pentose sugar, which doesn't get metabolized by the body for energy, but obviously still shows up as 4kcal/g when put through a bomb calorimeter (hence the reason why manufacturers label D-ribose as being calorically equivalent to any other carbohydrate, and why calories isn't exactly a good measure of metabolic energy delivery)

I personally think that heating D-ribose anywhere below boiling is fine. Above that, maybe it's still fine is you're just doing D-ribose alone, but most of the time, people want to use it in something like baking, and you'd get all the problems associated with carbs+protein at high temperatures (may be fine ordinarily, but not a good thing when you're trying to reverse disease)

I personally don't think that exogenous D-ribose is a good way of replenishing muscle glycogen. The value of D-ribose in my experience has been to aid your mitochondria in getting back to great working condition, and allow your body to enter all the fat burning pathways necessary to **(a)** reduce dependency of muscle on glycogen even at aerobic threshold, and **(b)** produce enough endogenous D-ribose through the PPP.

(Implications from the last paragraph: Pushing yourself past aerobic threshold when your mitochondria aren't optimal is probably not a good idea. Stick to stretching and resistance training, then maybe some HIIT once you start to recover, and then only add in threshold endurance efforts once you're really back to optimal.)

And lastly, I don't think I've ever seen a cheap source of D-ribose.

Stick to stretching and resistance training, then maybe some HIIT once you start to recover, and then only add in threshold endurance efforts once you're really back to optimal.)

Thanks yewwei. That's a great name by the way!

I posted a half-life temperature study of different forms of ribose in this thread:

[http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/d-ribose.9143/page-2](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/d-ribose.9143/page-2)

Would be interested in your interpretation of that in that thread. I share your concern on baking, particularly if you are thinking along the lines of Advanced Glycation End Products (AGEs).

What would be the evidence that your mitochondria are or are not working well?

The points (a) and (b) you make I don't think I have read enough of Jack's blogs to understand yet. But you are saying you have tried to replenish glycogen with d-ribose and you did not notice that it did this well?

For a prediabetic person, after you finish high intensity training, and your body is in that special mode where muscles are sucking up glucose outside of their normal insulin metabolism, how would you replenish glycogen? Would you superload carbs just for those hours after exercise, making sure that your glucose stays in control? Jack's EMF4 suggested D-Ribose could replenish glycogen, but maybe this is based on being in some special state of metabolism I am not in yet.

### yewwei.tan — 2014-03-12T10:15:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uncontrolled-weight-loss-high-glucose-high-lipid-particle-counts.10106/post-114971>

> [Pone said:](/goto/post?id=114961)
>
> Thanks yewwei. That's a great name by the way!
>
> I posted a half-life temperature study of different forms of ribose in this thread:
>
> [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/d-ribose.9143/page-2](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/d-ribose.9143/page-2)
>
> Would be interested in your interpretation of that in that thread. I share your concern on baking, particularly if you are thinking along the lines of Advanced Glycation End Products (AGEs).
>
> What would be the evidence that your mitochondria are or are not working well?
>
> The points (a) and (b) you make I don't think I have read enough of Jack's blogs to understand yet. But you are saying you have tried to replenish glycogen with d-ribose and you did not notice that it did this well?
>
> For a prediabetic person, after you finish high intensity training, and your body is in that special mode where muscles are sucking up glucose outside of their normal insulin metabolism, how would you replenish glycogen? Would you superload carbs just for those hours after exercise, making sure that your glucose stays in control? Jack's EMF4 suggested D-Ribose could replenish glycogen, but maybe this is based on being in some special state of metabolism I am not in yet.

"Ribose" usually refers to D-Ribose. The 'D-' prefix just refers to a particular isomer of the sugar. Wikipedia explains this here -- [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Monosaccharide#D.2FL\_nomenclature](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Monosaccharide#D.2FL_nomenclature) , but essentially, L-Ribose is synthetic, and doesn't really occur in nature.

The study you referenced in the other thread ([http://www.pnas.org/content/92/18/8158.full.pdf](http://www.pnas.org/content/92/18/8158.full.pdf)) used both **ribose 2,4-bisphosphate** (synthetic ribose phosphorylated at carbon atom 2 and 4), as well as the more natural **ribose 5-phosphate** (the sort that your body would make directly from ribose). The study showed that the synthetic 2,4-biphosphate was much more heat stable than both ribose and ribose 5-phosphate.

When you buy D-ribose, it's usually just the standalone ribose molecule (no phosphorylation).

To quote the study:

> The half-life of ribose decomposition at 100°C and pD 7.4 is 73 min.

Which is the data that you're after, and essentially says that the ribose you buy is basically pretty heat stable even at high temps for more than an hour. So I guess mixing it in a hot drink shouldn't be a concern at all, but baking it for long periods would potentially pose a problem.

\---

Well, my point with (a) was that if you're fat adapted, then your body doesn't actually need to use glucose despite you working pretty hard.

Peter Attia over at [http://eatingacademy.com](http://eatingacademy.com) has done some self experimentation to show that he could basically derive 90% of his energy from fat at 70% VO2-Max -- therefore the need for muscle glycogen is not great at all.

My personal opinion is that you should not engage in exercise that depletes liver glycogen. I'm actually not too concerned about replenishing muscle glycogen, which I personally don't think really matters that much in the disease reversal process. But again, I don't think that glycogen-depleting exercise is a good idea when you're trying to reverse disease.

**Sidenote:** I've done Lyle McDonald's Ultimate-Diet 2.0 a couple times, which calls for full body muscle glycogen depletion. It takes a LOT of exercise to actually deplete glycogen -- 12-20 reps for a total set time of 45-60 seconds just to drop about 5g of muscle glycogen. Muscles hold maybe 300-600g of glycogen depending on how big you are, and the liver has 100-150g, so yeah, you'll have to be doing a TON of exercise to actually deplete glycogen.

When we say to "top off glycogen stores" before a workout, we're talking like 10g of glucose to achieve that effect. Personally, I've been keto for the last 6-8 months (only started measuring with blood sticks 6 months ago), and do fine with any exercise; even a 2 hour bike ride is just fine.

That's a long way of saying that I wouldn't bother loading up on any carbs post exercise. Maybe I would if I were a hard-charging athlete with a pre-existing good redox potential who needed to perform. But in your case, I think you should focus on building up your redox potential, and staying in ketosis is a good bet.

\---

WRT mitochondrial function, I'm just on the lookout for various personal symptoms. For example, I posted a question in Ask Jack awhile ago regarding Shivering and Leptin sensitivity -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.ph...s-a-clinical-sign-of-leptin-sensitivity.8824/](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/ct-re-warming-time-as-a-clinical-sign-of-leptin-sensitivity.8824/)

In short, the time to re-warm after CT is one of my go-to signs of mitochondrial function, as are indicators like my hands taking awhile to warm up after any cold exposure. I also find that I more readily stay in ketosis (blood ketones consistently >1.5mM) when things are humming along fine.

\---

Hope that didn't come off as aggressive

### persistence — 2014-03-12T12:46:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uncontrolled-weight-loss-high-glucose-high-lipid-particle-counts.10106/post-114984>

> [yewwei.tan said:](/goto/post?id=114971)
>
> When you buy D-ribose, it's usually just the standalone ribose molecule (no phosphorylation).
>
> To quote the study:
> \[editor's insert: ribose half life at 100 c is 73 minutes...\]
> Which is the data that you're after, and essentially says that the ribose you buy is basically pretty heat stable even at high temps for more than an hour. So I guess mixing it in a hot drink shouldn't be a concern at all, but baking it for long periods would potentially pose a problem.

I wish you had kept this portion of response in the original thread, because that person was asking about heat stability of d-ribose, and this part of your discussion is more topical to that question.

That said, you are coming to the same conclusion I did, but you are saying it in a way that sounds like we disagree. I hate when that happens.  100 C = 212 F and most baking would be done at temperatures over 250F. Presumably on the curve showing cooking temperature versus half-life, as we crawl up from 212 F the half life gets shorter and shorter, and it is probably faster degradation than linear. Since someone might bake something for more than 60 minutes, I think that represents substantial potential degradation of the ribose.

I totally agree with you that mixing it into some hot liquid under maybe 150 F and drinking that fairly quickly is probably not worth thinking or worrying about.

> Well, my point with (a) was that if you're fat adapted, then your body doesn't actually need to use glucose despite you working pretty hard.
>
> Peter Attia over at [http://eatingacademy.com](http://eatingacademy.com) has done some self experimentation to show that he could basically derive 90% of his energy from fat at 70% VO2-Max -- therefore the need for muscle glycogen is not great at all.
>
> My personal opinion is that you should not engage in exercise that depletes liver glycogen. I'm actually not too concerned about replenishing muscle glycogen, which I personally don't think really matters that much in the disease reversal process. But again, I don't think that glycogen-depleting exercise is a good idea when you're trying to reverse disease.
>
> **Sidenote:** I've done Lyle McDonald's Ultimate-Diet 2.0 a couple times, which calls for full body muscle glycogen depletion. It takes a LOT of exercise to actually deplete glycogen -- 12-20 reps for a total set time of 45-60 seconds just to drop about 5g of muscle glycogen. Muscles hold maybe 300-600g of glycogen depending on how big you are, and the liver has 100-150g, so yeah, you'll have to be doing a TON of exercise to actually deplete glycogen.

First point is I am not doing long aerobic exercise. I am doing high-intensity sprints. I might walk five minutes and sprint hard 10 seconds. I repeat that pattern maybe 10 times. I am not jogging. I am not even at 40% of VO2 max while walking. I assume it is glycogen that drives those 10 second high intensity sprints.

I understand that fat burning can work great for long periods of aerobic exercise. That's not what I am doing. I am doing anerobic short bursts of energy that rely strongly on glycogen.

What you describe is a person who has filled glycogen stores and how much exercise they must do to deplete glycogen. Your assumption is that the glycogen stores are filled and are easy to keep full. In my case, the subjective feeling is that there was very little glycogen stored in the first place. Glycogen just is not metabolizing into my muscle efficiently. So just a single sprinting session completely exhausts the muscle glycogen store. And I literally have to drag my legs home at the end. The next day the legs simply have no energy source. Again, the subjective feeling is that there is no glycogen in those muscles, and none went back into the muscle during the first night of recovery.

> When we say to "top off glycogen stores" before a workout, we're talking like 10g of glucose to achieve that effect. Personally, I've been keto for the last 6-8 months (only started measuring with blood sticks 6 months ago), and do fine with any exercise; even a 2 hour bike ride is just fine.
>
> That's a long way of saying that I wouldn't bother loading up on any carbs post exercise. Maybe I would if I were a hard-charging athlete with a pre-existing good redox potential who needed to perform. But in your case, I think you should focus on building up your redox potential, and staying in ketosis is a good bet.

Here I think you are missing my situation entirely. I cannot use glucose before a workout to top off the glycogen stores. I'm insulin resistant. The muscles don't metabolize the glucose to glycogen efficiently. All this action would do is send my glucose rising.

You probably have healthy glucose metabolism, so for you your glucose might wander from 70 when you rise to 90 one hour after a meal. You simply don't appreciate the magic of what heavy exercise does to the muscle's metabolism of glucose. If I do not exercise and ingest 50 gm of carbs by itself, I will have my glucose over 150. And it will be three hours before it is back to 105. Now, look at the situation after I exercise heavily: my glucose is at 83. I take those 50 gm of carbs by itself after exercise, and I might peak at 99. Exercise puts the muscles into some special mode where they either grab glucose directly or simply use the available insulin more efficiently. I can't explain the actual biochemistry at all. But the numbers say everything pretty clearly. The first few hours after exercise represent some special magic window in which I can cheat and feed carbs to my body with - apparently - no bad effect on my glucose. I don't know what it would do to my insulin but presumably insulin doesn't go as high either because the muscles gladly take in the glucose and there is no insulin resistance.

You don't truly appreciate how profound this change is until you develop prediabetes. Once you do, and if you measure your glucose often, the effect is profound and repeatable.

With that said, one of my problems to overcome is how do I get more glycogen to stick to the muscle. D-Ribose looked promising but you say it won't work. Another alternative is to try to ingest a lot of glucose during that magic window and try to measure what is the maximum amount of carbs I can ingest without overwhelming my system. I just know I cannot function normally - either physically or mentally - when I am in a state of complete glycogen depletion.

I'm an engineering / scientist kind of person, so it's not crazy that my journey is going to be based around learning the science, understanding the facts, and seeing if I can use those things to change behavior and affect my result. There are often many different paths that can lead you to the same truth. So I can respect your approach, and please respect mine, even though it is a different method than the one you would use for yourself.
>
> I totally understand why you don't want to contribute to my threads, and I am at peace with that. I will be glad to interact with you in other topics on Jack's forums. Peace.

This is good information... this helps us help you. we need more info like this so we can support you on your journey... keep asking questions, but also journal what you're doing on a daily basis....

I am willing to change diet and lifestyle to effect changes. I'm not attached to anything as long as there is good proof for the change I am making.

I definitely think fixing circadian rhythm would make me feel better, but I doubt that sleep habit would account for the body not wanting to store glycogen. That's a dramatic enough symptom I think it has other causes. But there is no harm in changing the sleep pattern and seeing the effect.

Yes, I'm feeling urgency. And it is discouraging when people lay 20 different ideas down and then other people want to forbid me from asking questions about most of those 20 ideas because I did not in two days implement one or two of the proposed changes. Sometimes I ask pretty simple questions and the answer is to make me read 50 pages of material that covers 20 different topics. I can only find so much available time to do that reading and I can only process it - with good understanding - so quickly. I'm making progress on reading, and I guess that will be slow progress.

Pone, I just want to say - you have gotten some AMAZING input and time from a lot of AMAZING people on this board....and for free!!!!! ### sooperb — 2014-03-13T17:54:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uncontrolled-weight-loss-high-glucose-high-lipid-particle-counts.10106/post-115199>

I think all I could add is that if your health is in the dire straits that you are suggesting in some of your posts and if you are desperate to start a fix, then you need to stop procrastinating and make a start or you could still be here in a month asking questions. It's all very well to have an enquiring mind and want to try to resolve any doubts but that is going to take time which you are suggesting you may not have before the leak in your systems becomes a flood. I have an enquiring mind to a point but where this is concerned I struggle with most of the science. So I started with the reset whilst I continued to read the blogs and Jack's book, it means you can be trying it, you don't really need to know all the whys and wherefores, just make a start. You can tweak as you go along as some are finding they have to do. You're not short on knowledge anyway it seems, but I feel you do need to have faith and just get going. I've tried many diets, ways of eating etc. and I always skipped to the plan and then read up on the small print as I went along. When I found Jack's site, I read a little to find out what the whole place was about but as soon as I found the reset, I printed it off and got going, now I am backfilling the story.

If you just came along to argue the toss with everyone then to be honest, you picked the wrong place. If you don't believe that Dr. K. has the answer, maybe have the discussion with him, we all just want to help but you don't seem keen to accept it. I hope you find the answers but I also hope you don't take too long trying to screw everything down to a definitive fix, it's necessarily different for everyone.

### persistence — 2014-03-13T21:31:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uncontrolled-weight-loss-high-glucose-high-lipid-particle-counts.10106/post-115244>

> [Inger said:](/goto/post?id=115141)
>
> Pone... I think I just have to warn you  as your reading proceeds.. you are probably going to be shocked at HOW important the circadian rhythm is. And the natural light. It has dramatic heath effects beyond your imagination
> but if you want the proof for the changes you are making (sleep patterns) you will have to do hard work reading for sure  I suggest to you to print out the blogs and read in candle night before bed time

As one example of my problem: it's not uncommon for me to wake up and weigh five pounds less than the prior day. This is in spite of feeding myself a lot of calories from fats and proteins, relatively low carb (<80 gms/day). For a skinny person to lose five pounds in one day, when there was no heavy exercise, no unusual dietary restriction, plenty of fluid intake, is just kind of strange. In addition to losing five pounds in one day, the subjective feeling I have that goes with that weight loss is extreme weakness. It's like I took a 10 mile hike and never ate any food during or after. My body feels like it is screaming for calories. I am stuffing my face with more and more calories to keep the weight, but the general pattern feels not right, and it's hard to explain it as circadian rhythm because it never happened before, and my circadian rhythm has been this bad or worse in the past, for decades.

This said, I acknowledge circadian rhythm is important and I will correct it.

When I found Jack's site, I read a little to find out what the whole place was about but as soon as I found the reset, I printed it off and got going, now I am backfilling the story.
>

If you don't believe that Dr. K. has the answer, maybe have the discussion with him, we all just want to help but you don't seem keen to accept it. I hope you find the answers but I also hope you don't take too long trying to screw everything down to a definitive fix, it's necessarily different for everyone.

I have been making active changes to diet and lifestyle for more than two years now. It's an evolutionary process of changing macronutrients and micronutrients, reading more and studying the results on my own health, etc. In general, I love change. I'm making changes already based on what I have read here. But I can't change 50 things at once. Have a bit of patience with me.

As I posted in another thread, in the MBTI typing system I am what is known as an INTJ:

[http://www.personalitypage.com/high-level.html](http://www.personalitypage.com/high-level.html)

So what other people interpret as me being stubborn is nothing more than me acting our a very natural behavior. INTJ people like to see things proven. They tend to make good decisions but like to see facts and will challenge for facts. They will ask questions not only to get information needed to understand the subject, but also to see how comprehensively the person giving information understands that topic. INTJs do not get bogged down in too many facts. They make decisions, and they take action, but only when there are facts to support doing so.

So, if someone says "do these five things" it's pretty natural for me to want to know some facts behind those five things. Once I feel I have a mental model and understanding of those five things, I take action.

I understand that other people process information and decisions in a different way. Give me time to learn each person's style, and hopefully there can be some mutual accommodation.

### persistence — 2014-03-22T03:43:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uncontrolled-weight-loss-high-glucose-high-lipid-particle-counts.10106/post-117063>

Saw an endocrinologist for the first time today. Nothing ground breaking, but her ideas were:

1) I am losing weight because I don't eat enough carbs. These are her words, and I am neither agreeing nor disagreeing. She says she sees a lot of skinny people go on low carb diets, completely exhaust their muscle glycogen, and end up in a very weakened state. Her solution to that is eat more carbs. I wasn't going to argue with it, and I understand her point of view.

I will add the comment that my discovery that D-Ribose helps me with exercise recovery gives me a lot more flexibility to experiment with more or less carbs. I think more carbs will increase my glucose. I think less carbs will probably lower my glucose. I am less sure about how to recover glycogen without carbs.

2) She thinks it is unlikely I am insulin resistant. She claims that most people with high-mid A1C values <6 who are thin end up being very insulin sensitive, and their livers simply overproduce glucose. This was more interesting, but of course I wanted to get a glucose-insulin tolerance test to prove that I am not overproducing insulin without a response. I don't see how she can be correct about this, because in the morning I can eat starchy carbs and see glucose go 130 to 150 and take four hours to get under 100. That must be insulin resistance?

She does not want to run this test until I am on more carbs, because she points out I would fail the test for sure if I took 70 grams of liquid glucose coming from a low carb diet. She said the usual recommendation is for people to be on 200 to 300 grams of carbs per day for three days before the test, but she wanted me at 100+ grams of carbs for a month prior to taking the test.

3) She didn't want to do RT3, cortisol, or any of the tests that Jack likes to use to help diagnose Leptin resistance. So I'll have to find another way to get those done.

I guess this is going to be a slow, long, hard, contradictory, and frustrating path.

### fitness@home — 2014-03-22T14:08:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uncontrolled-weight-loss-high-glucose-high-lipid-particle-counts.10106/post-117134>

Persistence, I actually finally gave up on Endocrinologists in general.

Mine also would not do the all testing I requested and relied heavily on TSH. It was so frustrating. I would bring him articles and studies and all he would say is "Where did you get this? You shouldn't believe everything you read." I haven't seen him in over two years and won't go back.

You can order the tests you want. Canary Club, LEF, etc.

I now get my thyroid labs done 2-3 times per year thru different doctors. My Rhuematologist ran them for me in December, having my annual physical done in April so I will ask my Internist for them then, and in August my hormone doc will run them. It's taken me a while to find doctors that will work with me but it is so worth it.

There's no curiosity, no lateral thinking, no pondering on whether what they learned 20 years ago still holds good.

The sad truth is that lawyers have more curiosity and lateral thinking than doctors do. My story with doctors in the last three months is a nightmare.

One Stanford specialist I sent a detailed letter to didn't even get a copy of that letter for two months, and he saw it AFTER I screamed and kicked my way in to see him and showed it to him during the visit. I kept asking the people who screen calls to show the letter to him or to his nurse. Nothing. My general practitioner called and asked them to accelerate visit. Nothing. They have no triage system in place where a medically qualified person reviews data as patients requests come in. The system overall is a black hole. No one treats the whole patient. It's like this everywhere. When I complained to the doctor that no one gives him patient messages, I then get a call from his assistant about the letter I sent two months prior.

That's why blogs likes these have become popular. People feel abandoned by medicine.

> If your tests show a liver issue, have a look at Sandra Cabot, she's done a heap of work on the liver and its issues and has a diet and supplements she recommends. It's at the least an alternative view to give you food for thought.

Based on my symptoms, I don't think this is going to bear any fruit.

I'll try to post next week after MRI is done.

### Dali Dula — 2014-04-08T14:45:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uncontrolled-weight-loss-high-glucose-high-lipid-particle-counts.10106/post-120370>

Glad you have no cancer. I doubted you did. I was CAT scanned for pancreatic cancer upon diagnosis because I was atypical for T2D. I also have benign cysts in liver shown through ultrasound. My BG lately has been high especially FBG. Can't figure it out.

### persistence — 2014-04-08T20:31:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uncontrolled-weight-loss-high-glucose-high-lipid-particle-counts.10106/post-120414>

> [Dali Dula said:](/goto/post?id=120370)
>
> Glad you have no cancer. I doubted you did. I was CAT scanned for pancreatic cancer upon diagnosis because I was atypical for T2D. I also have benign cysts in liver shown through ultrasound. My BG lately has been high especially FBG. Can't figure it out.

I think A1C is what really matters. If you get one high reading in morning that doesn't mean much. Peter at HyperLipid has FBG of 110 and A1C of 4.4%, which is incredibly low. I measure mine enough times each day that I can make a reasonable guess at the typical average value. I tried ketogenic for a while and my average went up. Very frustrating. Only sustained calorie restriction will get my glucose down, and I simply cannot afford to lose weight. That isn't an option for me.

The fact that my steady state glucose wants to average to 100 to 115 even when I eat low carb, and my LDL particle counts are so high when my triglycerides are very low, makes me think it is either a leaky gut issue or some kind of infection that my body is responding to. Not sure how to even begin to trace that down. I suppose the right lipids specialist would think outside his normal box but probably wouldn't look deep. An infectious diseases guy would definitely have the tools and knowledge to look, but my guess is he would hear his patient coming in complaining about "high LDL and high glucose" and would say I am in the wrong office.

I still believe the LDL being so high is evidence of either leaky gut or the body fighting some kind of infection, but getting a Dr interested in pursuing that has been tough.

### persistence — 2014-06-05T00:55:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uncontrolled-weight-loss-high-glucose-high-lipid-particle-counts.10106/post-129687>

It's been a while since I updated, so here is where I am today. After many dead ends, it looks like what I have is something that behaves very much like chronic fatigue syndrome. The key characteristic of this disorder is post exercise malaise. Specifically, after aerobic exercise, there is a massive collapse in energy production. Some kind of acid accumulates in the muscle and leaves it completely fatigued. And the worst part is that on days two and three that acid somehow gets into circulation and creates what looks like a metabolic acidosis. I get a strong compensatory respiratory alkalosis, and I end up breathing deeply. After heavy exercise this can be extreme and could keep me up all night.

I'm at the point where I am just starting to understand what this condition involves metabolically, and trying to figure out who can best help me advance treatment. The disease appears to be a mitochondrial dysfunction in NADH recycling to NAD+. NAD+ is key to generation of ATP and regulates many processes in the body. Whereas a "healthy" person might have an NAD+ to NADH ratio of between 2000-to-1 and 500-to-1, in a person with chronic fatigue syndrome this ratio can be sub 200 to 1. Failure to generate NAD+ in sufficient quantity is associated with both glucose metabolism problems, and lipid problems, and both of these are issues for me.

There are a few supplement strategies for this. R-Lipoic acid apparently helps to convert NADH to NAD. I'm still trying to figure out safe dosing. The Europeans use R-Lipoic intravenously to treat diabetic neuropathy, so it is in use in high doses. It's very interesting that some theorize that diabetic neuropathy destroys and injures tissues because the overwhelming amount of NADH gets improperly metabolized by the mitochondria into free radicals. So fix the NAD+ problem, and the NADH to NAD+ recycling problem, and you may fix diabetes too:

[http://news.wustl.edu/news/Pages/4169.aspx](http://news.wustl.edu/news/Pages/4169.aspx)

In terms of producing NAD+, there are multiple interventions possible including tryptophan, niacinamide, and several other NAD+ precursors described in point 1 of James Watson's article below. The substance with acronym NR is available at Amazon with brand name Niagen.

[http://www.anti-agingfirewalls.com/...list-of-things-i-learned-about-aging-in-2013/](http://www.anti-agingfirewalls.com/2014/01/21/jim-watsons-top-12-list-of-things-i-learned-about-aging-in-2013/)

I'm at the very start of trying to understand which of these are safe to take, and at what doses. I have read there are some concerns that Niagen and NMN (in Watson article) may promote cancer.

Separately from the above, I have been learning how to speed the clearance of acid from my body. I have developed an electrolyte solution made up of 600 mg of elemental potassium in potassium citrate and 150 mg of sodium in sodium bicarb. I take that about three times a day in 12 ounces of water. I take citric acid (lemons or limes) and acetic acid (vinegar) about two or three times a day each, to support the citric acid cycle. For reasons I don't really understand, these "acids" seem to make my urine more alkaline. There is a lot of conflicting information about this online. I am trying to keep my urine above 7 pH, just hoping to increase alkaline buffers in my blood and make it easier for my body to fight the acidosis without using my bones for calcium carbonate. These solutions have made a huge difference. I have less fatigue after exercise, and the post exercise malaise is mostly cleared by day three. This is not a cure however, just treating symptoms.

### Josh (Paleo Osteo) — 2015-02-25T22:08:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uncontrolled-weight-loss-high-glucose-high-lipid-particle-counts.10106/post-156603>

Niagen is David Sinclair's product, he wrote the seminal paper on reversing aging targeting sirtuins and NAD+

### persistence — 2015-03-01T01:28:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uncontrolled-weight-loss-high-glucose-high-lipid-particle-counts.10106/post-156935>

> [Josh (Paleo Osteo) said:](/goto/post?id=156603)
>
> Niagen is David Sinclair's product, he wrote the seminal paper on reversing aging targeting sirtuins and NAD+

According to the anti-aging firewalls article I linked above:
"Sinclair and associates used an NAD+ precursor called NMN, but previous work showed that another NAD+ precursor could also work, Nicotinamide Riboside (NR). NMN is only one step away from NAD+, but is difficult to make and is very expensive. NR is two steps away from NAD+, but is much easier to make, is much less expensive, and is already available for sale under the proprietary name, _Niagen."
_
So Niagen is not the product Sinclair studied.

### Josh (Paleo Osteo) — 2015-03-01T01:54:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uncontrolled-weight-loss-high-glucose-high-lipid-particle-counts.10106/post-156937>

>

> "Sinclair and associates used an NAD+ precursor called NMN, but previous work showed that another NAD+ precursor could also work, Nicotinamide Riboside (NR). NMN is only one step away from NAD+, but is difficult to make and is very expensive. NR is two steps away from NAD+, but is much easier to make, is much less expensive, and is already available for sale under the proprietary name, _Niagen."
> _
> So Niagen is not the product Sinclair studied.

sinclair is the niagen founder.

### thomasdoud@gmail.com — 2014-03-11T17:24:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/toms-journey-to-optimal.10112/post-114807>

I am 52 years old and in good general health with some minor issues but trying to keep them from becoming big issues. I have been following the blog for some time but finally getting labs and trying to be optimal. Generally epi paleo diet but not perfect. Working on water sleep and emf as well. I have done CT but not consistently over time. My minor health issues are high iron (23 and me revealed processing problem, will post details) low free testosterone, high TSH, high BUN/CR ratio. I also have APOE 3/4. Considering getting local functional medicine doc to analyze MTHFR and 23 and me results. I would appreciate any and all comments regarding labs. Seems I need more in depth thyroid testing and not sure how to address low free T other than CT.

### nonchalant — 2014-03-11T17:40:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/toms-journey-to-optimal.10112/post-114809>

Hi Thomas, good to hear from you again. Are you getting some sunshine? Nothing like free energy...
I would have to agree that non-native EMF might be your biggest problem.

### thomasdoud@gmail.com — 2014-03-11T21:41:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/toms-journey-to-optimal.10112/post-114841>

Hard in Michigan these days. I have read on other sites that sun helps low T. Makes sense w optimal model. I do have a low EMF tanning panel but I am not sure if I should do that a lot if my redox isn't great. I did get a Vielight and hope that will help. I am turning fuse to room off at night but after last webinar wondering if I need to power more of the circuits down to really reduce EMF. It is interesting that both my wife and I have similar BUN/CR ratios. Hard to say but the field we sleep in could be it.

### caroline — 2014-03-11T23:01:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/toms-journey-to-optimal.10112/post-114853>

Welcome Thomas! Do you have all electronics out of your bedroom? Do you have a smart meter? do your neighbours have a smart meter? What is on the other side of your bedroom walls?

Can you tighten up your diet? seafood? spring water? C/T more?

always remember the three legged stool ..... water, magnetism and the photoelectric effect

# Thread: Fish in Ancient Israel
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-in-ancient-israel.10115/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-03-11T21:03:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-in-ancient-israel.10115/post-114833>

More food for thought on the desirability of fish -- a recent short round-up on fish in the Ancient Middle East, particularly Israel, from an Israeli newspaper.

A couple of telling quotes:

> Probably due to their abundance, fish became a biblical symbol of fertility, with Ezekiel the prophet promising that fish would even fill the fatally salty Dead Sea (Ezek: 47:9–10).

And this:

> According to recent archaeological finds fish even reached Jerusalem. Careful sifting of excavated soil from the City of David produced 10,600 fish bones, 90% of them bream and mullet from the Mediterranean.

[http://www.haaretz.com/archaeology/.premium-1.578294](http://www.haaretz.com/archaeology/.premium-1.578294)

### Tanya — 2014-03-11T21:15:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-in-ancient-israel.10115/post-114836>

I did not read the full article, but just wanted to say that there was not much seafood available when I was last time In Israel. And the way it was prepared also left much to be desired....Thank you for posting it. You do find the most interesting things. That is official now

### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-12T00:55:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-in-ancient-israel.10115/post-114885>

The article is about how things used to be.......not how they are. And considering the state or human affairs today you did yourself a disservice for not reading it.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/journalling.10121/post-114963>

I am going to start a journal today as Ameer suggested.

Anyone care to join me?

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-12T09:21:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/journalling.10121/post-114966>

So what's the purpose of the thread, Audrey?

### caroline — 2014-03-12T09:52:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/journalling.10121/post-114969>

Hi Mike .... I really like Ameer's ideas of journalling. Did you listen to the webinar today?
You keep a journal beside your bed and start as soon as you wake up.

It is sort of an audit of your life - he suggests 30 days.

You start by grading your sleep, clarity and then take 2 really deep breaths and continue... and things in your bedroom that may be different etc - bed, new sheets, pillow .....lovers!!!

then you do the same in the kitchen ..track time and what you had to eat and if anything is different in your surroundings ... and then thru out the day.

After a few weeks you should see patterns and what affects your sleep....

One thing that he said that I really find interesting ... old habits can come back..

He also said to map out the perfect day - I thought that was a super idea.

Tonite - the TV is going off at 8:00pm - don't know what I will do with myself - but that is another story I guess!

and I am going to sleep at the bottom of the bed......

He offered some giveaways and one is his sleep thing....

# Thread: Scallop week in Rye
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scallop-week-in-rye.10123/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-03-12T10:32:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scallop-week-in-rye.10123/post-114972>

> Back in the Middle Ages, the French made several forays across the English Channel to sack the picturesque little Sussex town of Rye.
>
> These days they arrive on a more frequent basis – to buy the most succulent scallops I’ve ever tasted, because the arc of Rye Bay is to this prized mollusc what crabs are to Cromer and oysters to Galway.
> This pre-eminence is celebrated each February at the annual Rye Bay Scallop Week, which this year celebrated its seventh annual staging.
>
> Scallops with black pudding bon-bons and roast parsnip purée at the ancient Mermaid Inn; wood roast scallops flamed on the shell with pastis and shaved fennel, dill and lemon at the elegant George Grill; seared scallops with Jerusalem artichoke, Serrano ham and squid ink sauce at the homely Ship Inn; scallop, potato and watercress chowder at trendy Webbe’s In Rye – the choices were certainly multiple, with 24 venues across the district taking part ...

[http://www.belfasttelegraph.co.uk/life/travel/ryes-shellfish-intentions-30062410.html](http://www.belfasttelegraph.co.uk/life/travel/ryes-shellfish-intentions-30062410.html)

### Tanya — 2014-03-12T20:08:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scallop-week-in-rye.10123/post-115046>

Mike, if I ever visit England again, this town would definitely be on my itinerary - beautiful place with so much history and now my favorite food - scallops.

# Thread: Huevos rancheros
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huevos-rancheros.10124/>
**Subforum:** Epi-Paleo Recipes
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-03-12T15:14:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huevos-rancheros.10124/post-114999>

Perhaps this would be more of a summer/autumn dish. If there are any tomatoes around where I am right now then they're grown under glass.

But anyway, does anyone do a version of these?

The first I heard of this dish was when the Two Fat Ladies cooked it on the TV. They used chilli con carne over the eggs, which seems not to be authentic ... but a pretty good idea.

I came across a version in the recipe section of Dr. Perlmutter's wonderful new book. And I just tried googling "paleo huevos rancheros". Loads of hits. This was the top one:

[http://janeshealthykitchen.com/paleo-huevos-rancheros/](http://janeshealthykitchen.com/paleo-huevos-rancheros/)

There she's paleoized it by replacing the tortillas with a kind of stew of sweet potatoes used as a base. Looks OK. I don't think I'd bother with sweet potatoes, though, myself.

If I make it, I'll usually have a couple of fried eggs topped with some chilli and with some salsa and some gucamole on the side -- or just some avocado if I can't be bothered to make gucamole.

What do others do?

### Lahelada — 2014-03-12T15:40:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huevos-rancheros.10124/post-115003>

I lived in Mexico for a good while and this is how you cook them there.
The sauce is made with chile ancho (dried chile) and some jalapeño or chile arbol (lady finger chili) for heat. Last I heard you could get them in the UK. harrods foodhall has them for sure. A sauce that you can make anywhere and store is this:
I litre of passata, as many finely choppedgreen or red chiles as you can stand , 2 or three garlic cloves , 2 or three allspice berries ,a clove and oregano. Water to keep thinning the sauce.Fry up the chiles,garlic and the allspice and clove, lightly of course so the garlic does not go bitter. Add the passata , simmer on low for as long as you can frequently adding water.In the final simmer cook down the liquid and add the oregano. This sauce gos with any meat too.
Ladle on a plate,put your eggs on top and grate cheese over them.If your sauce is very spicy you could also add sour cream. The fried tortilla is essentially a filler in everyday cooking but serves the purpose of giving a neutral element to teh palate. The cheese and sour cream serve the same purpose to cool down to mitigate the effect on teh palate. You could also try a pairing with cauliflower cheese for a larger meal.

Edit:If you use a blender puree the sauce if you don't like lumpy bits
Edit 2: add note about chile arbol finger chile

### Shijin13 — 2014-03-12T16:41:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huevos-rancheros.10124/post-115014>

When I worked at a local coffee shop after getting out of the AF - we made these every day. Called them Confetti eggs. We'd steam the eggs with the steaming wand from an espresso maker, add in cheese, diced tomatoes, green onions, jalapeños, and serve over refried black beans and with sour cream and guacamole.

here's a paleo recipe [http://janeshealthykitchen.com/paleo-huevos-rancheros/#.UyBx0\_SwKv0](http://janeshealthykitchen.com/paleo-huevos-rancheros/#.UyBx0_SwKv0)

# Thread: High saliva estrone due to butter consumption?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-saliva-estrone-due-to-butter-consumption.10126/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### CoolSwede — 2014-03-12T16:43:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-saliva-estrone-due-to-butter-consumption.10126/post-115015>

A while ago I received my saliva test results and noted the following.

\-Estrone (E1) 43.94 pg/ml (range male <20.0 pg/ml)
\-Estradiol (E2) <1.00 pg/ml (range male <2.5 mg/ml)
\-Estriol (E3) 25.45 pg/ml (range male <66 mg/ml)

Thus, my estrone is elevated and I have been thinking about possible causes. Then I read about hormone content in dairy and noted that butter typically have lots of estrone in it.

Edit: [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/20494161](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/20494161)

Questions:

1\. Could my high consumption of butter be a cause (~150-200 gram daily)
1\. Is it an issue?

### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-12T17:24:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-saliva-estrone-due-to-butter-consumption.10126/post-115019>

if you cows are no organic sure.......

### CoolSwede — 2014-03-13T10:24:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-saliva-estrone-due-to-butter-consumption.10126/post-115146>

Thanks Dr Kruse for your comment. The interesting part here is that 50% of my daily consumption is organic (the butter I eat at home) and the other half is non-organic (served by my local lunch restaurant).

I have been struggling to get rid of the last flab on my belly along with the love handles, but they are very stubborn. What I would like to find out is if the high saliva estrone is the cause or effect of the stubborn fat. Maybe my butter consumption is the cause...or maybe I have an issue with the estrone detox pathway? The questions keep piling up...

# Thread: Greece and fish
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greece-and-fish.10130/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-03-12T20:55:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greece-and-fish.10130/post-115050>

Having a look around online.

I knew seafood was much prized in Ancient Athens. I was looking to see what I could find on that.

This from the American School of Classical Studies at Athens confirms that:

> Meat, except sausages, was rarely eaten by ordinary citizens, but it could be bought for important occasions at the cook shops. It was also distributed to the citizens after the sacrifices at festivals. Birds and **fish** (9) **were esteemed delicacies, also sea urchins, octopus, eels, and oysters.** The poor relied on unleavened bread, porridge, soup, eggs, greens, salt fish, tripe.

[http://www.agathe.gr/icons/pdfs/agorapicbk-12.pdf](http://www.agathe.gr/icons/pdfs/agorapicbk-12.pdf)

Here's a nice artifact:

> Athenian and Apulian Red-Figure Fish Plates Both of these footed plates were made to serve succulent morsels of grilled seafood, like the fish, mollusks, and other marine creatures that are painted on their surfaces. Greece and Italy are peninsulas projecting into seas brimming with marine life. A primary source of protein, seafood was a basic staple of the ancient Mediterranean diet; it remains so today. ...

Click on the photo for an enlargement.

[http://www.artic.edu/aic/collections/artwork/255](http://www.artic.edu/aic/collections/artwork/255)

Finally … here's a mouthwatering catalogue of the seafood available nowadays in Greece. Just a bewildering variety of stuff. And while the fish are cooking, there are appetisers:

> While the big fish is cooking we will have ordered some _mezedes_ to go along with it. Among them is _tarama salata_ (fish-\[roe\] salad-photo) usually made from the eggs of carp but if you are lucky, from kefalo (gray mullet) which is what it was originally made from ... You have to ask if the restaurant makes it or if it comes from a container. … A plate of fried gavros (anchovies) is as essential as a plate of fried potatoes and are eaten the same way, with lemon or vinegar squeezed on them.

A plate of fried anchovies instead of a plate of fried potatoes? Count me in.

[http://www.greecefoods.com/seafood/](http://www.greecefoods.com/seafood/)

I guess people from Greece are probably a "heart disease paradox" just like the French.

### caroline — 2014-03-13T01:57:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greece-and-fish.10130/post-115092>

Ricardo was just telling me that when he lived in the Matimes he ate almost exclusively fresh fish off the fishing boats ....... And has never been healthier.

### JanSz — 2014-03-16T15:44:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-lab-offers-tests-for-10-and-only-1-drop-of-blood-needed.10131/post-115776>

How can improved processes actually save lives?

We’ve created a tool for physicians to look at lab-test data over time and see trends. We don’t usually think about lab data this way today. It’s “Are you in range, or are you out of range?” Instead, we like to think, “Where are you going?” If you showed me a single frame from a movie and asked me to tell you the story, I wouldn’t be able to do it. But with many frames, you can start to see the movie unfold.

\--------------
I think the problem is with carbohydrates.
Carbohydrates is a name that accompasses to large variety of foods.
I see need to divide carbohydrate into more categories, not only high or low GI or nightshades.
Some carbohydrates are eaten by us (people)
other carbohydrates are eaten by our friendly bacteria (so they can multiply and prosper).
I see very little being said about all this. I guess this is the source of my problem.
\--------------
I eat lots of oysters
oysters
carbs-23%
Fats-33%
Protein-44%

What type carbohydrates are in oysters?
\---------------

Anyhow, please share your details to your success.

### giskard — 2014-03-19T17:45:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-lab-offers-tests-for-10-and-only-1-drop-of-blood-needed.10131/post-116436>

Background: 45 y.o. male in decent health with semi regular weight lifting and no chronic diseases.

I was never much of a soda drinker or sweets/junk eater though I ate a lot of rice and bread. About 4 years ago I discovered the "5 rules of leptin", right about discovering slight gluten sensitivity (migraines after combining wheat and alcohol, or wheat beer) I followed the rules and lost about 20 lbs which mostly stayed off for years, then relaxed said rules:
[http://www.wellnessresources.com/weight/articles/the\_five\_rules\_of\_the\_leptin\_diet/](http://www.wellnessresources.com/weight/articles/the_five_rules_of_the_leptin_diet/)
It also cured my reactive hypoglycemia. I used to have to eat every 3 hours.

About 7 months ago I noticed that my A1c over the years crept up from 5.3 to 5.6 despite the above. (I did not have A1c numbers before I started the "leptin rules") My lab fasting BG was always around 95-99.
I found this article by Chris Kresser
[http://chriskresser.com/how-to-prevent-diabetes-and-heart-disease-for-16](http://chriskresser.com/how-to-prevent-diabetes-and-heart-disease-for-16)
So I bought this blood glucose meter based on Consumer Reports and other reviews
[http://www.amazon.com/Freestyle-Freedom-Glucose-Monitoring-System/dp/B001A5R2FK](http://www.amazon.com/Freestyle-Freedom-Glucose-Monitoring-System/dp/B001A5R2FK)
I was shocked at how sensitive I was to starch. For example, 2 buns with a hamburger would spike me to 150; an 8 oz "sweet potato challenge test" would get me to 160. Upon waking my FBG would be 95-105.
I discovered my ferritin was in the 80s, so I started donating blood every 3 months.

After reading Jack Kruse I did the giant morning high protein breakfast, and started eating more Kerrygold butter, coconut oil, seafood, shellfish, and crustaceans. I drastically reduced my starchy food intake, so that post meal spikes were <120. I also started eating homemade kefir regularly.

After 4 months (and 2 blood donations) I got:
\- weight loss of 10 lbs
\-FBG-90-99
\-A1c 5.3
\-insulin 0.5
\-ferritin high 40s
\- 90 minutes post typical meal BG 100-110
\- most headaches gone
\- flat, even energy
\- better sleep

I was still very sensitive to starchy food. I was hoping the blood donations would improve that.
Then in early December I read Nikoley's many blogs on Resistant Starch and Bob's Red Mill Unmodified Potato Starch.
Here's a good starting summary on the topic:
[http://www.gestaltreality.com/2014/02/27/resistant-starch-a-concise-guide/](http://www.gestaltreality.com/2014/02/27/resistant-starch-a-concise-guide/)

I started immediately and ramped up to 4 tbsp per day pretty quickly (with a week of backing off due to extreme butt-wrenching flatulence). The effects were dramatic:
\- greatly reduced sensitivity to starchy meals:
\- 8 oz sweet potato challenge test peak BG: 145
\- 90 minute post typical meal: 90-95
\- feel full for my typical 5-6 hours even after a meal with some starch e.g. half cup of rice... some starch in a meal used to get me hungry again after just 3-4 hours
\- FBG went down to 70-93
\- after about 2 months, my weight had stayed flat, but my abs started coming out. I now have a 4-pack (plus a bicycle tire!) I hadn't seen them since big hair was in. Alas now I have sparse hair.
\- more lucid dreaming

That is where I'm at.

BTW there is a variable I recently discovered wrt FBG. If I have a bit of alcohol with dinner (e.g. 4 oz wine or a small martini), my next morning FBG will be 70-85. Without, it's 80-93. I wonder if this is why moderate alcohol correlates with reduced mortality. I wonder if I should take a small dose slow release metformin when I don't have alcohol with dinner.

Next steps:

This just arrived, for CT. I'm hoping it will rid my spare bicycle tire  :
[https://www.coolfatburner.com/](https://www.coolfatburner.com/)

I just started AOR probiotics in case some species are extinct in my gut (I have had around 5 antibiotic courses in my life, including Chloramphenicol for Typhoid in my early teens.):
[http://coolinginflammation.blogspot.com/2014/03/health-diagrams-ii-curing-autoimmunity.html](http://coolinginflammation.blogspot.com/2014/03/health-diagrams-ii-curing-autoimmunity.html)
After 1-2 months of that I plan to try Prescript Assist soil based probiotics.

I'm also now looking for homemade style kimchi, perhaps from a small Korean mom and pop store. I just went through a jar of Costco kimchi.

# Thread: Probiotics article in BJN
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/probiotics-article-in-bjn.10133/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### sooperb — 2014-03-13T00:41:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/probiotics-article-in-bjn.10133/post-115077>

Study: Probiotics Help Women Slim Down

Specific probiotics could help women lose weight and keep it off, according to a recent study published in the British Journal of Nutrition by a team of researchers at Canada’s Université Laval.

Studies have already demonstrated that the intestinal flora of obese individuals differs from that of thin people. That difference may be due to the fact that a diet high in fat and low in fiber promotes certain bacteria at the expense of others. Professor Angelo Tremblay and his team tried to determine if the consumption of probiotics could help reset the balance of the intestinal microbiota in favor of bacteria that promote a healthy weight.
Sponsor

To test their hypothesis, researchers recruited 125 overweight men and women. The subjects underwent a 12-week weight-loss diet, followed by a 12-week period aimed at maintaining body weight. Throughout the entire study, half the participants swallowed two pills daily containing probiotics from the Lactobacillus rhamnosus family, while the other half received a placebo.

After the 12-week diet period, researchers observed an average weight loss of 9.7 pounds in women in the probiotic group and 5.7 pounds in the placebo group. However, no differences in weight loss were observed among males in the two groups. "We don't know why the probiotics didn't have any effect on men. It may be a question of dosage, or the study period may have been too short," says Tremblay, who is also the Canada Research Chair in Environment and Energy Balance.

After the 12-week maintenance period, the weight of the women in the placebo group had remained stable but the probiotic group had continued to lose weight, for a total of 11.5 pounds per person. In short, women consuming probiotics lost twice as much weight over the 24-week period of the study. Researchers also noted a drop in the appetite-regulating hormone leptin in this group, as well as a lower overall concentration of the intestinal bacteria related to obesity.

According to Tremblay, probiotics may act by altering the permeability of the intestinal wall. By keeping certain proinflammatory molecules from entering the bloodstream, they might help prevent the chain reaction that leads to glucose intolerance, Type 2 diabetes, and obesity.

The Lactobacillus rhamnosus strain used in this study belongs to Nestlé, which uses it in certain yogurts it makes for the European market, but Tremblay believes that the probiotics found in dairy products in North America could have a similar effect to the Nestlé strain. He stresses, however, that the benefits of these bacteria are more likely to be observed in a favorable nutritional context that promotes low fat and adequate fiber intake.

© 2014 NewsmaxHealth. All rights reserved.

Oh dear. At first glance it seemed a favourable article ...............

### Josh — 2014-03-14T04:54:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-free-t-high-iron-high-bun-cr.10135/post-115313>

I see:

1.  Possible B12 issues through combination of higher MCV (96), lower total protein (6.4) and SNP's that partially disrupt absorption and predispose one to Lithium issues that regulate B12 uptake. The low total protein and slight elevation of liver enzymes indicate stress to the organ, possibly due to intestinal inflammation and absorption issues. Always makes me wonder about intrinsic factor antibodies?
2.  Additional signs of liver issues include high SHBG and low Free T.
3.  Higher Eosinophils suggest some sort of allergy and or intestinal infection.
4.  HDL borderline low
5.  IGF-1 low=low autophagy/rest and regeneration
6.  Iron issues correlate with lower energy/higher stress/thyroid weakness
7.  Correlates with previous observation that energy is lower than optimal as suggested by higher TSH, higher CRP, signs of inflammatory stress and genetic predisposition to issues with supporting cycles necessary to maintain immunity and high level metabolic function. Worth getting full Thyroid panel with antibodies, gut study of some sort. Consider obvious food allergens as a source of gut inflammation and liver stress: dairy, gluten, eggs, nuts, grains in general, sugar.

Forget the basement, try a week in a debris hut on the Upper Peninsula....just joking.

I'm so grateful I found you guys...cant wait to do my blood tests next month and see the results after 6 months on high fat and 1+ years on CT...by the way, would free testosterone by itself be relevant? what else should I test for in terms of testosterone and hormones? the most basic please...I'm on a budget!

### Shijin13 — 2014-03-18T23:59:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-free-t-high-iron-high-bun-cr.10135/post-116286>

> [Cristi Vlad said:](/goto/post?id=116281)
>
> I'm so grateful I found you guys...cant wait to do my blood tests next month and see the results after 6 months on high fat and 1+ years on CT...by the way, would free testosterone by itself be relevant? what else should I test for in terms of testosterone and hormones? the most basic please...I'm on a budget!

Cristi - if you're on a budget - here's the minimum: hsCRP, DHEA-S and Vitamin D 25-hydroxel.

If you want to get a better picture:

full lipid panel
Thyroid panel (TSH, Free T4, Free T3, reverse T3)
dHEA-S
pregnenolone
Progesterone
highly sensitive Estradiol
Free Testosterone
Total Testosterone
Vitamin D 25-hyroxel
hsCrp

the full gamut:
Lipid Panel - VAP, NMR or Berkley or HDL Labs
hsCRP Test Number: 120766 CPT Code: 86141
HbA1C Test Number: 001453 CPT Code: 83036
Fasting blood glucose Test # 001818, CPT Code 82947
CBC Test # 005009, CPT Code 85025
Comp Metabolic Panel Test # 322000, CPT code 80053
Thyroid:
TSH, Test # 004259, CPT Code 84443
Free T4, Test # 001974, CPT code 84439
Free T3, Test # 010389, CTP Code 84481
RT3, Test # 070104, CPT 84482
Thyroid Peroxidase (TPO) Antibodies Test Number: 006676 CPT Code: 86376
Thyroglobulin, Comprehensive (With Anti-Tg Screen and Reflex to RIA) (Endocrine Sciences) Test Number: 500540 CPT Code: Call client services.
Progesterone, Test # 004312, CPT Code 84144
Estradiol, Sensitive, Test # 140244, CPT Code 82670
Total Estrogens Test# 004549, CPT Code 82672
SHGB Test #082016 CPT Code: 84270
Testosterone Free Direct, & serum with Total Testosterone: Test # 140103, CPT Codes 84402, 84403
Prolactin Test # 004465, CPT code 84146
cortisol Test # 004051, CPT Code 82533
DHEA-S Test # 004020, CPT Code 82627
IGF-1 Test #010636, CPT Code 84305
RBC magnesium Test # 080283, CPT Code 83735
Vitamin D25 Hyrdoxy Test # 081950, CPT code 82306
Serotonin Test# 120204, CPT Code 84260 (requires frozen specimens)
Pregnenolone Test# 140707, CPT Code 84140 (requires Frozen Specime

> [thomasdoud@gmail.com said:](/goto/post?id=115936)
>
> Neurt says .1 or less and my meter shows about 4

I believe that photo reveals a reading of 100+ Thus, your laptop is way above 4.

The Trifield has 2 magnetic scales. Your knob appears to be set on the 0-100 range.

Best method to check magnetic EMF is using the 0-3 range first ... if is it pegged to the very right (by the HIGH word on meter), then the 0-100 setting must be used.

### Josh — 2014-03-23T23:07:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-free-t-high-iron-high-bun-cr.10135/post-117470>

> I was really hoping to get some actionable information on SHBG from dr Kruse's blog. Jan SZ

Good redox=SHBG behaves and hormones orchestrate proper handling of electrons and protons to optimize function and detoxification. Poor redox=**_SHBG binds the wrong stuff and is more sensitive to EMF. So it does things like bind up all of my testosterone instead of estrogen or just getting bound up itself in the tissues that are supposed to bind it and keep it out of the way_**.

> To prove to you I might be correct about this quantum **_SHBG dance_**, biochemistry still does not have the [complete **_quaternary structure of SHBG_** even today.](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/12228253) The reason? They do not realize it changes with the electromagnetic force of the environment that the tissues are in. It also **varies with the redox potential of the tissues where it is made**.

> The **_only thing that changes in their structures is rotational bond angles and their atomic positions_** but the molecular structures printed are exactly the same. Estrogen, testosterone and SHBG do the exact same thing. When you get that it can and does happen in real biochemistry a lot more often than anyone realizes……**_then I can explain to you how the sex steroid hormones do their magic in health and in cancer states_**. They are not the cause of cancer, they act to help it when you understand the quantum molecular actions of how they change

> The blog shows you the first two folds are determined by DNA. **_The last two folds are determined by the redox state of the cell._** This is why so many proteins, like SHBG have so many varied actions. Their **_environment determines their folding and eventual function._** This theory of mine breaks the central dogma paradigm of protein folding. This is also why all neuro degenerative diseases have protein folding abnormalties in the brain. Lewy body, beta amyloid, synaptophsin.....etc.
>
> An extreme example is the involvement of **_chaperone proteins, which provide an environment favoring a specific folding_**. Therefore, we have the possibility that **_a single amino acid sequence, as a peptide, dictated by a gene coding sequence, can fold into more than one protein and therefore perform more than one biological activity: THINK SHBG._** This is why we still do not know its ultimate molecular formula. It is the ultimate chameleon chaperone protein for sex hormone binding.

> regarding your email about SHBG. Here is the response: **_It can act as both a key to open heaven (hormone health) or hell (fuel cancer growth) What determines it? The electronic and protonicty that induces the changes within the molecular structure of the protein to allow it to have multiple functions depending upon the environment it is in_**. Why can it do this? Think back to EE 8. I talked to you there about transition metals and the non native EMF and the special qualities of D shell electrons. **_In SHBG protein there are several atoms with D shell electrons that give it, its ability to transform rapidly._** Think about a Star.

> Now think about SHBG. S**_ex hormone bonding globulin (SHBG) is the wild card action in these hormone sensitive cancers and in optimal hormone health states and is tied to its special molecular acrobatics. It explains why it walks the hermophrodite pathways between estrogen and testosterone levels in the face of a low redox potential_**. How does it multitask? It too is loaded with transition metals and D Shell electrons.

> **_It all comes down to a transition metal at a key binding site_**. The primary and second structure of proteins are all controlled by nucleic acid codes. The tertiary and quaternary structure of proteins is the domain of epigenetic modifications of non coding DNA. This is where the action happens for SHBG. The primary structure of SHBG comprises tandem laminin G-like (LG) domains. The amino-terminal LG-domain includes the steroid-binding site and dimerization interface, and its tertiary structure, resolved in complex with natural and synthetic sex steroids, has revealed unanticipated mechanisms of steroid binding at the atomic level. This LG-domain interacts with fibulin-1D and fibulin- 2 in a ligand-specific manner, and this is attributed to the unique way estrogens reside within the steroid-binding site, and the ordering of an otherwise flexible loop structure covering the entrance of the steroid-binding pocket.**_Estrogen is designed to work in with SHBG because it is associated with a lower level of iodine in women._** This changes the delocalization of electrons of zinc’s D shell electrons. **_There are however two other transition metals binding sites (cation) The cation changes make SHBG resonant to different electromagnetic frequencies when different sex steroids bond to the loop ring. This mechanism enables estradiol to enhance the sequestration of plasma SHBG by the stroma of some tissues through binding to these extra-cellular matrix-associated proteins. Non native EMF cause changes to SHBG molecular function and this is why hormone sensitive cancer are observed. The hormones do not cause it........the non native EMF that changed the molecular action of SHBG did............BOOM_**.

Best I can do, seems pretty clear what I need to work on=redox, redox, redox via sane life, proper diet, perhaps some moderate supplementation..no magic bullets in an insane world.....

It would be good to hear more practical solutions for living in a high EMF environment. Moving into the forest might sound attractive to some and for some possible. For most people who need to work however the reality is they need to be around EMF and need to be in a city. If the response is tough luck you will never be optimal then fine preach it. Because if optimal means living in a hut near some beach then fine im happy not to be optimal. I cant think of an existence any worse to be honest. I would rather be dead than living some hermit lifestyle in southern Mindinao barefoot on some beach eating coconuts and oysters all day and bathing nude. But if you can live in a high EMF environment and can mitigate some of the risks and know you will never be optimal but "ok" then this is a much more realistic message. ### Inger — 2014-07-02T09:42:44+0300

Ok Michael here comes my ideas.

Then I rather travel a bit to my work place. That way I get the night and some more hours to rest and recover. It is all about the balance I think. If your work and workplace is much EMF filled you need to do more stuff to balance it out. Like Jack does. Cold also helps a lot. It helps one tolerate EMFs better. And do not forget to gorge on oysters!
There is so much one can do, endless possibilities.... no need to be asocial...

Of course the sicker you are... the less EMF you should tolerate in your life..... and if you are a woman... you are more sensitive too. If you have children.... too

One can also choose to live in a country / part of the country that is not so much into the EMF craze
