# Thread: Newbie intro
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/newbie-intro.10155/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 17
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Tabby — 2014-03-14T04:40:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/newbie-intro.10155/post-115312>

Hi I'm new and am just getting started. I somewhat reluctantly embrace anything Paleo & fats after spending many years eating low fat and vegan! (Talk about change). I am, however at a crossroads.

I found Epi-Paleo after starkly realizing that my vegan diet simply wasn't working. It was a difficult process because I felt the vegan diet had served me well and I had lost considerable weight at one point (nearly 60 pounds with lots of distance running, but maintaining running on empty proved to be impossible).

After about 3 years I was continually gaining weight with the same eating and increased exercise and it seemed as though my immunity was shot. I could get sick for over a week with a common cold that wouldn't phase others. Being 39 this was a wake up call, I felt like I was falling apart and something was definitely _wrong._ So here I am embracing change and learning informative scientific info that is helping to demystify this process that has kept me disillusioned.

I do have a quick question. I'm a bit of a foodie and cook a lot. I'm slightly confused on what you CAN have; ie fruits, vegetables. Also I thought dairy was out, so I'm not sure why I see recipes with cream/cheese, et. Any information would be appreciated and I look forward to learning more.

\*\*by the way I love seafood so this is pure joy right now!

### caroline — 2014-03-14T07:21:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/newbie-intro.10155/post-115318>

Hi Tabby and welcome! Please go to the home page and have a look at the easy start guides.

Do you have Dr. K's book? The Epi Paleo RX ....it is a great overview of all of this.

please listen to Dr. K's podcasts too - on the home page .... fast and easy info!

Dr. Kruse also has an Ecookbook.

Read about the Leptin reset using a seafood template...this should be up your alley!

some people can handle cream and cheese .... everything depends on your own n=1

Have you signed up for the free 90 Optimal reset? This an an amazing resource that Dr. Kruse is providing - don't miss it!

It would be great if you would start a journal in the journal section and tell us a little bit about yourself - This will give Dr. K. context and we can follow your journey. We all try to help each other.

What else can I help you with?

please put up a picture ..... Dr. K. has new rules .... he won't talk to us unless we have a pic!

### sooperb — 2014-03-14T12:50:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/newbie-intro.10155/post-115341>

Hi Tabby, if you're keen, you'll love it here, so many helpful people who are all happy to give their time to making it work for you.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-14T13:10:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/newbie-intro.10155/post-115347>

> [Tabby said:](/goto/post?id=115312)
>
> ... running on empty proved to be impossible ...

Yeah. There's only about two teaspoons worth of sugar in the bloodstream, so energy doesn't come from there. You could get it from glycogen stored in muscles, but IIRC there's only about 200Kcal there -- and that won't take you very far. If you could only access the energy stored in your fat ...

Think of an average person of perhaps 150 lbs. Let's say the person has 10% bodyfat. That's 15lbs, which is about 6800 grams. Now, obviously you couldn't burn all that, but that represents 6800 x 8 = approx 54,000Kcal.

That's a different proposition.

The curious (apparent) paradox now is how does anyone who's overweight ever get hungry when carrying around _that_ much stored energy?

> I do have a quick question. I'm a bit of a foodie and cook a lot. I'm slightly confused on what you CAN have; ie fruits, vegetables. Also I thought dairy was out, so I'm not sure why I see recipes with cream/cheese, et. Any information would be appreciated and I look forward to learning more.

The main reference to the diet used be a blog Jack wrote called Brain Gut 5. It's still good for a quick overview:

[http://jackkruse.com/brain-gut-5-paradigm-drifts-paradigm-shifts-epi-paleo/](http://jackkruse.com/brain-gut-5-paradigm-drifts-paradigm-shifts-epi-paleo/)

There's a whole set of resources listed on this page now, though:

[http://jackkruse.com/paleo-diet-easy-start-guide/](http://jackkruse.com/paleo-diet-easy-start-guide/)

As for the specific question there, the recipes in the eBook include some foods that would not be recommended for everyone.

Like Paleo, the Epi-Paleo Diet is basically "no grains, no dairy". However, certain types of dairy foods are included as occasionals for anyone who's not immuno-compromised. If you've any doubts about your GI health at all, leave them out at least temporarily.

### Tabby — 2014-03-14T17:21:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/newbie-intro.10155/post-115392>

Thanks for your reply all and I will check out the addtl info. I am 150 pounds actually and the difference in running with glucose versus epi Paleo (or very low carb) is vast. There's really no way I'd do it. To literally run on fat stores indefinitely would be a veritable dream come true and game changer for a LOT of people. Unfortunately it's near impossible and it feels like you run out of fuel at the 5k mark at best. Sooo, changing the exercise method is another area I'm working on. I'm currently walking mildly and doing body weight / gymnastic exercises for fitness. The folks at gymnasticsbodies.com are good with knowledge but it takes a lot to sift through and start from a foundational level. They also advocate traditional Paleo.

Will probably stay away from dairy at the present time as I am having inflammatory reactions and need to settle in to EpiPaleo.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-14T17:40:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/newbie-intro.10155/post-115401>

> [Tabby said:](/goto/post?id=115392)
>
> Thanks for your reply all and I will check out the addtl info. I am 150 pounds actually and the difference in running with glucose versus epi Paleo (or very low carb) is vast. There's really no way I'd do it. To literally run on fat stores indefinitely would be a veritable dream come true and game changer for a LOT of people. Unfortunately it's near impossible and it feels like you run out of fuel at the 5k mark at best. ...

Yeah. This should interest you:

[http://www.meandmydiabetes.com/2012...amarathon-steve-phinney-and-jeff-volek-study/](http://www.meandmydiabetes.com/2012/08/11/western-states-100-low-carber-wins-ultramarathon-steve-phinney-and-jeff-volek-study/)

> Will probably stay away from dairy at the present time as I am having inflammatory reactions and need to settle in to EpiPaleo.

Paleo has a whole bunch of reasons why dairy products are ruled out, as you probably know, not all of them cast-iron provable. In fact, Loren Cordain only just email-shotted an article on them yesterday:

[http://thepaleodiet.com/dairy-milking-worth/](http://thepaleodiet.com/dairy-milking-worth/)

But maybe some of this is a little overdone. And it is acknowledged there that it's not all cast-iron.

Jack regards Keith Woodford's work as particularly important. Woodford specifically points to problems that seem to be connected with a late-arriving mutation on the beta-casein protein in s_ome_ cows' milk. Woodford has got some impressive data. The story is summarised in this short piece by the WAPF's doctor, Tom Cowan:

[http://thebovine.wordpress.com/2009...e-enough-of-an-improvement-over-store-bought/](http://thebovine.wordpress.com/2009/03/20/the-devil-in-the-milk-dr-thomas-cowan-on-how-a2-milk-is-the-answer-to-the-mystery-of-why-even-raw-milk-sometimes-does-not-seem-to-be-enough-of-an-improvement-over-store-bought/)

If Woodford's right, then likely, goat's dairy and ewes' dairy are fine, and what you get from "the right cows" (if you can find them), too.

But best to play safe, if you've got inflammatory issues ...

This is where sports are today, a few do things like we like to do it and seeing a change for them...

Imagine when and if they would realize that calories do not matter... Guess if it is easy to tell athletes of today that instead of running in clothing, drinking sports drinks, eating bars and loading up on pasta, instead they should strip down, drink wild water and catch a fish... The ones that tell these stories must live that life by them self, otherwise nobody will listen.

# Thread: More seafood stuff ...
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/more-seafood-stuff.10158/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-03-14T12:17:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/more-seafood-stuff.10158/post-115340>

Durham Cathedral Kitchens.

Durham (pronounced with the first vowel as as in butter, the second sounded neutrally, and the "h" dropped: /ˈdʌrəm/ ) is an ancient city in Northern England on the River Wear.

> The shopping list for Whit Week in 1346 includes “600 salt herrings; 400 white herrings; 30 salted salmon; 14 ling (a member of the cod family); 55 kelengs (?); 4 turbot; 2 horse-loads of white fish and a conger eel, plaice, sparling (young herring), and eels and fresh water fish; 9 carcasses of oxen, salted; 1 carcase and a quarter fresh; a quarter of an ox, fresh; 7 carcasses and a half of swine in salt, 6 carcasses, fresh; 14 calves, 3 kids; 26 sucking porkers; 5 stones of hog’s lard, 4 stones of cheese, butter and milk; a pottle (half a gallon) of vinegar; a pottle of honey; 14 pounds of figs and raisins, 13 pounds of almonds, 8 pounds of rice, pepper, saffron, cinnamon and other spices, and 1300 eggs.

[https://www.durhamworldheritagesite.com/architecture/cathedral/intro/kitchen](https://www.durhamworldheritagesite.com/architecture/cathedral/intro/kitchen)

It turns out that archaeological investigation of middens there reveal that, contrary to expectations, an

> exciting, diverse array of whole, fresh, marine, freshwater and migratory species were consumed.

The assumption had been that they were comsuming largely salted herring and stockfish. That's not the tale the bones tell:

[http://hss.brookes.ac.uk/news/2013/11/01/something-fishy-at-durham-cathedral/](http://hss.brookes.ac.uk/news/2013/11/01/something-fishy-at-durham-cathedral/)

### sooperb — 2014-03-14T14:52:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/more-seafood-stuff.10158/post-115354>

More happy thoughts for Dr. Jack

### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-14T15:27:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/more-seafood-stuff.10158/post-115358>

You must seek the truth to get to your answer............after generations the story of how we colonized the world are shown to be just that, stories. When you understand the requirements of constructing the human brain........the questions you ask should be different. That is why this place exists. We ask questions no one is thinking to ask. The answers can be illuminating......and for some the answers can be daunting. It is better to live with a new answer than a false belief.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Matty Matty Bo Batty...
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/matty-matty-bo-batty.10159/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 43
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Matty — 2014-03-14T12:58:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/matty-matty-bo-batty.10159/post-115344>

Aspiring to be a starfish. Spreading knowledge and love. Helping others. Kaizen. Awesomeness.

That pretty much sums me up.

I was 300lbs back in 1999 and dropped to 170 in about a year. Pure determination and very little knowledge. In retrospect I did a lot of damage by doing it wrong and not fixing my brain first. In the years since I've made it my mission to improve myself everyday, to learn something new everyday that will help me make better choices, and to help someone else everyday.

Part of that was to gain back some of the muscle I'd lost after I lost so much weight. I got certified as a personal trainer after spending a few years lifting weights, in a quest to learn more. Now a happy 185, size 32, 5' 11" - I still exercise but not As much, but more intense. I walk a lot, fast on occasion and try to implement everything Dr Kruse recommends.

I've always been big on the idea of returning to a way of life resembling our ancestors. My paternal grandparents from Scotland, and my maternal GPs Mennonite, I grew up on healthy food and fairly traditional fare. Living in Canada I have always loved the cold so CT fit my lifestyle. I have tried to eat ancestraly for quite some time and have read a ton about evolution, Paleo nutrition, biology, etc so when I came across Jack's blog I was hooked immediately.

I've since immersed myself in this blog and forum and finally feel like I should say hello and introduce myself.

I'm a Daddy, first and foremost. 5 yr old Etta and 3 yr old Macy are my everything and why I am so dedicated to learning more about health. I am married to an amazing, beautiful exercise physiologist, Erin, who I am lucky to have but whose eyes are not yet open to this new perception of health. She's coming around one thing at a time, but is in awesome shape so not trying to fix anything.

I have worked as a personal trainer, but my career is in Social Services. I got to 300 lbs through a really fun combination of alcohol, cocaine, cigarettes, eating fast food at 2am, and playing rock n roll in bars. Mostly singing in metal bands, little drums little guitar. My quest to be a rock star left me NOT feeling or looking like one. This was 16-19 years old, dropped out of school, gave up 10 years of football for sex, drugs & rock n roll. No exercise, combined with the above mentioned lifestyle choices was devastating to my body.

So I work in social services trying to help people not make those decisions if they're young, or digging themselves out of the mire if it's too late. I am surrounded by overweight, unhealthy people at work, both clients and colleagues. So I try to pay forward the great fortune I've had in helping myself, and for all the knowledge, love and patience given to me by helpful people like Dr Kruse.

My mother has ALS though she's surviving better than DRs expected, so I'm always trying to help her. My dad had a stroke early in life but has survived and made many changes to improve himself. I'm cognizant of the risks in my genetic inheritance, and learning as much about epigenetics as I can.

I am always learning and adapting. No dogma is too strong to question. I will never stop searching for knowledge and will never stop learning or caring about others. The better I am, the more of myself I can give to those around me, those who need me, those who love me.

I just want to be the best me I can be, Optimal if you will, and I couldn't care less what others may think of my tinfoil hat. Wink wink.

### Matty — 2014-03-14T13:11:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/matty-matty-bo-batty.10159/post-115349>

I hadn't planned on making a journal, but my bio above in the meet & greet section warranted a redirect to here, so be it, here I am in a journal.

I implemented CT in the early summer of 2012, and being winter in Canada, have lots of cold exposure. I walk to work 5 days per week about 5km, and underdress for the temperature. I read all about Wim Hof, Tummo and this year I got frostbite. So I'm familiar with cold and it's power. I grew up playing American Football, in snowy Southern Ontario, Canada. So also as a kid had lots of time in the cold.

The frostbite was accidental, and occurred in -40 Polar Vortex, but it won't keep me from loving the cold. My toes are all still intact, and feeling has completely returned, though it was a bit scary for a while.

I am still working on Epi-Paleo eating, which I can pull off 6 days a week and have a break on Saturdays where I indulge in whatever I please, but keep it fairly normal with a few treats. I never liked seafood growing up, but reading Jack's work here convinced me that doesn't matters so I'm learning to love it, I just use a lot of spice to mask the fishy flavours.

When I adopted a Paleo diet, somewhat loosely, I found great success but never got the easy weight management I seemed. So far with avoiding blue lights at night, sleeping grounded, decreasing emf exposure, squatty potty, waking sans alarm and a million other things natural to our biology which I'll expand upon in the future here in my journal. Can I call it Dear Diary? That's makes me smile.

Thanks for shRing in my rambles and my journey to optimal.

### Matty — 2014-03-15T19:10:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/matty-matty-bo-batty.10159/post-115634>

Tandoori, jerked, hot sauce.... Bring on the seafood! Actually a Bangladeshi friend of mine whose parents own a restaurant here has helped get me used to the fishy flavour. When I was a kid, I couldn't eat their food, it was way too hot. He was so surprised when I grew up and came to him for a biryani, and chicken cashmiri!

Now Sooperb, if I tried to eat seafood exclusively, at the expense of grass fed beef or pork, the only recipe I'd get right is that for failure. Gotta have my bacon n steak, gotta stay sane. I totally agree with you though.

Thanks everyone for making me feel welcome! I may have only got one word from Jack, but that's all I need! Lol just knowing he read my bio means a lot to me. I really respect Dr Kruse and am astonished by he pieces he put together that I could not. What an awesome dude, seriously. Hope to shake his hand one day and thank him to his face!

### caroline — 2014-03-23T01:42:50+0200

### sooperb — 2014-03-24T11:01:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/matty-matty-bo-batty.10159/post-117557>

I suspect Audrey means ~McShite~ McDonalds. I used to eat them but when I started to read just how grim they are, I completely stopped and I tried to make sure my children understood why they shouldn't eat them. None of them do thank goodness, even the grandchildren are aware not to eat them and refuse even if they aren't with a parent.

Eating out is seldom a pleasure, I don't trust most places to provide decent quality food or to cook without additives. We cook and eat so well at home it seems pointless to dine out. I can't eat Chinese, which I love, because of the MSG and I'm fairly certain some Indian restaurants use it. I haven't ever been in a restaurant where they have claimed their beef is grassfed or the pork outdoor reared. Best you see is them trumpeting they use free range eggs and that can be pretty meaningless.

It's great to have another bloke on board, most men seem so resistant to the idea that diet and lifestyle has anything to do with poor health, obesity levels and fitness. It would be great if they could embrace it more.

### Matty — 2014-03-24T14:52:43+0200

I strive for the dark stuff most of the time, and we make our own raw chocolate on the rare occasion. I've yet to make one they actually like. TBH. lmao I can rock a grain free bacon grease dark chocolate chip cookie though!

### Matty — 2014-03-25T15:39:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/matty-matty-bo-batty.10159/post-117853>

This post is titled: MY LIFE; POST-JACK

No alarm

Squatty Potty

Optimal Coffee

BAB + Vitamin D3, Kelp Extract & Cod Liver Oil

Epi-Paleo diet (except the occasional snack/meal)

No sunglasses in early AM

Limited electronics

RO water – Magnetized & Aerated

Ceramic water dispenser

Walk 5KM to work Grounded, 5 days per week

Smile at Strangers

Himalayan Salt Lamp @ work

Grounded @ work (electric plug type)

Take breaks outside

Take late lunch to exercise in 1-4pm window (in sunlight)

Exercise intensely 3x per week, 30-45 minutes

Eat lunch post-workout

Walk home Grounded 5KM (3 day per week)

Drink a Malbec or NorCal Margarita 2-3 times per week (used to be beer, rum, whisky)

Dinner before 7, preferably outside w/ feet in grass

No snacking after dinner

No blue lights after dinner (red bulbs and red filters on TV/iPad)

CT 3x per week, 20-60 minutes, grounded water

Filtered Shower Water

Unplugged all non-necessary electronics

Threw out the micro-wave

WIFI off at 8pm

Bedrooms power cut before bedtime

Bedtime 10-11pm

Cold, pitch black & clean room

Sleep grounded (copper rod type) on Magnetico Core pad

Wake naturally 6-6:30am

Rinse & Repeat

I'd love to hear what you all think. Am I missing anything? I decided to keep my sex life out of the post so my wife won't kill me. a few of these existed pre-Jack, but their presence was justified as they fit in Jack's recommendations.

I get a lot of sun on my face in these cold months, and when the sun is shining I do often strip down to a T shirt for my walk home.

I'd try to get more sun exposure, but this was one cold year here in Ontario Canada. In fact, I got frostbite for the 1st time! lol Talk about cold adapted.

I am fortunate enough to not be dealing with any health problems of my own. My time spent here and Jacks blog is equal parts curiosity, self-improvement and for others health issues.

Mom has ALS but has kept living independantly for 6 yrs after Docs said she had 2 to live. Every bit of info is relevant in that steep a disease state. A few other family members by law too, T2D, MS, etc.

And I agree on the worm can, cuz looking healthy and being healthy don't always see eye to eye. Lol

Oh its hilarious, I assure you. Me in my compression shorts & top, usually with Blue Blocker sunglasses. Sometimes its inside the house in my studio, sometimes its out on the deck under a cedar tree. I'm sure my neighbours get a kick out of my appearance to/from the horse trough. However, my trough is awesome, fits me perfect. Knees bent, feet on the floor, leaned back, and I can get the water level up to my collarbone! In the tubby I'm lucky to 1/3 of my body submerged.

I've seen these troughs used for a mini garden or to separate a part of the garden. Maybe you can hide a CT tub in with pretty flower containing tubs? lol

# Thread: CT and Detox Symptoms?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-detox-symptoms.10160/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Ed Warnicke — 2014-03-14T14:50:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-detox-symptoms.10160/post-115352>

What have folks seen in terms of detox symptoms with CT and how long have they lasted?

I've been doing 10 minute cold showers and 1 hour ice baths... and I'm experiencing headaches and some generalized lethargy. I'm trying to figure out if this is normal detox, or something else, and if its normal detox, how long I can expect it to last (and possibly how I can mitigate the symptoms).

### fitness@home — 2014-03-14T15:30:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-detox-symptoms.10160/post-115361>

This is an old thread on the subject:

[http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/die-off-symptoms.48/](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/die-off-symptoms.48/)

### nonchalant — 2014-03-14T16:20:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-detox-symptoms.10160/post-115369>

Hi Ed, welcome! I'm not sure about the lethargy. But for the headaches, really push the non-fluoridated water. 2 gallons isn't too much...
Better hydration seems to be extra important when we adopt a new health strategy. When I was getting great results from starting grounding, Dr. K recommended I get lots of water, and yes, it was so helpful!

I don't drink as much now, but anytime I have the slightest headache, it has become a habit to automatically drink more water. Works like a charm.

### Ed Warnicke — 2014-03-15T21:40:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-detox-symptoms.10160/post-115671>

Thank you both for your replies!

I normally drink a lot of spring water, but have not been measuring my intake... I'll try to increase it and see if that helps

### Inger — 2014-03-15T21:56:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-detox-symptoms.10160/post-115673>

I got sinus infection first time in my life after starting CT 2 years ago... and I got huge appetite too.. and cravings for raw egg yolk foam lol
Dry skin for a few months.. and I recall I got a bit of dark moods too for a while!

All of these disappeared without going to the doctor or taking medicine

### Ed Warnicke — 2014-03-19T03:57:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-detox-symptoms.10160/post-116323>

CTforLife:

I think you may have misunderstood me a bit. I was not complaining about CT... just checking in to see if others had similar experiences

# Thread: According to this, I might be addicted to fat
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/according-to-this-i-might-be-addicted-to-fat.10162/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Tanya — 2014-03-14T16:45:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/according-to-this-i-might-be-addicted-to-fat.10162/post-115378>

Maybe I should not have been drinking my tea with ghee, and coconut oil in it while I was reading this http://[www.holistichelp.net/blog/what-craving-fat-means/](http://www.holistichelp.net/blog/what-craving-fat-means/)

### nicld — 2014-03-14T17:03:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/according-to-this-i-might-be-addicted-to-fat.10162/post-115384>

Very interesting.

Dr. Tim had mentioned that some of the stuff in my gut may be feeding on the fats I eat and that is causing a problem.

I guess that everyone needs to use their own N=1 to see if it is an issue.

### fitness@home — 2014-03-14T17:22:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/according-to-this-i-might-be-addicted-to-fat.10162/post-115393>

> Concentrated sources of fat include butter, ghee, cream, coconut butter, coconut cream, coconut flakes, coconut oil, **cheese**, and some cuts of high fat meat.

Cheese triggers something in me. Love and crave it. Have found it best (for me) to not even have any in the house. I agree with the dopamine theory...eating it gives me a sense of satisfaction and "feel good".

If I buy it for DH, it is sent to work with him. Can't have it in the fridge or it tempts me.

# Thread: If you like the doctor you have, you can keep your doctor"  Sure...
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/if-you-like-the-doctor-you-have-you-can-keep-your-doctor-sure.10165/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 18
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Tanya — 2014-03-14T18:12:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/if-you-like-the-doctor-you-have-you-can-keep-your-doctor-sure.10165/post-115407>

I hope that not too many people have forgotten these famous words I used in the title....

There was an article this morning in Hartford Courant titled: Newly covered, but by whom? Finding a doctor not always easy” . I searched, but could not find it in on-line edition, otherwise I would have shared a link here.

It is a long article, but these few sentences should give you an idea what it is about.

“People who called to check if a provider would take their insurance arrived at the appointment only to find out that their coverage would not be accepted.

There are several reasons that people are having trouble finding doctors who take their insurance. But perhaps the biggest source of confusion is that the plans sold on the exchange by Anthem Blue Cross and Blue Shield and Connecticare use different networks of health care providers than the plans each company sells outside of the exchange”

### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-14T18:23:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/if-you-like-the-doctor-you-have-you-can-keep-your-doctor-sure.10165/post-115412>

I told Dexter and gretchen 3 yrs ago this was gonna happen and it is going to get worse. Just watch

### Shijin13 — 2014-03-14T21:35:46+0200

Its this death spiral that occasionally inspires me to go back and get my CNP just so I can help my family if need be....

### NeilBB — 2014-03-14T22:15:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/if-you-like-the-doctor-you-have-you-can-keep-your-doctor-sure.10165/post-115459>

I wish I had something positive to add. But I actually believe it's already much worse than most of you think.

If I could sneak you guys in with me to our departmental meetings, your mind would absolutely be blown at the pettiness of what once-proud-academic-medicine has now been reduced to. It's 100% about red tape, HIPPA compliance, and corporate political correctness. The system is completely run by bureaucrats already today. The government and large insurers are in complete control. Even if doctors _wanted_ to embrace revolutionary new ideas of wellness, there is in my opinion, virtually no chance they could even get off the ground in such an environment.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-14T23:22:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/if-you-like-the-doctor-you-have-you-can-keep-your-doctor-sure.10165/post-115465>

^^^^^this. Maybe now you realize why this place exists?

### caroline — 2014-03-14T23:35:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/if-you-like-the-doctor-you-have-you-can-keep-your-doctor-sure.10165/post-115469>

I had just emailed my Doc a couple of days go and asked him to order blood work because it has been quite awhile.
He just emailed me back and said he would have to do two separate requests ...for "non essential" blood work because medicare would "pull him up" for over servicing.
He is very interested in all of this and Dr. K. and always wants to hear about what I am learning here.......but he struggles within the system. We get so excited talking about all of this together!

It would be so much easier to get testing if I was sick ....as it is - he will have to make up symptoms for me.

### Tanya — 2014-03-19T16:35:09+0200

### nonchalant — 2014-03-20T02:03:53+0200

Meanwhile, the older adults that have experience living in a system where people paid their way and doctors had more incentives, are all dying early. With a younger, less experienced population you can just tell them that "this is the way it has always been..." History revision is easier with fewer old folks around.

### RobH — 2014-03-20T16:55:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/if-you-like-the-doctor-you-have-you-can-keep-your-doctor-sure.10165/post-116690>

> [Shijin13 said:](/goto/post?id=116430)
>
> no incentive. my PCP told me she expects less and less of future doctors will go into Primary care....

This is very true. My brother in law is on the faculty at a large state University (not Alabama). Last year of ~30 internal medicine residents graduating 26 went into sub specialties. 3 went to hospitalist medicine (talk about red tape, don't ask me how I know) and 1 to primary care.

I love and respect many of the NPs and PAs that I work with. But they never had and won't have the type of training that will allow them to practice much as out of the box thinkers in the modern medical system, which is dead set against that type of thinking.

Our "disease-exacerbation-management-by-pharmaceutical-system" is really fraying. We're all going to have to be taking care of ourselves and our families more and more in the future, and ultimately paying for it out of pocket I think.

RobH

### Tanya — 2014-03-20T17:26:54+0200

### Gracelind — 2014-03-14T21:38:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-apple-turning-on-your-bluetooth.10166/post-115445>

[http://money.msn.com/technology-inv...e-stealthily-turning-on-your-phones-bluetooth](http://money.msn.com/technology-investment/post--is-apple-stealthily-turning-on-your-phones-bluetooth)

First comment mentions Bluetooth turning on--and not being able to be turned off--in a Walmart. Not sure if this happens at all Walmarts? At other stores?

### Lahelada — 2014-03-14T21:43:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-apple-turning-on-your-bluetooth.10166/post-115447>

Interesting . I caught my Android doing that but do not know what triggered it. I thought I had touched it by accident but it occurred too many times to be one really. I wonder ...

### Shijin13 — 2014-03-14T23:22:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-apple-turning-on-your-bluetooth.10166/post-115466>

> [Gracelind said:](/goto/post?id=115445)
>
> [http://money.msn.com/technology-inv...e-stealthily-turning-on-your-phones-bluetooth](http://money.msn.com/technology-investment/post--is-apple-stealthily-turning-on-your-phones-bluetooth)
>
> First comment mentions Bluetooth turning on--and not being able to be turned off--in a Walmart. Not sure if this happens at all Walmarts? At other stores?

Yes this is happening but its not apple turning it on. Its Walmart, Target, Bloomingdales, Whole Foods and any other commercial retailer you frequent on a regular basis. They're doing this for several reasons. they're anticipating demand based upon your previous purchases, and your regular shopping days. This is to ensure that the things you buy regularly are IN STOCK. the second reason they're doing this is to begin the ball rolling for re-stock. All this happens BEFORE you've finished grabbing your cart and heading for the items you want to purchase. So they're re-stocking the next truck to your store before you've even put that organic coconut milk in your cart....

### cinnamon — 2014-03-15T01:38:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-apple-turning-on-your-bluetooth.10166/post-115517>

I don't even \*have\* a phone. I wonder if sometime soon the automatic door opener at the supermarket won't be able to see me? LOL

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-15T08:54:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-apple-turning-on-your-bluetooth.10166/post-115544>

> [Lahelada said:](/goto/post?id=115447)
>
> Interesting . I caught my Android doing that but do not know what triggered it. I thought I had touched it by accident but it occurred too many times to be one really. I wonder ...

Updating it, possibly.

Every time I update my iPod Touch software, I notice that wireless is switched back on.

I'd guess the purpose is to make things easy for the user. This "making it easy for the user" is something that haunts the whole computer area. It's one reason why Windows used to have such bad security. Until Service Pack 2 of Windows XP, Microsoft had a whole bunch of daemons -- services in MS speak -- switched on by default. They were there _just in case_ someone wanted to do this or that: so it would work with no problem and no need for knowledge on the user's part. I gather that on an OS like OpenBSD intended for servers, not the person in the street, everything was switched _off_ by default.

Obviously iOs is locked down from the security point of view: in fact, you can't even download and install random software. (Just as well on a phone.) But it's the same kind of thing: make things easy so that they work seamlessly for the user. Wireless will "just work" without your having to go into the options and turn it on.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-16T10:12:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-apple-turning-on-your-bluetooth.10166/post-115739>

> [Gracelind said:](/goto/post?id=115731)
>
> When I updated IOS7 for today's security fix Bluetooth was switched back on.

Yep. What they do. Put it back to the default -- which is: immediately available for connection in any conceivable way.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-16T18:44:20+0200

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blood-clots-in-placenta.10169/post-115474>

Dear All-

I wanted to find out why a person may have a blood clot in the placenta, during pregnancy? Any input is welcome!

\- Digital

### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-15T00:13:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blood-clots-in-placenta.10169/post-115486>

Inflammation, obesity, HBP, and diabetes are the top ones.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blood-clots-in-placenta.10169/post-115494>

The person is 5'2, about 100lb (in shape, and has been like this forever). She has been following an epi-paleo template for the last two years, and prior eating an Asian diet. HS-CRP is 0.7, HBP (120/70), A1c 5.6 as of today.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-15T00:48:24+0200

### digital — 2014-03-15T01:03:34+0200

### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-15T02:18:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blood-clots-in-placenta.10169/post-115526>

K2 has little to do with clotting. K1 does.

### Jack Kruse — 2018-10-01T17:05:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blood-clots-in-placenta.10169/post-252063>

Blood plasma contains many types of proteins suspended in water, referred to simply as blood proteins or plasma proteins, that perform a variety of roles within the body. Some proteins act as transports for fats, hormones, minerals, and vitamins, while others play more complicated roles, acting as enzymes, kinin precursors or protease inhibitors. Apolipoprotein A-IV, which was the subject of recent studies on the link between unsaturated fats and cardiovascular health, is one such blood protein.
Previous research observed an association between higher levels of apolipoprotein A-IV and lowered incidence of cardiovascular disease. This lipid is related to eating seafood. The levels of apolipoprotein A-IV within blood are known to rise after eating, especially after digesting foods like seafood and olive oil that are high in unsaturated fats. In their recent investigations of the blood protein, researchers from the St. Michael’s Hospital observed apolipoprotein A-IV helps to be a key inhibitory factor for blood clotting. It helps in raising the redox potential in the plasma and keeping platelets apart in electromagnetic fashion. This protein has its own ultradian cycle tied to light and dark cycles.
Blood clotting occurs when platelets within the blood clump together, in a process called platelet aggregation, as a result of blood vessel injury or as a byproduct of cardiovascular disease. Platelet aggregation can cause complications such as the blocking of blood flow, a condition which can lead to potentially lethal thrombosis in the heart or brain. In the heart, this is called a heart attack and in the brain, it is called a stoke. What is necessary for this process to occur in the presence of a particular platelet receptor, integrin αIIbβ3. According to findings published in the journal Nature Communications below, apolipoprotein A-IV blocks this receptor from binding to platelets, thus inhibiting clotting and the development of thrombosis.
Platelets come together using a series of binding connectors. In order for one blood platelet to bond with another, the integrin αIIβ3 receptor binds with another protein found in abundance within blood, which is called fibrinogen. The fibrinogen molecules go on to bind with a second integrin αIIβ3 receptor located on another platelet. Fibrinogen molecules continue this bridging process, potentially also binding with other proteins, until ultimately leading to potentially dangerous platelet aggregation.
Both in lab models and human tests, the team observed that apolipoprotein A-IV links itself to the integrin αIIβ3 receptors and essentially blocks the fibrinogen molecules from bridging with platelets, thus inhibiting the aggregation of platelets within blood vessels. Not only does apolipoprotein A-IV decrease blood vessel blockages caused by platelet aggregation, but the protein also appears to morph its OWN SHAPE in order to allow for increased blood flow and more effectively protect from blockages. The shape-shifting comes from changes in water chemistry due to the effects of melanin in the skin above. Later today you hear more about that process which is also linked to circadian biology.
[https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-018-05806-0](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-018-05806-0)

Another fascinating discovery to come from these findings was the realization that apolipoprotein A-IV possesses its own circadian rhythm. Recall that during the day electric fields are stronger than magnetic fields. At night it reverses and this is critical in how this protein works to shapeshift. The higher magnetic fields at night appear to alter the hydrogen bonding network in the water of blood plasma to make this protein work properly to lower clotting risks at night when we sleep. This research confirms the observation that apolipoprotein A-IV is most active during the nighttime and at its least active in the mornings when the electric fields return to the ionosphere. Those fields change water chemistry in the blood plasma.
AM sunlight stimulates melatonin in the AM and sunlight also cause melanin in our skin to split the water molecule in our blood plasma to create hydrogen, oxygen, and 4 electrons. Mother Nature wants us to sleep well by using the sun's light and dark cycles in creative ways. Melanin absorbs sunlight and liberates its stored power under the cover of darkness. I believe those 4 electrons are critical in the shapeshifting of this protein at night time. Your melanin is located in your eye and skin to absorb this light. So we are protected from clotting by this protein while we sleep, and most likely to experience a cardiovascular event after waking up in the morning. Many people do not realize that most heart attack and stroke occur in the waking hours of humans who have melanopsin dysfunction.
These findings demonstrate that meals high in seafood with unsaturated fats (DHA/EPA), when combined with complimentary sleeping habits, are an effective combination for reducing the risk of cardiovascular complications like heart attack and stroke.
Being in nature lowers your risk of clots. Now you can understand why I don't advocate the use of aspirin for clot prevention. I advocate solar exposure during the day and darkness at night to avoid clots, heart disease, and stroke risk.

# Thread: Technical Diabetes Forums
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/technical-diabetes-forums.10172/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 16
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### persistence — 2014-03-15T02:08:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/technical-diabetes-forums.10172/post-115521>

Does anyone know of any good diabetes forums online with a technical user base, or alternately scientific forums where glucose metabolism is frequently discussed?

I am trying to find an audience that I can ask technical questions to pertaining to things like insulin testing.

### Shijin13 — 2014-03-15T02:14:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/technical-diabetes-forums.10172/post-115523>

No but ask away here. you've got several T2D folks here as well as T1D (they don't post as often)

Look up Dali Dula here on the forum he's got some great experience.

### persistence — 2014-03-15T04:05:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/technical-diabetes-forums.10172/post-115534>

I am looking for a west coast USA lab that does the insulin tolerance test. In a fasting state, they give you a small amount of insulin, and then observe how well your body uses that to take up your fasting glucose level in the first 15 minutes, before the body starts to create glucose again.

It's apparently a very simple test, but very rarely done, and has very strong correlation to the clamp tests for insulin resistance that are done in large research studies (but never done for a prediabetic patient). It's a direct and very useful measurement of insulin resistance that doesn't stress out the system with a full glucose insulin tolerance test.

### Dali Dula — 2014-03-15T15:01:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/technical-diabetes-forums.10172/post-115580>

I have never had a GTT. this test will tell you if you diabetic or insulin resistant. You can do this yourself with BG meter. You are young and already aware of your pre diabetic state. This is fortunate. More fortunate is you have discovered this site. Work the protocols and test yourself regularly. Test fasting and post prandial to see how activities, environment and food affect BG. I keep good control over BG without exogenous insulin. With some time you may be able to correct your BG metabolism. When it comes to lab tests I ask myself will the info I get change my actionable options. If it won't I don't see value in it.

### Shijin13 — 2014-03-15T16:46:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/technical-diabetes-forums.10172/post-115598>

> [persistence said:](/goto/post?id=115534)
>
> I am looking for a west coast USA lab that does the insulin tolerance test. In a fasting state, they give you a small amount of insulin, and then observe how well your body uses that to take up your fasting glucose level in the first 15 minutes, before the body starts to create glucose again.
>
> It's apparently a very simple test, but very rarely done, and has very strong correlation to the clamp tests for insulin resistance that are done in large research studies (but never done for a prediabetic patient). It's a direct and very useful measurement of insulin resistance that doesn't stress out the system with a full glucose insulin tolerance test.

I'm just gonna say this test sucks. had it done twice while pregnant to rule out gestational diabetes. oh the suck... and that insulin drink is horrid. I'm glad I only had to do the 1 hr test. the 3 hr would have sucked even more...

Dali's right - you can do this yourself at home with a blood glucose monitor, and a TBS or 2 of maple syrup. - prick your finger before taking, then take levels every 1-15min until your BG is at the same level as FBG.

### persistence — 2014-03-15T22:01:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/technical-diabetes-forums.10172/post-115676>

> [Dali Dula said:](/goto/post?id=115580)
>
> I have never had a GTT. this test will tell you if you diabetic or insulin resistant. You can do this yourself with BG meter. You are young and already aware of your pre diabetic state. This is fortunate. More fortunate is you have discovered this site. Work the protocols and test yourself regularly. Test fasting and post prandial to see how activities, environment and food affect BG. I keep good control over BG without exogenous insulin. With some time you may be able to correct your BG metabolism. When it comes to lab tests I ask myself will the info I get change my actionable options. If it won't I don't see value in it.

Hi Dali and Shinjin. You are both confusing different tests. Please distinguish the "Glucose Tolerance Test" from "Glucose Insulin Tolerance Test" from "Insulin Tolerance Test". These are three different tests. My imprecise definition of each:

A: Glucose Tolerance Test: They give you 70 grams of glucose liquid, then measure your glucose response every N minutes in order to diagnose possible diabetes

B: Glucose Insulin Tolerance Test: They give you 70 grams of glucose liquid, then measure your glucose AND INSULIN every N minutes in order to diagnose possible diabetes. This test shows insulin resistance or failure to produce insulin, which is something test A does not show.

C: Insulin Tolerance Test: NO GLUCOSE IS GIVEN IN THIS TEST. This test - as I described earlier - INJECTS only a SMALL amount of INSULIN and then they watch to see how your glucose responds to that. This test does not diagnose diabetes. This test measures insulin resistance.

Now that I have defined A, B, and C, let me answer your point.

Dali, I totally agree with you test A can be done at home. The glucometer is my friend, and I can certainly try these measurements if I am curious. It doesn't strike me as the best use of time because even 20 grams of carbs can send me to 130 Glucose. 40 grams can occasionally get me well over 150. I already know something isn't working well. But I would disagree that you would not want to run test B. Insulin cannot be measured at home. This disease is all about insulin resistance. We should be very interested in understanding how our bodies produce insulin, and what is the extent of our insulin resistance when ingesting glucose. But I agree with both of you that the test in A and B is awful, time consuming and a real hassle.

I also disagree with you about test C. In fact every prediabetic or type 2 should want test C. Because it is a SIMPLE easy test that gives a lot of information about insulin resistance. This test cannot be duplicated at home, unless you inject insulin. I'm hopefully never going to get to that stage (my father never did and he had the identical symptoms of prediabetes), so I would need to do test C in a lab environment.

That's why I am looking for a lab that does Test C.

### Dali Dula — 2014-03-16T05:32:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/technical-diabetes-forums.10172/post-115722>

You are correct. I try to use information to formulate actionable strategies to aid in my well being. The (A) GTT will tell you how you handle a glucose load. You already know that from your BG meter.
The (B) GITT would be useful to tell you that you produce little or no insulin and are Type 1 and will require exogenous insulin. This doesn't sound like you. You might want to do this, but what action would you take if your insulin production is low?
The (C) ITT seems to be most useful in gauging cortisol response to hypoglycemia not assessing IR. I would think that where you are at now even a small amount of exogenous insulin would drop your BG way low. How would this info modify your strategy?
These tests may be valuable for getting a baseline, and I have had quite a few tests over the past five years that in retrospect offered me little in actionable info. If you have the resources test away.
You are obviously a smart person willing to do the due diligence to figure yourself out.

### Shijin13 — 2014-03-16T17:29:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/technical-diabetes-forums.10172/post-115794>

You might consider running something like what HDL runs which looks at Glycemic control, Insulin Resistance & Beta cell function

### persistence — 2014-03-16T22:47:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/technical-diabetes-forums.10172/post-115838>

> [Dali Dula said:](/goto/post?id=115722)
>
> You are correct. I try to use information to formulate actionable strategies to aid in my well being. The (A) GTT will tell you how you handle a glucose load. You already know that from your BG meter.
> The (B) GITT would be useful to tell you that you produce little or no insulin and are Type 1 and will require exogenous insulin. This doesn't sound like you. You might want to do this, but what action would you take if your insulin production is low?
> The (C) ITT seems to be most useful in gauging cortisol response to hypoglycemia not assessing IR. I would think that where you are at now even a small amount of exogenous insulin would drop your BG way low. How would this info modify your strategy?
> These tests may be valuable for getting a baseline, and I have had quite a few tests over the past five years that in retrospect offered me little in actionable info. If you have the resources test away.
> You are obviously a smart person willing to do the due diligence to figure yourself out.

I have never had hypoglycemia. It's never been an issue for me. A typical morning pattern for me is:

1) Wake with fasting glucose 90
2) If I do not eat for an hour that goes to 105. If I eat a big protein meal right at waking, that goes to 115.
3) One hour after eating maybe 20 grams of carbs (from tubers like yam) that goes to 128
4) Two hours: 109
5) Three hours: 99

I overrespond to a glucose challenge, and my body shows insulin resistance because it takes a long time to work its way back down to a normal range. I NEVER get rebound hypoglycemia.

It's in no way clear at all that my response to the test in C would be hypoglycemia. I would be thrilled with such a response, because it would show I am not insulin resistant. I would expect that I would show a lower-than-normal lowering of blood sugar, because my body is not processing insulin efficiently. That's the entire point of prediabetes and type 2.

What actionable information do I get from C? First, I do get a baseline and I can use that to compare against the same test in the future, to see if the insulin resistance is getting better or worse after leptin-reset and other therapies. Second, it helps me to compare my own case against others by looking at population studies that have had that same test. Third, it does help establish that my problem is failure to process insulin efficiently rather than failure to create insulin. Fourth - as you can tell - I am a pretty deductive (and persistent  ) person and having a lot of relevant data points lying around is like having pieces to the puzzle, and slowly I start to put them all together to get a useful picture of the problem and the solution.

What's really interesting to me is that my problem appears to just disappear when I am asleep. I have had my glucose on waking up be 78 to 83, usually after I use GABA to help me sleep, but sometimes just after getting a lot of really sound sleep. GABA \*may\* help suppress glucagon, which is the counterpart to insulin that signals the liver to increase glucose production. The fact that my glucose suddenly rises as soon as I wake up - from as low as 80 to 105 - without even taking any food, is probably a clue. I don't know what to make of that.

One theory: The brain is signaling the body to upregulate glucagon to create sugar, and the endogenous levels of insulin just cannot deal with that added sugar efficiently because of insulin resistance. The result is that my background glucose levels rise, even before I eat any kind of food. Is it as simple as just insulin resistance, or is there some other possible explanation for that effect?

### persistence — 2014-03-16T22:51:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/technical-diabetes-forums.10172/post-115839>

### Josh — 2014-03-16T22:56:53+0200

### persistence — 2014-03-17T01:50:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/technical-diabetes-forums.10172/post-115877>

> [Josh said:](/goto/post?id=115840)
>
> See Tim Ferris's experiments in "The 4 Hour Body" with blood sugar for some perspective....
>
> Also Jimmy Moore's with glucose and ketone tesing on [www.livinlavidalowcarb.com](http://www.livinlavidalowcarb.com)...
>
> Hypoglycemia was probably years ago and you wrote it off as something else in your 20's and 30's.....see literature on hypoglycemic episodes while asleep, etc...

I have probably had hypoglycemia less than 10 times in my entire life, always after some strenuous exercise. Even after exercise, it is so rare for me. Symptoms of hypoglycemia include sweating, heart racing, shakiness, hunger and anxiety. I have never woken up at night with those symptoms. I had heart racing after over-training episodes, particularly when very dehydrated, but hypoglycemia I know the feeling and I don't think it would get past me.

I researched online and I guess you are referring to the so-called "Somogyi Effect" where there is a rebound in blood sugar after a nighttime hypoglycemia? I read about that and I am more inclined to believe I would be having the "Dawn Effect" defined [here](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dawn_phenomenon) and maybe the "Cortisol Effect" defined [here](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cortisol_awakening_response). I am a fairly high-strung person, probably way too tense, fairly overwhelmed by a large number of tasks. So it's pretty easy for me to believe that a deep sleep is lowering cortisol, and that upon waking that is getting very elevated. Would I catch this just by measuring a fasting cortisol level? Should I measure epinephrine as well? How does one diagnose whether you have "Dawn Effect" or "Cortisol Effect"?

Assuming the problem is cortisol in morning, how does one fight that? I have to retire and stop working? Short of having a permanent vacation, I don't see a way to avoid daily stress.

I hate to be such a science geek, but I would gladly wear a device 24x7 if it could spit out real-time graphs of glucose, insulin, glucagon, leptin, cortisol, etc. You would learn so much about yourself in literally weeks. I kid you not that it took me probably three months of continuous glucose testing just to figure out my glucose patterns. A real-time glucometer worn 24x7 would have given me the same level of understanding in two weeks. Having to make wild guesses about all of these other chemicals, and patterns of these things while you sleep, is just kind of insane. They put patients through years of drugs and non-effective diets and therapies without really every understanding their problems. Wouldn't it make sense for someone to develop the device I describe and start understanding the disease rather than spending $400 MILLION to develop yet-another-health-destroying drug? Smart people would start to quickly understand the relationships between their behaviors, diets, lifestyle, and underlying metabolic chemicals. I'm sure at least 20% of patients could cure themselves without drugs simply by having the right information as feedback to the corrective actions they try.

I'll take a peek at Four Hour Body and get back.

### persistence — 2014-03-17T07:14:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/technical-diabetes-forums.10172/post-115912>

> [Josh said:](/goto/post?id=115840)
>
> See Tim Ferris's experiments in "The 4 Hour Body" with blood sugar for some perspective....
>
> Also Jimmy Moore's with glucose and ketone tesing on [www.livinlavidalowcarb.com](http://www.livinlavidalowcarb.com)...
>
> Hypoglycemia was probably years ago and you wrote it off as something else in your 20's and 30's.....see literature on hypoglycemic episodes while asleep, etc...

Okay, I read the Glucose chapter in "The 4 Hour Body". I definitely "get" this guy.  I would love to wear a Dexcom and do similar kinds of experiments.

There were only a few ideas there that I had not confirmed or read about before. One of those was that Vinegar does not affect post-meal glucose. I had not realized that lemon does a better job. So maybe if I make some sugar-free lemonade with each meal that would help.

His recommendation on the cinnamon is a bit dangerous. Saigon Cinnamon is extremely dangerous stuff. It has more than 20 times the Coumarin content of Cassia Cinnamon, and Cassia is the one the Europeans have warned against using. Ceylon Cinnamon is the only really safe one in large doses, and wow I am bummed out to hear him say that Ceylon was not very effective. I wish he had published the details for each of those. Saigon Cinnamon is totally delicious (by far the most tasty) but you wouldn't want to touch that stuff more than a few times a month.

His point on saturated fat needed more elaboration. I had a lot of problems with dairy fats (which might be partly because I cross react to dairy protein as part of wheat intolerance) causing my glucose to jump. There are a lot of studies showing some saturated fats (usually they use butter) cause insulin resistance. So some types of fat reward you by slowing down absorption of glucose and then take it all back by making you temporarily less able to metabolize the glucose that does get through digestion. I am using lots of monounsaturated macadamia oil with non starchy meals and MCT Oil with starchy meals.

I don't claim to understand fats very well, but the studies I have read made pretty clear that fats are very powerful mediators of glucose metabolism. If you eat a lot of carbs together with a fat, expect the response of insulin and glucose to potentially be dramatically different, and it won't always be different in a good way. One study I saw showed insulin at 250% higher levels with the same carb meal comparing saturated fat (butter) to monounsaturated fats. Unfortunately, most of us cannot see these effects because the glucometer at home will not show insulin levels, only glucose. A lot depends on the metabolism of the population studied. Healthy people metabolize differently than people with high trigylcerides.

Regarding Jimmy's web site: I have seen some of his n=1 glucose readings testing low carb foods, and those are great. Generally I find his site very poorly organized, and it is difficult to follow any trend of ideas across different articles. I searched n=1 hoping to find all of his glucose tests, but I couldn't find any real trends or articles that jumped out.

### persistence — 2014-03-17T07:46:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/technical-diabetes-forums.10172/post-115914>

> [Shijin13 said:](/goto/post?id=115843)
>
> [http://www.hdlabinc.com/](http://www.hdlabinc.com/) I'll have to dig up the ranges, but IIRC there's some calculations based up age, weight, and sex as well...

Shinjin, where are you finding that page you posted from HDL lab? Their site is giving me really general information, and I cannot find any link to detailed test information.

# Thread: Connection between Omega 6 and eczema?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/connection-between-omega-6-and-eczema.10175/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Sammy — 2014-03-15T11:29:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/connection-between-omega-6-and-eczema.10175/post-115551>

Hi everyone,

I would like to find out the link between omega 6 and eczema.
I read that eczema/dry/flaky/ scaly skin is linked with an Omega 6 deficiency.
When I was younger(3-6 years old), I had terrible eczema, my doctor megadosed me with evening primrose oil. I was taking 6 capsules a day. This alone had definitely help improve my skin.

My eczema 'cured' around 7 yo. But I have had dry flaky scaly skin since then-nothing too serious. (no bleeding/severe itchiness), this can also being a swimmer and soaking in chlorinated water almost everyday(yikes!) Skin has improved since Epi-Paleo(more fats and seafood) + Leptin Rx started towards the end of last year. Going to the beach and soaking the in the seawater helped as well.

Since starting CT 2 weeks ago, my skin has been back to being scaly, dry and itchy-especially after shower(I've been doing cold showers since I started the Rx's)
Dr. Kruse talks about how Omega 6 gets incorporated into the cell membrane to increase fluidity during cold. Would this lead to an Omega 6 deficiency for the skin? How does Omega 3 play a role?
Omega 3 and 6 are the essential fatty acids that our brain requires. Skin and brain derive from ectoderm so skin would also require the same type of nutrients, I presume. Dr. Kruse has hinted that skin problems are tied to bad Vit A cycle and leaky gut. But does not mention about Omega 6/3. I tried to find some info about dry skin and CT on the forum and found that a few others have had the same 'detox' symptoms since starting CT.

I am interested to find out more about this, would love if anyone can give me some input.
Thank you

### Inger — 2014-03-15T12:27:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/connection-between-omega-6-and-eczema.10175/post-115561>

I got dry weird skin too as I started with CT... after a few months it disappeared

I use EVOO on my skin if I feel it needs some fat. I would suggest keep a bottle in your shower and put it on after the shower after you have dried you off with a towel = when your skin is still a bit moist
I can get a bit dry skin if I shower much with our well water, it is very hard water. I never get dry skin from dipping in the river.

I do use EVOO too pretty often with my raw fish chevice and I sometimes eat raw organic nuts too.. (lately a bit too much!)
Maybe you can measure your O6/O3 to be sure how to use O6 fats in your diet?

Fish heads/eyes/liver are rich in vit. A, I think other livers too...

If I eat 85% chocolate or other sugar I get dry skin on my face. Sugar is not good for my skin.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coconut-oil-2-problems.10176/post-115553>

Hi all,

I have been attempting for a while now to up the fat and lower the protein for breakfast. Unfortunately, if I even add another tablespoon of CO in there, after 1-2 hrs I start feeling nauseous.....I also get the runs.

I am following the Leaky Gut Rx(ontop of Epi-paleo Rx) which suggests using CO as the main fat in the diet. Why does this happen? Has anyone experienced this and does this get better over time? Ghee does not seem to cause this problem. (Ghee and CO are the main fats in my diet)

Thank you!

### SeaHorse — 2014-03-15T13:11:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coconut-oil-2-problems.10176/post-115572>

There are people who are sensitive to coconut...my daughter is immediately nauseous when she eats or smells it, but can chow down on other fats very well. Can you eat coconut itself without a problem? How about adding in duck, beef or pork fat?

### sooperb — 2014-03-15T15:13:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coconut-oil-2-problems.10176/post-115581>

I experienced bowel issues when I started taking CO when I hadn't ever used it before. It settled down and now it's not an issue. How are you using it? I add mine to coffee, my fruit salad (only occasionally!) my porridge (once a week). I don't think I could eat it straight off the spoon but it is quite tasteless in some drinks. I think gradually upping it might work but as seahorse suggests, there are other fats you can use if you can't tolerate it.

### Shijin13 — 2014-03-15T17:06:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coconut-oil-2-problems.10176/post-115602>

While some people do have tolerance issues with CO. However if you have gut issues - candida for example.... coconut oil can cause die-off . I had serious candida issues that didn't respond to diflucan. I cleared the candida by going keto and eating massive amts of coconut oil. I did have intestinal issues at first... but it cleared a short while later...

keep and eye on it. and you can always eat other fats.... and then bring CO back in later when your gut is healed and see if its still an issue

### nicld — 2014-03-15T17:19:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coconut-oil-2-problems.10176/post-115606>

### Rick — 2014-03-15T16:13:23+0200

Just curious if anyone has experienced a bad stomach ache, severe cramps and diarrhea within an hour of drinking this beverage at breakfast? Not ruling out the possibility of food poisoning but this has happened on two occasions and I'm wondering if the EVCO could be the culprit; using about 1/4 cup of c.o. in a 10 oz mug of coffee. Normally been creaming my coffee with KG butter but thought I would try eliminating the last and only dairy product to see what happens.

### Infinusss — 2014-03-25T04:54:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coconut-oil-coffee-in-the-am.10178/post-117779>

I can tolerate GF butter decently, but I believe that I do better with ghee. In my BPC I use A TBS or two of 100% GF ghee from pureindianfoods.com and a TBS or two of MCT oil. Because ghee doesn't emulsify, I do the following:

Make the coffee, pour it in the blender, add ghee and mct, blend on high for about 15, then add 1 or two eggs yolks and blend on high for another 5 seconds. Now the fat from the ghee and the mct oil won't separate and float to the top of your coffee.

### Josh — 2014-03-15T17:41:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-fairly-simple-explanation-of-methylation.10179/post-115617>

This podcast is Dr. Kruse's "simple" version:

[http://www.blogtalkradio.com/mthfrs...k-kruse-neurosurgeon-and-optimal-health-coach](http://www.blogtalkradio.com/mthfrsupport/2013/02/21/dr-jack-kruse-neurosurgeon-and-optimal-health-coach)

### Jack Kruse — 2016-11-21T15:03:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-fairly-simple-explanation-of-methylation.10179/post-204140>

This will be no surprise to my members but it may be new to you. Vitamin B12 and Vitamin D 3 are linked to solar exposure. If you lack sunlight, leptin resistance develops and diseases that ruin the NAD+/NADH redox couple at cytochrome 1 becomes ruined. Obesity and T2D are diseases of darkness due to a lack of sunlight. Obesity and T2D are effectively spectral solar deficiency diseases. A lack of solar light during the day is one side of T2D and obesity, but the presence of blue light at night effects another pathway that the human eye uses at night to optimize the mitochondrial in melanopsin, RPE, and the central retinal pathways that control the eye clock mechanism that controls circadian cycles. The absence of blue light at night is critical light effect needed to catalyze the solid state conversion of serotonin by using the process of methylation. Methylation during darkness mediates circadian clock plasticity both in the SCN and in the peripheral clocks. This solid state biochemical process requires a large exclusion zone (EZ) and the presence of vitamin B12 and folate. B12 is made in the liver under the presence of SOLAR daylight. Folate, on the other hand, is destroyed by full spectrum sunlight and it is produced under darkness when blue light is ABSENT. These are the two chemical arms that tell the quantum clinician if a patient is solar deficient and/or blue light toxic. Obesity and T2D are two such quantum diseases that manifest when this situation occurs in humans. B12 and Vitamin D3 are linked to specific sunlight frequencies and this is why pernicious anemia is associated with a lack of sun. In fact, most anemias are related to a lack of proper solar exposure. This link to anemia and B12 was found by Fritz Hollowich in the 1940's. In fact, I have found a link to low solar exposure and low B12 that dates back to 1927. It seems these links are well published on a long time ago but seem to be unknown by modern humans. It is nice to see medscape making the connections. T2D and obesity are epigenetic diseases of light that ruin cytochrome 1 and the Q-cycle. [http://www.medscape.com/viewarticle/872008](http://www.medscape.com/viewarticle/872008)

A methylmalonic acid test ( serum) is a functional marker for B12 deficiency. The type of B 12 in most vitamins is not methylated. (cyanocobalamin) and can still show high serum levels of B12 but it is not usable....There is a difference in an absolute deficiency and a functional deficiency. There are many SNP's that can effect this....upstream, downstream, point of conversion.....The SNP(s) can be too fast or too slow in their enzymatic efficiency.

### Lois Hollan — 2019-07-23T14:02:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-fairly-simple-explanation-of-methylation.10179/post-274847>

> [sooperb said:](/goto/post?id=115591)
>
> [http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com/mthfr/](http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com/mthfr/)

One has to be very careful in understanding forms of "folate" here. The synthetic folic acid that we eat like candy--and put in lots of our foods--is fine for some people, but deadly for those with the MTHFR polymorphism. That's because--with the polymorphism--we cannot break down the synthetic form of folate (folic acid) and it builds up in our systems, thus becoming a carcinogen. (Recall the scare years ago--that was the discovery that prompted the scare.) So it works for those with the normal C677T genes, but not for those with the mutation. If one has one or both mutations, avoidance of folic acid in synthetic form is necessary. It can be taken--and the person will feel great for a short time--then begin to feel absolutely terrible and wonder why. With the MTHFR mutation, one must take the natural form of folate--methylfolate. It is available. And many good researchers suggest methlyflolate and folinic acid. But there's one critical thing: Do not take a lot! Take very, very little every day. It will help. So, the warning is that this inability to synthesize folate is fixed only by supplementation of the natural form and is hurt by supplementation of the readily-available, drugstore form of folic acid. I can go on about this, but this is enough for now! Happy to answer any questions to the best of my ability.

It is a real,y pitty I feel like crap the last few days. I removed that bloody tooth (big old granulomas on both roots) and it is not healing well and my body is on fire, pains and pains just about everywhere. I feel exhausted and abandonded in all that pain but the bath was spectacular.

# Thread: Hairs wanted and unwanted
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hairs-wanted-and-unwanted.10181/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Lahelada — 2014-03-15T17:44:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hairs-wanted-and-unwanted.10181/post-115619>

So I noticed that my eyebrows are scarce in their terminations but are now very slowly getting more pronounced again. However I also note a fine almost transparent fluff of hair all over my face and arms. It is so fine that it is not noticeable but makes makeup look smeared on application.
I know about the eyebrow thyroid link but is this fluff a sign of under / Over anything ?
As you may know I am working without labs which is like reading a Chinese take away menu upside down. You see it as number 68 and hope that it really said Chow Mein beside it.

### Inger — 2014-03-15T18:22:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hairs-wanted-and-unwanted.10181/post-115628>

hmmm... Are you sure the fluff is not meant to be?
I have fluff too. All over my face... but it is very fine and not noticeable really. I use foundation on some places but I use very light make up only. Never use powder. I cant see my make up looks smeared
Maybe change your make up? I use Lavera organic foundation.. I love that. I also do not use it all over my face at all... just a tiny bit here and there

If the fluff is much... like beard (  ) maybe it is a hormone thing.. that you hormones are going crazy before balancing out?

### Lahelada — 2014-03-15T18:53:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hairs-wanted-and-unwanted.10181/post-115631>

Thank you for the reply . I thought the same and had changed my make up. It is a long term observation which I am only now voicing. I would think some fluff is natural but this is noticeably different to "before" whenever that was.
I hark back to something that is said about anorexia that extreme clorie restriction causes a fine film of hair to grow. Fat chance of me being anorexic or restricting calories but as the nutrition world is skewed towards calorie vision that is their explanation what do we put those hairs down to? As obesity is the other side of the coin of anorexia it must then also be the reason in obese people I would think. I think I have them ,am not anorexic and the last time I restricted calories to a diet level it was still another century.

### Inger — 2014-03-15T19:47:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hairs-wanted-and-unwanted.10181/post-115644>

where are the hairs/fluff, Lahelada? Are they on your back too? That is what I have read about anorexic people... that they have hair/fluff grow on their neck/back

My sister in Norway that has a 10 gauss magnetico too since last summer/fall said as I was there a few weeks ago that she have gotten a beard!!! But me and my others 2 sisters, as we examined her, could not see any beard... lol but she insisted that she has gotten so much "fluff" around her upper lip!

Many women today remove the fluff on their faces.. so if we do not it might seem it should not be there? IDK... but excess fluff sure could be some hormone changes. Maybe your testosteron is increasing? How are you feeling these days?

### caroline — 2014-03-16T02:30:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hairs-wanted-and-unwanted.10181/post-115708>

Yes - maybe a hormone thing? Does you DH have a smile on his face a lot more these days?

### sooperb — 2014-03-16T15:00:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hairs-wanted-and-unwanted.10181/post-115766>

I'm sure there's another thread about this somewhere. It's almost certainly a hormonal thing, ladies of a certain age. When I first noticed mine I was aghast, I had visions of turning into a female Grizzly Adams! They didn't thicken up thank goodness, but they are all over my jawline and under my jaw. I've also started sprouting the dreaded black moustache hairs, they are yanked out as soon as I see them but there seem to be quite a few popping up. I suppose I'll be standing buttock to buttock in the bathroom with OH eventually while we both shave

### Shijin13 — 2014-03-16T16:56:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hairs-wanted-and-unwanted.10181/post-115788>

Having PCOS I've always had the unruly hair on my face. Some areas are more peach fuzz. while others are more coarse hairs which are dark (those are on my chin!) I pluck them b/c they drive me nuts!!! They have been getting better as I work to balance my Pg:E2 ratios....
