### Ben1983 — 2014-03-15T17:58:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/best-direction-to-sleep.10182/post-115623>

What is the best direction to sleep in? For the past 3.5 years I have been sleeping with my head facing west. I can't remember what Dr Bonlie said is the best direction, but most health sites that googled say its best to have your head facing either east or South but not north or west.....would you agree with that?

### Lahelada — 2014-03-15T19:00:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/best-direction-to-sleep.10182/post-115633>

I could not hear all the sleep webinar recently but I think I heard that it may be down to personal preference how you feel best most refreshed on waking. Change your position for a couple of days and really observe how you feel.

### Shijin13 — 2014-03-15T19:29:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/best-direction-to-sleep.10182/post-115637>

I sleep facing N-E b/c of how the house sits....

Chaos and Havoc sleep all directions.... N, S, E, W. yup. they move around alot while sleeping...

### Hope — 2014-03-15T19:33:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/best-direction-to-sleep.10182/post-115639>

In Thursday's webinar, Dr Bonlie said....with magnetico it doesn't matter which way, bc of what the magnetico does to straighten out the field.

If you don't have a magnetico, HE said that sleeping toward the north, followed by sleeping toward the south, and East/West is not as good. Remember...that's what he said. There could be other opinions....

### caroline — 2014-03-16T02:20:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/best-direction-to-sleep.10182/post-115707>

That nite - after the webinar - I tried to sleep at the opposite end. I think I normally sleep N/W.

I was very uncomfortable sleeping upside Down and it didn't last long. It felt very wrong.....no quantum magic of any kind.

My takeaway was that you don't know what is in the walls at your head for sure - so try something else and see if it is better. I have another small bedroom and have thought of getting a single bed for that room and a magnetico - and I could sleep north ...But there is a smell in that room I don't like - lots and lots of old cook books... .. very old and worn and probably lots of flour thru them.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-16T11:00:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/not-a-surprise-autism.10186/post-115746>

Read the quantum autism blog caroline.......its all in the hindbrain development. males and females have different hindbrain development rates and this is why their area postrema size differs. It is also why females have spectrum disorders less but find it more difficult to lose weight. Area postrema = where vagus nerve connects gut to brain stem. and where CSF enters the internal SQUID. (see quantum brain blog)

**Posts:** 83

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-16T14:15:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/artworks.10188/post-115763>

Well, we've got a thread with pieces of music. How about one with some beautiful or inspiring images?

Here's one in saw at exhibition while on holiday in Paris last year. It's by Marc Chagall.

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

[http://www.wikipaintings.org/en/marc-chagall/window-in-the-country-1915](http://www.wikipaintings.org/en/marc-chagall/window-in-the-country-1915)

Chagall was newly married at the time, and the figures in the bottom right of the painting are he and his wife. They were living near Vitebsk in Russia at the time.

I don't actually care that much for the way he's depicted the human figures, but I think there's something serene about the painting. I like the sense of looking out of the window onto those trees -- birches? And he seems to have caught the way the light plays luminously through the twsited piece of white cotton perfectly.

### Shijin13 — 2014-03-16T16:37:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/artworks.10188/post-115782>

John Pitre is one of my favorite modern artists. I remember seeing his original piece ["Night Street"](http://www.johnpitre.com/fantasy.html) at his and Wayland's gallery in Honolulu. I asked how much it was - couldn't afford the original - its was the price of a small house. So I asked how much an Artist Proof was. that was reasonable. I saved for a year & 1/2 to buy the AP of "Night Street" Its currently hanging in my kitchen.

My other favorites of his are "[Restrictions](http://www.johnpitre.com/spirit.html)" I bet Jack would recognize this. Pitre gained his fame while selling this piece as street art in NYC.... "Sensing the Magic of the Universe" is another one I'd love to own. Just don't have the Bank right now or the wall space. Same for "Identity".

His sensuous form is amazing... so beautiful.

I currently have several AP from Toshi Yoshida [http://japaneseartsgallery.com/gallery/woodblock-prints/toshi-yoshida/](http://japaneseartsgallery.com/gallery/woodblock-prints/toshi-yoshida/)

I also have several AP from Watanabe [http://www.japanprintart.com/artist\_collection.cfm?artist\_lname=Watanabe&artist\_fname=Sadao](http://www.japanprintart.com/artist_collection.cfm?artist_lname=Watanabe&artist_fname=Sadao)

I wish I had the wall space to hang them...

### Shijin13 — 2014-03-16T16:39:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/artworks.10188/post-115783>

I also love Jack Bauer's photos [http://www.mountainphotography.com/gallery/sawtooths-idaho/](http://www.mountainphotography.com/gallery/sawtooths-idaho/)

as well as James Burrett's photos [http://www.mountainphotography.com/gallery/sawtooths-idaho/](http://www.mountainphotography.com/gallery/sawtooths-idaho/)

these two men take me home. I'd love to have a huge wall piece to hang up my walls so I could see the peace and serenity of home....

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-17T12:37:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/artworks.10188/post-115925>

> [spinergie said:](/goto/post?id=115803)
>
> It comes across to me as if there is a luminous quality in the glass panes and a repetitions of whites in various tones all over the painting. In it I see a sense of two's in my own eyes, two red fruits, the panes of glasses are by two, two faces together. The length of the faces almost take the length of a section of the window. Their expression is similar looks like a sign of togetherness. My mum was a painter and had the talent of a genius.
> She could free hand embroider and add depth to colour that was amazing. This is something she did from a picture.

A madonna and child embroidery. It must have taken ages to do! There's been a lot of religious iconography in the past using madonna and child images. One Australian sociologist says he thinks that the past ubiquity of that image has helped to spread an aura of sacredness around motherhood in the West. Of course, so much of our old art is religious in one way or another -- as it still is in one way or another pre-modern societies. The same sociologist has a lot of to say about Holbein's painting "The Ambassadors" (1533) as representing a more secularised view, and thinks it telling that Holbein has included a skull (at an oblique angle and not imediately obvious) suggesting perhaps that these men feel themselves as in control of their world through their learning (piled on the table behind them) but cannot solve the problem of death:

[http://www.nationalgallery.org.uk/paintings/hans-holbein-the-younger-the-ambassadors](http://www.nationalgallery.org.uk/paintings/hans-holbein-the-younger-the-ambassadors)

Among religious art I like Master Rogier's picture here:

[http://www.nationalgallery.org.uk/paintings/rogier-van-der-weyden-the-magdalen-reading](http://www.nationalgallery.org.uk/paintings/rogier-van-der-weyden-the-magdalen-reading)

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-17T19:55:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/artworks.10188/post-115969>

> [Gagnrad said:](/goto/post?id=115925)
>
> ... I like Master Rogier's picture here:

I was in a hurry.

For the historical interest, I'll add a little. This is Rogier Van der Weyden's "The Magdalen Reading".

I like this one because again it see it as a serene image. The "inwardness" of the woman reading in the foreground is striking. I also like the way you can catch a glimpse of the outdoors through the window. The sensuous beauty in the way the fabrics and how they fold is depicted appeals to me, too. It's particularly noticeable in the foreground figure.

The figure in the background in holding beads. Here's an interesting counting device, found in many cultures, and used as a way of keeping track of prayers. If you zoom in on it, as you can with the National Gallery's page, you'll see it's in a straight line, as many of the earlier ones were. They were known as paternosters (Latin: Our Father) -- hence Paternoster Row in London where they were made. One ascetic practce of monks was to read through the whole of the psalms. The laity, who lacked the education to do this do this, adapted the practice by reciting 150 "Our Fathers" instead. The beads help keep the count. Notice that they're loose on the string. I think people probably found the clicking sound as the beads dropped soothing -- their aural world was, of course, generally quieter than ours. In the later medieval period when the cult of the Virgin took off the _Ave Maria_ was often substituted for the _Pater Noster_. How many beads people used and quite what they did with them seems to have been variable, but the Roman Catholic Church laid down an approved version of prayer beads with a standardized configuration -- at the Council of Trent (if memory serves) -- and nowadays they're joined with chains instead of threaded on cord.

Another interesting feature is the cloth the Magdalen is holding. Books, of course, were rare and costly objects. She holds it with a cloth so as not to soil it with sweat.

I'm not quite sure how she's sitting. But I think that's hugely interesting from a historical point of view. Certainly the figure in the background is standing. The Magdalen seems to be leaning against the cabinet rather than sitting on a chair or stool. It looks like there's a red cushion behind her. But her thighs seem to be horizontal. Interesting -- I can't quite work the posture out.

The picture does seem to indicate a world in which people sat far less often though. One figure stands, and one seems to be sitting in a make-shift way and not on a chair.

What a wonderful thread! Thank you, all, for the visual treats.

One of my favorite photographers was Galen Rowell. We have two signed prints that we treasure. His rock and mountain climbing adventures took him to some remarkable places that he photographed with his extraordinary eye and technique. In the 1970s we sometimes saw him bouldering at Indian Rock.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-18T19:58:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/artworks.10188/post-116237>

The Bar at the Folies-Bergeres by Edouard Manet

I've often seen this one reproduced, but once I saw it at the Courtald Gallery in London. In the flesh it was stupendous.

[http://www.andrewgrahamdixon.com/archive/readArticle/121](http://www.andrewgrahamdixon.com/archive/readArticle/121)

There's a bottle of Bass in the bottom right-hand corner of the painting. You can tell by the red triangel on the label. AFAIK, the Bass red triangle is the oldest trademark in the world ... Bass ain't Paleo though. LOL

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-18T20:03:49+0200

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-18T20:44:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/artworks.10188/post-116252>

> [spinergie said:](/goto/post?id=116247)
>
> Those were oil on canvas I believe ? I haven't been to the national gallery for such a long time I should go.

I don't know. It may be on wood. Rogier van der Weyden's most famous painting -- the one in the Prado in Madrid -- is on wood.

Do you know that one -- The Decent from the Cross? It blew people's minds in the 15th century and was the most desirable and costliest artwork in the world at the time.

If you click onit once it enlarges, and if you click a second time it opens in a new window in a super-large size so you can roam over the detail with a mouse. You can see the tears on the faces:

[https://www.museodelprado.es/en/vis...pieces/work-card/obra/descent-from-the-cross/](https://www.museodelprado.es/en/visit-the-museum/15-masterpieces/work-card/obra/descent-from-the-cross/)

### caroline — 2014-03-19T00:15:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/artworks.10188/post-116292>

What a great thread .....Thanks Mike!

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-19T09:07:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/artworks.10188/post-116360>

> [spinergie said:](/goto/post?id=116257)
>
> Thank you  no I didn't know this one. I can well imagine that it received the praise it did. The intricate lattice wood work in the corners .. Gives me also the impression that it seem to hide a little lack of perspective in the corners of the sides of the enclosure?
> This was 1435 just when perspective was introduced I seem to see.

There's a heck of an interesting tale in there. And again I suppose there's a relevance to what a culture sees as appropriate for subject matter for an artwork. I guess the realism (not just with perspective) and the increasing tendency towards secular subject-matter track one another.

What I only found out fairly recently was that Eastern Orthodox icons do, at least sometimes, use perpective, but the vanishing point is at a _different_ place. (In fact, I think they use several different forms of perspective, and there can sometimes be multiple vanishing points ... but I'm rapidly reaching my limits here.) Those icons are also deliberately not realistic. No-one could say that the Byzantine Theotokos of Vladimir looks like a real woman -- it's not supposed to. No _child_ looks like that -- and what a neck he has!

I think it's supposed to express tenderness, although she also looks sad to me, which seems like it might be intentional.

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Theotokos\_of\_Vladimir](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Theotokos_of_Vladimir)

There's a gallery in London that specialises in Russian icons -- the Temple Gallery:

[http://www.templegallery.com/index.html](http://www.templegallery.com/index.html)

I've never been there, but it sounds worth a visit. Apparently, Richard Temple, who founded it, bought his first icon for a pittance when quite young. He just found the image overwhelming. "What is it?" he thought. So he took it to a top London museum, and the art experts looked at it, and said something like: it's a Russian icon; these are quite crude; there's no real value.

That was then. Now they can change hands for a lot of money, of course, and everyone knows they can be great works of art.

I suppose in the long run photography put a spoke in the wheel of the assumption that an artist should try to copy nature as accurately as possible and perhaps frees us to see the value in what someone who's trying to do something rather different may achieve.

> My mum loved Flemish painters.

So did Phillip Larkin ... to bring the conversation down to a less elevated level:

> Jan van Hogspeuw staggers to the door ...

[http://books.google.co.uk/books?id=2x0ey2DBcX4C&pg=PA282&lpg=PA282&dq="Jan van Hogspeuw staggers to the door"&source=bl&ots=rnShxQAchy&sig=KPMQ0vRVxDjSX-a6LDzM6eWRNLY&hl=en&sa=X&ei=4DwpU5f\_Nc2JhQfyyoEI&ved=0CB4Q6AEwAA#v=onepage&q="Jan van Hogspeuw staggers to the door"&f=false](http://books.google.co.uk/books?id=2x0ey2DBcX4C&pg=PA282&lpg=PA282&dq=%22Jan+van+Hogspeuw+staggers+to+the+door%22&source=bl&ots=rnShxQAchy&sig=KPMQ0vRVxDjSX-a6LDzM6eWRNLY&hl=en&sa=X&ei=4DwpU5f_Nc2JhQfyyoEI&ved=0CB4Q6AEwAA#v=onepage&q=%22Jan%20van%20Hogspeuw%20staggers%20to%20the%20door%22&f=false)

I guess all life is there in those old Flemish paintings. ...

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-19T20:41:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/artworks.10188/post-116479>

> [spinergie said:](/goto/post?id=116398)
>
> Your knowledge is vast ..

Not really. Just a little I've picked up over the years.

> I have a Byzantine picture my mum kept in an art book she really liked I wonder if it the very same you have posted. I will have a dig.

Yes, do and let us know.

An amazing civilisation, and it lasted for more than 1000 years. The best art exhibition I ever saw was on Byzantium -- at the Royal Academy of Arts:

[http://content.time.com/time/photogallery/0,29307,1853552,00.html](http://content.time.com/time/photogallery/0,29307,1853552,00.html)

They'd assembled this stuff there from all over the world, room after room. You couldn't put a value on the stuff there. Not that how much it's worth was really the point ...

I read a historical novel once:

[http://www.amazon.co.uk/The-Historian-Elizabeth-Kostova/dp/0751537284/](http://www.amazon.co.uk/The-Historian-Elizabeth-Kostova/dp/0751537284/)

She has the ghost of Count Dracula say that the greatest disaster in history was the fall of Constantinople. Of course the words are in Dracula's mouth, since non-European cultures can never do anything bad these days ... but one wonders ... Byzantium had its faults, but the culture that replaced it locked women up, beat people on the soles of the feet (bastinado), kidnapped small boys and turned them into imperialist shock-troops (janissaries), and castrated black slaves. I found I had some sympathy with her Dracula's suggestion. LOL.

But I mustn't shock people.

It's said that the final collapse of the Byzantine Empire probably helped get the Renaissance underway -- it's suggested that people who understood Greek and had Greek texts fled West with them.

> I feel I have just had a wonderful trip back down some really good memories I hold of her thanks to your knowledge and time like you have brought back a little bit of her soul into this day. Thank you

That's really nice. That's worth more than anything I've posted.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-21T20:02:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/artworks.10188/post-116977>

> [spinergie said:](/goto/post?id=116660)
>
> And this is one of the pictures. She used to keep pictures and postcards everywhere like precious treasures.

That's an interesting image. It looks Roman -- from Pompeii or something?

I'm going to the Vikings exhibition at the BM soon. Apparently, it's getting rave reviews. I don't know what's in it, but I'm hoping there's some artwork. They do mention "ostentatious jewellery of gold and silver":

[https://www.britishmuseum.org/about...ss\_releases/2013/vikings\_life\_and\_legend.aspx](https://www.britishmuseum.org/about_us/news_and_press/press_releases/2013/vikings_life_and_legend.aspx)

Metalwork seems to have been one of the strong points of the old Northern culture. Imagine how excited the person who found the Alfred Jewel must have been!

> The Jewel was discovered in 1693 at Newton Park, North Peneton and called the Alfred jewel because it bears the inscription: AELFRED MEC HEHT GEWYRCAN which translates as 'Alfred has this made' Its exact purpose is somewhat debated however it is believed to be the jewelled head of an ornate pointer.

[http://www.southsomersetheritage.org.uk/\_845.aspx](http://www.southsomersetheritage.org.uk/_845.aspx)

I don't know how people even did such delicate work. Their eyesight must have been better than ours -- and I bet there's a dietary story there.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-21T20:13:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/artworks.10188/post-116979>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=116662)
>
> ... The sculpture of Botticelli ...

Nice paintings, too!

The National Gallery has one of those and all ... "Venus and Mars":

[http://www.nationalgallery.org.uk/paintings/sandro-botticelli-venus-and-mars](http://www.nationalgallery.org.uk/paintings/sandro-botticelli-venus-and-mars)

I particularly like this one. It's a term I've already used twice in this thread, but this is another serene image. She's very beautiful and calm but alert. The picture has a symbolic meaning typical of the re-purposing of classical imagery in the Neoplatonic thought of the age:

> Here Mars is asleep and unarmed, while Venus is awake and alert. The meaning of the picture is that love conquers \[discord\] ...

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-22T08:53:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/artworks.10188/post-117108>

> [spinergie said:](/goto/post?id=116993)
>
> He was quite a painter representing detail and emotion in those ways. Sounds like the exhibition at the Bm is really good I do love metalwork and jewellery yes such meticulous precision
> There are sometimes good exhibitions of a smaller scale at the Sainsburys centre on the university grounds in Norwich. There was one around a year ago on tribal art.

That sounds good.

By the way, there's one if the best essays on art I've ever read online explaining why the modernist movement arose. The writer says that basically modernism -- which involved making things _difficult_ (think Eliot, Pound, Joyce, and, of course, people like Picasso in the visual arts) -- was a defence against _kitsch_.

I was reminded, because tribal art is touched on, because that was free from _kitsch_ until tribal peoples came into contact with Western civilisation. There seems to be something about modern civilisation that does it:

> They were surely right that kitsch is a modern invention. But pre-modern people are not proof against it. On the contrary, their immune systems seem helpless in the face of this new contagion; today the mere contact of a traditional culture with Western civilization is sufficient to transmit the disease, rather as tribes were once rescued from their darkness by colonial adventurers and missionaries, only to die at once from smallpox or TB. A century ago, no African art was kitsch. Now kitsch is on sale in every African airport—antelopes, elephants, witch doctors, and hobgoblin deities, skillfully carved in ivory or tropical hardwood, imitating the enchanted figures that inspired Picasso but, in this or that barely perceptible detail, betraying their nature as fakes.

Kitsch and the Modern Predicament:

[http://www.city-journal.org/html/9\_1\_urbanities\_kitsch\_and\_the.html](http://www.city-journal.org/html/9_1_urbanities_kitsch_and_the.html)

> There are sometimes good exhibitions of a smaller scale at the Sainsburys centre on the university grounds in Norwich.

I don't know Norwich, although I've wanted to visit it.

I've currently got a quotation from Julian of Norwich in my autosignature. I'd like to visit the church where she was a anchoress. What an extraordinary woman! I believe part of the function of anchorites and anchoresses was to listen to people's problems and offer advice. I think I'd sooner take advice from Julian than a psychoanalyst. LOL

[http://www.julianofnorwich.org/](http://www.julianofnorwich.org/)

### Gagnrad — 2014-04-10T08:22:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/artworks.10188/post-120744>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=120534)
>
> I always wonder how they laundered these long gowns ..............

Did they bother overmuch?

I think people mostly washed garments that were next the skin. The little girl is wearing silk there, but most outer clothing was of woven wool. That could take a very long time to dry if it got thoroughly wet, which made rain a great nuisance to people in the past.

Underwear was made from linen, which will wash and dry easily. Hence the name _lingerie_ in French -- a word which I think was used to mean "washables".

This was one use for a linen head-covering -- a coif:

[http://www.blacksheepemporium.co.uk/#/coifs/4538090110](http://www.blacksheepemporium.co.uk/#/coifs/4538090110)

You could wear it under a hat to stop oil from your hair getting on the hat.

### caroline — 2014-04-10T11:00:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/artworks.10188/post-120767>

You are such a fountain of information! I can understand they wouldn't wash outer clothes that often .... but those long dresses would have been dragging in the dirt

### Gagnrad — 2014-04-10T20:39:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/artworks.10188/post-120827>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=120767)
>
> You are such a fountain of information! I can understand they wouldn't wash outer clothes that often .... but those long dresses would have been dragging in the dirt

I suppose so. But the hooped skirt of the infanta's dress doesn't quite reach the ground.

In other periods I suppose they did. The wife in the Arnolfini Wedding picture (1434) is wearing a dress with a fairly long train:

[http://www.nationalgallery.org.uk/paintings/jan-van-eyck-the-arnolfini-portrait](http://www.nationalgallery.org.uk/paintings/jan-van-eyck-the-arnolfini-portrait)

But perhaps that's for a special occasion and not everyday wear. (I don't know: it'd be interesting to ask a costume historian.) And I suppose that if venturing into a muddy street, she'd put wooden pattens on over her shoes (bottom left of painting) and hold the train of the dress in her hand. On a ceremonial occasion I'd assume there'd be attendants to hold the train for really exalted personages such as princesses.

Could you even wash a dress made from several different fabrics, some of them delicate, and with pearls and bows and so forth sewn on it? I assume there are different layers here, but each one seems very complicated and not something you could dump in warm water with lye or soap or whatever:

I think the costume historians have a fine old time looking an old paintings, which can tell you a lot. IIRC, you can see when buttons appear -- from about the fourteenth century onwards. It's been suggested that this may be connected with the Little Ice Age:

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Little\_Ice\_Age](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Little_Ice_Age)

Maybe that's jumping to conclusions, though. Correlation is not causality. LOL

### Gagnrad — 2014-04-14T09:27:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/artworks.10188/post-121391>

It's just that I've thought about these things over the last few years. Here's an example of a thought-provoking article -- Dr. Thomas Cowan, the WAPF doctor, on gout:

> We know of cases of gout dating back at least 500 years as some of the most notorious of the England’s monarchs were apparently afflicted with the disease. There are treatises explaining the causes and treatments of gout that also date from the Middle Ages. The typical description of a patient with gout was a wealthy, corpulent aristocrat, particularly one with an enormous appetite for food, drink and women. It was almost always associated with men, and in fact still has a male predominance, and was considered a consequence of overindulgence. …
>
> The newer theories about gout recognize the fact that the disease is not simply a result of high-protein intake. For example, we have discovered that uric acid levels are high in those patients with syndrome X, otherwise known as insulin resistance. This means that excessive carbohydrate consumption, particularly of refined flour and sugar, foods that in previous times were solely the domain of the wealthy. ...

[http://fourfoldhealing.com/2010/06/08/gout/](http://fourfoldhealing.com/2010/06/08/gout/)

I think sugar first started being available in any quantity in England in the fifteenth century. By the Elizabethan Age it was around more, if you could afford it. Good Queen Bess had black teeth by the time she was an old lady.

You could track some of these illnesses across time and situate them nicely, I think, if you knew enough about social history, medical history -- and economic history, so that you knew what foods were available to whom when.

I know little about it, but bits seem to keep dropping into place, like with a jigsaw.

### caroline — 2014-04-14T09:36:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/artworks.10188/post-121394>

It is all so, so interesting .......you do such a good job of bringing thought provoking stuff to us....

### Gagnrad — 2014-04-14T20:11:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/artworks.10188/post-121458>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=121394)
>
> It is all so, so interesting .......you do such a good job of bringing thought provoking stuff to us....

Eighteenth century people drank a heck of a lot, Audrey.

Paul Johnson has some interesting snippets in _The Birth of the Modern: World Society 1815-1830_

[http://www.amazon.com/Birth-Modern-World-Society-1815-1830-ebook/dp/B00FAS43KK/](http://www.amazon.com/Birth-Modern-World-Society-1815-1830-ebook/dp/B00FAS43KK/)

> By 1820 everyone in London agreed that drinking was very much on the decline by the by the standards of the late 18th-century.

Some people were still going strong, though:

> Dr. Addington \[the Prime Minister\] drank "perhaps 20 glasses of wine at his dinner" to "invigorate himself" before going to the Commons. \[Johnson does add in mitigation that contemporary glasses were very small\] ... Louis XVIII drank four bottles of port at dinner. … Dr. George Fordyce … one of the most knowledgeable doctors in London "drank a bottle at dinner, one after dinner and one at supper". …
>
> Middle-aged clergymen, like Parr and Sydney Smith, were probably the greediest men in London … The Rev. Dr. Paley, the great theologian … 'would eat from all the main dishes and the side-dishes too -- he called it skirmishing."

There was terrific disagreement about what was a medically appropriate diet (as now).

One expert "recommended no wine .. white biscuits instead of bread … no webb-footed animals … game instead of other meats … plenty of salmon, mackerell, whitings, soles, haddocks, turbot … He asked me if I had warm feet … _that_ \[he said\] was a good sign."

That doesn't sound too silly, but Byron seems to have got the idea that to lose weight he must forgo meat and eat only potatoes in vinegar. And Sir Joseph Banks, the president of the Royal Society, "eat nothing but puddings and vegetables" silly fool. He seems to have been one of the first vegetarians.

Then there's more on drinking.

> Spirit drinking was much more a problem in the United States than in Europe; by the late 1820s, American men were drinking, per capita, a yearly average of four gallons of ... 200-proof alcohol. This in turn produced a reaction …

But we all know about that reaction. LOL

### kovita — 2014-09-29T20:41:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/artworks.10188/post-142174>

I just finished reading a biographic book about Alphonse Mucha written by his son Jiri. I always admired him not just as a shockingly diverse and prolific artist, but as well as a human being and this book only confirmed what is radiating from his work is nothing but the truth. Too bad he is and was so misunderstood. I am posting here a link to the web where you can see Slovanska Epopej (Slav Epic). This is his most important work, his personal dream and his gift to his beloved Czech nation. No need to say we, the Czechs, spent more time critising his work and life than absorbing the incredible amount of emotions about his own roots he gave us through this masterpiece. The Epopej was finished in the late 20s and given to the city of Prague with a condition there would be a suitable building to display the whole epopej which is non existent up today. I was lucky to see it and it was unbelievable. Too emotional really. Most of the paintings are at least 6x8 meters and i must say the funding for this whole project including extensive study travels was provided by Ch.R.Crane, a wealthy American.
[http://www.slovanskaepopej.cz](http://www.slovanskaepopej.cz)

# Thread: On Becoming a Doctor
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 219
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Casey Inman — 2014-03-16T14:17:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-115764>

Hey everybody,

I have been considering becoming a doctor for the past year or so and have spent quite some time looking into it, however, a few aspects of medical school are preventing me from going...

-   The lack of sleep that most residents go through
-   The massive amount of EMF I'd be exposed to
-   The false dogmas that pervade medicine (why learn a bunch of lies that support government, pharmaceutical and health care interests? )

What are your thoughts? Are my assumptions wrong? Should these factors prevent me from going?

### NeilBB — 2014-03-16T15:27:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-115772>

I went to medical school and residency in the 90's. Your assumptions are spot on. Those are all major concerns. If I had kids I would encourage them to go in a different direction. But you do have to follow your heart. For some, I suppose it could still be the right move. You have to weigh all the pros and cons. Depends on who you are and what you want to do. It's going to be frustrating though. And it's going to be a battle. And your health will likely take a beating.

### NeilBB — 2014-03-16T15:40:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-115775>

Casey,

I just checked out your website. From what I see there, I seriously doubt you're going to like much about conventional allopathic medicine. Perhaps you should also consider functional or other alternative medicine types of options as well...

### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-16T17:07:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-115791>

[http://www.forbes.com/sites/robertp...l-people-what-its-really-like-to-be-a-doctor/](http://www.forbes.com/sites/robertpearl/2014/03/13/malcolm-gladwell-tell-people-what-its-really-like-to-be-a-doctor/)

### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-16T17:09:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-115792>

I was one of the nine out of ten that responded to that survey by the way..............

### Shijin13 — 2014-03-16T17:15:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-115793>

great article. This highlights why consortiums or ACO's are becoming attractive for doctors, IMO. The ACO helps manage the stuff docs don't want to deal with, so they can just practice medicine. It gives them greater bargaining power with hospitals, as well as insurance companies....

There's a small part of me that still is considering going back to school and get a BSN and CNP and IBCLC certification. and once I have it... move to some where remote and trade my skills for goods. Not sure if that's what I'll do considering the current state of the medical system here in the US....

### NeilBB — 2014-03-16T17:48:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-115796>

Pretty good article. But Gladwell and most of my colleagues think it's just a matter of doctors "not having time to deal with the business side" because they have been trained in medicine not business. Blah. Blah. Blah.

In actuality, it's far worse than that IMO. The "business side" of medicine has been hijacked by a socialist style government. This is not a business now. This is a quasi-nationalized extension of the government.

Shijin, I know your are an admirer of Ayn Rand. Have you read any of Peikoff's articles on the the morality of a "right to health care?" He has been opposing government intervention into medicine since 1965 when the King-Anderson bill created Medicare and set us on the path to economic destruction we now find ourselves in.

[http://afcm.org/hcinar.html](http://afcm.org/hcinar.html)

### NeilBB — 2014-03-16T18:22:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-115801>

So to summarize, American medicine's moral/economic half is on the road to bankruptcy and is thoroughly morally corrupt. (See Peikoff)

And, it's scientific/biologic roots are drowning in outdated and false dogma which gleefully ignores most of the last century's scientfic breakthroughs in physiology and physics that don't fit the "old model" preferring to rely on useless clinical RCTs, which have little to do with reality, and can be easily manipulated by those in power to profit. (See anything on this website by Dr Kruse)

And you definitely will be abused in training. It's part of the process. Sleep is considered non-essential by the overwhelming majority of MDs today. Going without sleep is considered to be character building.

So, my friend, if you choose to enter the field of medicine, that's what you're up against.

### Josh — 2014-03-16T19:28:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-115810>

The deal breaker for me was when I sat down with 2 doctors and veterinarian at a Yoga retreat and we discussed whether I would stay in Med School. I questioned the cost and graduating with the debt. The veterinarian spoke up and said that when he looked back, it was not the financial cost that he felt he could not afford. In retrospect, he felt it was the "life energy cost" that was too high for the degree. 24 years later, I look at my friends who were in various schools at the time and see what they have paid for their degree and careers. I envy their financial and job security, but not their stress levels and how limited they feel in their practices. When I left, I realized that many of the interesting things in life required energy that I had in my 20"s and might not have after 20 years of medical practice. So I took the risk based on the limit of my consciousness at the time. I know I have treasures that I would never have received without the change of course and there are moments that I wish I was as employable as MD's currently are. The ND path seems to be the best compromise available at this time. Oriental medicine offers many extraordinary opportunities and experiences, and the path in practice varies more state to state. As an ND one can do almost anything in the states with licensure. One is more limited as an Oriental practitioner. Chirpractors have interesting choices as well.

I was not talking to my father at the time (also a doctor and son and grandson of a doctor) because he was living in an ashram in India, so I got no comment from him.

### Josh — 2014-03-16T21:06:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-115819>

I liked this article and it highlights some interesting possibilities based on other less well known parts of the ACA:

[http://magazine.columbia.edu/features/winter-2013-14/wages-health](http://magazine.columbia.edu/features/winter-2013-14/wages-health)

> Despite this attention, the problem only worsened. ‘DIABETES EPIDEMIC’ DECLARED AS NEW YORK CITY DEATHS TIED TO THE DISEASE HIT ALL-TIME HIGH, ran a New York _Daily News_ headline in June 2013. Around the same time, the New York City Department of Health and Mental Hygiene found that 667,000 New Yorkers have diabetes — a 33 percent increase from a decade ago. And East Harlem (population 108,000), half Latino, a third African-American, with an influx of professionals, mostly white, filling the new residential buildings — a neighborhood of high obesity rates and gang activity, where 38 percent of residents live below the poverty line — is New York’s diabetes epicenter: as many as one in five people here has the disease.
>
> For Kaur ’05BC, ’12BUS and Singh, a SIPA professor, this quiet catastrophe points up a fatal failure in the US health-care system. It also presents a bold opportunity.

In the spirit of Don Quixote, in the early 1980's I joined the groups resettling the refugees from Vietnam and Cambodia in the slums of New York and Philadelphia. 3 years of working with people a lot worse off from a background of unspeakable stuff that I felt partially responsible for gave me a lot of humility about the limits of all systems and well-meaning individuals.

When I was 11 I got off of a bus in Port Authority and went up the street to the ILGWU clinic where my 75 year old grandmother stood in a large room with a long line of patients working as a volunteer doctor. She was 5 feet tall, stooped by osteoporosis and her hands had trembled for years. Still she listened to and reassured each patient...I watched for a couple of hours and then she drove me uptown in her 1973 sky blue Impala...A year later she more or less forgot her own name and spent the last 6 years of her life in a nursing home....

My parents worked at Bronx State Hospital when I was a kid and pioneered community mental health in the South Bronx...I got to see some of that as well...

Later they and their colleagues escaped the Bronx and setup a community health center in New Rochelle where we lived. It went well until the "visionary" Omnibus Reconcilliation Act of Ronald Reagan and friends cut funding for such unnecessary things....I knew many of the clients. Some went to hospitals, some to the streets, some took their own lives...

From what I have seen in medicine, no good deed goes unpunished by those in power....

### Josh — 2014-03-16T21:09:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-115820>

In the future, PA's, FNP's and nurses ingeneral will probably be doing the frontline general medicine stuff. The training is more modest. In the end, if one is conscious, one will need to understand and be able to work with everything we are reviewing here to remain sane....

### Josh — 2014-03-16T21:12:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-115822>

It is never just "them" we all created the system we have now...more than one great man has said that "you are the world"...the only liberation is understanding how our consciousness or lack of it contributed/es to the current situation...One of my favorite images is a story of Chogyam Trugpa where he was asked about his teacher. He said, "well, while we were all running away from Tibet and the Chinese, my teacher was seen heading for Beijing...."

Of course, the world is round so any direction that one runs in eventually leads back to where we started.....

### NeilBB — 2014-03-16T21:20:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-115823>

> In the future, PA's, FNP's and nurses ingeneral will probably be doing the frontline general medicine stuff. The training is more modest.

Right. The "system" is gearing up to replace doctors with lower paid less qualified "practitioners" because they can implement [CMS's](http://www.cms.gov) edicts just as well at a lower cost and with less backtalk.

A traditionally trained doctor who actually understands the underlying science (the old fashioned model of medical education) is an impediment to the implementation of the current blueprint of American industrial healthcare. And Jack Kruse is its worst god-damn nightmare!

### Josh — 2014-03-16T21:37:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-115824>

I am remembering stuff...when my grandparents did their residencies in the 20's, they lived at the hospital...when my parents did theirs, they had 60 hour shifts on weekends...in NY State the rules came in the 90's restricting interns and residents to no more than 12 hours on without 8 off...my friends said they of course had to stay longer to give report and show up early to take report so they got 5-6 hours off between 12 hour shifts. Not much support for circadian rhythm.

Neil write it and I will post it but I wont post its from you........you'll be the wizard.

### Josh — 2014-03-16T22:14:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-115832>

A couple of articles from Dr. Jake Fratkin OMD, my mentor in Oriental and Functional Medicine who gave me the now rare blessing of a four year internship in his private practice:

> Oriental medicine has meant many things over the ages. While building on common foundations of the ancient classics such as the _Huang Di Nei Jing_ and the _Shang Han Lun_, it has developed differently in isolated pockets of the numerous countries of its origin, often in family traditions. Only in this century have the various traditions formally organize themselves in the educational institutions of China, Japan, Taiwan, Korea and Vietnam. This institutional setting, with its various publications and educational opportunities, has benefitted practitioners in the West, who have enjoyed a rich exposure of the various medical models from Asia. In addition, newer approaches to Oriental medicine have emerged from England, France, and Germany. Educated and dedicated practitioners in the West are now forging an eclectic clinical practice incorporating the best that the various traditions have to offer.

[http://drjakefratkin.com/articles/future-of-energetic-medicine](http://drjakefratkin.com/articles/future-of-energetic-medicine)

> If you had to select one area of focus that to you represents a “key” to the future of OM, what would that be and why?
>
> Study Western medical sciences to a level equal to a medical doctor or physician’s assistant or nurse practitioner. This is followed in the Chinese TCM schools, and should be here. Why? To gain both knowledge and credibility.
>
> We are now more sought after than ever as a profession compared to Western medicine. What are the significant factors that you have seen causing this shift and why?
>
> First, we act like doctors who practiced in the old days. We listen to our patient’s complaints, and give time to trying to figure it out. Western doctors are forced, economically, into a 5-10 minute visit. They throw medicines at symptoms, without understanding the deeper aspects of the condition or accepting the responsibility of the side effects of combining drugs. We, on the other hand, listen to the whole history, and try to fix all of the problems simultaneously, which we can do. Patients come to us because we act like doctors, not drug pushers. Of course, they also come because our medicine works.

[http://drjakefratkin.com/articles/pioneers-and-leaders-of-tcm](http://drjakefratkin.com/articles/pioneers-and-leaders-of-tcm)

### Marie — 2014-03-16T23:01:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-115842>

I'd recommend TCM school, but study outside of the US. TCM school costs $100K for the 3-4 years of grad schooling required in the US, which is why I don't recommend it. You can study abroad (saving money) and challenge the licensing exams in the state where you wish to practice, while networking like crazy to get a good internship/mentor relationship. (TCM takes about 10 years of practice, and good mentoring, before you _begin_ to understand how it works, so expect to be "getting good at it" when you hit 70.)

Then, look on [http://www.nccaom.org/](http://www.nccaom.org/) for information on which states currently have practice acts in their state in addition to looking for a market with very few practitioners. I wouldn't look for long-term employment in CA, Boston, or NY as the markets are pretty saturated. These are great markets to learn the trade, though. Lori and Mina are great resources at NCCAOM and if you do go this path, speak to them every chance you get.

And, yes, Josh, the Western innovations will revolutionize how Traditional Medicine is practiced. China is watching the US very closely....

The other reason I'd recommend TCM is that the ICD codes will be changing Oct 2014. These new codes will give greater visibility to the SAFETY and efficiency Traditional Medical practitioners offer. The risk of using TCM is extremely low and insurance rates are reasonable, when compared to MDs, so there will be a lot of movement to integrating TCM to lower risk. (BTW, who causes the vast majority of acupuncture lawsuits? Yah, MDs... something about doing an online 100 hour certificate course makes them think they know how to handle a needle. Hhurrrummmfff... If you are an MD reading this... for God's sake, hire someone who is trained if you want to offer TCM.)

Marie

PS When I was considering the DVM route 25 years ago, a vet told me no matter if I went to med school or vet school I'd be "practicing business" within 5 years of graduation. He suggested I get an MBA ... because it was cheaper and had higher returns than an MD/DVM. It was a very disheartening discussion, but I listened ... and I'm glad I went to biz school instead. However, if I were to do it over, I wish I'd gone to TCM school.

### Josh — 2014-03-16T23:21:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-115851>

It was cheap in the 90's. I would study in China or Taiwan where there are TCM/OM hospitals and full clinics of people who use it as their primary care. Also, self-cultivation of one's own qi is as important so this may be more available in those contexts. Many of the China universities qualify for the NCCAOM I believe. I agree about the 70 part...the stuff here on quantum biology applied to TCM/OM theory illuminates it in new ways and makes it more explainable to people who have trouble with the language and translation of the languages of TCM/OM. Japan also has clinics and contexts. Stephen Birch is in Holland and is one of the best Western resources for Japanese Acupuncture if you feel like going to the EU. It is a rich and varied world and I agree with Dr. Fratkin that one needs a strong biomedical background to practice in the West. The focus on "acupuncture" is a little like saying that allopathy is basically about "surgery", it is just one technique and modality in a diverse holistic system. To represent TCM/OM as "acupuncture" is selling it short.

### Josh — 2014-03-16T23:41:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-115855>

If money is not a concern, I will say that it appears that the US academic culture is maturing with graduate programs and more comprehensive programs that integrate with conventional care settings and the larger CAM community.

### Josh (Paleo Osteo) — 2014-03-18T03:22:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-116105>

i chose osteopathy in australia because it is the only health care profession that truly teaches you to look for the root cause of dysfunction
whilst osteopathy in australia has become largeley musculo-skeletal and mechanical in focus, the mindset that is instilled allows me to apply the holistic thought process in problem solving to any application. we are versed in biochemistry and physiology at the same level as medical school in australia, but we view it through a different lens. this is why Jack's page resonates with me. it is asking the deeper questions.
i believe osteopathy is very different in the states.

when it started there in 1870s it was more holistic and against the grain of allopathic med, now i believe you need to be an MD before becoming an osteopath, so things have kinda flipped...

### Casey Inman — 2014-03-18T13:57:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-116170>

Hey guys,

Thanks for the replies. You've all been more than helpful.

For me, I can't fathom spending money on any over priced education for TCM, ND, MD, whatever it is. Aside from the real reasons why I want to get into healing people (which serve as the foundation for why I wanted to become an MD), becoming an MD seemed most feasible because there was a payback (by that I mean I could make a living and pay off my loans) from it.

NDs, functional medicine doctors, TCM, chiros all pay a huge amount of money for school with no guarantee of any payback. These fields all focus on important issues, but I feel that I can serve a purpose helping people heal without a degree if thats the case.

Neil- Writing a blog about your experience would be extremely helpful. You seem to be covering important issues that people need to hear about. From what I've seen there aren't many blogs about med school written by someone who can write like you have here. It's mostly doctors who follow pop culture and want to be cool for having a blog.

And cynical? If anything we need to be more cynical about the healthcare system.

I mean come on, a group of people who say they want to heal people but continue to follow outdated dogmas. People who after getting smacked in the face with real new evidence, deny it and bury their heads in the sand. People that don't actually care about helping their patients other than giving them prescriptions they're getting paid for.These are the people that are leading the healthcare system. And in my opinion, if they're going to do things like this they should be picking up my garbage or stocking grocery store shelves. At least they won't hurt anybody that way. This situation is beyond fucked up. (not sure if I can swear here) The worst part is, the list goes on and on....

### NeilBB — 2014-03-18T17:24:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-116215>

> For me, I can't fathom spending money on any over priced education for TCM, ND, MD, whatever it is. Aside from the real reasons why I want to get into healing people (which serve as the foundation for why I wanted to become an MD), becoming an MD seemed most feasible because there was a payback (by that I mean I could make a living and pay off my loans) from it.
>
> NDs, functional medicine doctors, TCM, chiros all pay a huge amount of money for school with no guarantee of any payback. These fields all focus on important issues, but I feel that I can serve a purpose helping people heal without a degree if thats the case.

I would say that there is not necessarily a "correct career path" for an individual to pursue. Ultimately, it comes down to: (1) What you want to accomplish, (2) Your natural ability, (3) How much bullshit you can tolerate, (4) How much you want to be compensated for your effort. You have to somehow plug in the variables and do the math.

You have to also realize that when (1) and (2) clash with (3) and (4), which is probably the case since you are here, it ain't gonna be easy no matter what.

### Josh — 2014-03-18T18:21:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-116218>

I am not calling you out, just noticing that it is different at 50 than it was at 26. At your age, I had already tried to save the world directly by going into the ghettos, working with refugees, political organizing, AIDS activism, traveling to less developed countries and lamenting their problems. After all of that, medicine was all I could think of to do that gave me something to offer in all of these contexts. In retrospect, I pretty much agree with Marie that the most effective tools for action in this day and age are actually learned in business school. I am now engaged in an exercise that is teaching me those things as well. Exchange is required no matter what one chooses. The currencies vary depending upon the context. Perhaps read " Fourth Uncle in the Mountain" and then go to Manchester, VT and see if you can find him. I met a client of his a few years ago who said he was practicing there. Beings such as him have rare treasures that they may or may not be willing to share. Read carefully and make notes on all of the things that were involved in his training and what he paid for them in time, energy, money, etc..

One does not go on the path to be a doctor/healer for "payback". Even the most avaricious and self-centered person can only handle so many rounds in hospitals filled with decaying people in pain. I have watched the faces of interns and residents faced with my dying mother. I will never forget the smell of the St. Luke's ER on a busy day in the late 80's with everyone from dying AIDS patients to demented old ladies to young children with colds running around. I can not forget the faces of the Cambodian families that had crawled through jungles and sat patiently in horrific refugee camps for years to finally end up in the Bronx with me as their guide to the brave new world. I can not forget my grandmother and her patience with the garment workers in NY. Take a look at the story I put in the "mental illness" thread. How would you respond to that? What is the lesson and blessing there? I have to respond to these things for myself in a way that is meaningful to me. It is a work in progress. I may know how it went by the time I die. Once upon a time it is reputed that before anyone got to learn anything, they were taught how to be present and responsive. They lived in the forest at a school called a "gurukhul" run by someone who had actually managed to finish the basic work before leaving. Somewhere around your age, I realized that I had missed that 20 or so years of training and that I might have some work to do before I could really "choose" anything. I thought I would be there by now....so much for plans.....

Good luck and enjoy every moment, there are no guarantees.....

### NeilBB — 2014-03-18T18:22:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-116219>

Regarding making a living, unfortunately the world will pay you handsomely if you smile and feed it comfortable lies and penalize you heavily if you are the disseminator of painful truths. Why?

"Truth" is the subjugation of our thoughts to correspondence with nature.
Civilization promotes "dogmas" which are only very loosely based on truth.
"Conventional wisdom" typically includes outdated info and deliberate falsehoods that serve certain political, economic, and traditional interests.
A "career" is the the subjugation of an individual's time and effort to correspond with the perceived needs of society for remuneration, it actually has very little to do with serving the truth.

It is the story of history. Read about any scientific revolutionary ever. Read "Going Somewhere" by Andrew Marino. Its all there.

### Josh — 2014-03-18T18:52:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-116222>

One more note on the "doctor" part. Being called a "doctor" is a mixed blessing and has become down right confusing as there so many kinds of "doctors" in the health world. For me, I could have gotten an OMD (Oriental Medical Doctor) by simply taking the herb test and getting a New Mexico license when I graduated acupuncture school. That door had been open for about 10 years and many practitioners are grandfathered in as OMD's who have half the training I do. Because I am not a "doctor" patients often have trouble putting me in that position and I have felt it keeps us closer to a partnership. On the other hand, in this day and age being a "doctor" of whatever gives some value to one's brand. I am amused by the latest trend of people being called Dr. "first name". To me it feels like kids playing medicine. Perhaps this is appropriate.

Consider that before one can "first, do no harm", one must "heal thyself", I believe it is the law....interesting how they have become separated from each other....

### NeilBB — 2014-03-18T19:08:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-116227>

The only hope for humanity is that when society's dogma strays too far from reason, a real scientific revolution typically follows at some point. But it is a long painful process and the revolutionaries are typically tormented or worse. And the old guard never admits it was ever wrong about anything.

But in the meantime, we all still have to try to "earn-a-living" and hope that civilization hasn't strayed too far (committed too many crimes against nature) to recover this time (aka extinction event), which is debatable at this point.

Thats what i meant by you have to do the math and plug in the variables of where you want to fit in to all of that. Maybe you are the next Aristotle or Copernicus. Maybe you'd rather lay low in a remote cave. I don't know.

I hope this helps some. I do know that you probably won't get a speech like that from any premed counselor at your local college. Good luck.

### Josh — 2014-03-18T21:07:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-116259>

I guess this is therapeutic for me because I keep remembering more family/doctor stuff. On the subject of the military, I remember my father's father. He volunteered in 1942 to go to England as a urological surgeon. He was 42 years old. Throughout my childhood, he told me many happy stories of being in England and operating on officers. He gave me his insignia and I kept them in my drawer. Our time together and these stories were my good times with him. Only a few years ago, I was talking with my father and I found out that my grandfather also went to France after D-day and worked in field hospitals for the remainder of the war. He never said a word of this to me before he died when I was 30. For me, this is not a small omission. After the war, my grandfather divided his time between golf, bridge and surgery. In his 70's, he moved to Florida because of his asthma which had not been helped by a lifetime of smoking cigars and pipes. In his 80's, he still flew to NY to see Irving Berlin who was one of his favorite patients. Last night a friend told me how he survived being a door gunner on a Huey in the Marine Corps in 1965. He arrived and his sergeant told him he must learn detachment or he would not survive. I can not imagine more powerful lessons in detachment than the ground war in France 1944-45 or the door of a Huey in Vietnam in 1965. My friend completed his tour unwounded after 13 months. He stills wakes up screaming 2 nights per week. I have been wondering where PTSD fits in our conversations about physiology and brain function?

He gave me his insignia and I kept them in my drawer. Our time together and these stories were my good times with him. Only a few years ago, I was talking with my father and I found out that my grandfather also went to France after D-day and worked in field hospitals for the remainder of the war. In his 70's, he moved to Florida because of his asthma which had not been helped by a lifetime of smoking cigars and pipes. In his 80's, he still flew to NY to see Irving Berlin who was one of his favorite patients. Last night a friend told me how he survived being a door gunner on a Huey in the Marine Corps in 1965. He arrived and his sergeant told him he must learn detachment or he would not survive. I can not imagine more powerful lessons in detachment than the ground war in France 1944-45 or the door of a Huey in Vietnam in 1965. My friend completed his tour unwounded after 13 months. He stills wakes up screaming 2 nights per week. **I have been wondering where PTSD fits in our conversations about physiology and brain function?**

Josh I've been thinking about this alot of recent. I have PTSD diagnosis, and I have several friends who have it as well. There was an indepth conversation over of FB a week or two ago... that has me researching a future blog post. we have a whole generation of my brothers and sisters in arms returning who need help coping with PTSD, and TBI. having been a responder on 9/11, I have additional experiences that have added to my PTSD. Yet I physically wasn't at the Pentagon for the response. And I worked in the Pentagon for years w/o issues. Yet on 9/11/12 - I physically got so ill I couldn't go in the building. there's definitely something there. I have an idea how I beat it the first time around. and I'm getting closer to beating it the second time... but what works for me may not work for everyone else.

### Josh — 2014-03-18T21:33:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-116262>

My dad worked the psychiatric ER at the Lebanon, PA regional VA a year and got really obsessed with the topic. He even wrote a book of sorts that remains unpublished. We are still looking at whether to do anything with his ideas. He is a bit of a character. I am working on some other things in your part of the world that may be related with a partner whose father is a retired Air Force Reserve Brigadier. My dad got bounced out the Army Reserves for playing hooky on a weekend stint to play golf. He almost got deployed in the Berlin Crisis of all things....He recently helped another friend of ours get a PTSD disability for the issues related to his Vietnam service as a pilot. My dad has another couple of years in him and is still interested in participating in something. He might even show up here...

### Hope — 2014-03-18T23:02:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-116275>

I often wonder what exactly Americans mean by US medicine being "socialized". I am not sure if there is another country whose ~healthcare~ symptom suppression and disease management system is _more_ based on money and profit than in the US. It seems to be all about control, money and profit......drugs, insurance, everything. (and I've heard some amazing MDs and dentists even say, the system is meant to feed you bad food to make you sick, so you need an MD to give you drugs, so you get worse, may need expensive, cutting-edge tests and possibly surgery and more pieces of the puzzle make money off you). Everything related to American disease management is based on money, as far as I can see, even if it may be very little. I see socialized medicine more as in the failed attempt to create communism, when medical care was _super cheap_, technology _sucked_, but it was affordable for nearly everyone, with _long wait times_ - as it was in Eastern Europe during the Cold War, for instance.

And the original meaning of "doctor"...from docere (Latin), which means to teach.....as in teaching people to live well. The kind of work Jack does. Now that's a real MD if there ever was one. I guess generally, there isn't much money to be made in teaching people to watch their circadian rhythms and the many things we learn here.

And acupuncture - now there is an amazing modality that insurance mostly doesn't cover.....probably because it doesn't pay the other pieces of the symptoms billions of dollars a year. And it may actually heal....not just suppress so you lose clientele = power = money. Christiane Northrup, MD, one of my heroes, talks about how it helps heal/release the emotions that are held in our bodies that we accumulate from the trauma we experience in our lives - and especially in our childhoods.... some MD's do make it big. Dr Northrup is one. Deepak Chopra MD seems to have found a great path for himself too....

I hope I didn't offend anyone with this.....and mostly just embarrassed myself and lol.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-116295>

> I often wonder what exactly Americans mean by US medicine being "socialized". I am not sure if there is another country whose healthcare symptom suppression and disease management system is _more_ based on money and profit than in the US. It seems to be all about control, money and profit......drugs, insurance, everything.

"Socialized" medicine means basically that the government has fundamental control over health care decisions and the utilization of resources. It does not prevent (actually it encourages) looting by greedy individuals, as we see today.

### Hope — 2014-03-19T22:11:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-116520>

> [NeilBB said:](/goto/post?id=116295)
>
> "Socialized" medicine means basically that the government has fundamental control over health care decisions and the utilization of resources. It does not prevent (actually it encourages) looting by greedy individuals, as we see today.

Hm. So how has the government here have fundamental control? I see corporations as having the control, the government is just the puppet playing an act, with everything...they can't do a thing really, except cave to the corporations and through them, they keep themselves in power. In Eastern Europe, it was the government - no rich corporations (well, maybe some individuals?).....and in Germany now, I could see it too. But here? I must be missing something....in Germany with insurance, it's not a for profit thing....insurance is supposed to be non-profit and the government has a hand in the budgets etc.

I just am not smart enough. I think I'll go out and hug a tree.

### NeilBB — 2014-03-19T22:29:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-116536>

> [Hope said:](/goto/post?id=116520)
>
> Hm. So how has the government here have fundamental control? I see corporations as having the control, the government is just the puppet playing an act, with everything...they can't do a thing really, except cave to the corporations and through them, they keep themselves in power. In Eastern Europe, it was the government - no rich corporations (well, maybe some individuals?).....and in Germany now, I could see it too. But here? I must be missing something....in Germany with insurance, it's not a for profit thing....insurance is supposed to be non-profit and the government has a hand in the budgets etc.
>
> I just am not smart enough. I think I'll go out and hug a tree.

I'm sure you're plenty smart. In essence it's pretty simple. In the U.S., the government decides what medical services are worth and who can get them through its control of coding and pricing at [CMS](http://www.cms.gov/). Corporations are pressured to adopt the same standards that the government sets and it quickly becomes the national model. It is basically a commodity model where everything is lumped into a box and the government decides what the relevant boxes are and what treatments fit with what boxes and how much payment is appropriate for each. Government regulations also control hospitals and physicians through an ever-increasing complexity of regulations. And they control the private insurance industry through regulations and taxation policies which go back to WW2.

### RobH — 2014-03-20T17:01:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-116692>

> [NeilBB said:](/goto/post?id=116284)
>

>
> You will still be working in the same system... Why do you want to be an engineer? Or a doctor? Or a biochemist? I suggest addressing numbers (1) and (2) in my earlier post. What is it that you want to accomplish and what are your talents?

Mine too. Electrical and Computer Engineering. In retrospect I'm thinking so many nights spent in the computer labs drinking Mountain Dew to stay awake may not have been such a great thing...

The only class in my ECE training that I didn't really like that much was... semiconductor theory. \*sigh\* guess I have to dig out those books again.

RobH

### RobH — 2014-03-20T17:19:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-116699>

> [Casey Inman said:](/goto/post?id=116279)
>
> Do you guys have any thoughts about "becoming" a biochemist or biomedical engineer?

Once my friends in engineering school found out I was going to med school I told them (as I was sure myself) that I'd go into designing MRI machines or some field of biomedical engineering.

Out of EE school I had interviews with the big software companies of the time and opportunities. Would have been in early and I'm sure made lots of money as they got bigger and bigger (Think Microsoft in 1991). I know people in those positions. Some are happier than others.

I became an EMT because I wanted to be a ski patrol at my local ski area. Started working night shifts in a local ER.

Once I started getting into the third year of med school and clinical medicine I started realizing how much I enjoyed the patient interactions.

No matter how cynical and frustrated I am by the system that I practice in (I am), no matter how much I complain about it (and I do) I will say this: there is something incredibly rejuvenating and special to me about being a doctor. When I can walk into a room and focus on just that patient, for some limited amount of time (and it is limited and that is frustrating), to apply the knowledge and skills I have learned, for that persons benefit, in a very intimate and personal way, to have them trust me to do that, is tremendously satisfying. (sorry run on sentence)

It all really comes down to that for me. But everything else surrounding it and pressuring it makes it more challenging for sure.

I constantly ask myself if I would do it again. Honestly I don't know. Somedays yes somedays no. Most days no. Knowing what I know now I would have steered a different path through residency and the last 15 years of my life, but of course I didn't know what I know now and never could have. And I will know and perceive things very differently in a few months or years -- of that I'm certain.

I have 3 kids getting to the age of making those sorts of decisions ... well not too close yet but they will be ...

I really don't know what to tell them. I think there is and always will be tremendous opportunity and satisfaction in some fields of medicine. There will always be a need for providers of quality healthcare, and I think as the "system" frays more and more people will be willing and have to pay for what works. It will never be cheap, financially, health wise, or time wise to get there.

But then what rewarding profession will?

Sorry, I doubt this adds any clarity here... just random thoughts drifting through my head after a late shift last evening.

RobH

### Josh — 2014-03-20T17:20:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-becoming-a-doctor.10189/post-116700>

I majored in East Asian History. Did all of the most semesters in 2 all-nighter weeks in front of my early IBM PC drinking TAB and Diet Coke and eating bagels to add insult to injury. After graduating, I went to Bryn Mawr College's Post-Bac program and took all of the pre-med courses in 13 months. I loved any class that was well taught. It has left me with an appreciation of Organic Chemistry because of the enthusiasm of the young professor who taught the first semester. They did not keep her because many students complained she was "too hard". I found this a great tragedy. Everyone got better grades the second semester and learned 1/10th of what they had the first semester....This is pretty much the story of my education, I respond to anything interesting and above all, well taught.....
