# Thread: MY N=1 "I TOLD YOU!"
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-n-1-i-told-you.10201/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 17
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### kmonty — 2014-03-17T17:41:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-n-1-i-told-you.10201/post-115946>

Early years 1966-1980. Very active BMXing, Martial Arts, all kinds of sports. Looking back my eating habits were not good. Cereal (a lot) and a lifelong candy addiction (that’s a food group..right) Hot Tamale’s, Skittles, Whoppers, Mentos, you get the idea!

I am 48 now and I ate this way all my whole life up until the last 10 years when I became a little more aware of my quality of food.

My health, I believe, has been outstanding in spite of my food quality, eating habits. I consider myself as never getting sick. As I got older I told myself “I don’t have time to be sick” Even when I would get sick I continued on with my daily routine and rub some dirt on it LOL.

I guess around the age of 25 I started to get migraines they weren’t very often; normally it was after working out I just thought/assumed/contributed it to dehydration. Migraines would be from mild to debilitating, crawl in bed and sleep it off. Between 35 – 45 they got more frequently about 2 x per month not very many debilitating ones, I could function, so I continued my daily routine. Since I started doing bulletproof this has improved very much and I get them every couple of months.

In 2000 is when my eating habits started to change, I had moved to Colorado, I was not training as much although still active, Golf, young kids chasing them. We were having food delivered from a farm in Colorado grass fed, range chicken and organic veggies, for the most part.

Although I was feeling good physically I started to notice that I had put on 10 lbs. I don’t want to blow this out of proportion; I have never had weight issues. I have always been thin/lean I am 5’ 11” 135 when I graduated High school in 1984, then most of my adult years 140-145. When I decided that I needed to loose some weight I was 150-155. It was my belief that I felt better at 145, stronger, faster! (Six Million Dollar Man).

In (2002-2003) I did the Body For Life to help me shed the 10 Lbs. I did feel better and dropped the weight that I wanted. The other cool thing at the time is I had no problem cutting out the candy even with the “free day” I still found myself wanting to eat healthy. Not to say that I cut out all foods that had sugars but I did pretty well. (His shake mix that I used had sugar). I followed this protocol for a year and then slowly moved away from it, but keeping some of the principles for another 6 months. I began to eat mostly organic foods, but I went back on the serious sugar candy binging I was used to my whole life.

### kmonty — 2014-03-17T17:43:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-n-1-i-told-you.10201/post-115950>

Foods that are a problem for me.
· Onions, use to give me smelly gas (funny to drop boms and talk of my family) about 8 years ago it began to upset my stomach really bad, not so funny. I just stay away from them now.
· Garlic as well no bad ass gas but lots of belching and upset stomach this is recently started about a year ago.

Work wise since 1989 I have been around computers either sitting in front of one or in an environment that has had them.

My father had a scare with Prostate cancer and unfortunately he had the surgery. My PSA #’s have been kinda high but we heard in Dr. K’s webinar that the PSA number is worthless.

I never drank alcohol, about 3 years ago my primary care physician told me I was pre-diabetic so I cut down on candy intake and that brought my labs back to normal however, I didn’t stop eating the candy until about 7 months ago when I went Bulletproof and I haven’t looked back since at that time I also stopped eating wheat, corn, dairy and all grains (I eat some rice now and again). Now I am eating a lot more seafood because my girlfriend eats epipalio so I eat what she eats.

I hurt my neck on a roller coaster about 10 years ago since then it is always bothering me every day and when I do certain moves surfing it will really hurts.

At the age of 43-44 I had shoulder surgery Labrel ligament tear from skateboarding with my kids and at 46 I had ACL surgery from tae kwon do. I have recovered well from both.

Up until about 1 ½ years ago I always felt my health was pretty good, I felt good had good energy. I keep a really regular schedule up at 4:00am M-F in bed between 8:30 to 10pm.

· Monday, Wednesday, Friday I do core and light cardio. Then I teach an hour Martial Arts class then go to my full time job.
· Tuesday and Thursday ecliptic weights and some cardio followed by my full time job.
· Saturday and Sunday is reserved for Surfing.

I never ate before working out in the past and then 7 months ago I started Bulletproof intermittent fasting and now I don’t eat anything until noon-2:00 everyday. I hoped this would help with the below mentioned heart issues but it hasn’t.

About 1 ½ yrs ago I started getting heart arrhythmias. My heart has never been an issue or even a concern for me until now. I get so many arrhythmias every day I lose count. I also get bouts of light headedness and tingling sensation in my head.

My girlfriend KyraKitty (Betsy) on this forum believes it is probably due to EMF’s. The apt we lived in, for 5 years, had 13 smart meters installed sometime after we moved in. They were located on the wall of our kitchen along the crawlspace area. I would spend hours siting at the kitchen table on my laptop with the 13 smart meters behind and below me.

She also had heart issues really bad 2 ½ years before me, as outlined in her optimal journal, when we lived in that apartment. Hers were as bad as mine are and she got really sick shortly after and couldn’t work but now her heart issues have completely stopped. We recently moved to a new apt on 12/1/2014 the smart meter is nowhere near us now. I also have a computer/phone line server room in a small office I work out of and run in Cupertino, CA for our company that is based in SF. I plan to have Michael Neurt come out to evaluate the EMF situation at our home and at my shop too very soon.

Well, you've come to the right place. Nothing like starting at the top, kmonty. This is the place where Nature is quoted left and right, and the horse\*\*\*t is left behind.

# Thread: Beads of plastic in a toothpaste
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/beads-of-plastic-in-a-toothpaste.10203/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Tanya — 2014-03-17T18:05:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/beads-of-plastic-in-a-toothpaste.10203/post-115955>

[http://www.dentalbuzz.com/2014/03/04/crest-imbeds-plastic-in-our-gums/](http://www.dentalbuzz.com/2014/03/04/crest-imbeds-plastic-in-our-gums/)

### nonchalant — 2014-03-17T20:18:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/beads-of-plastic-in-a-toothpaste.10203/post-115975>

> Like I said, I’ve been seeing these blue particles flush out of patients’ gums for several months now. So has the co-hygienist in our office. So have many dental hygienists throughout the United States and Canada who have consulted with each other and realized that we have a major concern on our hands.

Nice to see people networking and taking health concerns into their own hands.
These are professionals who don't just read the glossy pamphlets and listen to the reps during their lunches together. They are observing and deducing and being proactive.

### Tanya — 2014-03-17T21:00:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/beads-of-plastic-in-a-toothpaste.10203/post-115996>

> high density polyethylene. That’s what [plastic trash cans](http://www.ehow.com/list_6918522_types-trash-cans-made-from_.html) are made from! If you throw away the box like most people do, the ingredients aren’t actually listed on the tube (sneaky, sneaky, Procter & Gamble!) However, I was able to track down the box [here at this link](http://dailymed.nlm.nih.gov/dailymed/lookup.cfm?setid=fbe1298d-77b1-4866-9883-5d744b184042). We’re not talking about polyethylene glycol, which is soluble in water. This stuff won’t dissolve in water, or even acetone or alcohol for that matter.

We were given travel sizes of that toothpaste during a dental check-up. It ended-up in a trash can because of the multitude of the ingredients in it, but it escaped my attention that one of them was polyethylene.

### sooperb — 2014-03-18T16:10:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/beads-of-plastic-in-a-toothpaste.10203/post-116201>

I haven't used mainstream toothpaste for years and years, I can only say thank goodness! My alternative, health food store toothpaste has come in for a lot of stick over the years, I'm the one smirking now. Thanks for the heads up, I will be spreading this far and wide.

### Tanya — 2014-03-18T16:17:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/beads-of-plastic-in-a-toothpaste.10203/post-116204>

> [sooperb said:](/goto/post?id=116201)
>
> I haven't used mainstream toothpaste for years and years, I can only say thank goodness! My alternative, health food store toothpaste has come in for a lot of stick over the years, I'm the one smirking now. Thanks for the heads up, I will be spreading this far and wide.

That is exactly what I have also been doing, Sue

# Thread: Toddler Constipation
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/toddler-constipation.10205/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Madison — 2014-03-17T20:25:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/toddler-constipation.10205/post-115979>

My 2.5 year old goes though cycles of being constipated, and then being ok for a week or so. I have listened to Dr.Kruse in many podcast interviews, and read though some of this blog. My son's diet is clean, no gluten, and we cut out dairy but still include grass fed ghee. I know Dr. Kruse talks a lot about factors outside of diet. Is there anything that anyone can suggest for my child? I am considering doing a $400 comprehensive stool analysis, what other testing should I do? I know that having stool sitting in his bowl for days on end is leading to a pretty damaged gut. Any advice is greatly appreciated.

### nonchalant — 2014-03-17T20:28:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/toddler-constipation.10205/post-115980>

Hi Madison. Does your son drink non-fluoridated water? Does he get much sunlight?

### kovita — 2014-03-17T20:40:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/toddler-constipation.10205/post-115983>

madison, I happen to be mother to a boy who had intestinal surgery aged 8 mnoths which resulted in resection of whole ileocaecal piece. I learnt a lot about small child digestion ever since. My son tends to have more soft stools since the valve controlling the movement from small intestine is gone. He has accasionally constipation which mostly must be treated through enema. this is always followed by a few days of bloated belly (dysbiosis). In case of my son. i can see clear connection between the load on immune system and his digestion. Every small viral disease is pronounced in his digestion. What works for him is good amounts of healthy fats regulary, lot of fish, resistant starch, lots of veggies (soluble and insoluble fiber in balance), bone broths or at least great lakes gelatin daily. If the belly is bloated after some infection i give him course of sacharomycc.cerev., special e.coli strain used for ulcerative collitis, good probiotics. i have noticed, taht this winter he is doing better. What we did different is fermented cod liver oil daily, liver weekly at least, 3 hours daily outside, cold house, open windows in the night, wearing less cloths than other children and a lot of walking running on the fresh air. I try to control emf, we do switch off the whole circuit for the night. There is no universal recipe, but I can see one can do a lot with small adjustments to the daily routine.

### Madison — 2014-03-17T21:10:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/toddler-constipation.10205/post-115999>

wow thank you for the responses.

> [nonchalant said:](/goto/post?id=115980)
>
> Hi Madison. Does your son drink non-fluoridated water? Does he get much sunlight?

We are outside every day, and we live in Florida so there is plenty of sunshine. We are just using the purifier that we routinely change from our refrigerator. I have been thinking that this could be the next step in trying to treat this. What kind of filtration system would you suggest?

### Madison — 2014-03-17T21:15:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/toddler-constipation.10205/post-116005>

> [kovita said:](/goto/post?id=115983)
>
> madison, I happen to be mother to a boy who had intestinal surgery aged 8 mnoths which resulted in resection of whole ileocaecal piece. I learnt a lot about small child digestion ever since. My son tends to have more soft stools since the valve controlling the movement from small intestine is gone. He has accasionally constipation which mostly must be treated through enema. this is always followed by a few days of bloated belly (dysbiosis). In case of my son. i can see clear connection between the load on immune system and his digestion. Every small viral disease is pronounced in his digestion. What works for him is good amounts of healthy fats regulary, lot of fish, resistant starch, lots of veggies (soluble and insoluble fiber in balance), bone broths or at least great lakes gelatin daily. If the belly is bloated after some infection i give him course of sacharomycc.cerev., special e.coli strain used for ulcerative collitis, good probiotics. i have noticed, taht this winter he is doing better. What we did different is fermented cod liver oil daily, liver weekly at least, 3 hours daily outside, cold house, open windows in the night, wearing less cloths than other children and a lot of walking running on the fresh air. I try to control emf, we do switch off the whole circuit for the night. There is no universal recipe, but I can see one can do a lot with small adjustments to the daily routine.

Thank you for all the suggestions. I agree that there is no universal recipe, and like you I am willing to try any and all adjustments. We do not turn off our curcuit at night, my husband thinks I'm nuts to unplug the wifi, so I am slowly trying to move in that direction. We are at least going to move to hard wired internet in the next couple of months. I have never heard of that strain, I will look into it. What probiotic brand do you use? I have kept him on a clean diet with a multi, probiotics, and fats. I probably could up his fat intake. I will look into cod liver. Any other supplements? I have been giving him extra magnesium.

### kovita — 2014-03-17T22:16:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/toddler-constipation.10205/post-116026>

I use different brands of probiotics and rotate them. My favourite is biocult. The truth is a just use them when I see bloated belly, normally both kids eat homemade sauerkraut or juice from it daily. They love it. My husband thinks I am nuts as well but after a few nights with the curcuit shuted down, he admitted he sleeps better. I do not use any other supplements but try to feed the kids real food instead. We do eat seaweeds regulary. I usually crash them in to small pieces while still dried and add it to the broths along with vegetables. They seem to like it. Magnesium is good idea.

### nonchalant — 2014-03-17T23:39:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/toddler-constipation.10205/post-116043>

> [Madison said:](/goto/post?id=115999)
>
> wow thank you for the responses.
>
> We are outside every day, and we live in Florida so there is plenty of sunshine. We are just using the purifier that we routinely change from our refrigerator. I have been thinking that this could be the next step in trying to treat this. What kind of filtration system would you suggest?

That's great that he gets sunshine. When I was occasionally constipated, I used the sunshine as a stimulant. Works quickly.
Jack has several recent blogs on water. He recommends buying reverse osmosis (RO) water for reversing disease. Filters can't remove much fluoride.

# Thread: Hello my new friends!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-my-new-friends.10208/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Mare — 2014-03-17T22:44:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-my-new-friends.10208/post-116029>

Hi Everyone, I, like all of you have been on the hunt to feel better/optimal. I'm diagnosed Hashi's and have been battling to get well for several years. I'm a single mom with a wicked 14 y/o boy that I love to death and I'm hoping that by finding my way on this journey, I can help him avoid some pain down the road...

Here's a nutshell of my progression thus far:

-   Diagnosed Hashi's 2007-synthroid prescribed, continued to decline, extreme muscle/joint pain, headaches, brain fog
-   August 2010- Found lil' article linking gluten to hashi's quit the stuff and within' 24 hrs, almost all pain resolved (found later that dairy in certain amounts causes' me fits too) Quittin the G was like hell on earth for this philly girl weaned on cheesesteaks, pizza and tastykakes!
-   I eat a fairly clean/paleoish diet, but I'm at a plateau...
-   So basically all the muscle pain is gone, but left with less than stellar energy/stamina and I feel a bit dumber than I once believed....things just take longer to sink in, it stinks
-   Chronic constipation, since childhood (Not breastfed?) Take tons of mag, but it's hit or miss on if I get a movement
-   Last year learned via a Genova labs stool test I have a level 4+ yeast. Spent the last year chasing that beast and after about 10k in alt docs and supplements I don't think it made a dent.
-   My other devilish symptom is cystic acne, it comes and goes and is less severe since altering my diet, but none the less a total downer. PMS, FIBROID at 25, Estrogen dominance?? have labs if anyone can help me interpret??
-   Mom diagnosed with BC at 55 now at 63 it's moved to bone, she's suffered her entire life with hormonal disorders, endo, fibroids, acne, now breast cancer that's taking her life. It's a horror to watch.

Tried to make it to the point! I'm fascinated by the EMF and CT connections. Dr. Jack Kruse is my hero even though he hurts my head daily!! I'm trying to make this very technical info work on a daily basis, would be most interested in the links that give specifics on CT duration, etc. This is the one I dread the most. My temp runs at 97 degrees, constantly bundled up, so the submerging in cold is tough for me, but I will do it to get well...any tips on getting started would be much appreciated

I look forward to joining you all on this path.
Mare

### caroline — 2014-03-17T23:22:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-my-new-friends.10208/post-116037>

Hi Mare and welcome! Please start a journal so we can all follow your journey! and as you probably already know - Dr. K. needs context if he is able to offer suggestions to you. You could just transfer this to the journal section.

Please think about becoming a "Gold" member and then you have access to all the webinars and live Q&A whenever it is convenient for you. Think of it as an amazing investment in your health and future and that of your precious son.

Of course - it isn't necessary - we will all support you in any way we can.

Welcome to our world of brain Pain! This is a great journey to optimal health!

### caroline — 2014-03-17T23:24:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-my-new-friends.10208/post-116038>

about C/T .... have you read thru the C/T protocol on the home page? just start with face dunks.....

Keep reading and asking questions ... Dr. kruse doesn't know what we don't know unless we ask!

### Mare — 2014-03-17T23:32:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-my-new-friends.10208/post-116040>

Hi Caroline, Thanks so much for the warm welcome! I'm hoping to join the GOld club soon, just lost my job so I'm being careful now with money, but will definitely look into that membership asap.

This is where my feeling dumber part comes in. When you say add context, what exactly do you mean? I would love any help I can get from Dr. Kruse, I'm feeling pretty alone and frustrated right now. Everything I've tried ends with no changes to the constipation, skin issues and fatigue...

I will def check out the C/T protocol, I think I saw something on face dunks in the forum, but don't recall from Dr....that would be my preference for sure..how long do you do them to start?

I will attempt to move this to the journal section, I"m assuming that where we provide progress updates....

Thanks you again! I'm feeling a little better just having someone on the other line that gets the battle
M-

### Mare — 2014-03-17T23:54:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-my-new-friends.10208/post-116046>

AHHH...I went over the CT info and there it was ....thanks

### caroline — 2014-03-18T01:36:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-my-new-friends.10208/post-116075>

Dr. K. alo tells us there are no stupid questions ..... and I have had more than my fair share ....LOL

so - if you don't know something .... ask away!

We have all been there .....

Have you joined the free 90 day optimal reset? It has been fantastic - so many great guests and Dr. Kruse is giving us a ton of his time.......on the home page. There is a webinar in a few days ...please don't miss this!

### caroline — 2014-03-18T01:41:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-my-new-friends.10208/post-116076>

Mare - you have started off great with context! Do you have Dr. K's book? The Epi-Paleo RX .... it is a great overview of all of this in simple terms. kindle or paper.....

Also - listen to all Dr. K's podcasts - on the home page - they have so much quick and easy to understand info....

We look forward to following you on your optimal journey - we are all a work in progress ....and you will help us too!

### cantweight — 2014-03-18T01:44:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-my-new-friends.10208/post-116078>

As a former philly girl myself I feel your pain...it is a diet free of every comfort food we have been raised on. Hard as hell breaking that habit!

I battled yeast for years....follow DrK's protocols and it is no longer an issue for me....shocking as I went to great lengths to get rid of that crap without success.

Good for you for getting here....I think thats the hardest part.

### Steph Corby — 2014-03-18T18:37:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-my-new-friends.10208/post-116220>

Mare~ I live about an hour north of Philly, and I know what you mean! You can still endulge in those cheesesteaks, just a modified version! Ive been GF for 3 years and on LR for 3 weeks, but even before LR I would order a cheesteak just without the bread- after awhile you dont even miss it - you certainly dont miss stabbing pain and the OTHER lovely symptoms.

### Mare — 2014-03-18T21:53:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-my-new-friends.10208/post-116265>

Hey Cantwait and Steph, so nice to have some home town girls in the mix..I recently moved to Charleston to escape the winters!! And the cheesesteaks, lol! I still go home a few times a year as my family is still up those parts....

They like their oysters here in Chastown, so I'll be indulging in them more often...I've been avoiding seafood because my D.O. was concerned with high mercury.

And Steph I hear you on the bread, I'm still waiting for the day I don't crave that Amoroso roll with my cheesesteak!! You're right though pain free is a better option!

### Mare — 2014-03-18T21:59:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-my-new-friends.10208/post-116266>

Cantwait,
wondering how long you've been on the protocols to see results...I know we're all different but curious

### Mare — 2014-03-20T18:39:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-my-new-friends.10208/post-116728>

Can anyone tell me how I can determine if I'm Leptin Resistant?
I'm Hashi's, thin, always have been,
Eat paleo for years, no gluten or dairy

Dr. K indicated that reverse t3 is the better marker for someone fit/lean.

My Reverse t3 was 194 in march, 13, my new doc did not pull it but i have no reason to believe things are much different..

Also, I listened to an interview Dr. K gave regarding Leptin RX and I thought he said that we don't produce Leptin, we get it from breastfeeding?? If you weren't breastfed what happens? Did I misunderstand his response in that interview?
BTW Excellent interview! I've sent it to several friends already.

### Mare — 2014-03-17T23:34:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-or-bust.10209/post-116042>

Hi Everyone, I, like all of you have been on the hunt to feel better/optimal. I'm diagnosed Hashi's and have been battling to get well for several years. I'm a single mom with a wicked 14 y/o boy that I love to death and I'm hoping that by finding my way on this journey, I can help him avoid some pain down the road...

Here's a nutshell of my progression thus far:

-   Diagnosed Hashi's 2007-synthroid prescribed, continued to decline, extreme muscle/joint pain, headaches, brain fog
-   August 2010- Found lil' article linking gluten to hashi's quit the stuff and within' 24 hrs, almost all pain resolved (found later that dairy in certain amounts causes' me fits too) Quittin the G was like hell on earth for this philly girl weaned on cheesesteaks, pizza and tastykakes!
-   I eat a fairly clean/paleoish diet, but I'm at a plateau...
-   So basically all the muscle pain is gone, but left with less than stellar energy/stamina and I feel a bit dumber than I once believed....things just take longer to sink in, it stinks
-   Chronic constipation, since childhood (Not breastfed?) Take tons of mag, but it's hit or miss on if I get a movement
-   Last year learned via a Genova labs stool test I have a level 4+ yeast. Spent the last year chasing that beast and after about 10k in alt docs and supplements I don't think it made a dent.
-   My other devilish symptom is cystic acne, it comes and goes and is less severe since altering my diet, but none the less a total downer. PMS, FIBROID at 25, Estrogen dominance?? have labs if anyone can help me interpret??
-   Mom diagnosed with BC at 55 now at 63 it's moved to bone, she's suffered her entire life with hormonal disorders, endo, fibroids, acne, now breast cancer that's taking her life. It's a horror to watch.

Tried to make it to the point! I'm fascinated by the EMF and CT connections. Dr. Jack Kruse is my hero even though he hurts my head daily!! I'm trying to make this very technical info work on a daily basis, would be most interested in the links that give specifics on CT duration, etc. This is the one I dread the most. My temp runs at 97 degrees, constantly bundled up, so the submerging in cold is tough for me, but I will do it to get well...any tips on getting started would be much appreciated

I look forward to joining you all on this path.
Mare

> **The Takeaway: The link between electromagnetic signals and water**
> **An electron-rich body charge encourages oxygen to penetrate cells, prevents junk molecules from sticking to cell membranes and DNA base pairs, vanquishes pathogens and powers our immune cells.** [It also decreases the need for glucose in your diet.](http://jackkruse.com/emf-4-why-might-you-need-carbs-for-performance/) The interchange between electromagnetic signals and water is where we need to focus for optimal health.

Otpimal water/EZ=best defense

More glucose metabolism increases oxidative stress, reduces redox potential, destroys EZ=methylation defects because other things bind to DNA and or methyl groups do not bind to properly inhibit/promote=altered phenotypic expression which may include mutagenesis. EZ water preserves optimal methylation and phenotypic expression, glucose metabolism often destroys it. All may be promoted by EMF as demonstrated by the studies of V, A and B.

> cause increase ROS at cytochrome one.... EMF 4

Sinclair: EZ water=optimal oxygen levels, he sees NAD as the key for signalling, I am not a good enough biochemist yet...NAD=Oxygen=EZ optimized by cold and ketosis

> As mammals age, says Sinclair, levels of NAD drop by 50%; with less of the compound, the communication between the cell and its mitochondrial energy source also falters, and the cell becomes vulnerable to common aging assaults — inflammation, muscle wasting and slower metabolism. By tricking the cell into thinking it’s young again, with adequate amounts of NAD, aging can theoretically be reversed. “When we give the molecule, the cells think oxygen levels are normal and everything revs back up again,” Sinclair says.
>
> Read more: [Reversing Aging: Scientists Say Yes, It May Be Possible | TIME.com](http://healthland.time.com/2013/12/19/reversing-aging-not-as-crazy-as-you-think/#ixzz2wH5nqs6A) [http://healthland.time.com/2013/12/19/reversing-aging-not-as-crazy-as-you-think/#ixzz2wH5nqs6A](http://healthland.time.com/2013/12/19/reversing-aging-not-as-crazy-as-you-think/#ixzz2wH5nqs6A)

# Thread: Sherpa in Action....
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sherpa-in-action.10211/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Josh — 2014-03-18T02:00:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sherpa-in-action.10211/post-116087>

I watched the movie "The Summit" over the weekend. In between all of the melodrama of Western climber bemoaning their fate and the loss of partners, one had to pay attention to notice Pemba. He did not sleep the night before the climb. He had to get out of his tent to rescue someone before climbing who almost blew off of K2 in the middle of the night at 25,000 feet. He climbed K2, and came down to camp (3,000 feet in each direction). He came down, did not sleep and then went back up part way to rescue a few people. Some friends died in front of him on this round. He came down, slept a few hours and went up again to rescue someone else. He did not sleep more than a few hours in 3-4 days and performed at this level above 25,000 feet. He was no doubt dehydrated and probably did not eat much. He climbed down with some rescued climbers and walked out from K2 base camp.

[http://www.pemba.com.np/The savior and the storm on K2.php](http://www.pemba.com.np/The%20savior%20and%20the%20storm%20on%20K2.php)

### nicld — 2014-03-18T03:13:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sherpa-in-action.10211/post-116103>

I LOVE watching shows on Everest. The little hair on my little toe would love to try that but then the rest of my body yells HELL NO. I know my limits.

We watched Everest: Beyond The Limit and was just amazed at the Sherpa's. They would not think twice about helping the climbers, they are machines.

### caroline — 2014-03-18T04:18:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sherpa-in-action.10211/post-116111>

When I was there we were ill prepared for the snow and unseasonably cold weather. Only a light jacket, no boots etc. I was doing C/T and didn't know it .....and thrived!

### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-18T15:33:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sherpa-in-action.10211/post-116197>

I actually mentioned the Sherpa's in the new podcast with Ameer to explain a tough concept.

### Shijin13 — 2014-03-18T15:57:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sherpa-in-action.10211/post-116200>

This is a great book on Summiting Everest. its quite chilling how very few of the people on this trek were not prepared. [http://bit.ly/In2thinAir](http://bit.ly/In2thinAir)

I've actually heard Edmund Hillary talk about his first ascent - and the amazing abilities of the Sherpas. I've also heard Krackauer speak as well... fascinating...

# Thread: Anxiety after dietary meltdown?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anxiety-after-dietary-meltdown.10213/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Julie5 — 2014-03-18T07:08:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anxiety-after-dietary-meltdown.10213/post-116128>

What a bad day I had a couple of days ago! I don't know what was going on. I have been doing so well the last couple of months even with the death of my father in law and guests who stayed with us for 7 weeks. Then they left, and BAM, I got hit with weak will and ate whatever I could find. I repented and the next day I got right back on the wagon. But there has been fallout from it. Primarily, anxiety. I don't have anxiety problems. Any idea what the connection is between a one day deviation and anxiety a few days later?

Also, it has been hard to get back to feeling satisfied with 2 meals a day. The cravings are back. Of course, I am out of ketosis. Ugh! It was NOT worth it!

### caroline — 2014-03-18T07:28:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anxiety-after-dietary-meltdown.10213/post-116133>

Hi Julie.....just jump back on the leptin reset .....with the BAB ...enough protein and it will knock out the cravings! don't worry about 2 meals - go for 3 meals if you need them. Lots of folks here have 3 meals a day.

You have been under so much stress ..... and I can't even imagine having guests for 7 weeks ...YIKES! I would have been a basket case - no question!

Up your water intake and get lots and lots of sunshine and grounding. Don't forget the three legged stool!

Cut yourself some slack - go for long walks and climb over some rocks - make yourself a mini movnat!

Make yourself a gratitude journal and hug your DH and family.......yoga? meditation?

Sometimes life happens ..... xo

I probably would have fallen off for way more than a day! you did good!

### Julie5 — 2014-03-18T07:46:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anxiety-after-dietary-meltdown.10213/post-116136>

THanks! I needed that!

# Thread: R Alpha Lipoic Acid
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/r-alpha-lipoic-acid.10214/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### persistence — 2014-03-18T07:08:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/r-alpha-lipoic-acid.10214/post-116129>

To my surprise there is no thread created to discuss R Alpha Lipoic Acid (R-ALA and ALA), so let's create one.

My questions on this supplement are:

1) At what level are we possibly going to see toxicity?

2) If you are taking this to improve insulin resistance, how far ahead of a meal should you take it?

3) How long does the chemical remain active and affect insulin resistance?

4) Is it important to always take it with some other supplement, specifically for the indication of insulin resistance? I have been taking with Creatine HCL (a more absorbable form of Creatine Monophosphate) on the thought that ALA will help creatine get into the cells.

5) By upregulating GLUT1 and GLUT4, is this chemical helping glucose to get stored as glycogen preferably over body fat? Or will storage to body fat be equally favored to glycogen?

My early experience with this supplement suggests it is extremely powerful as a way to regulate glucose levels. But I'm having tremendous problems getting the dosage correct and getting the timing correct.

I always start by reading studies on toxicity. I couldn't find any animal or human studies that showed any measurable toxicity, even at very very high doses. That's good. I seem to have a pretty wide runway here to experiment without putting myself at much risk.

I have been using up to a maximum about 1,800 mg per day, usually one 200 mg pill right before each meal, and up to two 200 mg ALA doses when glucose is over 130 and I am having any kind of starchy carb.

I have been seeing a pattern last few weeks where I lose control of glucose in the morning, and recover it in afternoon and at night. Typically I wake with fasting glucose around 80 to 95, which quickly rises with no food to 105. With "Big Ass Breakfast" just the protein alone can take me to glucose at 138. Then I take maybe 20 grams of carbs with MCT Oil and I top off 140 to 150. It's downhill for the rest of the day, and I am back to the 80s and 90s by evening.

The ALA is slow to have its effect in morning, but once it does take hold I start coming down rapidly. If I combine it with exercise I can start printing glucose numbers in the 60s mg/dL and that's too low. So this is a bit of a shotgun when I need a hunting rifle, and it can result in my going from 130 mg/dL to 70 mg/dL and sticking under 90 mg/dL for an extended time.

Has anyone seen any studies that show a methodology for taking ALA before/with meals, and what is the minimum required dose that achieves most of the benefit? I see a lot of people online recommending a single 200 mg dose per \*day\*, and that certainly has not had any noticeable impact on my glucose. Larger doses with meals alternate between slight and massive effects, and controlling the result is proving hard.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: What to eat the day before a blood draw?

# Thread: Beyond CRON
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/beyond-cron.10220/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Josh — 2014-03-18T19:07:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/beyond-cron.10220/post-116226>

> Dietary restriction (DR) famously extends lifespan and reduces fecundity across a diverse range of species. A prominent hypothesis suggests that these life-history responses evolved as a survival-enhancing strategy whereby resources are redirected from reproduction to somatic maintenance, enabling organisms to weather periods of resource scarcity. We argue that this hypothesis is inconsistent with recent evidence and at odds with the ecology of natural populations. We consider a wealth of molecular, medical, and evolutionary research, and conclude that the lifespan extension effect of DR is likely to be a laboratory artifact: in contrast with captivity, most animals living in natural environments may fail to achieve lifespan extension under DR. What, then, is the evolutionary significance of the suite of responses that extend lifespan in the laboratory? We suggest that these responses represent a highly conserved nutrient recycling mechanism that enables organisms to maximize immediate reproductive output under conditions of resource scarcity.

[http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/bies.201300165/abstract](http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/bies.201300165/abstract)

[http://www.foodnavigator.com/Scienc...-low-nutrient-diet-may-offer-anti-aging-clues](http://www.foodnavigator.com/Science-Nutrition/Dietary-restriction-Very-low-nutrient-diet-may-offer-anti-aging-clues)

### Josh — 2014-04-03T05:07:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/beyond-cron.10220/post-119552>

But they keep trying to sell CRON....

> Extreme calorie counting boosts lifespan in monkeys, according to new research.
>
> Until now, the rationale for following an ultra-low calorie diet to ward off ageing has been based on experiments in worms and mice.
>
> Studies reported in [Nature Communications](http://www.nature.com/ncomms/index.html) found primates also benefited from the regime.
>
> A nutritionist urged caution over severe calorie restriction in humans, saying more research was needed.
>
> Perhaps Benjamin Franklin was right when he said, 'To lengthen thy life lessen thy meals'”
>
> Dr Emma Williams British Nutrition Foundation
> Advocates of the Calorie Restriction (CR) diet claim that by severely restricting the number of calories they consume they will live longer, perhaps into their hundreds.
>
> They cite a wealth of scientific evidence dating back more than 75 years.
>
> Much of the research is based on experiments in animals such as mice and worms, with primate studies giving conflicting results.
>
> Now, a US team has published new evidence showing CR also shows benefits in primates.
>
> Less is more
>
> "CR works to delay ageing in primate species," Dr Rozalyn Anderson of the department of medicine at the University of Wisconsin-Madison, told BBC News. "Our study data is consistent with that."
>
> The study found CR boosted survival in a group of rhesus monkeys studied over the course of decades.

[http://www.bbc.com/news/health-26837211](http://www.bbc.com/news/health-26837211)

### Jack Kruse — 2014-04-21T04:41:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/beyond-cron.10220/post-122741>

A National Institute on Aging study published in Nature shows that more than 20 years of limiting calorie intake by 30 percent in a group of rhesus monkeys did not allow them to live longer than a control group of monkeys who were eating a normal, unrestricted diet. “I think the message is that a caloric restriction response, if it exists for monkeys and by implication humans, may occur only under the **narrowest conditions**,” says Steven N. Austad, a professor at University of Texas Health Science Center San Antonio, who wrote an accompanying commentary in Nature.

# Thread: Mystery of a Neolithic Switch from Seafood To Milk
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mystery-of-a-neolithic-switch-from-seafood-to-milk.10221/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-03-18T20:31:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mystery-of-a-neolithic-switch-from-seafood-to-milk.10221/post-116245>

This is published on a site called Bio-News, Texas, which I hadn't heard of before. It's a story that's been doing the rounds, but is here covered very fully.

A team from Bristol University think they can show that there was a switch from a high use of marine resources in the Mesolithic period to a dpendence on dairy products in the Neolithic in the British Isles, including "mainland Britain, the Scottish isles and the isles of Man, and Ireland":

> The Bristol team used a compound-specific carbon isotope technique they have developed to identify the actual fats preserved in the cooking pots, showing that dairy products dominated the menu right across Britain and Ireland as soon as cattle and sheep arrived.

The story of the markers they were looking for is quite interesting -- this is based on static isotope analysis and on the analysis of fat remains left on cooking pots. There's controversy about the interpretation of results. For example, there's an argument that the skeletons used may not have been representative.

On the cooking-pot residues:

> This is a highly sensitive means of investigating the significance of marine product processing in pottery. Although the characteristic long-chain polyunsaturated fatty acid (PUFA) distributions of fresh marine fats and oils are lost from degraded marine lipids owing to rapid oxidation and polymerization, their recent work has identified more stable marine biomarkers, including alkanoic acids and vicinal dihydroxy acids originating from degradation of poly- and monounsaturated fatty acids, respectively. Although these products may exist only at low concentrations, operating the gas chromatograph/mass spectrometer (GC/MS) in selected ion-monitoring mode greatly enhances sensitivity of the analyses and makes picogram per gram concentrations routinely detectable.

Here's the conclusion:

> Viewed together the study findings show that Early British hunters ate a diet rich in venison and wild boar as well as eating quantities of seafood, including seals and shellfish. With the introduction of domestic animals some 6,000 years ago they quickly gave up wild foods and fishing was largely abandoned with people adopting a new diet based around dairying.
>
> Dr. Cramp notes that: “Amazingly, it was another 4,000 years before seafood remains appeared in pots again, during the Iron Age, and it was only with the arrival of the Vikings that fish became a significant part of our diet.”
>
> Why people in the British Isles changed so abruptly from a seafood to farming diet remains a mystery.

[http://bionews-tx.com/news/2014/02/...ish-peoples-switched-seafood-milk-4000-years/](http://bionews-tx.com/news/2014/02/20/mystery-neolithic-british-peoples-switched-seafood-milk-4000-years/)

And here's the original paper:

[http://rspb.royalsocietypublishing.org/content/281/1780/20132372.full.pdf+html](http://rspb.royalsocietypublishing.org/content/281/1780/20132372.full.pdf+html)

### Josh — 2014-03-20T01:05:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mystery-of-a-neolithic-switch-from-seafood-to-milk.10221/post-116574>

Maybe that's why the vikings were able to come and make so much trouble....

### nonchalant — 2014-03-20T02:20:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mystery-of-a-neolithic-switch-from-seafood-to-milk.10221/post-116586>

I think that the local chieftains and shamans got a tribute on each head of livestock so they encouraged the locals to eat dairy.

### bionaut — 2014-03-19T00:18:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gettin-mah-labs-on.10223/post-116294>

I finally bit the bullet and got my labs. I wish I had done this sooner instead of guessing! Just so you have some context, I am about 50-70 lbs over weight, 30 yr old male also I am a shift worker and am in a high emf environment. I know I know that is hardly optimal... but that is life for a little while until I get that country home on the range. Was over weight as a child, in late teens I evened out a bit. I had a year of heavy metal contact on my hands from oil paints in college.

Here is what I see:

High LDH, warburg effect? Had a tickle in my chest a week ago. Lets hope it was indigestion.
Hydration looks good but low D probably means I have low mag too.
Potassium is solid
Reverse T3 high means cytokines up somewhere in the body, CRP is down so is IL-6 high?
Cortisol is likely high due to my environment.
Hypothyroid with no signs of hashi's yet.
SHBG is high, could this be from insulin?
Testosterone, DHEA Pregnenlone need to be supplemented

Lets make some lemonade out of these lemons.

I eat off and on epi paleo, and keto. Do CT occasionally and supplement with some mitochondrial RX supps. And drink RO water strictly.

What do you all see?

### cinnamon — 2014-03-20T04:22:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-posts.10224/post-116600>

I think it works by tagging the posts as "read". The "new posts" list is in two parts. First it shows all the posts that you have not "read" that were created since the last time you did something on the forum (could be reading or posting or even just refreshing the new post list). Then it shows posts that are older than that but which have not yet been tagged as "read" for your user id (posts that showed up the last time you clicked "new posts" but that you haven't yet got to looking at would be here) .

Now, what it means by "read"? My guess is that if you visited the page that the post is on, it gets tagged as read. Also, if you actually created the post. And there is also an option to "mark all posts as read". You would use this last one if you were away for a few days with no chance of getting caught up.

And yup, I don't think the thread you posted in will show up in "new posts" until there is a new post _after_ yours.

# Thread: Bun to Creatinine Ratio?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bun-to-creatinine-ratio.10225/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 31
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### persistence — 2014-03-19T01:52:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bun-to-creatinine-ratio.10225/post-116305>

I have seen references in several places to the importance of the BUN to Creatinine ratio. Can someone please provide some guidance on what numbers are considered high, normal, good, etc?

What does this ratio show us?

### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-19T06:10:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bun-to-creatinine-ratio.10225/post-116330>

It is the quick easy dirty way to asses your redox potential on your inner mitochondrial membrane. Ideally it should be below 7:1

Crow's Lugol's Solution 2%, but I could use another if you prefer it. I have always put iodine off because of my food intolerance issues, and I figured iodine might amplify immune response \*if\* I have a leaky gut (not diagnosed yet). But I think I should test using iodine now. In terms of dosing, is there any reason to take this more than once a day or is once in morning sufficient?

What you said about my brain and body not being in synch strikes a deep chord with me. I think there is some kind of disconnect there. At the same time, going low carb did put my body into some kind of real distress, and even in ketosis with high ketone levels my body never seems to really adapt. It's like my brain is not able to reset correctly in that new metabolic environment.

As one example of that, the other day I did a very moderate workout (30 minutes walk / light jog and later that day 30 minutes lifting four sets in gym). That night I lost four pounds of body weight (calibrated against two different scales). I never went to the bathroom, so that body weight must be just water loss through my breathing. That's two liters of water being lost in less than nine hours, just by breathing. This is reinforced by my having a sense of thirst, having somewhat labored and deep breathing, etc. Yet I go to the doctor with those symptoms and they tell me my bun/creatine levels say I am not dehydrated. As I increase the level of carbs in diet, these symptoms lessen. At this point I am going to start increasing carb levels just to establish how the body responds to that and how much resolution I can get on symptoms.

If I go to neurologist about this, I guess he is just going to look at me in a funny way and not really be able to address the issue?

### persistence — 2014-05-09T23:47:31+0300

I have done some brief experiments with iodine and - wow - I think you are onto something. It appears to have some very calming and neuroprotective effect for me.

Since about the age of 18, I have had food intolerances that have neurological side effects. I normally feel this as either a brain fog or something outright toxic/irritating to my brain that makes it impossible to sit down and focus. I literally sometimes want to stick my hand into my brain and rip out the toxins.

The more recent problem with low carb diet has manifested with additional symptoms, like the constant fatigue, dehydration, deep breathing, rapid water weight loss, etc. Since those things are apparently not being explained by my electrolyte panel or my BUN/Creatine ratios, now you have me wondering if this is really some kind of neurological condition around CSF and the blood brain barrier. Alternately, does the Iodine suppress some kind of virus or bacteria, and what I experience is just temporary stopping of toxins produced by the bacteria...?

Both the long-term food-related neurological toxicity and the more recent symptoms of dehydration, deep breathing, rapid water loss appear to almost immediately calm down when I take a few drops of Lugol's 2% Solution. But this calming effect only appears to last about eight hours, then the symptoms come back. So far no allergic or bad reactions to iodine.... I could drop the dose to one drop and take three times a day I guess, to spread the effect out over the day.

Based on this, maybe I need to see a neurologist?

### persistence — 2014-05-14T01:42:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bun-to-creatinine-ratio.10225/post-126779>

> [nonchalant said:](/goto/post?id=126729)
>
> I have taken a couple of mailorder urine iodine tests from Hakala Labs. IIRC, you need to stop supplementing 2 days before the test.
>
> As far as I know, no PCP ever orders a test for iodine.

So I did a little research and found one of the doctors in US - Dr Flechas - who builds an entire practice around iodine:

[http://cypress.he.net/~bigmacnc/drflechas/iodine.htm](http://cypress.he.net/~bigmacnc/drflechas/iodine.htm)

I think the other is Dr Brownstein, who has some great videos and books on why to not take certain types of prescription drugs.

Dr Flechas has a 24 hour urine collection for iodine that looks like a very good test. They get you off dietary iodine for 24 hours, then they challenge you with a single 50 mg iodine dose. They follow that with a 24 hour urine collection to see what part of that your body excretes. I suppose their theory is that if the body absorbs most of that 50 mg challenge you must be deficient. And I'm sure that must be a controversial premise by itself.

I'll also look at doing a one time iodine measurement when I am not supplementing.

### persistence — 2014-06-12T22:40:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bun-to-creatinine-ratio.10225/post-130677>

> [thomasdoud@gmail.com said:](/goto/post?id=130676)
>
> any recommendation on how often to check BUN/CR?

It's a standard part of any basic metabolic panel. I think Labcorp has it on the CHEM8 Basic Metabolic Panel. You get BUN and Creatinine values and calculate the ratio yourself.

Assuming you have bad values and are actively trying to treat this by some therapy, maybe check once a month?

### persistence — 2014-03-23T22:12:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/reverse-t3.10227/post-117452>

Thanks for the pointer to your test list!

I think the overall description of my problems is in my journal here:
[http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.ph...igh-glucose-high-lipid-particle-counts.10106/](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/uncontrolled-weight-loss-high-glucose-high-lipid-particle-counts.10106/)

However, the quick summary:

\- I was following a low carb diet to bring down prediabetic glucose numbers. This started a weight loss that I had problems controlling, and that weight loss left me extremely weak.

\- I continue to see prediabetic glucose numbers, typically high in morning and tapering off over the course of the day.

\- I have extremely high LDL particle counts (using NMR Lipoprofile test).

I have started to supplement with D-Ribose and this has helped me recover from exercise and regain energy quite a bit.

I am going through the Leptin Rx slowly and we will see how much that changes.

Having done a lot of reading, my best theory on what might be happening with me:

1) Failure of glucose metabolism = prediabetes. \*probably\* I am slightly insulin resistant, but this is yet to be proven.

2) Assuming I am insulin resistant, high insulin = high production of HMG CoA Reductase = high production of small, dense LDL particles

3) I may have some milder version of familial hypercholesterolemia, or alternately it may just be high levels of cholesterol I get from a Paleo diet, but either of those can cause LDL receptors that would normally clear LDL from the blood to downregulate. So the extra LDL I biosynthesize is not getting cleared from the body.

There is a famous researcher named Goldstein who has studied LDL receptors and Figure 3 in this summary from 1984 shows how the LDL receptors can be broken when you eat a high fat diet rich in cholesterol (which I certainly do):

[http://www.jlr.org/content/25/13/1450.full.pdf](http://www.jlr.org/content/25/13/1450.full.pdf)

It's not the cholesterol that causes high LDL!!! Rather, the cholesterol downregulates your LDL receptors. And if you have \*something else\* causing generation of large numbers of LDL particles, the body won't process them correctly and they hang around in your blood.

So - in the big picture - I need to find a way to get my insulin levels low, to stop overproduction of small, dense LDL. And I \*may\* also have some issue with needing to limit maximum cholesterol intake in diet.

My guess is if I could get glucose and insulin levels low, I would take out the prediabetes and probably make a big dent in the LDL cholesterol particle counts. Whatever LDL-P particles remain after "fixing" insulin I would probably need to address through diet.

### JanSz — 2014-03-23T23:46:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/reverse-t3.10227/post-117479>

It may be about resistant starch.

Not sure how resistant starch fits EpiPaleo-Rx.
EpiPaleo-Rx is about being without carbs. Starch is a carbohydrate.
By definition we (the people) do not consume (directly) resistant starch, just pass it thru.
But our intestinal bacteria have a field day with it.
With out it, we end up with less of intestinal bacteria.

Try cold boiled potatoes and rice.
Or better yet 4 tablespoons/day of unmodified Potato Starch.

[http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B004VLVCGU/ref=oh\_details\_o00\_s00\_i00?ie=UTF8&psc=1](http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B004VLVCGU/ref=oh_details_o00_s00_i00?ie=UTF8&psc=1)

\------------

they contain natural resistant starch
they make hydroxy-buthyrate
no cooking of nuts
increase bifido bacteria
also
include fermented meats, salami, prosciuto, ham

Likely variety of unmodified starches is long range way to go.
So likely nuts have their place. I will just have to figure out if natural (not heat treated) nuts are still available.

> Nuts---walnuts, almond, pistachio, pine nuts
> they contain natural resistant starch
> they make hydroxy-buthyrate
> no cooking of nuts
> increase bifido bacteria
> also
> include fermented meats, salami, prosciuto, ham
> \============================================
>
> After I listened to that webinar I realised that many nuts are heat treated.
>
> ..
>
> You may also try
> Butyrate (Calcium/Magnesium) 250 capsules (600mg)
> [http://www.bodybio.com/storeproduct361.aspx](http://www.bodybio.com/storeproduct361.aspx)
>
> //
>
> People eating 4 tablespoons/day of unmodified Potato Starch,
> report plummeting HgA1c.
>
> I will tell you latter if I am among those people.
> I have just started on unmodified starch and plan on adding cold potatoes and rice.
> Since I started following my version of EpiPaleo-Rx my fasting glucose went up significantly.
>
>
> //
> Right now it would be about reversing trend, unmodified potato starch is a single bullet type of help. We are usually more complicated than that.
> Likely variety of unmodified starches is long range way to go.
> So likely nuts have their place. I will just have to figure out if natural (not heat treated) nuts are still available.
>
>
>
> ////

I tried raw potato starch at night. It certainly improves sleep and increases stool size. I did NOT see lower glucose. In fact next day I would get higher glucose.

My reading suggests that potato starch would not just result in the good chemicals leading to ketone butyrate, but might also greatly increase the production of the neurotransmitter serotonin?

Didn't Jack say that too much serotonin produced in the gut will hinder new bone formation?
[http://jackkruse.com/osteoporosis-part-one/](http://jackkruse.com/osteoporosis-part-one/)

Definitely the potato starch had some powerful effect with me, but it's hard to say how much is good and how much becomes toxic.

### persistence — 2014-03-24T20:45:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/reverse-t3.10227/post-117651>

> [JanSz said:](/goto/post?id=117636)
>

>
> Load up on vit D, K2, magnessium and anything else you may think of that may improve your bones.
> Then,
> for a year go max with resistant starch.
> Evaluate if all that helps you or hurts you and where.

I'm popping so many pills, making so many lifestyle changes, etc. No one would accuse me of paralysis.

But at the same time I usually have a method for deciding if something is safe to take. The first thing I do is look for toxicity. What does the literature say about a dose where something is dangerous? Then I establish the dose at which there is a benefit. The things that are a "no brainer" to take are ones where there is a benefit at a low dose, and the toxicity is at very high doses.

The items I tend to avoid completely are the ones where the benefit and the toxicity live within a hair width of each other. I won't name anything specific, but as an example if I read somewhere that the average diet gets 2 mg/day of X and there is benefit for supplementing up to 3 mg/day of X, but other studies say that you go bonking crazy and develop neuromuscular diseases at 5 mg/day, then probably I am not going to do that experiment. The tolerance for error there is too small.

Potato starch is for me a case somewhere in between. I have problems defining the toxicity, or quantifying where it occurs. I would like to go slow and read more. And for now potato starch is not stopping me from going anywhere I need to go, in the short term.

> You said that you follow **Paul Jaminet diet** at least good part of it.
> [http://perfecthealthdiet.com/the-diet/](http://perfecthealthdiet.com/the-diet/)
>
> There is a one pound a day of starches there.
>
> Make sure that you stick to their recommendations in regard to safe starches
> potatoes (cold)
> rice (cold)
> then mind dr Jack emphasis on
> nuts (not heat treated)
> fermented meats, salami, prosciuto, ham
> ....

I have many food intolerances related to a wheat intolerance, so my starchy carbs for now are limited to tubers (yams, taro, etc). Yes, I am definitely eating from the "super safe" starch list.

I have been playing with 40 to 120 grams of starchy carbs per day. From the standpoint of prediabetes, I suspect that going ketogenic would be a very good direction. But so far when I get too low on carbs I drain glycogen and get weak. My endo wants me to be on 120 grams/day of carbs. Finding D-Ribose has allowed me to recover faster from exercise, so this gives me a little support to continue experimenting on the low end of carbs.

### Josh — 2014-03-20T06:37:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/todays-webinar-with-dr-patel-and-dr-kruse.10230/post-116613>

Towards calrification of the Gerson confusion:

1.  Doctor Gerson was a brilliant and iconoclastic doctor along the lines of Dr. Kruse.
2.  He fought the good fight until the end and may have been poisoned for his efforts.
3.  To understand the protocol, one must actually read his books, including his biography by his grandson.
4.  The "protocol" is actually quite similar conceptually and in implementation to much of what we are doing with the notable exception of a higher carb vegetarian diet.
5.  Understand that Dr. Gerson never did a coffee enema himself. He ate raw beef once per week and fish twice per week. He lived to 79 when he was mysteriously poisoned with arsenic. Simultaneously, his finished book was stolen from his desk. He recovered enough to rewrite the book and die a year later.
6.  The famous coffee enemas were an accident of WWI. The Germans had no analgesics at the end of the war. The only pain relief after surgery was from enemas. There was also a water shortage, so the nurses did not want to waste the leftover staff coffee. They put it in the enemas and noticed that it improved the analgesic effect. I have confirmed this in an acute episode of back pain. It is more work, but the end result is better than a few days on opiates and NSAIDS. It appears that there are references to coffee being used in this way much earlier in history. I am not really interested in that. Ayurveda and Unani (ancient Greek Medicine still taught and practiced in India) use enemas with herbs and oils extensively. Our distaste for the other end of the digestive tract does not diminish its effectiveness as a delivery system for a variety of compounds. There is a huge venous system going from the colon to the liver- it does not pass go or the stomach/small intestine and delivers stuff quickly in quantity to the liver....seems helpful with small children and the elderly from experience....
7.  Gerson decided to try them when he started treating cancer which happened after he became very successful treating TB with juices and nutrients. Someone said could we try this on my cancer? The rest became history.
8.  Much of "Functional Medicine" can be traced to Gerson and others of his generation.
9.  The Gerson therapy and its evolution since his death with doctors Kelly and Gozalez is more than juices and enemas. All of the pieces have some efficacy and the synergy is greater than the sum of the parts.
10.  Reading Gerson's " 50 Cases" , his daughter's "The Gerson Miracle"and his biography gave me clues into the clinical choices made and refinements routinely applied to the full protocol. Listening to Dr. Gonzalez talking with Dr. Mercola offers further insights into the evolution of the concepts and therapeutics. At that time, Dr. Gonzalez said he did one every morning to remediate the effects of living and working in NYC. He said he used the time to read journal articles. I have learned most of the Functional Medicine I know under similar circumstances....
11.  Consider Gerson's first challenge was being a Jew in Nazi Germany. He was the only Jew not removed from a train going to Vienna from Berlin. He was on the way to a conference and the soldiers asked him where he was going, but never asked if he was Jewish. It was early and the other Jews answered "yes". He moved through Europe to the UK and then to the US just before the start of the war.
12.  Gerson did use extensive lab testing so there is data to satisfy the skeptics...
13.  He identified low intracellular Potassium as the cause of the loss of metabolic function and the cause of disease. He may have understood Ling, but I do not know.

14.  I will say he had a pair.....

I have done a few thousand coffee enemas and they were mostly helpful based on my criteria. Of course in the absence of extensive lab testing, cynics could say something to the effect of "what, you are still breathing, you call that a result?" I have used them with many clients with good effect. Whether there are better and or different ways to skin the cat, I will not get into that. I will say, it is worth knowing whether one has sulfur and methylation issues as it makes demands on these systems. It would be helpful if our great leader would elaborate a little conceptually as to his reservations and concerns? I do not doubt that most of the claims are misunderstandings, yet I feel there is real efficacy in the method when used appropriately. Especially if the system is sub-optimal.

[http://doctordonato.com/wp-content/uploads/pdfs/Biological-Basis-Gerson-Therapy.pdf](http://doctordonato.com/wp-content/uploads/pdfs/Biological-Basis-Gerson-Therapy.pdf)

### caroline — 2014-03-20T07:21:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/todays-webinar-with-dr-patel-and-dr-kruse.10230/post-116616>

Thanks Josh..........

### Tanya — 2014-03-20T18:51:18+0200

> [Josh said:](/goto/post?id=116613)
>
> Towards calrification of the Gerson confusion:
>
>
> 1.  Doctor Gerson was a brilliant and iconoclastic doctor along the lines of Dr. Kruse.
> 2.  He fought the good fight until the end and may have been poisoned for his efforts.
> 3.  To understand the protocol, one must actually read his books, including his biography by his grandson.

Josh, we are so lucky to have you here - my hat’s off to you for everything that you post on this forum.

What I have to say about the Gerson therapy is not meant to be any kind of criticism at all. I just want to share my experience with it. There are many devoted followers of that protocol and stories after stories of people whose lives were saved by it.

That was the reason why I also tried it at one time in my struggle to get better. Unfortunately, it did not happen for me and I was actually getting worse - having a huge difficulty breathing and unbearable headaches among other things. In addition to the initial dx of CFS and FM, I got a few more added to it....

Even then, I was still committed to the protocol, and the only reason that I stopped it was because my test showed that I had a significant amount of arsenic in me, there was none in the water I was drinking, but it was known that apricot kernels that I was consuming as part of the diet would contain. I had another test done and sure enough was shocked to find out that there was cyanide in me as well, also thanks to apricot kernels. I am glad that it was caught in time..

### Josh — 2014-03-20T19:50:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/todays-webinar-with-dr-patel-and-dr-kruse.10230/post-116735>

As I mentioned, to really "understand" the protocol, one needs to look at its evolution under Gerson and its continuing evolution since then. AN integral part of the "Gerson Protocol" was Dr. Gerson himself , the context and his clinical skills....As we have reviewed many times, there is still some "art" left in "medicine"......What is being promoted now as "Gerson" may be true to some of the principals, but more typically is some version of Functional Medicine packaged as "Gerson" which I consider a disservice to all involved. 50 years after Dr. Kruse's death, does he want people to be practicing the "Kruse Protocol"? Will it bear any resemblance to what we are doing and be relevant to the context?

### Hope — 2014-03-21T01:09:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/todays-webinar-with-dr-patel-and-dr-kruse.10230/post-116814>

> [Josh said:](/goto/post?id=116613)
>
> Towards calrification of the Gerson confusion:
>
>
> 1.  Doctor Gerson was a brilliant and iconoclastic doctor along the lines of Dr. Kruse.
> 2.  He fought the good fight until the end and may have been poisoned for his efforts.
> 3.  To understand the protocol, one must actually read his books, including his biography by his grandson.
> 4.  The "protocol" is actually quite similar conceptually and in implementation to much of what we are doing with the notable exception of a higher carb vegetarian diet.
> 5.  Understand that Dr. Gerson never did a coffee enema himself. He ate raw beef once per week and fish twice per week. He lived to 79 when he was mysteriously poisoned with arsenic. Simultaneously, his finished book was stolen from his desk. He recovered enough to rewrite the book and die a year later.
> 6.  The famous coffee enemas were an accident of WWI. The Germans had no analgesics at the end of the war. The only pain relief after surgery was from enemas. There was also a water shortage, so the nurses did not want to waste the leftover staff coffee. They put it in the enemas and noticed that it improved the analgesic effect. I have confirmed this in an acute episode of back pain. It is more work, but the end result is better than a few days on opiates and NSAIDS. It appears that there are references to coffee being used in this way much earlier in history. I am not really interested in that. Ayurveda and Unani (ancient Greek Medicine still taught and practiced in India) use enemas with herbs and oils extensively. Our distaste for the other end of the digestive tract does not diminish its effectiveness as a delivery system for a variety of compounds. There is a huge venous system going from the colon to the liver- it does not pass go or the stomach/small intestine and delivers stuff quickly in quantity to the liver....seems helpful with small children and the elderly from experience....
> 7.  Gerson decided to try them when he started treating cancer which happened after he became very successful treating TB with juices and nutrients. Someone said could we try this on
>
>
> [http://doctordonato.com/wp-content/uploads/pdfs/Biological-Basis-Gerson-Therapy.pdf](http://doctordonato.com/wp-content/uploads/pdfs/Biological-Basis-Gerson-Therapy.pdf)

Thanks Josh, indeed, for sharing this. I know Dr Nick Gonzalez, a prominent cancer fighter, recommends enemas as well, in addition to many other alternative practitioners or MDs,...

I have no doubt it works for some people....and for some it doesn't bring great results. Just like everything else in life. I also wonder why people often look to one magic bullet to be so important....when often many things are part of a healing process.

### Josh — 2014-03-21T02:27:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/todays-webinar-with-dr-patel-and-dr-kruse.10230/post-116837>

As I mentioned earlier, "Gerson Therapy" is being marketed which may or may not be close to what Dr. Gerson actually practiced in the first half of the 20th century. One huge difference is that Gerson used 16oz of fresh raw calf's liver juice daily in his regimen and last time I checked there are no safe sources. It is hard to replace the good raw liver part and it is not readily availble anymore in a safe form. Oysters would probably be a better substitute. Of course there was no EMF then and we have no idea of the nutrient density and relative toxicity of the vegetables they used. At its essence it is a "hyperalimentation" program, sound familiar...eat a lot of electrons/nutrient dense food and drink a lot of fresh vegetable juice-best source of highly structured water....low or no salt...lots of K+ in juices and they added a K+ salt mix to everyone's regimen as well. Lots of Iodine and Thyroid Hormone....beginning to sound familiar...time outside in the sun a la the old German/Swiss TB sanitarium model...read "The Magic Mountain" by Thomas Mann for an intimate portrait of that...our boy Hans and the whistling women....

So I have no idea what your cousin is doing and whether it bears any resemblance to what was done in the 50's. The principles are all the same so if you have access to the details, it should be pretty easy to assess the strengths and weaknesses. I wish him the best.

### Josh — 2014-03-21T19:09:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/todays-webinar-with-dr-patel-and-dr-kruse.10230/post-116966>

> [Tanya said:](/goto/post?id=116959)
>
> I was just hoping for a quick answer from you regarding the 5 items I listed, because I doubt the possibility of reading this book in my near future. It's ok, though, Josh - that protocol is behind me now, anyway and I am hard at work reading all Jack's writings, including today's one.....

Drinking 13 juices a day: _if they are the right juices from quality produce, okay. Notice they stick mostly to high water content greens, not high sugar content stuff like carrots. Dilute green juices are basically highly structured water if they are uncontaminated._
No water drinking allowed: _this was probably because they did not understand the different types of water and Ling's work._
No animal protein, used potatoes as a main source of protein. _Serious mistake in my experience and understanding. Notice the Liver Juice in the original protocol after Gerson's experiences. He ate meat himself. Practically, if someone cannot digest food for all of the reasons that happens, concentrated animal protein can be rough, especially during rapid detoxification._
No seafood: _he ate seafood and the reasons for limiting animal protein is a misunderstanding of the role of electrons and the density of electrons in food and physiology. Practically, if someone cannot digest food for all of the reasons that happens, concentrated animal protein can be rough, especially during rapid detoxification._
No fats at all: _I thought they used a lot of Flax Oil for the Omega 3's, ultimately a misunderstanding based on Dr. Kruse's explanation of fats._

Dr. Gonzalez covers many of these and discusses how he and Dr. Kelly sought to resolve many of these issues and make the therapy more of a conceptual exercise with improved individuation for N=1. Kelly healed himself from pancreatic cancer with his early efforts.

### Tanya — 2014-03-21T21:49:29+0200

> [Josh said:](/goto/post?id=116966)
>
> Drinking 13 juices a day: _if they are the right juices from quality produce, okay. Notice they stick mostly to high water content greens, not high sugar content stuff like carrots. Dilute green juices are basically highly structured water if they are uncontaminated._
> No water drinking allowed: _this was probably because they did not understand the different types of water and Ling's work._
> No animal protein, used potatoes as a main source of protein. _Serious mistake in my experience and understanding. Notice the Liver Juice in the original protocol after Gerson's experiences. He ate meat himself. Practically, if someone cannot digest food for all of the reasons that happens, concentrated animal protein can be rough, especially during rapid detoxification._
> No seafood: _he ate seafood and the reasons for limiting animal protein is a misunderstanding of the role of electrons and the density of electrons in food and physiology. Practically, if someone cannot digest food for all of the reasons that happens, concentrated animal protein can be rough, especially during rapid detoxification._
> No fats at all: _I thought they used a lot of Flax Oil for the Omega 3's, ultimately a misunderstanding based on Dr. Kruse's explanation of fats._
>
> Dr. Gonzalez covers many of these and discusses how he and Dr. Kelly sought to resolve many of these issues and make the therapy more of a conceptual exercise with improved individuation for N=1. Kelly healed himself from pancreatic cancer with his early efforts.

Thank you so much, Josh for your analysis of this.  Yes, you are right about the flax oil - I completely forgot that that was my only fat source then... There was no liver juice in my list, but if it would have been, I am not so sure of my compliance with it...lol but maybe if I did - my hemoglobin and hematocrit would not have been so low... No regrets here. I was just trying to see if what I was given was real McCoy or not. Thanks again.

### Hope — 2014-03-22T00:31:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/todays-webinar-with-dr-patel-and-dr-kruse.10230/post-117027>

being outside in the cold air even during the night has been a long, long therapy in Europe and Germany......hospitals in certain places, like in mountains or away from cities even in WW one and two recommended this....especially for people with TB and other issues. So amazing. Of course this is before the big bucks Pharma came along...which has been really bombing the world since around WW two.
