### GGinPG — 2014-03-22T18:47:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ggs-journal.10271/post-117190>

Sooperb - I take Betaine before every meal, I have since my doctor switch about a year ago. He had me take a stomach acid test (the burping test), and as I can drink about a gallon of liquid before maybe burping...I went with it. He said specifically to take extra with the iron supplements and if I've eaten anything around that time, it's usually a small handful of nuts to hold me over until dinner. I did do some moderate research, but nothing came up like your link. Thank you - I will read more and possibly adjust.

Thanks for directing me once again, Dr. Kruse. I'll do just that.

Edited to add: Gracelind and Sooperb mentioned awakening with heart pounding and anxiety. You know, it doesn't happen like that. I'm just instantly awake. Like turning a light off or on. One minute I'm asleep and the next I'm awake. My heart isn't pounding, really. The anxiety comes after I've been unable to fall right back asleep. The most noticeable thing about the situation is (1) the consisent timing and (2) the fact that I'm hot and/or in a sweat. I also notice that I cannot usually find a comfortable position for long. That's where the thrashing comes in and I'm sure my heart rate goes up then!

Sooperb - my house was built in 1978 and everything was already grounded when we moved in 17 years ago. I don't know any more than that because what I understand about electricity, apparently, could fit on the head of a pin

### sooperb — 2014-03-22T19:26:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ggs-journal.10271/post-117201>

Same age roughly as my present abode so I would think it was built to certain standards which would include proper domestic earthing.

I'm of the mind that once something you do is recurring, has it become inbuilt like a habit? Consistently waking up at the same time is like having an internal alarm, it starts to go off. How to reset it is the puzzle. One tip I read is to switch off your brain by thinking in pictures rather than words, apparently this is effective because conjuring up a picture uses a different part of your brain. Maybe thinking creates a chain of thoughts and that's when your brain starts to whirr, pictures perhaps aren't connected to thinking and you can be an observer rather than a thinker. I use it to get off to sleep, I last about 5 minutes lol.

I don't know what to suggest about your internal thermostat lol, I just throw the covers right off and retrieve them when I'm too cold, which may be several times a night depending on how wakeful I am.

### GGinPG — 2014-03-22T21:20:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ggs-journal.10271/post-117224>

"I don't know what to suggest about your internal thermostat lol, I just throw the covers right off and retrieve them when I'm too cold, which may be several times a night depending on how wakeful I am."

Yeah, that's my history, too. But since this very specific timing thing started happening (I'm going to say it's been at least a year, possibly even 18 months?) I don't USUALLY wake up before 3 unless there's a sudden out-of-place noise like the cat yakking (which, if you have a cat, you know what an event that can be and they seem to love doing it in the middle of the night) and so I don't usually play on-off with the covers until after 3. And then I'll usually push them off until I feel cooler and then pull them back up one by one, starting with just the sheet. But I'd actually rather go back to doing what you're currently doing. It wasn't nearly as devastating to my energy levels the next day.

I'll try the picture thing. I would swear I'm already doing that (without knowing I should, if you know what I mean), but I guess I'm really going to need to pay attention to see if I truly am.

I love your signature - "Sex - not very often". I'd really love to pretend I don't understand what you mean by that, but sadly...

GG

### sooperb — 2014-03-23T00:11:24+0200

If you're benefiting from your earthing, isn't that suggestive that the sheet is earthed and working? I seem to recall something about using an actual alarm to break the "habit" of frequently waking at the same time but I can't remember the details.

I do have a cat, two in fact. One is a Maine Coon and very quiet, the other is a Bengal and very vocal. He doesn't go quiet until he gets what he wants, thankfully he's not noisy at night but if I'm not up when he decides he wants his chicken drumstick, he yowls outside the bedroom door.

### GGinPG — 2014-03-23T00:59:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ggs-journal.10271/post-117274>

Caroline - Hubby and I have slept (mostly) in different beds for nearly 2 years now. It had to happen - but I hate it. He can fall asleep at the drop of a hat, but I sure did keep him up through the night.

According to my tester, I'm grounded. I also know because my natural coloring is what my mother likes to jokingly call "death warmed over" but my cheeks are rosy pink after spending the night on the Earthing sheet. I actually look healthy even if I don't feel it most of the time. So I know my circulation has improved by it. Also, I had terrible restless legs from time to time. Magnesium helped somewhat, but when I began Earthing it stopped.

Haven't tried a gratitude journal; I've heard of it, never thought of it for sleep purposes. Never tried a sleep mat, but I'll try (almost) anything if there's a chance it'll work

As far as worrying goes, well, that's certainly true. But I tend to do that the morning after when I think about all the damage I'm doing.

sooperb - I'll see if I can find anything on my own about using an alarm to break the "habit". It doesn't sound like too much fun, but it can't be much worse than what's already going on, right?

Tonight we're going to shut the breaker to the bedroom off and see what that does. I know I don't know a whole lot about EMF yet, but it's something easy enough to try.

For what it's worth, I believe I inherited my poor sleep to some degree. My mother is nearly exactly like me and her father (who was an alcoholic, so it's a bit of chicken-and-egg...) was known to sit up all night (drinking, yes) and go to bed close to daybreak. Every night. My mother has no known health issues though (at 64) and my grandfather lived to 84 despite very heavy drinking and smoking, terrible sleep habits and a 40-year career working with nuclear materials. He died of prostate cancer although I think he lost his will to live when my grandmother died three years earlier.

### GGinPG — 2014-03-28T18:48:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ggs-journal.10271/post-118520>

Ten days in. I continue to read, read, read, although not so much online as I'm trying to limit my exposure to those nasty things coming out of my laptop. Just received two of Dr. Kruse's recommended titles, Cross Currents and Zapped so this may be my last blog post ever. (Kidding.) (Sort of.) Also just received a fresh supply of orange light bulbs so that I can have low blue light in all my living spaces at night. Planning on getting filters for TV, monitors, etc.

What I have been doing faithfully: 50 grams protein in the morning (60 minutes after rising and taking my thyroid meds.) This is becoming tougher, not easier. I'm thoroughly sick of my breakfast before I'm halfway done. Please tell me this gets easier? I start with a can of sardines, this gets me 20+ grams of protein right off the bat, and if I'm unable to finish my plate, I figure I've gotten the healthiest part of breakfast down. Then I have either leftover protein from dinner the night before, or some eggs, plus a veggie of some sort and a small amount of carbs (sweet potato cubes sauteed in duck fat, lard or beef fat - also helps to increase the fat grams). Roughly every other meal is fish or seafood (in addition to the sardines at breakfast), but I'm finding that at breakfast virtually nothing tastes good to me. For instance, I love scallops. Had some for breakfast and had to choke them down. I'm not complaining, I know I am doing my best, but am wondering if this is typical. And short term. Strangely, I am not full after breakfast, just sick of it. After breakfast I have a cup of DECAF with a tablespoon of coconut oil. The coffee is new. I'm only drinking it to get the coconut oil down. I can't stand the taste of it in food and I can't take it plain, so I figure it's a solution to getting it down. By noon, I'm a little hungry.

"Lunch" is a cup of bone broth with a tablespoon of grassfed gelatin added and a small handful of soaked and dehydrated macadamia nuts. I'm already spending a lot of time in the kitchen with breakfast and dinner so I don't want to have to prepare anything. This is a reasonably healthful way to get me through until dinner. I realize with autoimmune issues I should ditch the macadamias and I will; right now it's just a solution until I can stock up on better choices (I do my food shopping at the beginning of the month). Since having gone Paleo, lunch hasn't really been much of a meal for me anyway.

Dinner between 4 and 4:30. Smaller than breakfast, heavy on protein and veggies. Every other meal has seafood. By dinner time I am usually fairly hungry but I don't need to eat much to feel satisfied. I may have a piece of fruit afterwards, but I haven't "needed" it (I've always had a sweet tooth).

I walk away from my computer and the TV no later than 7 pm (it is still daylight here) and take a cool epsom salt bath (3X/wk) or a cool shower. I know this isn't the true CT protocol, but I'm hoping to work my way up to that. If I'm a little active (say warm from washing the dishes in hot water) prior to my bath/shower it's much easier to tolerate the temperatures, which are roughly that of the ambient air, right now it's still cool enough for the furnace which I have set to 65. I was already taking an epsom salt bath 2X/week (in warm water), and I feel better afterward, so I added the third. I can't do it every night -- too expensive. After bathing, I put on my blue light blocking goggles, do a few light evening chores (15 minutes) and then go sit in orange light to read away from television and computers. I had been on a schedule of in bed by 10, lights out 10:30, but I'm not making it that long. Sometimes lights out at 9:30 now. This is fine with me, but my husband resents the heck out of it as we have a very limited amount of time to spend together (he works until fairly late in the evening most days). We are working on it - but there's some added stress because of it and I try to avoid stress at all costs.

My sleep has been consistent this past week. I'm still falling asleep fairly easily, but waking at roughly 3 am every night. Thanks to a link shared by sooperb, I believe I at least know why it's happening (liver, estrogen), but nothing I've changed these last 10 days has reversed the trend. My husband has been cutting off the power to my bedroom each night (he sleeps in another room) and turning off the WiFi, and I think that's been helping in that I am calmer overall when I wake. But the nights he's forgotten to turn the WiFi off weren't noticeably worse.

I have noticed that I'm queasy in the evenings after dinner and also in the morning when I rise. I'm definitely hungry when I get up, although that quickly turns to revulsion once I've had a few bites of food (no matter what that food is). I know I have to work on getting more breakfast down. Most days I do manage to finish most of it, but it's taking increasingly longer to get it down. This morning it took me an hour and twenty minutes to eat roughly 2/3 of my breakfast.

Onward and upward!

### sooperb — 2014-03-28T19:32:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ggs-journal.10271/post-118533>

The thing that strikes me is that if you don't like or enjoy what you're eating you're going to give up sooner or later. You have to eat food you like and enjoy, if you don't you're making it really tough on yourself. I'd suggest you vary it so that at least some times you are eating without wanting to barf. Eggs can be eaten in lots of ways, are pretty cheap and a reasonable source of protein, you could add avocado, smoked salmon or I guess even a little cheese. If you don't like CO, use butter  Poached eggs over wilted spinach if it's available. Ring the changes and make sure to include what you like rather than food that you probably wouldn't eat. I would have said at one time that I couldn't possibly eat "xxx" for breakfast ever, but I have changed substantially in that respect and now I wouldn't rule out hamburger or even steak lol.

I'm impressed with your dedication and determination, especially in the face of opposition from your husband, that's a big sticking point for me, he's not enthusiastic at all.

### GGinPG — 2014-03-28T21:26:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ggs-journal.10271/post-118543>

sooperb - Other than the sardines I do try to vary what I eat for breakfast. You mention eggs. I LOVE eggs - have always been a huge egg girl. At breakfast even eggs are hard to get down. Eating this early (7-7:30) is really new for me as is eating so much protein at one time. I'm just hoping this is just a hurdle to get over and the road to come is smoother sailing.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-29T01:29:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ggs-journal.10271/post-118581>

"The thing that strikes me is that if you don't like or enjoy what you're eating you're going to give up sooner or later. You have to eat food you like and enjoy, if you don't you're making it really tough on yourself."

I don't buy this at all. So if you like booze you should not give it up if it hurts you? Opiates? Hookers with AIDS?

Here is the rub........if something causes inflammation in your body, regardless of your wants or desires, when it comes to wellness you need to let good choices dominant over your wants.

Will it be easy........no. Nothing life worth obtaining comes easy.

### Inger — 2014-03-29T07:54:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ggs-journal.10271/post-118625>

I think Jack is right. I eat stuff I do not enjoy too much... and you get used to it. Taste changes. So in the beginning it might taste bad and then it tastes way better after a while - especially if our body likes it and starts to want it! I think we need to listen to our body's needs not our taste buds too much because they are manipulated through years of eating artificial foods.

I do give my self some threats once in a while... but I take care it is very clean, natural stuff like 100% organic chocolate. Or enjoy taste boosters like raw onion slices + ACV and EVOO on my chevice maybe a tiny tiny amount of raw wild honey in my egg yolk foam..
After eating very simple for a while you will enjoy one spoonful of fermented cabbage like it is candy

Taste is a weird thing. I think it also helps to tell oneself - yum! I like this! Eventually you will  (ok fish head smoothie I do not like yet but who knows when is that day! For such cases we can just hold our nose lol)

### caroline — 2014-03-29T08:34:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ggs-journal.10271/post-118627>

I Love what you say here Inger........I started out with the Leptin reset and after years of feeling like a failure, I was pretty darn sure that Dr. K. had it right and it was worth it to give it my best shot and commit 100%. Why would I care if someone else didn't like what I ate? .... not their life ..... mine!!!\*

my health, my life, my journey .......I am responsible to myself and for myself. My life has been hard in lots of ways and so I have always known that I alone need to make it happen if I want it.

### sooperb — 2014-03-30T12:53:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ggs-journal.10271/post-118808>

" think Jack is right. I eat stuff I do not enjoy too much... and you get used to it."

But GG isn't liking it! That was my point, there are a wide variety of optimal foods to choose from, you don't have to force down things you don't like. I agree though Inger, you can change your taste buds and if you've spent years of eating mass produced pap then they are probably just dying for a day out out on real food and real flavours. I've never been a total junk food eater, I was taught to cook from an early age and for years cooked all my meals but yes, convenience food started to sneak in via marketing and being time poor when working but it was never the mainstay of my diet. There are still food items that I really don't like, anchovies for example, but it have tried and really like raw sauerkraut, that was a first and I tried raw oysters a couple weeks ago, I wasn't impressed by them though so I think I'd have to cook them if I was to eat more of them. Hubby loves them, and anchovies, we're all different.

"I don't buy this at all. So if you like booze you should not give it up if it hurts you? Opiates? Hookers with AIDS?"

I realise good things don't come easily and of course we have to accept that our choices should be informed and that we should be prepared to dump what isn't right for us that isn't the same as choosing to eat what we enjoy (if it's right) and avoiding stuff that causes the gag reflex to operate. It's got to be about balance too, we are trying to balance our systems to achieve optimal are we not?

### GGinPG — 2014-03-31T16:18:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ggs-journal.10271/post-119053>

Actually, I see a lot of valid points on all sides, here. I think what's going on with me right now (it's still very early in the process, which I expect to be a long one) is more an issue of timing and quantiy, than about a reaction to a specific food. I'm having problems getting ANYTHING down, even foods I've loved all of my life. So yes, while it's true that my body isn't used to scallops or sardines for breakfast I think my mind and stomach are rebelling because I'm forcing myself into a new habit after years of waiting several hours after rising to eat a "gentler" breakfast. Yes, I have been Paleo for several years so my breakfast (and lunch and dinner) choices were already miles better than a typical SAD meal. Plus, I've never eaten so much protein in one meal before in my life (let alone quite so much fat). I have confidence that this food revulsion will correct itself (I have to hold to that hope or else I wouldn't be subjecting myself to this much misery!); I only questioned whether it was indeed, temporary. In truth, even in the few days since I posted the update, the situation has worsened. Today, about 1/3 of the way through breakfast I had to get up from the table - the nausea hit me like a lightning bolt. I waited about 10 minutes for it to subside and then (slowly) finished as much as I could. Now I have to mention that I had leftover slow-cooked pastured pork shoulder on my plate (about 6 ounces, which is a lot) and that I am almost always a little queasy some time after eating pork shoulder or Boston Butt (I think it's because they're such fatty cuts, even though I remove as much of that fat as possible). Also, I had already had my sardines. So, it's entirely possible that my extreme reaction today was because of a specific food, but I've never before reacted to the pork while I'm eating it. So I guess what I'm saying is that I fully accept that necessary change can and will be painful; I'd just like to know that I don't have months of this pain ahead of me, that it will eventually become easier.

And while I'm on that topic, I will mention that my sleep, despite all of my positive changes so far (which are, I must admit, still very limited), is right back where I started - horrible. No need to rehash the whens and whys and hows here, that's all documented in earlier posts. What's important at this point is that I've learned that my sleep can be improved through small changes, but ultimately, until I fix THE thing (if it is indeed just ONE thing) that is causing it, I will always have this problem. sooperb's article pointed my issue originating in the liver - too much estrogen in my system? This is another post entirely, but suffice it to say that with what I know about my hormone history, this rings very true. So I know that until my hormones are in proper balance, I will not have proper sleep. Of course there's more to it than that, but at the same time it is a basic truth. Simple. But not so simple to achieve and not without (probably) going through some "have to get worse before I get better" changes - such as what I'm going through with breakfast.

So I will continue to do what I'm doing for as long as I can until something "clicks", and if it never does then I'll need to consider trying something else. In the meantime, I'll keep reading, reading, reading to find even more connections and possible avenues of approach.

Thanks everybody for your thoughts!

GG

### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-31T17:19:59+0300

### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-31T17:37:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ggs-journal.10271/post-119077>

If you feel fatigued consider dehydration as a top issue. Few do.

You should never rely on thirst to dictate water consumption, because it lags the real effect. Calculating your [total body water deficit](http://www.ccmtutorials.com/problems/explore/name/hypernatremia.htm) is another way to try to measure how badly your engine is working. We covered this in the [April 2013 webinar](http://www.jackkruse.com/april-webinar-2013/). By the time thirst kicks in your serum osmolarity is already impaired. Dehydration is a major cause of day time fatigue as well, and dehydration slows your metabolism by 2-3%. As time goes on this imbalance can steepen dramatically in a person in an altered environment. In fact, just a 2% drop in total body water can cause neurologic changes to show up. How do I know this? I am a neurosurgeon, and we see these swings all the time in trauma cases and and brain tumors that are associated with syndromes called SIADH, cerebral salt wasting syndrome, and diabetes insipidus. Now think about what I mentioned in the [April 2013 webinar](http://www.jackkruse.com/april-webinar-2013/) for our members.

### GGinPG — 2014-04-11T21:49:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ggs-journal.10271/post-121026>

A little over 3 weeks in and....status quo. There's very little difference (yet!) in how I feel day to day and my routine (posted above) is pretty darn consistent. I'm still having some trouble with the BAB, but (!) I've been grounding myself while I eat (Earthing mat) and that's been helping. There still comes a point where I know I've had enough and I put my fork down (it's not really a feeling of fullness, just a strong signal to stop), but the aversion to all foods at breakfast is all but gone. I am still leaving food on my plate regularly, but I get most of it down. I've also upped my seafood intake, I'm having seafood two out of every three meals instead of every other meal. I can't say that I'm craving seafood, but I will say that I have a lot less interest in beef, pork and chicken. Could be a moon phase thing  or a sign that something's about to shift. While I am not obese, I do have twenty pounds that came with my hashi's diagnosis and pretty much never went away\*, and so I have been pretty strict about the 50g/day carb limit. Some days I'll have a few grams over 50; many days I have much less. I haven't snacked at all and my fruit consumption is way down (1X-2X/wk tops). My husband requests a (Paleo) dessert once a week, and I have indulged a bit especially when chocolate is involved. For a healthy person, my few bites of dessert once a week are no biggie. I am not a healthy person, so I recognize that this habit has to go (if you can call two fork/spoonfuls a habit!), and soon. It would be easier if the hubs decided to forgo his weekly need for a treat - if it isn't around I can't sample, right? - but I can't blame my own weaknesses on him. I plan to report in my next update that no "dessert", Paleo or not, touched my lips. It's a goal.

Bedtime habits and sleep are roughly the same. Still waking around 3 (and a few more times after that), but still mostly calm and relatively cool(er). I've had exactly 2 naps in 3+ weeks, both of which I felt I "needed" at the time, one of which negatively affected my sleep that night. This is progress; I've needed 2 or 3 naps/week for many years. My baths and showers are consistently cool and becoming cooler. I haven't taken the face plunge yet, not out of fear, but because I think I'm not ready for that step. Even though my current diet ratios of Omega 3/6 are good (and have been so for a while now), I haven't tested my levels yet - I suspect they are not where they should be, and so I'm holding off for now.

Other (hormone) testing is on hold; I am having a period roughly every 10 days (bad) and it sneaks up on me every time (good! no PMS symptoms to alert me!) This pattern predates my Leptin Rx somewhat, but I believe the daily low carb counts are having an impact. Overall, I think this is a good thing and I'm not really concerned (yet) as it's probably a necessary side effect of "normalizing". It's a bit inconvenient, and I'm sure it's not helping my ferritin levels at all, but overall it's something I can deal with.

Still reading everything I can get my hands on, online and off. I posted my labs some time back and received a recommendation to add resistant starch to my diet. I knew it was the latest big thing in Paleo circles, but I didn't really know where to start with it, so I did my research and ordered some (green banana flour, no nightshades for me) as well as some soil-based probiotics (Prescript Assist, also new to me). Haven't taken either yet, plan to start the PA first, probably Monday. Here's hoping!

My (facial) skin is looking better. I have good skin, really (other than extreme paleness), but even at 43 I get the occasional blemish or unevenness. Been pretty clear since starting the Leptin Rx, so I'm counting that as my first VICTORY on the road to optimal!

GG

\*When I had my NDT increased from 60 mg (1 grain) to 90 mg (1 1/2 grains) I lost that twenty pounds fast and kept it off for several months. My weight eventually crept back up. My diet/exercise habits were nearly identical before and after the NDT increase, so I can't credit anything I did for the loss or the re-gain. Nothing I've done since has made that 20 pounds budge beyond a pound or two here and there. I accept that the weight won't come off until all the little duckies are lined up in a row, and that won't happen tomorrow!

\*\*\*Edited to add: I've been experiencing heart palpitations (not unusual with thyroid meds) for about a week now. First few days only at night, since then day and night. They are mild and I attribute them to the Leptin Rx as I haven't had a change in my NDT dosage lately. My next scheduled bloodwork is in about 2 weeks; next doctors appointment toward the end of May. Here's hoping the reset is decreasing my need for medication!

### GGinPG — 2014-04-12T01:41:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ggs-journal.10271/post-121060>

More thoughts for a Friday.

I was a skinny kid, pre-adolescence. Not too tall, but miles of bony legs. I've been told my entire life that I was a cry baby as an infant - cried constantly. I learned from my paternal grandmother (never a word from my mother's lips) that my mother was advised by my doctor that I wasn't getting enough nourishment from her breast milk and that she should feed me formula. My grandmother claims my mother resented this remark and refused to comply. I really have no way of knowing for sure, my mother won't talk about it, even now, but it certainly makes sense. I ate pretty well as a kid, as far as it goes, my mother didn't keep many sweets in the house. I wasn't much of a fruit eater, but I adored blueberries (still do). Spent a lot of time (every weekend for years) with my maternal grandparents. Upon picking me up for the weekend, my grandfather would stop at the Dolly Madison shop and fill a cart will all manner of goodies: brownies, fruit pies, donuts. None of this was off limits at my grandparents' - it was offered cheerfully, and I ate up, happily. I developed quite a sweet tooth. Stayed skinny.

Until I turned twelve and then I went from skinny to, not really chubby or heavy, but soft. I was an early bloomer (needed my first training bra at 9; menarche at 11) and I can't say for sure if the "softness" came from the change in hormones or the need to "disguise" the fact that I was developing a womanly curvature - in the third and fourth grade! By the fifth grade, I was still the only girl that had rounded out without the use of Kleenex - and believe me the boys noticed and teased. I was extremely self-conscious, so it's possible I subconsciously ate more (junk) so that my chest would stand out less. Nothing more obvious than boobs on a skinny girl! But I don't know. There's no "naturally" skinny members of my family on either side, so it's possibe I was just settling in to my destiny. I never became "heavy", just always had a softness about me that wasn't alleviated by activity. I was a reader, not a softball player or swimmer. As a teen I walked a lot, and enjoyed it. (I still love to walk.) But I didn't do it rigorously enough to change my shape much.

I was mostly healthy, as a kid. The usual colds, the flu once or twice. Chicken pox (mild case), strep throat, tonsilitis once each. My younger brother (the "happy, never-crying" baby) was the sick one. Frequent tonsilitis, strep, Epstein Barr. He was diagnosed with Graves Disease at 16 and had his thyroid removed with radioactive iodine treatment. Synthroid for the rest of his life. He's also had vitiligo from a very young age.

Right before I got married at 22 I lost all of my "baby fat" (I believe it was the stress of planning a wedding, I wasn't trying to lose the weight). I weighed 115 when I went to have my blood test done prior to the wedding. I was very thin (I'm 5' 4") and my wedding dress was two sizes too big when I walked down the aisle (it's obvious in the photos, I was too nervous to notice it at the time, I think). I wanted to keep the weight off and thought that "low fat" was the way to do it (it was the mid 1990's, low fat was all the rage). As a newly married couple, we didn't have a lot of money. I ate a lot of spaghetti with no-fat sauce. A LOT. Also plenty of sugar. I also started a running and weightlifting routine. The running was a bad idea, but I felt fantastic when the runner's high kicked in. The weightlifting was fine (I'm sure I didn't have the greatest form) and even though I gained about 10 pounds doing it, I looked fitter than I had at 115, and my clothes still fit great, so I kept it up.

I had always had "bad periods", since practically the first one. Every PMS symptom you can think of was a 10 on the 10 scale, and my god, the cramps! In my twenties all of this got worse. At thirty I made my first gynecological appointment, and I was handed a sample of birth control pills to "manage" my PMS symptoms. Even though I desperately wanted relief, something made me hesitate and I never took a pill. I suffered on. In my early thirties I noticed that I worked backwards. Never had a huge sex drive, but I grew to see that I had a strong aversion to sex leading up to ovulation. I wasn't at the point of making connections yet, not by a long shot, but I remember speaking the words "I don't think I want to have children" and not really understanding where that was coming from. In high school, I could have been voted "most likely to become a mommy", not because I was in any way promiscuous (quite the opposite), but because I had a "way" with children. I loved children! I loved babysitting. I loved babies! If you asked me at 18 if I would be childless at 43 my answer would have been "not a chance". And yet. And yet now I believe my body was telling me in my thirties that my becoming pregnant was a bad idea, hence the constant aversion to sex at the time in my cycle when I should have been biologically driven to have sex.

When I was thirty five I came down (in the middle of the summer) with a fever, a high one. I spent four days on the couch insisting it would come down before my husband made me go to the emergency room. My white cell count had dropped to virtually nothing; I was told I came in the nick of time. I was tested for everything (my only symptom was a fever) and I mean everything. I was poked with needles so many times I lost count. Eventually, diagnosed with "viral infection". I was attached to an IV for four days (laced with doxycycline) and released to a long recovery. I did eventually improve but I wasn't the same. I was tired all of the time, needed naps to get through the day, and my vision was "fuzzy". I ate very little and continued to run, but I was gaining weight. I suspected my thyroid was out of sorts and asked my doctor for tests. Not knowing much, there wasn't a whole lot to say when my TSH came back "normal" and I was told I was fine. I knew I wasn't fine, but didn't have the energy or knowledge to argue the point. So I went home, spent several more months with extreme fatigue and eventually found a naturopath who supplemented me to within an inch of my life. When nothing worked, she admitted it was probably my thyroid and gave me a referral to a doctor who would treat me with Armour, rather than Synthroid, which she felt would be harmful to me.

My new doctor ran a more thorough workup and I was diagnosed with Hashimoto's. In the course of doing tons of research on the hashi's I came to believe that I had a problem with gluten and so I had myself tested for Celiac. That test came back negative, but I knew that wasn't uncommon. I eliminated gluten (eventually all grains, in fairly short order) and felt light years better almost immediately. I had further testing done (on my own) including a stool test and genetic testing, which confirmed what I already knew. It wasn't long before "gluten free" became "Paleo". Since my diagnosis, I've had ups and downs, but I'm certainly miles away from where I started. I still feel less than optimal - like I still have miles to walk before I reach my destination, but I'm constantly learning and tweaking. And now having found Jack Kruse and this community, I have hope that that road terminates eventually,

GG

### GGinPG — 2014-05-09T17:59:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ggs-journal.10271/post-126039>

This is me just shy of 2 months into the reset: The Good, The Bad, The Ugly.

I have an appointment with my doctor in 6 days, during which I will hopefully convince him to order me a plethora of exotic tests. Meanwhile my thyroid labs are due in my mailbox any day now. But for now...

The GOOD:

I am having a much easier time with my BAB. Some days are easier than others, but I don't have to force myself much and I'm (mostly) finishing everything on my plate. I'm still follwing a strict ketogenic diet; still roughly the same pattern - every other meal is seafood. (Sometimes five meals in a row will be seafood, followed by two or three meals straight of beef or duck or pork, but it's all evening out in the end.) I had a few bites of (Paleo) "dessert" for Easter (and only because it was a treat made especially for me by my mother); other than that I have been as clean and pure as the driven snow.

The Bad:

Perhaps I should rename this category "The NEUTRAL". There, I just did.

The Neutral:

I am still waking in the night. It is no longer consistently 3:00, though. Whenever I am too hot (which is a lot) I wake up. This is a deceptively simple problem. It goes something like this: If I am even the slightest bit cold, I cannot fall asleep. If I am even the slightest bit hot, I cannot stay asleep. And for 43 years my biggest challenge has been to find the sweet spot, and I haven't found it yet. I sleep in a room by myself now so that I can control my environment as much as possible. I sleep with earplugs and a sleep mask and the door closed because my husband stays up later than I (and is noisy), also because an open door is an invitation to a cat on the bed and further sleep disruptions. I try to keep the room as cool as possible (easy in the winter, virtually impossible in the summer, so my summer sleep is much worse than my already lousy winter sleep. It is essentially summer here now...) I hate fans. If I run the A/C even a degree or two lower than typical, the electric bill - already high - skyrockets. Plus my husband complains he's freezing. If it's cool enough (and there's enough of a nighttime breeze to circulate air) and I keep the windows open, the noise from the street below keeps me up, so I'm seldom able to keep the windows open. Basically, I'm temperature-challenged. Ultra-sensitive. (More on this topic in "The Ugly".)

I've been taking resistant starch, green banana flour, (alongside soil-based probiotics) for nearly a month now. Because any change I make takes what seems like forever to manifest itself in me, I held off documenting what I've noticed. Mostly because neither thing seemed to be doing anything dramatic for me. For a while my digestion slowed down somewhat and I experienced a smidgen of gas in the evenings, roughly 12 hours after I took the RS; timing was very consistent. Digestion (meaning transit time) seems to be mostly back to normal. Two changes of note (which have only happened in the last few days): One, I am MUCH sleepier in the evenings, even before I get up to prepare for bed. As in, can't even keep my eyes open. This hasn't translated into my falling asleep any easier but I feel like my sleep, albeit more disrupted lately, is deeper in the periods in which I am actually sleeping. I am having more specific dreams, easily explainable (I tend to be a cryptic, nonlinear dreamer), mundane, almost, but very clear and I usually remember all the details come morning. I almost feel as if I COULD get a decent (full) night's sleep if the conditions were right - they're clearly not, yet.

The second change leads directly to The UGLY.

The UGLY:

I am constantly hot. Uncomfortably hot. Miserably hot. Virtually the only time I am not too hot is when I am sitting in a cool tub or taking a cool shower. And once I'm out of the water, I'm almost instantly hot again.

One of my defining characteristics is that I am truly miserable when I'm hot. Probably because I don't sweat properly (never have) and so that's not a cooling mechanism for me. (I hate to sweat, too, even if my perspiration isn't quite up to standards.) Also, the wonky thyroid doesn't help, I'm sure. I know that one of the signs (as mentioned by Dr. Kruse) of a reset is that my body will be hotter but I will feel cooler (poor paraphrasing). I'm MORE than ok with that. But I'm hotter and feeling hotter, so I'm not sure what to make of it. I almost feel as if I'm burning fuel, strange as that sounds, and I'm kind of surprised that I haven't lost any weight. Not that I'm expecting weight loss at this stage, I know myself better than that. But hot = irritable, so basically I'm snapping at my husband a bit and finding myself avoiding activity because it only contributes to more HOT. I'm Jonesing for some cold adaptation.

Everything takes longer with me. If the standard time frame (for a change to show benefits) is one month, rest assured that five, six months out, I'm still waiting. So I've learned to be patient. Meanwhile, I'm staying away from the bad EMFs, I've put filters on my shower and drop Vitamin C into my tub. I'm having my water delivered from Crystal Springs and my seafood delivered in bulk (or nearly) - although I haven't eaten it raw yet. (By the way, anybody else out there have some issues with Crystal Springs' service? I've only been a customer for a couple of weeks and I've had to call with problems three times already.)

Onward,
GG

### GGinPG — 2014-09-10T18:44:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ggs-journal.10271/post-140548>

Five months in. Four months since my last update.

Took a few weeks of debating with myself if I SHOULD update, because my progress is slow. But there ARE signs of progress, and that's not nothing, and certainly small gains are very worth sharing, if only to give other Slowies a reason to keep going

I've been faithful to the Reset Protocol. For a long while I was coasting along at minimum (eg. 50 grams protein at BAB max) but then I decided a few weeks ago to shake things up a bit. Now I shoot for 60-70g protein at BAB and instead of waiting an hour before BAB (due to thyroid meds), I begin to eat 30 minutes after rising. Technically, my thyroid Rx says "Take on empty stomach", not "Take 1 hour after rising" :/ Anyway, I'm hoping that consistency is more important than timing. I have become mindful of sunrise and I'm adjusting my sunrise alarm to better align with the actual lightening of the day. I also make an effort to get outside within 10-15 minutes of rising, before breakfast. I maybe only get 5 minutes out there before I need to see to breakfast, but it's something. I live in a fishbowl (or at least it feels like it), on a residential street with a county park a mile away, so there is always a car going by, probably wondering why this crazy woman is outside staring up at the sky in her (modest) nightgown. (Wearing any less isn't optional - and definitely illegal!) I wasn't consistent with this at the beginning; I've been doing it every day for about a month now, even on cloudy/rainy days.

I began using resistant starch back in May and I did notice (1) that I was much sleepier at bedtime and (2) that my sleep was somewhat improved. The RS did not "cure" my middle of the night waking/misery, but it did help to lessen it, I believe, because I made no other changes to account for it! I take about 30-32g/day (with Soil-Based probies). I've read a lot of conflicting reports about RS - so I do have some concerns about using it long term. But I think that (so far) it has been helpful to my sleep, so unless I learn of a cut-and-dried reason to stop using it, I will continue. I should mention, however, that I am bothered by the fact that my (well, frankly distended) belly hasn't gone, if not away completely, at least down somewhat, in five months of Reset. I don't know if perhaps RS isn't the reason for this. Any thoughts?

I started daily face dunks in June and this is where I can see progress. I do them immediately after my BAB (I drink 16 oz of ice water immediately prior to dunking), and I've worked my way (easily) to 10 minutes, doing 20 second dunks). I could go longer, but my back and legs don't like the bending over much. About a week after starting face dunks, I believe I had a detox reaction. I was miserable with cold-like symtoms and swollen glands for a few days, and then, fine. Two weeks into face dunking and my sleep improved dramatically. I actually had - have - nights where I slept - sleep -consistently (or at least I wasn't awake long enough for it to register) until the alarm began to light. This is so big I can't possibly overstate it. I can sleep through the night, more or less! I have to qualify this somewhat, when I update my hormones, but for now this is a breakthrough of GINORMOUS proportions!

I'm still roughly 50% seafood/50% everything else. I'm keeping my carbs <50, usually 0\. I'm having trouble, though, even after all these months, with cravings. I want chocolate in the worst damn way at times. (I'm a lifelong chocoholic, so this isn't new, I was just hoping it was over.) I will take the edge off with some 90% dark chocolate (and that's the only carbs I'll eat for the day), but I've got to be honest, an ounce of dark chocolate doesn't really take the edge off, and if someone handed me a solid chocolate Easter Bunny I'd have a hard time not ripping the poor guy's ears off! I take plenty of magnesium and take epsom salt baths, so this shouldn't be happening, right?

It has been extraordinarily difficult (because I look and feel so blah), but I have not "exercised" (meaning weights) at all. I do walk daily, 1/2 - 1 mile or so with my dog, and then I go back out on my own (not every day) for 2-3 miles. I miss walking. I don't think I've read that Dr. Kruse says not to walk, specifically, but I've been holding back because I'm afraid it will will further delay my already slow progress. I would like to walk 5-6 miles a day.

OK, so here is where I screwed up big. My hormones. Very long story short (at least I'll try), my cycle as of the last two years is just about all over the place. Last two periods were 60+ and then 80+ days apart. Before that they were coming every two weeks for a few months. But more importantly, what I've noticed over the summer is that I am not cooling down at all. Typically, a "month" for me means a period (however it happens), and then (roughly) two weeks of "coolness", ovulation (which doesn't seem to happen much, if at all, anymore) and then two weeks (roughly) of "hotness". Hotness meaning exactly that, at all times of the day, but particularly at night, which makes it difficult to sleep DESPITE the face dunking. Well, I haven't had a cooling phase all summer. Of course I noticed this, but for some reason I kept assuming that my cycle was correcting itself due to all of the Reset changes - a good thing, right? The head smack moment happened just prior to my 3 month doctor's visit in August, when I realized I was probably estrogen dominant and needed intervention beyond the Reset. So anyway - now taking a small amount of bioidentical progesterone - had a period at 31 days,yay! (With cramps, not so yay.) So, about 3 weeks of 50mg Progesterone helped bring on a "normal" period. But, I'm still not getting the cool phase. Period's over, still feel like estrogen is surging (is it possible to feel such a thing?) Now I'm thinking I need to increase my progesterone dose. Not a problem, doctor gave me leeway here, the Rx is in a premeasured "clicker" applicator, to use between 1 (25mg) and 4 (100mg) clicks. I've been doing the necessary research, but finding it conflicting. My doctor says use enough (whatever that amount is) to stop symptoms. Many sources agree and some go so far as to warn that using too little is harmful. I'm a cautious person when it comes to medicine, and sensitive to direct hormone application, to boot, but my body is telling me something isn't quite right here, maybe I should go higher, maybe use it more than once/day, maybe use it through my period? Any thoughts on this would be most welcome! I am also cognizant of the fact that Dr. K said that the CT would eventually make our bodies run hotter but feel more comfortable. Well, that sort of applies here, too. At night when the warmth wakes me, it's moderately uncomfortable versus miserably so. I credit the CT for that. But, for instance, in the mornings making breakfast (not an inordinate amount of physical activity) I become hot and lightly sweaty easily. Not comfortable at all. So, I'm thinking it's ultimately a hormone imbalance. I forgot to mention that I began taking DIM in May, on the advice of my doctor, so estrogen dominance SHOULD have been on my radar way before August. And yet it wasn't.

Testing/bloodwork. Outside of my regular 3 month bloodwork for thyroid and ferritin, there isn't any. This is too long to get into here, but suffice it to say, for the time being I am doing all of this without benefit of testing. I know this is far from ideal, but it is out of my hands. At some point in the near future I will need to force the issue, but for now... For now I continue to travel down the Yellow Brick Road, it will just take me longer to get to Oz.

My baths are getting colder, little by little (they were already pretty cool). I haven't tried icing yet or a by-the-book CT bath session (I'm a little afraid to do this without O3/O6 ratio testing), but every little bit helps, I hope.

My dog is sick. This is incredibly stressful. I don't know of a way to make it less so. I have no children, she's my baby. 12 1/2 year old greyhound.

Onward,
GG

Edited to add that I think better sleep is worth smiling about - so I've updated my photo.

### GGinPG — 2014-09-11T02:28:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ggs-journal.10271/post-140575>

I'm still doing a BAB because I don't believe I've "reset" yet. I weighed in at 150 (about 20 pounds from where I should be) when I started the Reset back in April. I still weigh 150, still look bloated overall, still have my tummy pooch. My energy levels are acceptable - barely. By 2-3 pm I'm dragging and want a nap. I don't crave carbs, per se, but I do regularly want chocolate - badly. That's all to the negative, and I didn't want post a negative update, mostly because I know how long it takes for changes to take hold in me. I still come to the blogs/forum regularly, but try to keep it to "read only" in order to limit my time in front of a computer screen. BTW, how does everyone manage to keep up with all the information on this site without overdoing it on the computer? (Today I spent all day zigzagging through things trying to patch together some ideas for N=1, and I know I'll pay the price later.) Actually, I already am. Somehow, sitting, I managed to pull a muscle in my backside, I can barely stand up. Dummy.) Anyway, today I was fixated on the Earthing sheet debate. I use one, or did. Probably not after today.

But back to the BAB - I will reread the "after the leptin reset" (it's been a few months). I don't recall reading to only do the reset for 4-6 weeks, but rather that it takes the average person that long to begin to see results. I, unfortunately, am not the average person so I assumed at the outset that I would need more time. But again I will revisit. And more seafood? I will try to add more wherever possible

Thank you for your kind compliment; you will never know how good it felt to see those words. After a lifetime of hiding my smile, I had enough and had my teeth "fixed". I owe my smile to my amazing dentist and to my husband, as it was his gift to me. Thank you, Caroline

GG

### nonchalant — 2014-09-11T04:13:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ggs-journal.10271/post-140603>

Hi GG, sounds like you are coming along. I would think if your O6 levels are too high, you might have problems with ice packs, but not so much with an ice bath. What temp water are you using for baths now? Face dunks are good, but they are nothing like a CT bath.

### GGinPG — 2014-09-11T14:48:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ggs-journal.10271/post-140634>

nonchalant, my bath water temp varies a bit. I would say most of the time it's in the 60-degree range (or roughly 10 degrees colder than ambient air). I do occasionally dip down to 55 - and boy that 5 degrees is noticeable. I don't shiver at 60, I do off and on at 55. My bath length hasn't changed much, either, I stay in 30-35 minutes, so I should probably bump that up. (It's hard to find an hour, sometimes, isn't it?)

I found the CT thread a few days back and read through everybody's experience. Lots of talk about moving vs nonmoving. I'm such a wuss I stay as completely still as I can. But I guess that's not doing me as much good as it should, so I'll have to suck it up and move about some. ( I moved my face slowly while dunking - and it did make for a colder experience.) I submerge up to my chin though, and keep my forearms out so I can hold a book. Without that book (and I make it heavy reading, too ) I'm sure my baths would be even shorter!

GG

### nonchalant — 2014-09-11T16:58:40+0300

GG, as long as you aren't coasting too long, you're probably doing fine. If you've read that thread, you've got all the hints.

### Calum — 2016-03-03T01:32:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ggs-journal.10271/post-190106>

> [GGinPG said:](/goto/post?id=118520)
>
> Ten days in. I continue to read, read, read, although not so much online as I'm trying to limit my exposure to those nasty things coming out of my laptop. Just received two of Dr. Kruse's recommended titles, Cross Currents and Zapped so this may be my last blog post ever. (Kidding.) (Sort of.) Also just received a fresh supply of orange light bulbs so that I can have low blue light in all my living spaces at night. Planning on getting filters for TV, monitors, etc.
>
> What I have been doing faithfully: 50 grams protein in the morning (60 minutes after rising and taking my thyroid meds.) This is becoming tougher, not easier. I'm thoroughly sick of my breakfast before I'm halfway done. Please tell me this gets easier? I start with a can of sardines, this gets me 20+ grams of protein right off the bat, and if I'm unable to finish my plate, I figure I've gotten the healthiest part of breakfast down. Then I have either leftover protein from dinner the night before, or some eggs, plus a veggie of some sort and a small amount of carbs (sweet potato cubes sauteed in duck fat, lard or beef fat - also helps to increase the fat grams). Roughly every other meal is fish or seafood (in addition to the sardines at breakfast), but I'm finding that at breakfast virtually nothing tastes good to me. For instance, I love scallops. Had some for breakfast and had to choke them down. I'm not complaining, I know I am doing my best, but am wondering if this is typical. And short term. Strangely, I am not full after breakfast, just sick of it. After breakfast I have a cup of DECAF with a tablespoon of coconut oil. The coffee is new. I'm only drinking it to get the coconut oil down. I can't stand the taste of it in food and I can't take it plain, so I figure it's a solution to getting it down. By noon, I'm a little hungry.
>
> "Lunch" is a cup of bone broth with a tablespoon of grassfed gelatin added and a small handful of soaked and dehydrated macadamia nuts. I'm already spending a lot of time in the kitchen with breakfast and dinner so I don't want to have to prepare anything. This is a reasonably healthful way to get me through until dinner. I realize with autoimmune issues I should ditch the macadamias and I will; right now it's just a solution until I can stock up on better choices (I do my food shopping at the beginning of the month). Since having gone Paleo, lunch hasn't really been much of a meal for me anyway.
>
> Dinner between 4 and 4:30. Smaller than breakfast, heavy on protein and veggies. Every other meal has seafood. By dinner time I am usually fairly hungry but I don't need to eat much to feel satisfied. I may have a piece of fruit afterwards, but I haven't "needed" it (I've always had a sweet tooth).
>
> I walk away from my computer and the TV no later than 7 pm (it is still daylight here) and take a cool epsom salt bath (3X/wk) or a cool shower. I know this isn't the true CT protocol, but I'm hoping to work my way up to that. If I'm a little active (say warm from washing the dishes in hot water) prior to my bath/shower it's much easier to tolerate the temperatures, which are roughly that of the ambient air, right now it's still cool enough for the furnace which I have set to 65. I was already taking an epsom salt bath 2X/week (in warm water), and I feel better afterward, so I added the third. I can't do it every night -- too expensive. After bathing, I put on my blue light blocking goggles, do a few light evening chores (15 minutes) and then go sit in orange light to read away from television and computers. I had been on a schedule of in bed by 10, lights out 10:30, but I'm not making it that long. Sometimes lights out at 9:30 now. This is fine with me, but my husband resents the heck out of it as we have a very limited amount of time to spend together (he works until fairly late in the evening most days). We are working on it - but there's some added stress because of it and I try to avoid stress at all costs.
>
> My sleep has been consistent this past week. I'm still falling asleep fairly easily, but waking at roughly 3 am every night. Thanks to a link shared by sooperb, I believe I at least know why it's happening (liver, estrogen), but nothing I've changed these last 10 days has reversed the trend. My husband has been cutting off the power to my bedroom each night (he sleeps in another room) and turning off the WiFi, and I think that's been helping in that I am calmer overall when I wake. But the nights he's forgotten to turn the WiFi off weren't noticeably worse.
>
> I have noticed that I'm queasy in the evenings after dinner and also in the morning when I rise. I'm definitely hungry when I get up, although that quickly turns to revulsion once I've had a few bites of food (no matter what that food is). I know I have to work on getting more breakfast down. Most days I do manage to finish most of it, but it's taking increasingly longer to get it down. This morning it took me an hour and twenty minutes to eat roughly 2/3 of my breakfast.
>
> Onward and upward!

Can you please help me out,

I was wondering what goggles you are using

Cal

# Thread: When to trust your instinct and when not to
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-to-trust-your-instinct-and-when-not-to.10273/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BJK77 — 2014-03-21T22:50:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-to-trust-your-instinct-and-when-not-to.10273/post-117015>

I know there's a lot of talk about trusting your instinct and going with your gut when it comes to health, but are there times when we're too sick and maybe shouldn't do so?

At times I find myself craving protein like crazy, sometimes liver too and not wanting seafood at all - like now! Knowing that I'm dealing with some high sulfur and ammonia issues right now makes me question why my body is drawn to animal proteins.

I also lately have found I'm wanting more carbs. Is that seasonal as things are FINALLY starting to warm up and I'm actually getting some sun and earthing in?? Hard to say. I felt almost shakey and weak after lunch today - 8 oysters and an avocado, so I randomly had an idea pop in my head to make coconut chips. I ate a handful and felt fantastic afterwards, better than I have in the last few weeks.

I want to believe my instincts are spot on, but I know I am very sick, so I'm wary. Thoughts?

### Josh — 2014-03-21T23:15:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-to-trust-your-instinct-and-when-not-to.10273/post-117017>

Good to have a practitioner or at least a friend that is tracking along with you intimately day to day to review ones "instincts" with...one can only share so much on a forum....

### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-22T02:02:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-to-trust-your-instinct-and-when-not-to.10273/post-117039>

You are sick because of what is flying all around you.........and it is causing molecular chaos inside you..........and the docs.........have no clue why how or where..........CPC 8 tells you all that. Rely on you..........what did the blog say? Why dont your docs know? READ THE DAMN BLOG.

### BJK77 — 2014-03-22T16:20:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-to-trust-your-instinct-and-when-not-to.10273/post-117150>

I do read the damn blog! I haven't read the latest blog post (because I do try to limit my EMF exposure and hadn't printed it yet, but I do plan to read it today). Yes, I know that I am sick and I do know why. I am trying every damn thing I can think of to reverse my f'in nightmare. I do know docs aren't worth their own shit, but I can only understand half the blogs (if that) thanks to my freakin' sick brain, so I do still hold out hope that I may find a decent practitioner who will understand at least a small portion of this and be able to help me. The half of the blogs that I do tend to understand leaves me about 10 minutes after I've read it as my sick brain just isn't able to store the information like some of the other great minds on here who I truly admire.

I am relying on me. I am relying on me to be a fighter, to continue on when I don't have a freakin' clue, to do what I would encourage anyone I loved to do who was in the same position, to never give up. So yes, my questions may be silly and may annoy some, but I'm just trying to learn and do the best I can considering the position I'm in right now. Instead of driving my freakin car off a cliff like I want to do on many days, I instead jump in a cold bath, eat my oysters and pure fat, put my feet on the earth and tell myself that yes, some day I will be better. Some day I will create that new me and life will be amazing.

Speaking of that cold bath, I'm heading there now with a copy of CPC 8. Here's to hoping I'll be able to understand it!

### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-22T16:23:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-to-trust-your-instinct-and-when-not-to.10273/post-117152>

That is all you need...........you. You just need motivation to trust the person buried in your head. I am that voice telling you to listen to the doctor buried in your head.

### caroline — 2014-03-22T21:43:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-to-trust-your-instinct-and-when-not-to.10273/post-117231>

I think we all need to cut ourself some slack sometimes..........even Neil and Rob sometimes don't quite get it .....for sure they are miles and miles ahead of me.....but that doesn't stop me from trying.
It is freakin hard! .......but I love to be stretched like that! I love the challenge even tho I know I can't retain a lot of it.

I have started reading the blogs out loud to myself ...helps a little!

# Thread: Folic Acid, The FDA and Our Bodies in the light of CPC8
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/folic-acid-the-fda-and-our-bodies-in-the-light-of-cpc8.10277/>
**Subforum:** Redox Rx
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Josh — 2014-03-22T03:49:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/folic-acid-the-fda-and-our-bodies-in-the-light-of-cpc8.10277/post-117065>

More fuel for the CPC 8 fire.....

> Without it, no cell can divide. It prevents birth defects like spina bifida. It reduces our risk of cancer, heart
> disease and Alzheimer’s disease. It supports our immune system and fuels our mitochondria to give us
> energy.
> Yet, despite having eight EFSA-approved health claims to its name, this humble vitamin can also be too
> much of a good thing; there’s growing evidence that overdosing can increase our cancer risk. Welcome to
> the double-edged sword of folate, or to be more correct, folic acid.

[http://www.bakeryandsnacks.com/Regulation-Safety/Folic-acid-The-double-edge-sword-of-the-golden-micronutrient/?utm\_source=newsletter\_daily&utm\_medium=email&utm\_campaign=Newsletter+Daily&c=T5DwZFxZmFEawBlRTASUNzL4H5+TlVMh](http://www.bakeryandsnacks.com/Regulation-Safety/Folic-acid-The-double-edge-sword-of-the-golden-micronutrient/?utm_source=newsletter_daily&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=Newsletter%2BDaily&c=T5DwZFxZmFEawBlRTASUNzL4H5%2BTlVMh)

> Folic acid supplementation may prevent the development of cancer in normal tissues but may promote the progression of established (pre)neoplastic lesions. However, whether or not folic acid supplementation can promote the progression of established (pre)neoplastic mammary lesions is unknown. This is a critically important issue because breast cancer patients and survivors in North America are likely exposed to high levels of folic acid owing to folic acid fortification and widespread supplemental use after cancer diagnosis. We investigated whether folic acid supplementation can promote the progression of established mammary tumors. Female Sprague-Dawley rats were placed on a control diet and mammary tumors were initiated with 7,12-dimethylbenza\[_a_\]anthracene at puberty. When the sentinel tumor reached a predefined size, rats were randomized to receive a diet containing the control, 2.5x, 4x, or 5x supplemental levels of folic acid for up to 12 weeks. The sentinel mammary tumor growth was monitored weekly. At necropsy, the sentinel and all other mammary tumors were analyzed histologically. The effect of folic acid supplementation on the expression of proteins involved in proliferation, apoptosis, and mammary tumorigenesis was determined in representative sentinel adenocarcinomas. Although no clear dose-response relationship was observed, folic acid supplementation significantly promoted the progression of the sentinel mammary tumors and was associated with significantly higher sentinel mammary tumor weight and volume compared with the control diet. Furthermore, folic acid supplementation was associated with significantly higher weight and volume of all mammary tumors. The most significant and consistent mammary tumor-promoting effect was observed with the 2.5x supplemental level of folic acid. Folic acid supplementation was also associated with an increased expression of BAX, PARP, and HER2. Our data suggest that folic acid supplementation may promote the progression of established mammary tumors. The potential tumor-promoting effect of folic acid supplementation in breast cancer patients and survivors needs further clarification.

[http://www.plosone.org/article/info:doi/10.1371/journal.pone.0084635](http://www.plosone.org/article/info%3Adoi%2F10.1371%2Fjournal.pone.0084635)

### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-22T04:13:38+0200

3 women in my family: paternal grandmother, sister and stepmother. Surreal moments:

1.  Grandmother's lung's hemorrhaged on the white rug while I was visiting in Florida...came back from a movie to find it, like a horror movie...eerie really. It had gone to the lungs and spine. Took 8 years from mastectomy at 75, died at 83.
2.  Sister and husband arguing about what size to reconstruct...she will kill me for mentioning this, but she hasn't talked to me in 12 years anyway. Jones fracture on sidewalk outside GCT on the way to NYU Hospital day after reconstruction finished my 3 year "Cancer Patrol"-I guess I needed a strong message. (Sister-Breast, Mother -Ovarian (deceased), brother in law-leukemia (tough German survived stem cell transplant and is alive 14 years later, snuck out of isolation out for a wurst and a smoke with no immune system. )) My sister is still alive according to Facebook...
3.  Mother had hyperthyroid at 4 with 3/4 removal and radiation. Made her lie immobile in a dark room every afternoon for 2 years...
4.  Stepmother has done everything Alternative except lump removal and still has her breasts and is doing well.

Went to chemo with mother and sister for 3 years at the 3 story facility on 31st St? WHole thing paid for by drug companies. Doctors paid no rent for offices. Met dozens of under 40 women with cancer. Anaphylaxis and other stuff was pretty routine...the joys of Taxitol and Taxatir at I think 5 or 10k/dose.

# Thread: Laptop or monitor?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/laptop-or-monitor.10278/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 18
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### caroline — 2014-03-22T04:17:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/laptop-or-monitor.10278/post-117073>

Does a laptop emit way more Emf? Or is it just being closer?

### yewwei.tan — 2014-03-22T04:52:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/laptop-or-monitor.10278/post-117076>

From where I work, my little laptop (Macbook Air 2013 mid model) doesn't emit much EMF in the 1.5-8GHz range. Usually around 0.01-0.03 uW/cm2

The ELF magnetic field (in the 20-2000MHz range) is horrible close up, basically around 4-10mG where my hands are. then around 0.5-1.0mG about 10cm away, and once you're a foot away, the magnetic field from the laptop is basically not as significant as other sources around me (I turned off the laptop to check, and my ambient ELF field hovers around 0.2-0.3mG where I work).

Michael Neuert's standards for reference: [http://emfinfo.org/guidelines-exposure.html](http://emfinfo.org/guidelines-exposure.html) . As usual, we need to test for ourselves.

### caroline — 2014-03-22T07:29:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/laptop-or-monitor.10278/post-117095>

So I got this horrible rash up and down my arms - to my shoulders ....rash doesn't even properly describe it. I am positive it was EMF from my laptop.
The night it happened - for some reason - I had my right hand on the keyboard a lot and my left arm leaning on it and I was leaning over it.

If I got a separate mouse ... would that solve the problem Yew? I have a separate keyboard - but find it frustrating so don't use it - but I will have too.

Even thou I am wired in - close up it is emitting huge EMF???

But I guess the biggest problem is the keyboard? I can also feel it in my face.

so - short of throwing it in the garbage - which will be Dr. K.'s suggestion ....a separate mouse -that is not wifi - should help considerably>

but maybe a whole separate monitor ..... so everything is farther away?

### Inger — 2014-03-22T07:50:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/laptop-or-monitor.10278/post-117096>

I would say separate wired mouse and keyboard. Also keeps you further away from the computer.

I have 2 computers one laptop and one with monitor, wired mouse and keyboard. The laptop has pretty bad readings close to where you put your hands when using it. I use a wired mouse when I use my laptop, always. If I have to write more I try to not use the laptop but the other computer instead.

### yewwei.tan — 2014-03-22T08:04:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/laptop-or-monitor.10278/post-117097>

Basically anything that would get you away from the actual processing unit of the computer (which is likely the thing that emits the most EMF) is a good thing. I'd second Inger's idea of a separate wired mouse and keyboard; I did do EMF measurements of an external mouse and keyboard, and found them to be pretty low (<0.01 uW/cm2).

I'm reminded by the common quote from Dr Kruse that "skin and brain are both formed from neuroectoderm". Skin issues always trigger a circadian system warning in my mind. I assume you're still using the blue blockers when on the computer?

Do you suspect any other thing that could have led to a change in your EMF environment? I know that my neighbours are starting to turn on the heating more often now that the mornings are getting into the low teens.

### Inger — 2014-03-22T08:30:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/laptop-or-monitor.10278/post-117103>

One more idea.. I do this too. Just take your hand off the mouse/keyboard/laptop always when not actively typing or browsing. Try to touch it as little as possible. Easily one can forget and let the hand rest on the mouse - big mistake!
Remember what Jack has told us about collagen.... and the skin... touching the mouse will affect your whole body!!!

### Inger — 2014-03-22T08:32:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/laptop-or-monitor.10278/post-117104>

I am not using blue blockers when I am on the computer at day time... maybe I should... But I sit by the window and I want the daylight in...
I should try it out and see if there is a difference. But I hate using any glasses at daytime... I never use them, not even when driving in strong sunlight.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-22T08:39:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/laptop-or-monitor.10278/post-117105>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=117095)
>
> If I got a separate mouse ... would that solve the problem Yew? I have a separate keyboard - but find it frustrating so don't use it - but I will have too.

Much the best idea to. Make sure it's a USB keyboard and not a bluetooth one, though.

EMF aside a separate keyboard is better for biomechanical reasons -- whenever you're not actually on the move and can.

There's a reason for that — besides saving wear-and-tear on the built-in keyboard. It's this: on a laptop the keyboard and screen are _too close_.

With DSE (Display Screen Equipment) you get the chair adjusted so that you're sitting normally. This means sitting on what the Germans call the _Sitz_ bones (and not on the end of the spine):

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tuberosity\_of\_the\_ischium](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tuberosity_of_the_ischium)

and with your feet flat on the floor.

The keyboard should then be at such a height that your forearms are horizontal. And the top of the screen should be just below eye-level when sitting up comfortably straight (relaxed and not rigid).

This isn't possible with a laptop. You must needs either: (1) have the screen at the right height, at which height your wrists will be cocked up — which is courting RSI (Repetitive Strain Injury) and unnecessary shoulder and neck tension; or (2) set the keyboard at the right height which means you must slump or slouch in some way to see the screen properly — and that's again causing shoulder and neck tension with consequent pain, headaches and so on, and also possible problems with the lumbar spine.

An external USB keyboard solves that problem, because it decouples the keyboard from the screen. You can use a desk or table (or adjust one) to put the keyboard at the right height and put the open laptop up on a box or a pile of large books or something to bring the screen up high enough.

One large company I worked at had dedicated "docking stations" for every laptop user. When at work they'd plug in and make use of an external keyboard _and_ an adjustable desk-mounted external VDU. I don't know how common that is, but companies don't spend that money for nothing. They're legally required to provide a safe environment for DSE users and have to show wiling by doing that sort of thing.

> but maybe a whole separate monitor ..... so everything is farther away?

It's probably better, if possible. But using the laptop's own screen and raising it up on books or something would work instead and be a cheaper option.

On your original question -- "Does a laptop emit way more Emf" -- the video Jack has online seems to imply that laptops are not ususally grounded. Else why have a video called "How to ground a laptop"?

IIRC, if yours isn't, you snip off a USB cable and plug the it into a USB socket on the laptop, attaching the other end to a grounding cord.

[http://jackkruse.com/grounding-your-computer/](http://jackkruse.com/grounding-your-computer/)

### caroline — 2014-03-22T08:45:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/laptop-or-monitor.10278/post-117106>

Thanks Inger and Yew........I am just thinking that I have changed locations of where I use my computer. I used to use it at my breakfast bar and sit on a stool. now I sit at a table on my balcony or my dining table. At the table it is in line with my chest. At the breakfast bar it is more in line with my hips. Even as I type now I can feel burning in my face and arms. This is scary!

I don't think anything has changed in my environment.........

But it sees to me that a monitor might be considerably better?

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-22T08:59:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/laptop-or-monitor.10278/post-117110>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=117107)

>

>

Maybe it's jumping to conclusions to put it down to EMF. I expect it could be an allergic reaction to a cleaning product on the keyboard. Then again, I expect those moulds that Dave Asprey worries so much about can get a hold on a keyboard, too, and maybe those could do it.

Give it a wipe down with something non-toxic to be on the safe side?

### caroline — 2014-03-22T09:08:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/laptop-or-monitor.10278/post-117113>

I have just changed my location - back to the breakfast bar ....my body is farther away - especially my arms. Mike it took me a fair bit of time to come to this conclusion - nothing else was different ...and then the way it started with my hands and just kept moving up my arms to my shoulders .... all of these body parts in direct line with the computer.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/laptop-or-monitor.10278/post-117122>

> I have just changed my location - back to the breakfast bar ....my body is farther away - especially my arms. At the table my arms rested on the table and I think I was often hunched over the computer ...

### cantweight — 2014-03-24T14:52:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/unfortunately-not-optimal-fitness-for-this-young-lady.10279/post-117573>

> [spinergie said:](/goto/post?id=117404)
>
> There could have been other additional reasons that neither her nor her parents were aware of that resulted or contributed in the sad outcome.
> Competitive sports for some people me included make and have made a positive part of who we are so I understand why participating in an event such as that was a good thing for her.
> There have been incidents such as this reported before some due also to lack of preparation and adaptation, suitability..
> It would be difficult on large events such as those monitoring whether participants maybe fit to take part as we know the underlying causes of what may be off with our physiology can be many and over an intricate level of interconnections.

I think competitive sports are great. I am one of the loudest moms cheering my kids on from the sidelines. They play soccer, softball, basketball, and even participate in the middle school running club. I love that they are outside and having fun, building camaraderie, and strengthening their bodies. But I can not see where a 16 yr old child can run a half marathon, that is too big of a stressor for their growing bodies.

I believe in other countries it may be fine, but our teenagers are burnt out. I have an almost 14 yr old daughter and the state of the children I see scares me. These kids are stressed out, caffeinated, selfie obsessed, zombies. The girls are substantially worse than the boys. Visually, anyway.

When I think back to my grade school and high school years I can not remember ever having a child with cancer, kids on medications, even seeing lots of broken bones and injuries. Now the amounts are staggering. Granted I have raised my children in area with a horrendous field, but I think we are more the norm than not these days.

### nicld — 2014-03-24T15:20:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/unfortunately-not-optimal-fitness-for-this-young-lady.10279/post-117580>

Just so sad but running is the new "it" thing.

I did a half marathon about 2 years ago at almost 200 pounds because that was the "thing" to do to show that you were physically fit. All it did was mess me up even more then I was before plus I gained weight during the training (HELLO, major inflammation).

I will NEVER do it again but would not mind doing some 5K's again, if and when the body heals from the years of physical abuse I put it through.

Now biking 150 miles in 3 days along a scenic bike trail in northern Minnesota or a 5 day bike ride in the Aspens of Colorado in September, that might be a different story.
