Now the amounts are staggering. Granted I have raised my children in area with a horrendous field, but I think we are more the norm than not these days.

and NoVA is worse than probably a lot of other cities even in the US, given the "RAT RACE" the kids parents are running, and pushing on their kids.... how much of her running/finishing was driven by the need to achieve the bar her parents set? How much was driven by the need to ensure she had something on her HS transcript that would make her stand out against other kids when she applied to college...

IMO we've placed college as the end all be all. I'm telling both Chaos and Havoc - that trades & skill are valuable too, and in our current society - more so than a piece of paper. We have family member who's a master pipefitter (plumber) who makes more than we do! He works hard, takes care of his family.

WE've pushed to the point - of keeping up with the joneses that we've forgotten that happiness and satisficaiton with life should be our priorities. I want Chaos and Havoc to grow up and BE HAPPY. I also want them to enjoy what they do. But lets be honest - Work Sucks. All work sucks. If you're lucky enough to enjoy work - that's a good thing....

But these days Kids and parents hate the life they're stuck in. Yet no one ever wonders why? maybe its time to stop reaching for the brass, silver, gold, platinum rings... and instead focus on reaching for happiness..... we might live longer....

We have family member who's a master pipefitter (plumber) who makes more than we do! He works hard, takes care of his family.
>
> WE've pushed to the point - of keeping up with the joneses that we've forgotten that happiness and satisficaiton with life should be our priorities. I want Chaos and Havoc to grow up and BE HAPPY. I also want them to enjoy what they do. But lets be honest - Work Sucks. All work sucks. If you're lucky enough to enjoy work - that's a good thing....
>
> But these days Kids and parents hate the life they're stuck in. Yet no one ever wonders why? maybe its time to stop reaching for the brass, silver, gold, platinum rings... and instead focus on reaching for happiness..... we might live longer....

I couldnt agree more. One of the best moments for me last fall came when my daughter was talking to me about the popular girls at school. She said they are obsessed with how many "likes" they get on pictures of themselves. They take a picture in the morning getting ready for school and inevitably there is a bra strap or or bit of underwear showing. And their mood when they get to school is entirely dependent upon how many likes they got.

She said I just dont understand that. Their entire self worth is based upon others opinions. What makes them happy is not even something real...a virtual thumbsup from a bunch of strangers you have "friended" to make your friends list bigger than other to inflate your ego even more. She said she was so glad she realized how stupid it all was before she got sucked in. I had never been prouder.

Did you know they are earning high school credits in 7th and 8th grade??? This is nuts. Since when should a 12 yr old child be stressing about college?!?!

I think I come across as a slacker parent but I dont give a shit. I am not stressing my kid out for the sake of standardized tests and bragging rights. I have my own set of bragging rights, just no one sees their value like I do.

### Casey Inman — 2014-03-22T15:05:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/less-emf-products.10283/post-117139>

Has anyone tried these products from less emf...

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

Imagine a portable cocoon, large enough for you to stand, sit, or sleep comfortably while escaping the ever-present barrage of wireless radiation.

The Quite Zone Retreat is just that. Available in 2 sizes, completely free-standing and self supporting, with large zippered doors for easy access. All surfaces are made with durable 600D Mylar fabric shielding material which locks out >99% of wireless radiation from every direction.

The inside is dark when fully closed. Bring in a flashlight, or set up more permanent battery operated LED lighting. Several vents allow passive air circulation, or you can use the included variable speed fan with 25 foot duct for increased air flow.

Completely portable, sets up in minutes. Internal steel support poles with corner brackets included. Add a carpet or roll out linoleum floor for comfort and protection. Intended for indoor use.

Standard size is ~4x8 feet, x 7 feet high (big enough for a twin mattress, couch, or table and 2 chairs) Large size is ~8x8 feet, x7 feet high (big enough for a king mattress, small dining table, or several upholstered chairs.)

and then theres this one

**_"Portable RF Haven"_**

What do you do when you find yourself in a hotel room, at a friend’s house, or any place where you don’t control the environment and you just need relief from wifi, smart meters, cellphones, and other wireless signals?

This tent, made from aluminized Mylar (well known for its good RF shielding ability, toughness, and light weight) is so handy, versatile, and effective you won’t want to travel without one. It is basically a tube, 8 feet long, with a perimeter of 10 feet, and open at both ends. Use it the way it comes as a Faraday sleeping bag or tent (tent will need some structural support which is not included, such as rope, stakes, tape, and so on). Or slit it open to a giant 8x10 foot sheet which is big enough for a king size bed blanket, ground cover, personal wrap, or window curtain. Trim it with scissors to make a poncho, skirt, or smaller enclosures for wifi modems, etc.

Will shield you from the hot rays of the sun, keep you warm when temperatures drop, reflects light and heat, and best of all offers >20 dB microwave shielding. Completely waterproof and windproof too.

We call it disposable, but you can certainly re-use it. Weighs only 7 ounces, and 6x8 inches and when folded. Stores safely for years, ready when you need it. Perfect for car, suitcase, camping or home use.

**If not, do you think they would work based on what you know?**

# Thread: Raw liver and the runs
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/raw-liver-and-the-runs.10284/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### healthman — 2014-03-22T15:48:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/raw-liver-and-the-runs.10284/post-117141>

Why would raw liver cause the severe runs?

### seanb4 — 2014-03-23T13:26:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/raw-liver-and-the-runs.10284/post-117348>

Parasites or bacteria from being raw? I had the same with raw fish

### Inger — 2014-03-23T14:11:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/raw-liver-and-the-runs.10284/post-117350>

I have eaten raw liver for years and it never caused me the runs. hmmm.. Was it good quality? I eat only grassfed beef liver, game liver, lambs liver or other high quality stuff. That said.... I have at work (waitress) had some regular beef liver raw the chefs offered me and that was not organic for sure - did not make me sick either. I have eaten raw liver that has stayed in my fridge for months on a wooden plate open to air.. and tasted pretty sour! But it did not make me sick either.

You might need to be careful with quality if your gut is not strong. Not plastic wrapped long time stored stuff either or if it tastes odd. Did it have an odd taste? It needs to taste nice - like fresh liver - you really can taste the quality IMO, especially if eaten plain and raw...

Maybe try to fry it gently on the outside and leave it raw inside thus killing some bacteria on the surface your gut is not used to yet? I have also heard if eating too much raw liver one could get the runs because the body just do not wanted that much vit. A (or something else in it who knows!), but I never had that issue. But I know someone else who had. Have you eaten much liver lately?
Liver is something I eat here and there, never regularly or force me to. When I get appetite of it I eat it! But that is not very often. I sometimes eat much and sometimes I go months without.

# Thread: Pam's Journal
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pams-journal.10286/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 58
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Pamela — 2014-03-22T17:39:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pams-journal.10286/post-117162>

I feel the first thing I want to say is I've been fat my whole life, even though this isn't necessarily true it is how I feel. I've been dieting and trying to lose weight since the age of 13 when my mother took my to Weight Watchers with her. That was the start of my yoyo dieting. Over my lifetime I have not been more than 50 lbs overweight but for me that is major, presently I am about 30 lbs overweight.

From the first WW experience and eating tuna fish every day for lunch in 6th grade there were several years I couldn't even look at the stuff!

For the next many years I went up and down and did all sorts of calorie restriction type diets which I'm sure set me up for where I am now.

One interesting thing I have noticed is my body comp has always been poor. Even breast development was never good, always saggy. Sad story but true.

I couldn't breast feed my baby because it was so extremely painful neither me or she would stand it. I think I gave her a few drops of colostrum in the first day or two and then we both quit and I had to put her on formula.

Fast forward to about 2008 when I had my first gallbladder attack, had no idea what it was. Walked into a doctors office on a Saturday morning, he listened to me and said it was a GB attack. He took some blood, liver enzymes were up, he told me to see my surgeon. Really? Who has a surgeon just hanging around? I told him thanks and that I'd look into it. Over the next few years I had a few more attacks and the last one was in Feb of 2011 when I was in Las Vegas for a trade show. This happened right after I had come off of 30 or so days on HCG. At the time I really didn't realize that the having no fat at all for 30 days and then putting fat in afterwords would have caused a doozy of an attack. It was the worst one, I actually had to go to the hospital where they admitted me and told me the stone had taken a wrong turn towards the pancreas and I needed to get the GB removed. So I came home and a month later had it removed.

In 2012 I was diagnosed with Hashimotos, who knows how long I've actually had it.

I've done the Leptin reset but never long enough to really see a change so that is what I am doing now and I am making myself commit to at least 6 months. 6 months can seem like forever when you don't like how you look but at least I'm not starving!

As of this writing I am at almost 1 month on the reset, eating one meal meats, the other main meal fish. I've been eating liver a few times a week too, I guess that was one thing WW did for me was get me used to eating liver, haha! Back when I first did WW we had to eat liver once a week.

That's all for now.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-22T18:09:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pams-journal.10286/post-117172>

Pam..........things are going to change for you. Next blog is going to be about your problem, ironically. Here is my advice for today inspired by someone statements earlier today: Disciplined? Discipline means creating an order within you. As you are now and have been all your life, you are a chaos. When you change your library of thoughts, you find order and change the way you perform.........and evolve the way you live.

Ignore the critics, only mediocrity is safe from ridicule. You should be dare to be different.

Sometimes the greatest thing to come out of all your hard work isn't what you get for it, but what you become for it.

I feel you will be doing great things here. Just some instinct I have...........

### sooperb — 2014-03-22T18:11:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pams-journal.10286/post-117173>

I wish you the best of luck, I've carted fat around for years so I can relate to your despair. There's no easy way for some of us to lose it, especially those in the "I've had my gallbladder removed" club  so, some of us have found our way here, well at least we are given multi-choice reasons for the "why" that I'd never heard before lol.

The first step is opening the door, then we have to start looking around, then who knows ...... It's not so much about weightloss as changing how you life and how to do it, it's quite a journey. At least you've made a start and there is plenty of help on offer here.

### Pamela — 2014-03-23T19:42:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pams-journal.10286/post-117418>

I've been wearing my blue blocking glasses at least an hour before bed and I think it's helping my sleep along with my mask. Wake up once at the 4-5 hour mark regularly, not sure why.

I also started taking vitamin K with my D and some CoQ10. Only thing is the CoQ10 I have is in soybean oil.

It was finally warming up a tad here so I went for a walk 2 days.

Last week I went to Las Vegas for a trade show and walked for 2 1/2 days. My body was shocked. I think inflammation happened since I could hardly move the next day. Now it's getting back to normal.

I also listened to the Dr Tim podcast in the Optimal program and had some good take aways. Glutathione is something I probably need and I am probably estrogen dominant. Now to figure out how to fix it.

All for now.

### caroline — 2014-03-24T09:14:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pams-journal.10286/post-117549>

Hi Pam ..... have you tested? supplements are expensive - so unless something is really indicated by your PCP - I would wait until you test.
Dr. K. aways says that a steak is the best supplement!

just a thought anyway....

How are you?

### Pamela — 2014-03-24T15:29:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pams-journal.10286/post-117584>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=117549)
>
> Hi Pam ..... have you tested? supplements are expensive - so unless something is really indicated by your PCP - I would wait until you test.
> Dr. K. aways says that a steak is the best supplement!
>
> just a thought anyway....
>
> How are you?

Thanks for asking Caroline, I am doing pretty good.

I feel good and am sleeping good, just working on the weight issues. I am considering doing the Dr. Tim hormone bootcamp. I did testing a few years ago on hormones so would need to get new ones. I have my 23&Me test in to the labs waiting for results. I had a full blood panel done last month which indicates inflammation with HScrp at 3.2. Not happy with that marker.

I am really enjoying the Optimal Reset webinars, there is some great information on those podcasts. I do quite a bit of driving so I use that time for my podcast education, it lessens the stress of city driving and gives me something to look forward to.

I'm half way through the latest blog on Brain Cancer and am listening to the CT podcast from the Optimal Reset. I find it very helpful to listen to Dr. K talk rather than try to wade through some of the high end science lingo that I just don't get.

Food did pretty good yesterday, I still find I have a sweet craving at night. Some dark chocolate helps.

I decided I'm not stepping on the scale any more than once a week, if that. There is nothing I can do differently so since I've committed to at least 6 months on the reset there is not much more to change other than tweek the main foods.

### sooperb — 2014-03-24T18:18:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pams-journal.10286/post-117609>

"I decided I'm not stepping on the scale any more than once a week, if that."

I'm sure that's what I should do, as my weight never goes down it just depresses me.

### caroline — 2014-03-24T22:39:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pams-journal.10286/post-117708>

Sounds good Pamela! your hsCRP sucks! have you thought of getting a saliva ASI done?

Are you doing C/T? it will help to reduce inflamation.

The webinars are great aren't they ..... I love to listen to Dr. K. ....I wish he could just read all the blogs to us! LOL

### Pamela — 2014-03-27T17:42:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pams-journal.10286/post-118295>

I finally started the CT protocol. I've been listening to the Cold Thermogenisis webinar as part of the Optimal Reset and he finally coaxed me to just start. Today I did the face dunks in 50 degree water. Did it several times when my timer didn't work right because I couldn't see what I was doing on my iphone. I did get 5 readings going from 14 seconds up to 17.6. Last time I did CT I think I bypassed this step altogether so I'll do this for a while first.

Sleep is sporadic with some nights doing much better than others. Hopefully as I progress with CT the sleep will even out and be better.

Continuing epi paleo eating, getting close to 8 hours of sleep with waking at least once. My lower back, sciatica, acts up and it bothers me during the night. When I put arnica gel on it the pain really lessens which is good.

Just bought a slew of meats from USWellness so my BAB will be strong.

It's interesting how much I know from the past several years reading this blog compared to my friends and associates. One fellow was talking to me yesterday about his gallbladder and should he have it removed. In discussing, he told me his doctor had him on a low fat diet and last weekend he went out and had a hamburger and cheesecake and had a mild GB attack. I told him maybe the low fast wasn't the right thing and eating the burger was ok without the bread. He looked at me wide eyed.

I didn't quite make a week staying away from the scale, went on today and was the same as I was 4 days ago. Not good, not bad. At least not going up. I will work to not get on it for a week.

### sooperb — 2014-03-27T18:51:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pams-journal.10286/post-118324>

The face dunks are problematic I think because maybe success depends more on how long you can hold your breath, for me, not that long face down in a sink of cold water lol. The cold doesn't bother me but I have to surface to breathe. I find I'm preferring the cold showers, I am surprising myself with my tolerance and I feel great afterwards to the point where I want to get back under! I don't feel the same about sitting in the bath, I'm usually glad to get out. It may be better if I could immerse all of me at one go. I've been eying my wheelie bin up, I'm not sure how you'd get out of one though or how long it would take to fill or whether I want to stand up for an hour.

### Pamela — 2014-03-28T21:51:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pams-journal.10286/post-118549>

OMG only 2 days on face dunks and I'm freezing all day. Crazy! Yesterday I was starving and today I am freezing, tired and boobs a little sore. All from about 10 face dunks at 17 or so seconds each. My skin is pretty cold to the touch when I'm done, I don't have a skin thermometer but the water is 50 degrees so its cold enough.

### Pamela — 2014-03-29T05:44:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pams-journal.10286/post-118615>

Thanks Doc, I'm not a science geek but what I got out of all that was my brain was not firing correctly. Doing the Leptin RX and now CT with epi paleo will align all the right signals together. I can see why this can easily take a year to do given it took a good 50 to really mess it up. I think I was born with some of it but as time went on the systems got more and more out of alignment causing over weight, hashi's, bad gallbladder and sciatica. ### Pamela — 2014-03-31T17:50:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pams-journal.10286/post-119082>

Into week 2 of face dunks and this week I'll do them longer than last week for a total of about 15 minutes. My skin definitely gets cold and I'm using 50 degree water. I'm at about week 5 of the Leptin RX, not noticing any weight loss but not surprised either. I have a little problem because I like to get some walking in as much as I can but when I do my sciatica starts to bother me and then it affects my sleep. I'm hoping that when I start CTing in the tub this will help the nerve from my back down leg.

I was reading another thread recently where the fellow did a Dear Dad entry. It prompted me to think and while I've thought about this alot I've never written it down.

Dear Dad,
I'm sorry I found the answers too late. You were depressed a good part of your lifetime, it was torture for you and for us and you assigned it to a wrong cause. The antidepressants didn't help and you knew that. What we didn't know until it was really too late and you were in the last months of life was that the real problem was, in my opinion, an undiagnosed thyroid disease that resulted in so many of your problems. Of course behind that was leptin resistance and everything else I've learned in the last few years. I'm hoping you are in a better place now and I am pretty sure you are doing quite well.

Dear Mom,
I'm sorry that you are stuck in the past way of handling weight and won't let go of the low fat and everything in moderation. I am glad though that you are reasonably happy and healthy at 79 years old and you are lucky to come from a long line of women who live into their 90's without losing their mental health.

Dear Husband,
I am so happy you listened to me and changed two major things in your life. You changed your schedule from being a night time computer programmer to a daytime worker on the correct circadian cycles. I'm glad you turn off all your electronics at 8pm. I'm really glad you don't eat your overflowing bowl of oatmeal with strawberries and bananas every day (because this is what you did when you were a runner) and now have a high protein and fat breakfast. I'm glad you are sleeping better and with a history of pretty bad insomnia this is a huge step forward. I feel happy that we will live long together.

Dear Daughter,
I am glad that I know what I do so I can guide you and hopefully you will listen. I am glad the first major step of removing gluten from your diet has helped you stay thin and beautiful. We have work ahead so at 35 you are as beautiful as you are at 21. Sometimes you don't like to listen to me but I know eventually you will.

Dear Me,
It's a good thing you have always been drawn to the cutting edge. Whether it be health or spiritual. You are going in the right direction.

### Pamela — 2014-04-07T20:14:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pams-journal.10286/post-120258>

Ok, I DID IT. First CT cold bath for 30 minutes at 50 degrees. As I recall from the first time I did this the hardest part was after coming out, I would be freezing for hours. So what I'm doing is after the bath I get out and dry off and then just hang out in the bathroom for another 15-20 minutes while my skin gets a little warmer before putting clothes back on. I think this helps. My hands are so cold right now I have fingerless gloves on as I type and they weren't submerged at all. In the CT protocol Jack says to do the bath 2-5 times a week so I'll start out with 3 times. My upper body is not in the water and I don't have ice packs to put on so for now it is a lower body CT and I lay back to get a good part of my back in the water. I have to say I feel like a beached whale!

Good news is I took a face photo before I started the reset and then yesterday 6 weeks later. There really is a nice change.

### Pamela — 2014-04-17T17:52:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pams-journal.10286/post-122176>

Spring has finally (kind of) sprung here in the midwest even though it snowed it's hopefully final snow the other day. I feel happier. There is something about those short light cycle days that I really hate, especially the getting dark at 4:30pm. Since changing the clocks it is now light up to about 7:30pm.

For my records here are things I am doing as of now:

\-- Sleeping straight for 6 hours (an improvement from 4-5) and then getting back to sleep for another 2. Getting 8 hours of sleep each night for the most part.

\-- Wake up and do leg stretches for my tight muscles upon waking. I have no idea what this is about but it has been this way for a few years. By doing the stretches as I lay in bed and stretch straight up it makes it better and I do this so that 20 years from now my muscles will still be working well.

\-- Go outside and look at the sun for a few minutes to set my circadian cycle. It is amazing how this small thing really works. My husband, who has had sleep problems for many years is now doing this and he says it is helping him quite a bit too.

\-- Eating within 1 hour of waking now, moving over to the After Leptin RX. Cravings are gone and my hunger is definitely under control. I'm not sure if I'm losing much fat yet but I figure if I do this correctly it should happen sooner or later.

\-- Trying to drink more water though sometimes this doesn't happen. I am drinking one 16oz cup of bone broth daily for some good electrons.

\-- CT 3 times a week right now, MWF, for 45 minutes each at 50 degrees. Getting in is a cringe but once I'm in it is fine. Warming up afterwards is sometimes harder than others. While I warm up without clothing I do some exercises like squats and various standing exercises. Next up will be to go out in the yard and run a few sprints.

\-- At night wearing my amber glasses at least an hour before I go to bed.

\-- Gut seems to be in good working order with decent stools. I was drinking my magnesium drink every night and after a few months of that I think I went into Mag overload because I had diareah. I stopped for a day and went back to normal so I'll have to do less of the mag as needed. I liked it because it helped with sleep.

\-- Skin looks pretty good.

\-- Ordered the Reservratol Dr. K recommended and will start taking that.

\-- I have pretty much stopped all vitamins and hormone supplements for right now. I do take Vit D & K just for maintenance. I had signed up with Dzugan and was taking a handful of pills and various creams and I just got tired of taking it not to mention I think my body had adjusted and I just didn't need it anymore. Now, with no hormone supplements I sleep well and am not having any hot flashes. Mood is pretty good. No headaches. The reason I will go back to this if I need to is to help lose this dang 30 lbs. I'll be dong a hormone blood panel as a next step soon.

\-- I feel I am on the right path finally. I feel good, am not hungry, don't crave things, can easily pass up treats, and even look forward to eating fish, haha. Eating fish 7 days a week for 1 meal in the form of salmon or canned sardines.

\-- I've listened to all webinars of the Optimal Reset and am going to start over and relisten. There were some great discussions there.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-05-20T21:22:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pams-journal.10286/post-127709>

The Role of Charge

Since a nucleophile is a species that is donating a pair of electrons, it’s reasonable to expect that its ability to donate electrons will increase as it becomes more electron rich, and decrease as it becomes more electron poor. So as electron density increases, so does nucleophilicity. There are 4 factors that affect nucleophilicity.
1\. charge:
electron density increases charge. My bet is you have lost more DHA than you added via oxidation
2\. electron negativity: Loss iodine and gain in fluoride or bromide means you can not use the electrons you do have because they tightly held
3\. solvents: Likely tied to dehydration. Check your BUN/creat ratios.
A [polar protic](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Protic_solvent) solvent like water can participate in hydrogen bonding with a nucleophile, creating a “shell” of solvent molecules around it like mobs of screaming teenage fans swarming the Beatles in 1962. In so doing, the nucleophile is considerably less reactive; everywhere it goes, its lone pairs of electrons are interacting with the electron-poor hydrogen atoms of the solvent.

The ability of nucleophiles to participate in hydrogen bonding decreases as we go down the periodic table. Hence fluoride is the strongest hydrogen bond acceptor, and iodide is the weakest. This means that the lone pairs of iodide ion will be considerably more “free” than those of fluoride, resulting in higher rates (and greater nucleophilicity).

4.steric hinderance: Not likely a layer in your issue.
the bulkier a given nucleophile is, the slower the rate of its reactions because it has a higher atomic mass......

Higher mass means less energy.................due to mass equivalence

\=

> 2\. electron negativity: Loss iodine and gain in fluoride or bromide means you can not use the electrons you do have because they tightly held
> 3\. solvents: Likely tied to dehydration. Check your BUN/creat ratios.
> A [polar protic](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Protic_solvent) solvent like water can participate in hydrogen bonding with a nucleophile, creating a “shell” of solvent molecules around it like mobs of screaming teenage fans swarming the Beatles in 1962. In so doing, the nucleophile is considerably less reactive; everywhere it goes, its lone pairs of electrons are interacting with the electron-poor hydrogen atoms of the solvent.
>
> The ability of nucleophiles to participate in hydrogen bonding decreases as we go down the periodic table. Hence fluoride is the strongest hydrogen bond acceptor, and iodide is the weakest. This means that the lone pairs of iodide ion will be considerably more “free” than those of fluoride, resulting in higher rates (and greater nucleophilicity).
>
> 4.steric hinderance: Not likely a layer in your issue.
> the bulkier a given nucleophile is, the slower the rate of its reactions because it has a higher atomic mass......
>
> Higher mass means less energy.................due to mass equivalence
>
> \=

Can we pin this responce somehow? I love this ...couldn't be more clear!

### Jack Kruse — 2014-05-21T02:31:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pams-journal.10286/post-127742>

DHA is the key.........electrons are always the answer. When people get confused what they should do they should read this..........

To your question... hmm. Maybe the strongest one is I am more centered now. Actually this is pretty awesome!!! Like something in me is so peaceful, I cannot even explain but I like a it a lot. It is almost a physical feeling around my heart and abdomen area. It can be thrown off by something that happens but i regain it fast... like such a strong well-being feeling.

And yeah some increased pain thrown in but it is like the pain belong to the increased joy too so it does not really matter. So - maybe in the end I just feel more alive? And I like that.... I enjoy so much to feel alive - that is.

I feel all the natural darkness and seafood and sunlight has made me more sensitive to life. and, I see more possibilities, like my view is broadening?

Welcome! RE: Subjective experience of life since going EpiPaleo - Well, I thought I knew my body and its reactions to things pretty well BEFORE going Epi-Paleo, but the sensitivity has intensified. Im a lot more scientific about finding causality, and notice the effect of even a tiny change (such as forgetting to turn off wi-fi at night, or too many carbs) as soon as it occurs.

In general- feel much awesome-er all around - little aches and pains are gone, more energy. Also I feel and look younger as well.

# Thread: Transcendental Meditation-your experience
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/transcendental-meditation-your-experience.10289/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 42
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Sammy — 2014-03-23T08:49:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/transcendental-meditation-your-experience.10289/post-117328>

I am interested in transcendetal meditation to help me destress and handle stress better.

Anyone here practice this on a daily basis? How does it work for you? Are there other types of meditation or activities that you routinely do to help de-stress?

How do I get started??

All types of meditation courses come up on a quick search in google.
Is it necessary to enrol in a course to learn meditation?
Is there more to it than breathing in and out and going "ommmm........."

Thanks you guys!

### Josh — 2014-03-23T09:07:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/transcendental-meditation-your-experience.10289/post-117332>

I tried TM when I was 8 I think...It did not click for me. I have no idea how it is being taught these days. It is not necessary to enroll in a course from my experience and the support of other people who have the "knack" in a technique is helpful at the beginning for sure. I started with the meditations of Osho which do not really require anyone to teach. He suggested that most people are now a little wound up to just sit and watch, so he developed meditations to take people from movement and expression to stillness. After a few years of these, I found that I could sit and watch for long periods and I still sit every day after 24 years. It is the center of my life. Anything that gets you into your body is a good preparation including yoga, dancing, exercise, etc..All of the people I know who meditate regularly had something or someone that introduced or seduced them into exploring their inner world. It was an accident for me as I experienced it. I was in a new martial arts school and they would sit zazen after training. I had never been able to sit comfortably and they had zen benches for kneeling. As soon as I sat on one, I disappeared and was shocked when I opened my eyes and people told me that an hour had passed. I practiced irregularly after that. However, slowly it became easier to get to that "space" and I explored it from many directions with many techniques both active and passive. Meditation is a great adventure...worth the price of admission which is free or everything you have depending on how you look at it....off to the my midnight weekend sit....

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-23T10:31:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/transcendental-meditation-your-experience.10289/post-117337>

> [Sammy said:](/goto/post?id=117328)
>
> I am interested in transcendetal meditation to help me destress and handle stress better.
>
> Anyone here practice this on a daily basis? How does it work for you? Are there other types of meditation or activities that you routinely do to help de-stress?

AFAIK, "transcendental meditation" it's a term people use for a form of meditation where attention is focused by means of a _mantra_ -- a repeated phrase. This, as you say, isn't the only form of meditation.

As for these phrases, it's been said that it's irrelevant what the phrase is -- anything will do. (But I should think it likely that the people who first started doing this were repeating the phrase because they thought it had special significance.)

You could focus your attention in other ways. For example, you could take an object in the palm of your hand -- a raisin, say -- and just look at it. Or you could look at the flame of a candle. You could just stop what you're doing briefly and allow yourself to _listen_ -- to become aware of all the different sounds around you. You could also focus attention on your breath.

All such practices are sometimes referred to as "concentration practice". In these traditional systems, there's a belief that once you can keep an unwavering attention you will be able to engage in more complex meditational practices, such as visualization (being able to look at an image of a Tantric deity in your mind, for example).

Anyway ... "Concentration" isn't perhaps a good word, since it usually implies effort. That's not what you want. The purpose is to quiet the mind, and this is done by just gently easily returning to the practice as soon as you notice you're distracted.

> How do I get started??

There's a guided meditation using the breath available as a free podcats in iTunes. You might try that. Google:

"mindfulness of breath meditation for beginners itunes"

> All types of meditation courses come up on a quick search in google.
> Is it necessary to enrol in a course to learn meditation?

No, it's not necessary to take a course, but you probably want guidance of some sort. It might be nothing more than using guided meditation recordings, like the one I mentioned above, or reading a book. Here's one that intended for beginners:

[http://www.amazon.com/The-5-Minute-Meditator-Quick-meditations/dp/0749924594/](http://www.amazon.com/The-5-Minute-Meditator-Quick-meditations/dp/0749924594/)

> Is there more to it than breathing in and out and going "ommmm........."

If you are using your breath as something to focus on, then you shouldn't think of it as "breathing in and out" as if it were something you were doing. You don't need to _do_ breathing: it just happens. The point is to watch it. In fact, you probably shouldn't try to regulate your breath or breathe in any special way.

As for "om.." well, there you go, that's a sound seen as having special significance. It's apparently pronounced more like "aum" and, I think, is seen by Buddhists as a sound that contains every other sound, and as therefore representative of the macrocosm. But I may have that quite wrong. ...

J. D. Salinger has a pair of short stories, _Frannie and Zooey_, about a girl who keeps repeating a sound, or in her case a phrase, seen by her as significant:

[http://www.amazon.com/Franny-Zooey-J-D-Salinger/dp/0316769495/](http://www.amazon.com/Franny-Zooey-J-D-Salinger/dp/0316769495/)

Here it's the "Jesus Prayer" (_kyrie eleison_) [used by some Eastern Orthodox believers as a repeated phrase](http://www.amazon.com/Way-Pilgrim-Continues-His/dp/0385468148/). (Presumably, meditational techniques made their way into the practices of Christian monks and hermits from eastern religions at some time in the early Middle Ages.)

Frannie's brother sees the practice isn't helping her, and seems to suggest that without understanding behind it, the phrase in itself doesn't actually have an autonomous efficacy, and he gets her to stop. It's a very clever book.

Anyway ... there we go. You don't need a course, and you don't need a specially significant sound. And you could probably wander into some strange psychological areas if you took it too seriously in the wrong way.

For most people nowadays it's just a way of quietening down. As you do so you become aware of the restlessness of the mind. There's always something distracting our attention.

You asked at the beginning about other activities. There's Tai Chi -- that's sometimes called "meditation in movement". There's something meditational about many "somatic" (body/mind) disciplines, too.

### Sammy — 2014-03-23T10:43:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/transcendental-meditation-your-experience.10289/post-117338>

Thank you for such a comprehensive response Gagnrad. I agree, I think there are many many ways of meditating. The aim is to quiet the mind. Finding one that works for you will require experimenting with different styles and forms. I actually find it very very difficult to meditate. Staying still is hard for me. I have even fallen asleep in my attempts to meditate many times. Maybe taichi that incorporates movement is more suited for me, I will look into that.

### sooperb — 2014-03-23T11:46:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/transcendental-meditation-your-experience.10289/post-117344>

I remember it cost me a lot of money to be given a mantra and participate in a lot of mumbo jumbo! The meditation side was very useful though and as a whole I think everyone should be taught how to do it, it is scientifically proven to change your brain wave patterns.

If you find it difficult, ask around for a recommendation for a relaxation CD, there are 100s if not 1000s but they aren't all equal. Anita Goswhani has done some really good ones or look out for a Yoga Nidra, relaxation by relaxing your body one bit at a time. Many are free on iTunes.

Whatever it takes, it's worth the effort to experience the stillness and peace. Om shanti.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-23T16:08:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/transcendental-meditation-your-experience.10289/post-117358>

> [Sammy said:](/goto/post?id=117340)
>
> Thank you for such a comprehensive response Gagnrad. I agree, I think there are many many ways of meditating. The aim is to quiet the mind. Finding one that works for you will require experimenting with different styles and forms. I actually find it very very difficult to meditate. Staying still is hard for me. I have even fallen asleep in my attempts to meditate many times. Maybe taichi that incorporates movement is more suited for me, I will look into that.

Does it matter if you fall asleep?

Perhaps someone's answer to that depends on the conception he has of the activity. Myself I can't see it matters.

It's sometimes said that the aim is to be relaxed but alert. (And, thinking about that, isn't that how Venus is supposed to be in the Botticelli painting in another thread here?) I guess falling asleep violates that. There is something interesting about that: in our current conceptions we tend to see relaxed and alert as opposite poles … but maybe they don't have to be.

Awareness … My sister, who's a nurse, tells me that some patients from the Far East will refuse painkillers. They dull the senses.

F. Matthias Alexander, a Tasmanian, who independently discovered a form of "bodywork" that has many parallels with meditational practice wouldn't take them in his final illness either:

[http://www.amazon.com/Mans-Supreme-Inheritance-Conscious-Civilization/dp/0952557401/](http://www.amazon.com/Mans-Supreme-Inheritance-Conscious-Civilization/dp/0952557401/)

I've tried AT, and it's an interesting practice, and while it has some interesting parallels with these traditional practices is totally secular and agnostic. It's much used by musicians and actors (and taught at [RADA](http://www.rada.ac.uk)) and, interestingly, used by the Israeli Airforce -- who obviously know a thing or two about a person's need to respond to what's going on around him in a calm manner at high speed.

There's also Feldenkrais and a number of other disciplines.

Mr. Sisson (of Mark's Daily Apple) recently described the different forms of bodywork as "woo-woo" (whatever that means) and implied he was justifiably sceptical of them. It is, of course, easy for any of us to be "sceptical" of matters we don't understand, and that aren't even easily captured in language, and experiences we've never had.

But that's "standard paleo" and its limitations for you.

If you want a more open-minded description of some of these disciplines try Todd Durkin's account here:

[http://www.amazon.com/IMPACT-Body-Plan-Muscle-Flatten/dp/1609611829/](http://www.amazon.com/IMPACT-Body-Plan-Muscle-Flatten/dp/1609611829/)

Todd was an American footballer who suffered a really critical injury and had to go beyond the usual methods to get back to normal functioning.

All this is quite interesting, and turns our assumptions upside-down.

But I was talking about awareness before I rambled … I guess you can do anything "mindfully".

I think the book I linked in the previous post (The Five Minute Meditator) suggests trying to eat your dinner mindfully now and then -- paying attention to every mouthful.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-23T16:27:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/transcendental-meditation-your-experience.10289/post-117366>

I do usually in the AM.

### Josh — 2014-03-23T17:43:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/transcendental-meditation-your-experience.10289/post-117378>

Honestly, one of my favorite books is this one because cops and soldiers like surgeons need something that works...period or someone may die fast....

[http://www.amazon.com/Meditation-Warriors-Practical-Soldiers-Martial/dp/1490594035](http://www.amazon.com/Meditation-Warriors-Practical-Soldiers-Martial/dp/1490594035)

This is a classic compilation of Osho. In the world, there are all kinds of people doing all kinds of things in his name. Much of it without deep connection to his teachings and all good PR as a master with a wicked sense of humor such as his wouold say.....

[http://www.amazon.com/Meditation-The-First-Last-Freedom/dp/0312336632](http://www.amazon.com/Meditation-The-First-Last-Freedom/dp/0312336632)

These are my 2 favorite that I have done daily for several years each with good results...simple, instructions included, people talking about them on youtube...ask me questions if anyone is interested.

[http://www.amazon.com/OSHO-Nadabrah...d\_sim\_b\_7?ie=UTF8&refRID=0YBYRG5R2GGVH7H052CW](http://www.amazon.com/OSHO-Nadabrahma-Meditation-Deuter/dp/B0000009CC/ref=pd_sim_b_7?ie=UTF8&refRID=0YBYRG5R2GGVH7H052CW)

[http://www.amazon.com/Osho-Kundalini-Meditation-Deuter/dp/B0000009C8/ref=pd\_bxgy\_m\_img\_y](http://www.amazon.com/Osho-Kundalini-Meditation-Deuter/dp/B0000009C8/ref=pd_bxgy_m_img_y)

For the daring, there is...started at 12, too early for what I experienced...later di several years. My Dad did it every day for 12 years. He would stop the car on the way to Maine to go in the woods and leave us to wait. SOmetimes once one starts a technique, one needs topay attention and keep going until things come to a new place.

[http://www.amazon.com/OSHO-Dynamic-Meditation-Deuter/dp/B0000009C6/ref=pd\_bxgy\_m\_img\_y](http://www.amazon.com/OSHO-Dynamic-Meditation-Deuter/dp/B0000009C6/ref=pd_bxgy_m_img_y)

Eckhart Tolle has many videos and techniques to bring one to witness oneself and what is happening, just search google.

Goenka has set up places to teach Vipassana around the world. A particularly inspiring example is the movie "The Dhamma Brothers" where they work with prisoners...

Jon Kabat Zinn has a good starter program that he has studied at harvard. I have taught this in a drug and alcohol rehab and it works well. It includes an optional yoga program. There are many studies online and his source I believe is the work of Thich Nhat Han a Vietnamese Buddhist monk with a worldwide following as well.

There are many "stasang" teachers from the lineage of Ramana Maharshi. I sat with one who is now deceased for a couple of years named Poonjaji. Living teachers include Gangaji, Mooji and others.

Enjoy the adventure...

### Josh — 2014-03-23T17:51:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/transcendental-meditation-your-experience.10289/post-117380>

On TM, I have known people that have been close to Maharishi Mahesh Yogi and my teacher has talked to me about his tradition and his master who was from the area I lived in for a while. There is something authentic in the tradition and it takes a longer road. Deepak Chopra was an intimate disciple and writes about his experiences and separation.

[http://archive.chopra.com/wordsdeepakarchives/threemaharishis](http://archive.chopra.com/wordsdeepakarchives/threemaharishis)

I feel that the source of one's practice has some relevance and many people start spontaneously through things experienced during sex, trauma, sports, being in nature, etc...

### Josh — 2014-03-23T17:54:40+0200

I was literally preached at about meditation from age 7 on by the old man who has showed up here. While I tried things. the sales pitch turned me off and it took me longer to find my way to a meaningful practice. When I got it, I was humble both about what I had missed and how hard it is to share it in a meaningful way with people who do not have relevant experiences already.....It took me almost 20 years to discover it for myself and realize what I had missed...

### Josh — 2014-03-23T17:58:33+0200

As Pema Chodron says to students and writes in one of my favorite books: "When Things Fall Apart" which I read weekly as my mother was dying the last 6 months,

"When people ask me how long it will take, I tell them figure the rest of this life..."

### Josh — 2014-03-23T18:45:19+0200

The fascinating thing is that Tom Brown Jr's teacher actually shares how it was an old Taoist Chinese man that his teacher met in the forest somewhere in the Western US that helped his teacher ("grandfather") finish his "spiritual" quest. The man had come over with the the railroad workers. The first half of the book is the story, the second half is meditation and relaxation techniques.

If you think he has a tourist program, he sends his advanced students into the wilderness in Montana in winter with nothing to live for a month......

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-23T21:00:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/transcendental-meditation-your-experience.10289/post-117429>

> [Josh said:](/goto/post?id=117420)
>
> One last option from my list of favorites. Thomas Hanna was one of Feldenkreis's student and his guided work is my favorite...

Moishe Feldenkrais apparently knew martial arts, particularly judo. That went into his system, and he also drew on F. M. Alexander's work and a number of other disciplines. I have read that he would use manipulation on people and had a very keen sense of touch, so that as soon as he felt resistance he'd go back the other way. It's been suggested that there's something rather judo-like about that refusal to force things.

I think with some therapies where there's manipulation it can be rather forceful and not always particularly helpful. I can remember a long while back listening somewhat aghast to a podcast as Rob Wolf explained how a physical therapist had _stood_ on his pelvis and left him in agony. I guess more forceful techniques may have their place, but …

Feldenkrais seems more to have tried to re-awaken functionality than to put people's body parts where he thought they should go. And here's another theme, common to meditational practice and to several of these systems -- _minimal effort_. It's also there in Taoism (and Tai Chi which is grounded in Taoist thought, of course) in the form of the notion of _wu wei_ (effortless effort). But you will know more about this than me, having done more Tai Chi and in Hong Kong at that.

There's an interesting lecture by John Nicholls, an AT teacher in New York, online where he attempts to relate some of these disciplines. The diagram (Appendix 1) right at the end is quite fun. He's got all these different practices joined by lines, according to how they relate to each other, and what concepts and practices they share.

[http://www.johnnichollsatnyc.com/wp-content/uploads/2010/10/ATLargerCnxt-STAT-Mem-1.pdf](http://www.johnnichollsatnyc.com/wp-content/uploads/2010/10/ATLargerCnxt-STAT-Mem-1.pdf)

So you _can_ trace certain links between "depth psychology" and "bodywork' and "eastern religion" -- and also western esotericism, such as Rudolf Steiner and your old friend Gurdjieff. And this isn't always "influence" but I think sometimes simply because it's the same human reality being dealt with.

But, of course, the nice thing, particularly for modern Westerners who shy away from some of that, is that you don't have to be religious or esoteric -- or believe in the doctrines of particular psychologists.

### Josh — 2014-03-23T22:37:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/transcendental-meditation-your-experience.10289/post-117461>

My wife is in the 2nd year of a 4 year Feldenkreis Guild training. Moishe's Judo work was rare and he discusses it in one of his books and how the tradition has fallen apart, lowered its standards. How he was literally recruited by the Japanese in Paris is an amazing story. Apparently Moishe was not unfamiliar with Gurdjieff. I found this a little while ago and thought I would put it here:

> **Einstein: When Language Interferes**
> “The words of the language as they are written or spoken do not seem to play any role in my mechanism of thought. The psychical entities which seem to serve as elements of thought are certain signs and more or less clear images which . . . are in my case of visual and some of muscular type. \[These elements take part in\] a rather vague play… in which they can be voluntarily reproduced and combined… This combinatory play seems to be the essential feature in productive thought, before there is any connection with logical construction in words or other kinds of sign which can be communicated to others . . . In a stage where words intervene at all, they are, in my case, purely auditive, but they interfere only in a secondary stage.” [Hare Brain](http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/0060955414/ref=as_li_ss_tl?ie=UTF8&tag=thefeldemetho-20&linkCode=as2&camp=217145&creative=399349&creativeASIN=0060955414), p. 56.
>
> **Feldenkrais: I make a special effort not to think in words.**
> “When I am presented with a trouble in function, I make a special effort not to think in words. I try not to think logically and in correctly formed sentences. It has become a habit with me to imagine the relevant nervous structures by seeing them with my mind’s eye. I imagine a part which produces a flow of fluid. Part of the travel of the fluid is electrical, then becomes chemical, and again electrical. After many transformations the flow will end in a muscular contraction, and the muscular play will result in some apparent outside action involving the body, or parts of it, that will affect or transform the immediate environment. Sometimes I am stuck at a point where I cannot imagine the pattern of the flow, nor the possible obstacles in its way. Then I ask, is the obstacle a diffusion, damping, deviation, loss of impetus, break of continuity, or an impossibility of one of the transformations.
>
> I have found this way of imagining so fruitful that I cannot do without it. It often shows me where my knowledge is insufficient so that I know exactly what I am after and therefore in which books I am likely to find the information. I form a working theory and change it in the light of new observations I must add to make the theory work. This mode of thinking is often successful in situations where specialists with greater knowledge than mine have failed. Nobody is omniscient enough to think mechanically.” [Body Awareness](http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/1883319080/ref=as_li_ss_tl?ie=UTF8&tag=thefeldemetho-20&linkCode=as2&camp=217145&creative=399349&creativeASIN=1883319080), p. 16

I had only an introductory training af 3 weeks, 7 days a week with about 6 hours on the floor per day doing FI. I have used Hannah's sessions to continue my explorations when I get inspired and have taught them to clients occasionally. I have had about 25 FI sessions over the years and look forward to my wife being able to do them. Most martial arts schools and yoga studios miss this level of "self sensing" and awareness. They start with too much doing and effort. I spent some times learning Pilates as well and it teaches self-sensing from a different angle. It is really thrilling as it comes together.

As far as strong manipulation goes, if it is done really well with skill, nothing touches it. I have experienced things that others have worked on with everything from acupuncture to FI and rolfing resolve in a minute. However truly skilled and courageous practitioners are now few and far between. Also, after a big structural shift, one needs to maintain one's redox and potentially have other people balance the more subtle energetic, facial, muscular and structural imbalances to achieve the desired result.

One of the first bodyworkers who worked on me was from the Alexander world and I appreciated his work a great deal when I was a teenager. He has now become an Oriental Medicine practitioner.

The whole issue of "engramic" information "held" in the body is an interesting subject approached differently by different traditions. I feel that the key is the holgraphic nature of memory as it is help in the 3D collagen matrix throughout the body. One can access things from many places and they can move around. There is a separate question of that which may be "connected" to a given body rather than "in" the body. One can skin this cat many different ways in my experience and I now feel that the highest good is empowering people to do their own work in a conscious state. This creates conscious evolution or learning, rather than just moving the furniture around so to speak....

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-24T10:45:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/transcendental-meditation-your-experience.10289/post-117556>

> [Josh said:](/goto/post?id=117461)
>
> As far as strong manipulation goes, if it is done really well with skill, nothing touches it.....

Well, maybe. There's a lot of related issues here. One could again say that the "soft martial arts" are not all softness. Indeed, one very famous Scottish Tai Chi teacher over here likes to make that point:

> The essential combat theory of Tai Chi Chuan is to use softness or Yin to overcome hardness or Yang and to use hardness or Yang to overcome softness or Yin. So rather than blocking the opponent's attacks we divert or redirect them using evasion and/or footwork at the same time. This is using softness to overcome hardness. The attack has then become 'dead' force and has changed from Yang to Yin. At this point we must also change from Yin to Yang by striking (Yang) the vital points of our opponent (Yin). This is using hardness against softness. In order to train this evasion it is necessary to do a lot of practise on the pushing hands exercises I mentioned earlier. ...

[http://www.taichichuan.co.uk/information/articles/tai\_chi\_gladiator.html](http://www.taichichuan.co.uk/information/articles/tai_chi_gladiator.html)

So it's both ways round and hard and soft. Partly, I guess it's that Tai Chi is more a health activity nowadays and less something used for combat, although it was originally used by the Emperor's guards. Docherty himself won an all-comers South East Asian Martial Arts Championship with Tai Chi.

But the wider issue of using minimum effort for what you want to do interests me. All the time all round us people use far more effort for things than they need to. You see them, for example, huffing and puffing and contracting all over the place just to get out of a chair ... yet if you do it in a coordinated, balanced and smooth way it takes _almost no_ effort at all to stand up from a chair.

It turns out that as soon as most people merely _think_ of doing some action they start to tighten in various muscle-groups unecessarily. Fascinating!

They "get in their own way". It seems to be exacerbated by modern educational systems that emphasize effort. The truth is that much of the time one needs not to "try harder" (a familiar school report comment) but to work smarter. I recall a lecture by Alan Watts (whom I don't actually care much for) in which he mentions a boy in his class at school who would clench his teeth and grunt every time he tried to read. Wasted effort.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-24T11:51:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/transcendental-meditation-your-experience.10289/post-117560>

Here's a Zen saying that also pulls in some of these themes:

> When standing, just stand. When sitting, just sit. Above all, don't wobble.

There you've got "being in the present" -- just doing whatever it is you're doing. We tend always to be thinking about the next thing and anticipating outrselves. And I think the notion of "minimum effort" is in there, too: this is when everything comes right.

Zen in Japanese is the equiavalent of Ch'an in Chinese. There's a view that the Ch'an form of Buddhism in China was actually much influenced by Taoist thought.

### siajanai — 2014-03-24T14:54:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/transcendental-meditation-your-experience.10289/post-117574>

> [Sammy said:](/goto/post?id=117328)
>
> I am interested in transcendetal meditation to help me destress and handle stress better.
>
> Anyone here practice this on a daily basis? How does it work for you? Are there other types of meditation or activities that you routinely do to help de-stress?
>
> How do I get started??
>
> All types of meditation courses come up on a quick search in google.
> Is it necessary to enrol in a course to learn meditation?
> Is there more to it than breathing in and out and going "ommmm........."
>
> Thanks you guys!

I learned TM 40+ years about and I've been pretty regular with it ever since.

The thing about TM is that it is meant to be learned from a trained teacher, not from a book. It is meant to be a single practice that distills all the wisdom of the teacher of the founder of TM, Maharishi Mahesh Yogi, into a single technique that can be easily mastered. You can read more about maharishi's teacher here: [https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brahmananda\_Saraswati](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brahmananda_Saraswati) -he was the first person to hold the position of "Shankaracharya of Jyotirmath" \[head swami of northern India\] in over 150 years. The entire TM organization is dedicated to his memory.

TM is taught over a period of 4 days because that is about the fastest time that Maharishi could devise to teach it as he traveled from village to village in India back in the mid 1950's.

TM teachers undergo training that teaches them how to teach TM just as Maharishi did.

the basic outline of the course is:

you learn to meditate then go home and practice.
Then you come back the next day and, based on your first day's experience meditating, the teacher tells you things. Then you go home and practice.
Then you come back the next day and, based on your two day's experience meditating, the teacher tells you things. Then you go home and practice.
Then you come back the next day and, based on your three day's experience meditating, the teacher tells you things. By this time, you should have a "fairly clear" idea what it means to "do" TM and what the long-term outcome might be. Then you go home and practice.

The formal TM program has been expanded a bit since 1955. These days, with local teachers in most large towns, they recommend that you return to your local center once-a-week for the first month, and once-a-month for the first year in order to get your meditation checked. The 3 days instruction I mentioned above is sometimes called the "Three Nights of Checking." When you come back to your local center, you get a simple procedure simply called "checking" which is kinda a mini version of the above. In most countries, the first year of checking is free. In the USA, it is free for the rest of your life.

That is a very simplified explanation of the TM teaching process. In fact, the TM teacher spends the entire 5 months, in-residence, rehearsing the exact words, hand-gestures, body language and tone of voice he or she will use when teaching and answering questions, in accordance with how Maharishi Mahesh Yogi set up the training. When people (such as Deepak Chopra) claim they have devised their own teaching method for meditation, recall that they were never trained as TM teachers, and it is the difference between someone taking a play they have seen and trying to reproduce it from memory (or make up a new one they think is better, just because), versus someone rehearsing for 5 months, 7 hours a day, 7 days a week, with the playwright (or someone he trained) in order to present the original play as the author intended.

The "technique" of TM is so simple, that many people think that they can learn it from a book or over the internet, and no-doubt, a few do. However, when the American Heart Association reviewed all the research on how meditation or relaxation effects high blood pressure, they found that despite there being huge numbers of studies published each month on mindfulness and other practices, compared to only one or two studies published each month on TM, the difference in quality of research, and consistency of outcome made it impossible to recommend other meditation practices for the clinical treatment of high blood pressure. As it stands right now (and they just updated their report without changing the conclusions on meditation/relaxation), they say "The overall evidence supports that TM modestly lowers BP." ... "TM may be used in clinical practice to treat hypertension" \[as a secondary treatment\]... \[due to the poor quality of research and inconsistent results\] "other meditation techniques are not recommended in clinical practice to lower BP at this time." See Summary and Clinical Recommendations \[for meditation/relaxation\] in: [http://hyper.ahajournals.org/content/61/6/1360.full?sid=a3b8b00a-919b-4027-8ac5-15317c283df1](http://hyper.ahajournals.org/content/61/6/1360.full?sid=a3b8b00a-919b-4027-8ac5-15317c283df1)

TM's effects can be noted literally over night, or it might take months or even a year or two for YOU to notice a change in yourself. A few months before I learned (during teh summer between high school and college), a couple of my worst enemies in high school, who were constantly tormenting me and bullying me, happened to learn TM. The effect on them was crazy. The Friday before they learned, they were in full-on bully mode. By the next Monday, something had changed: I said or did something in my usual defensive-obnoxious way that would normally have triggered a violent reaction, even a beating, and they just looked at me and laughed and walked off. Very, VERY strange.

On the other hand, I learned TM a few months later and didn't notice any change in myself. The TM center was simply a safe place for a lonely kid to hang, so I continued to meditate regularly, just because. And then one day, I was chatting on the phone with this cute high school girl I was madly in love with. She hadn't spoken to me since school let out. She suddenly interrupted me and said "Whatever you have been doing different for the past few months, it has changed you. You are suddenly years older. It is very attractive." Needless to say, I had a new reason to continue doing TM, but to be honest, I think it was as long as another year (or more) before I actually noticed changes in myself.

There's a saying amongst TM teachers: the more stressed the student is, the faster teh changes tend to show up.

Recently, researchers went to Uganda and taught TM to a bunch of war refugees. These were people who had fled into one of the poorest countries in the world because where they were born was even worse. They were victims of the most horrible violence: watching their husbands murdered, then being gang-raped while their children watched, etc. -you know: the stuff that war movies leave out. They were living in a country where they couldn't speak the language, and couldn't find a job. Sleeping on the floors of churches, or in rude mud huts ("temporary rental shelters") or under bushes. They were completely in survival mode, barely able to procure food from teh relief agencies. In fact, researchers paid them to show up for the initial pre-test by giving away bags of cooked beans. You get the idea....

After working out details (30% of the people weren't really interested in learning TM, they just wanted food), researchers managed to devise a "delayed start control group" study with 21 people who learned TM immediately matched with 21 controls who would learn TM at the end of the study.

The results were overwhelmingly good:

**Reduction in posttraumatic stress symptoms in Congolese refugees practicing transcendental meditation.**
[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/23568415](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/23568415)

90 percent of the TM group tested non-symptomatic for PTSD after only 30 days practice. None of the control group improved that much, and the average score continued to get worse.

A second study tested a new group of TMers.

**Significant Reductions in Posttraumatic Stress Symptoms in Congolese Refugees Within 10 days of Transcendental Meditation Practice.**
[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/24515537](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/24515537)

They showed almost the same levels of improvement after 3o-days of TM but researchers found that 2/3 of the reduction in PTSD symptoms took place after only 10 days of TM.

It's pilot studies but already the United Nations has contacted the David Lynch Foundation (which sponsored the study) to explore how fast the program could be expanded to meet the needs of teh 100 million Africans thought to be suffering from PTSD or other stress-related illnesses. See the last minute or so of the top video: [http://www.huffingtonpost.com/2014/02/21/meditation-ptsd\_n\_4825609.html](http://www.huffingtonpost.com/2014/02/21/meditation-ptsd_n_4825609.html)

Anyway, no guarantees that the above research will be verified, it is very possible/probable that it is an "outlier" for the effects TM will have on PTSD, but the advantage for TM is that mindfulness practices take 2 months to teach, and then researchers do testing on the effects of mindfulness after 3 months practice. TM takes 4 days to teach, and researchers have found striking results within 10 days of practice. No-one even attempts to test the effects of mindfulness 14 days after the first lesson.

TM is taught by a not-for-profit organization and the current fee has been lowered a quite a bit from its high. Right now, TM is $960 to learn for adults and $360 for 10-18 year olds and full-time college students. The David Lynch Foundation gets celebrities like Ringo Star and Paul McCartney to do fundraising concerts

and then they hire TM teachers for a set rate to go and teach disadvantaged people for free. Browse the videos at the DLF website for more info: [http://www.davidlynchfoundation.com](http://www.davidlynchfoundation.com)

Hope this gives you a taste of what TM is about and what is going on with it. You can find your local TM center via the international website: [http://www.tm.org](http://www.tm.org)

Hope this helps (without being Too Much Info).

### siajanai — 2014-03-24T15:06:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/transcendental-meditation-your-experience.10289/post-117577>

> [Josh said:](/goto/post?id=117332)
>
> I tried TM when I was 8 I think...It did not click for me. I have no idea how it is being taught these days. It is not necessary to enroll in a course from my experience and the support of other people who have the "knack" in a technique is helpful at the beginning for sure. I started with the meditations of Osho which do not really require anyone to teach. He suggested that most people are now a little wound up to just sit and watch, so he developed meditations to take people from movement and expression to stillness. After a few years of these, I found that I could sit and watch for long periods and I still sit every day after 24 years. It is the center of my life. Anything that gets you into your body is a good preparation including yoga, dancing, exercise, etc..All of the people I know who meditate regularly had something or someone that introduced or seduced them into exploring their inner world. It was an accident for me as I experienced it. I was in a new martial arts school and they would sit zazen after training. I had never been able to sit comfortably and they had zen benches for kneeling. As soon as I sat on one, I disappeared and was shocked when I opened my eyes and people told me that an hour had passed. I practiced irregularly after that. However, slowly it became easier to get to that "space" and I explored it from many directions with many techniques both active and passive. Meditation is a great adventure...worth the price of admission which is free or everything you have depending on how you look at it....off to the my midnight weekend sit....

The adult form of TM is never taught to 8-year-olds. In fact, it is almost unheard of for anyone under 10 to be taught the adult technique, and that is people who stalked up to Maharishi Mahesh Yogi at the age of 9 and insisted that they were ready to learn, so he taught them himself.

There's a kid's technique for 4-10 year-olds, but it isn't the adult technique.

And TM has been taught the same way since the TM teacher training course that the Beatles walked out of in 1967 and no accredited TM teacher in the past 40+ years has taught TM differently since all TM teachers have made a promise to only teach TM as authorized by the international accreditation organization that the old monk set up.

It may sound a bit obsessive-compulsive, but the strict attention to detail pays off. Recently, the federal government of Brazil announced that they were setting aside funds to train 48,000 TM teachers, one for each public school in Brazil, so that all 45 million school age kids could learn TM for free.

### siajanai — 2014-03-24T15:24:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/transcendental-meditation-your-experience.10289/post-117581>

> [Gagnrad said:](/goto/post?id=117560)
>
> Here's a Zen saying that also pulls in some of these themes:
>
>
>
> There you've got "being in the present" -- just doing whatever it is you're doing. We tend always to be thinking about the next thing and anticipating outrselves. And I think the notion of "minimum effort" is in there, too: this is when everything comes right.
>
> Zen in Japanese is the equiavalent of Ch'an in Chinese. There's a view that the Ch'an form of Buddhism in China was actually much influenced by Taoist thought.

Dhyan Buddhism entered China and became Ch'an Buddhism. Ch'an Buddhism entered Japan and became Zen Buddhism.

Maharishi Mahesh Yogi referred to Transcendental Meditation as "the simplest and most important of the dhyan techniques." Outside of the West and Tibet, Buddhists generally don't insist that there's a "Zen"-only way or a "Ch'an"-only way to meditate, and there's plenty of practicing Buddhists who learn TM. In fact, there is a TM teacher who specializes in teaching Buddhist priests and monks to meditate, being a Buddhist priest himself: [http://www.tm.org/blog/meditation/buddhist-monks/](http://www.tm.org/blog/meditation/buddhist-monks/)

In Thailand, the founder and principal of the only Buddhist all-girls boarding school, is a Buddhist nun who decided that TM might be good for her students. Based on watching the results in her students, she eventually learned TM herself, and finally became an accredited TM teacher:

[http://www.tm-women.org/blog/helping-at-risk-girls-in-thailand-succeed.html](http://www.tm-women.org/blog/helping-at-risk-girls-in-thailand-succeed.html)
[http://www.tm-women.org/blog/the-personal-journey-of-acharn-yai-buddhist-nun.html](http://www.tm-women.org/blog/the-personal-journey-of-acharn-yai-buddhist-nun.html)

.

The Beatles don't believe that they made Maharishi famous. They decided to go listen him speak because THEY thought that HE was famous, as Sir Paul McCartney explains in this interview with David Lynch at the McCartney-Starr reunion concert sponsored by the David Lynch Foundation:

it's fun to quote people who attack people you resent, but its proper to go to the most current statements by the source if you want to know what they really think.
