To distill his 55 year career into a brief interview at teh very end of his life, when most people DO tend to be a bit curmudgeonly, and to imply that that is all that he ever was, is well, disingenuous. To quote such an article while posing as a spiritual person, speaks volumes about you, you know.

### Josh — 2014-03-24T17:42:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/transcendental-meditation-your-experience.10289/post-117604>

> [siajanai said:](/goto/post?id=117577)
>
> The adult form of TM is never taught to 8-year-olds. In fact, it is almost unheard of for anyone under 10 to be taught the adult technique, and that is people who stalked up to Maharishi Mahesh Yogi at the age of 9 and insisted that they were ready to learn, so he taught them himself.
>
> There's a kid's technique for 4-10 year-olds, but it isn't the adult technique.
>
> And TM has been taught the same way since the TM teacher training course that the Beatles walked out of in 1967 and no accredited TM teacher in the past 40+ years has taught TM differently since all TM teachers have made a promise to only teach TM as authorized by the international accreditation organization that the old monk set up.
>
> It may sound a bit obsessive-compulsive, but the strict attention to detail pays off. Recently, the federal government of Brazil announced that they were setting aside funds to train 48,000 TM teachers, one for each public school in Brazil, so that all 45 million school age kids could learn TM for free.

I learned TM from my father and his friends who were all in their 30's. so I guess it was the adult version. In some ways I have never been a child and in some ways I still am....I was not ready for meditation at 8 and it all just pissed me off. I wanted to go fishing....

I already gave a positive perspective on the Maharishi, traditions send the appropriate people out to publicize the work and get people started. This I know from personal experience. I never met him personally and I know several people who knew him have even before he left India. I also know people who spent time with his teacher. I know a number of followers in the West who spent time with him.

For me, if one is blessed to become close to a teacher and a tradition that opens ones heart and grows one's consciousness, it will be embodied in one's being and needs no lengthy justifications and defense of whatever has or has not happened in public. While TM has been successful, you guys have not touched The Church of Scientology or the Catholic Church. Your response embodies what I have always experienced from long time TM practitioners and adherents, a rigidity and lack of playfulness/sense of humor. It is a stage in every spiritual journey and whatever happens longer-term with TM, seems to create these conditions. Most of the people I know who started with TM have moved on to other traditions or practices. It is all a "take what you like and leave the rest" or the "truth is what works" on the path for an individual's N=1. no matter how big the organization, we all walk alone......

Here was an innocent inquiry from a young woman enthusiastic to explore herself and she ends with a sales pitch that includes the defense of the entire history of the organization and university data, etc.. It does say "your experience". If you have participated in all that was listed, then I would love to know how you felt and how it "grew your consciousness", if not, perhaps this was the wrong thread. For me, I listed what I have actually explored myself where I felt my heart was touched. From my experience, note the word "my" versus everyone's or university study or whatever, TM seems to be as good of a starting technique as any and takes people as far as it takes them, N=1 stuff.

Finally, the mystery of a teacher or master is beyond the organization and people around him. As one notorious mystic said "there is no bad publicity".........

Namaste

### siajanai — 2014-03-24T18:47:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/transcendental-meditation-your-experience.10289/post-117618>

> [Josh said:](/goto/post?id=117604)
>
> I learned TM from my father and his friends who were all in their 30's. so I guess it was the adult version. In some ways I have never been a child and in some ways I still am....I was not ready for meditation at 8 and it all just pissed me off. I wanted to go fishing....

The practice of the adult version of TM is strongly discouraged in children that young and, as I said, no TM teacher would ever dream of teaching it to someone that young.

> I already gave a positive perspective on the Maharishi, traditions do not always send their "best and brightest" out to publicize the work and get people started. This I know from personal experience. I never met him personally and I know several people who knew him have even before he left India. I also know people who spent time with his teacher. I know a number of followers in the West who spent time with him.

Swami Brahmananda Saraswati died in 1953, and there are very few people alive who studied with him. The youngest would be about 80 now, and most would be far, far older. Maharishi Mahesh Yogi was one of his younger students, and he died 5 years ago at age 90.

And just who do you know that knew Maharishi before he left India in 1958? I'm gathering you think Maharishi isn't a "best and brightest" in his line, and yet, I know someone who spoke with the hand-picked successor to Maharishi's teacher who asked him "What about this Maharishi who is with teh Beatles? Is he legitimate?" and the response of the Shankaracharya was "let me put it to you this way: he would be my first choice as my successor but they won't allow it due to the caste laws."

Anoop Chandola is the name of my friend. [https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anoop\_Chandola](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anoop_Chandola) he mentions his own attitude towards Maharishi whom he never met (never learned TM either, as he learned to meditate from teh Shankaracharya he was quoting to me) in his book _In the Himalayan Nights [http://www.amazon.com/In-Himalayan-Nights-Anoop-Chandola/dp/0982998708](http://www.amazon.com/In-Himalayan-Nights-Anoop-Chandola/dp/0982998708)
_
BTW, for the first 40 years of his organization, ALL TM teachers had spent some time with Maharishi, so that isn't as big a deal as you seem to think.

> For me, if one is blessedto become close to a teacher and a tradition that opens ones heart and grows one's consciousness, it will be embodied in one's being and needs no lengthy justifications and defense of whatever has or has not happened in public. While TM has been successful, you guys have not touched The Church of Scientology or the Catholic Church. Your response embodies what I have always experienced from long time TM practitioners and adherents, a rigidity and lack of playfulness/sense of humor. It is a stage in every spiritual journey and whatever happens longer-term with TM, seems to create these conditions. Most of the people I know who started with TM have moved on to other traditions or practices. It is all a "take what you like and leave the rest" or the "truth is what works" on the path for an individual's N=1. no matter how big the organization, we all walk alone......

Being willing to "toe the line" with teaching TM often means being less adventuresome, but not always. And most people who leave TM do so because they fail to see the significance of an open-ended effortless practice. And I sense a certain need to bring personality into this discussion. If I did so first, I apologize.

> Here was an innocent inquiry from a young woman enthusiastic to explore herself and she ends with a sales pitch that includes the defense of the entire history of the organization and university data, etc.. It does say "your experience". If you have participated in all that was listed, then I would love to know how you felt and how it "grew your consciousness", if not, perhaps this was the wrong thread. For me, I listed what I have actually explored myself where I felt my heart was touched. From my experience, note the word "my" versus everyone's or university study or whatever, TM seems to be as good of a starting technique as any and takes people as far as it takes them, N=1 stuff.

Since you learned to meditate from your father, who (it sounds very much like) was not a trained TM teacher, I'd say that it's really N=0 stuff here. You never learned TM in the first place as your father had not a clue how to teach it. As I pointed out elsewhere TM teaching isn't the same as having gone through the 4-day course.

> Finally, the mystery of a teacher or master is beyond the organization and people around him. As one notorious mystic said "there is no bad publicity".........
> Namaste

### Josh — 2014-03-24T19:22:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/transcendental-meditation-your-experience.10289/post-117630>

All selling of spirituality is a great seduction and the reality is that each person has to face different things when they "go in". What seduces or forces one to "go in" is an N=1 thing. Most authentic teachers sooner or later admit that it tends to become rough at a certain point and that help and support is useful at this stage, "dark night of the soul" stuff. Most authentic teachers also admit somewhere that it is a miracle that anyone starts and that the real starting point is when an individual comes to the point of suicide or utter despair....Many suggest that people "start with the body" meaning to stabilize their physiology, think yoga asanas, tai qi and qi gong, sitting, routine life, routine diet, etc...Sounds a bit like what we are doing here.....staying playful can be tough at times....

### patgrif@hotmail.com — 2014-03-24T19:42:20+0200

Not to lower the tone or anything..................but after the Beatles had been in India studying under the Maharishi and John Lennon at least was getting tired of him and announced they were leaving/going home. Maharishi asked him "Why" and Lennon supposedly said "Since you are so f...... cosmic you should know" Somehow I always remembered that, I find one of John Lennon's later 'guru's' Arthur Janov who wrote "The Primal Scream" and promoted 'primal therapy' MUCH more interesting and feel there is a kind of 'missing synergy' even between his writings and Dr Kruse.

### Josh — 2014-03-25T19:45:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/transcendental-meditation-your-experience.10289/post-117886>

Perhaps for objectivity, TM is "mantra practice" and there are many subtleties to mantra practice as well as stages. This is my favorite piece on mantra practice:

> DISEASE, LANGUOR, DOUBT, CARELESSNESS, LAZINESS, SENSUALITY, DELUSION,
> IMPOTENCY AND INSTABILITY ARE THE OBSTACLES THAT DISTRACT THE MIND.
> ANGUISH, DESPAIR, TREMORS AND IRREGULAR BREATHING ARE THE SYMPTOMS OF A
> DISTRACTED MIND.
> TO REMOVE THESE, MEDITATE ON ONE PRINCIPLE.
>
> PATANJALI BELIEVES – and not only believes, he knows also – that sound is the basic element of
> existence. Just as physicists – say that electricity is the basic element, yogis say that sound is the
> basic element. They agree with each other in a subtle way.

From: Osho, Yoga, The Alpha and the Omega: Vol2, #7

[http://www.oshoworld.com/e-books/eng\_discourses.asp?page\_id=16](http://www.oshoworld.com/e-books/eng_discourses.asp?page_id=16)

Further mysteries can be explored in the books about Swami Rama:

[http://www.amazon.com/Living-Himala...NZ2\_1\_2?s=books&ie=UTF8&qid=1395769317&sr=1-2](http://www.amazon.com/Living-Himalayan-Masters-Swami-Rama/dp/0893891568/ref=la_B002882NZ2_1_2?s=books&ie=UTF8&qid=1395769317&sr=1-2)

[http://www.amazon.com/Walking-Himalayan-Master-Americans-Odyssey/dp/0936663375/ref=sr\_1\_1?s=books&ie=UTF8&qid=1395769375&sr=1-1&keywords=walking with a himalayan master](http://www.amazon.com/Walking-Himalayan-Master-Americans-Odyssey/dp/0936663375/ref=sr_1_1?s=books&ie=UTF8&qid=1395769375&sr=1-1&keywords=walking+with+a+himalayan+master)

[http://www.amazon.com/At-Eleventh-Hour-biography-Swami/dp/0893892122/ref=sr\_1\_1?s=books&ie=UTF8&qid=1395769416&sr=1-1&keywords=swami rama biography](http://www.amazon.com/At-Eleventh-Hour-biography-Swami/dp/0893892122/ref=sr_1_1?s=books&ie=UTF8&qid=1395769416&sr=1-1&keywords=swami+rama+biography)

These are the ones I have read and experienced. I ahve not read any of Maharishi Mahesh Yogis books as I have not been drawn to them. Each of us seem to have our "preferences" and "tunings".....

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-25T20:35:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/transcendental-meditation-your-experience.10289/post-117887>

Well … not to maintain the controversy, _but_ …

"Osho" was an alias of the Baghwan Shree Ragneesh, another figure who was surrounded by a certain amount of controversy:

> In mid-1981, Rajneesh relocated to the United States, where his followers established an intentional community (later known as Rajneeshpuram) in Oregon. Almost immediately, the commune's leadership became embroiled in conflicts with local residents (primarily over land use), which were marked by hostility on both sides. The large number of Rolls-Royce cars purchased for Rajneesh's use by his followers also attracted criticism. The Oregon commune collapsed in 1985 when Rajneesh revealed that the commune leadership had committed a number of serious crimes, including a bioterror attack (food contamination) on the citizens of The Dalles.\[2\] He was arrested shortly afterwards, and charged with immigration violations. Rajneesh was deported from the United States in accordance with a plea bargain.

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rajneesh](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rajneesh)

Hmmm … Rolls Royces again.

Doesn't exactly sounds like Saint Francis -- even if this patchwork of rags wasn't actually his:

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

[http://uk.reuters.com/article/2007/09/05/oukoe-uk-italy-saint-idUKL0586082220070905](http://uk.reuters.com/article/2007/09/05/oukoe-uk-italy-saint-idUKL0586082220070905)

Nor like Tsung Tsai, the Ch'an monk befriended by the poet George Crane, who gave away the boots and coats given to him by Crane:

[http://www.amazon.com/Bones-Master-Journey-Secret-Mongolia/dp/0553379089/](http://www.amazon.com/Bones-Master-Journey-Secret-Mongolia/dp/0553379089/)

In short, there's a heck of a lot of questionable stuff going on in the whole arena of "spiritual search".

Mick Brown's _The Dance of 17 Lives_ is very revealing on this. Mick is something of a searcher in rehab:

[http://www.amazon.com/The-Dance-17-Lives-Incredible/dp/0747568715/](http://www.amazon.com/The-Dance-17-Lives-Incredible/dp/0747568715/)

I probably should not have provoked our visitor -- who seemed to have joined the board for a very specific purpose on that day, made a handful of comments in one specific thread, and has now, perhaps, vanished. (But, if he hasn't, good -- there's lots of interesting information for any visitor to read on all Jack's boards.)

However, wasn't his response interesting?

I'm not quite sure what people coming from this mindset mean by "spiritual" or "very spiritual". It's an alien kind of discourse to me. It seems to confuse advice on particular practices that seem to reduce "stress" -- _which was what was asked for by the original poster_ -- with … what? … morality or religion or something?

And notice his peculiar accusation came even though I had earlier said:

> For most people nowadays it's just a way of quietening down.

And then:

> But, of course, the nice thing, particularly for modern Westerners who shy away from some of that, is that you don't have to be religious or esoteric -- or believe in the doctrines of particular psychologists.

I'm also somewhat sceptical of the view that religion boils down to mysticism and all mysticisms are much the same. I'm something of a religious sceptic, but I'm no less wary of mysticism. C. S. Lewis almost convinces me in _Letters to Malcolm_ when he says:

> … one thing common to all mysticisms is the temporary shattering of our ordinary spatial and temporal consciousness and of our discursive intellect. The value of this negative experience must depend on the nature of that positive, whatever it is, for which that makes room …

He adds:

> I shouldn't be at all disturbed if it could be shown that a diabolical mysticism, or drugs, produced experiences indistinguishable (by introspection) from those of the Great Christian mystics. Departures are all alike, it is the landfall that crowns the voyage.

Al Ghazali was a mystic -- I think he's included in Evelyn Underhill's book -- yet Al Ghazali said that the good Moslem should go on _razzia_ (raiding against unbelievers) every year, and that killing people and burning their crops was a meritorious act.

I'd sooner the extravagant "spiritual" claims were toned down, and people just said: do something like this if it helps you. Maybe try a guided meditation tape. Or you might find "other activities" (also asked about by the O.P) -- say Feldenkrais, or just walking in the woods or listening to music -- could be helpful.

### Josh — 2014-03-25T21:44:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/transcendental-meditation-your-experience.10289/post-117896>

Respectfully, "faith without acts..." intellectual exploration of mysticism and criticism without experience is relatively futile. It is similar to the contrast between working what Dr. Kruse proposes and thinking and reading about it.

"There is no bad publicity", consider how Jesus, Buddha, Mahavira, Zarathustra, Junaid, Heraclitus, Bayazid, Gurdjieff, J. Krishanmurti, Bal Shem, Al-Mansoor, Thomas, John the Batist were received in their times....."take what you like and leave the rest"...Read Osho/Bhagwhan/Rajneesh's 300 plus books in English, learn Hindi and read the other 200 or so and then go find the people who lived with him; all of which is available...then make a judgement based on real data and experience.....prematurely judging a being such as him has been one of the great personal tragedies in this life for me. I lost a lot of time.......

This is not stuff that can be experienced from an armchair by most people....

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-26T19:51:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/transcendental-meditation-your-experience.10289/post-118096>

> [Josh said:](/goto/post?id=117896)
>
> ... intellectual exploration of mysticism and criticism without experience is relatively futile. ...

Hmmm … I'd have expected better from you, since you do say some interesting things elsewhere in the thread. There's a lot of meat in what I wrote, and frankly you're not even trying to address any of it. Perhaps some of it went too close to home. Be that as it may, I'll leave it now.

### Josh — 2014-03-26T21:24:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/transcendental-meditation-your-experience.10289/post-118115>

I have respect for your perspective and sources and I have experienced their limits. I am someone who has lived half of my life on each side of the "fence" so to speak. There is a place where intellectual discourse about this does not serve anyone. I agree that anything that gets a person more in harmony with themselves and their surroundings and decreases the stress response is a big win. The question of what is "meditation", "mysticism" and a "valid" or "authentic" "tradition" is a vaster topic even than what we are exploring here regarding the body. Some people make academic careers out of investigating this without taking the first step themselves. I have met people in this position and others who started there and then actually entered the work and saw things radically differently. I have no idea where you are and it is not my business. And yes, intellectual inquiry is one version of the "path" in every "tradition" and it is called "Ghyan" in Sanskrit...I decided not to be a professor of East Asian History and Culture and entered a different path. My teacher's response to my intellect was "teaching you is like trying to teach a brick wall". He is not an uneducated man and has a degree in mathematics and was the assistant to the governor of his state in India before he became and wandering saddhu and other explorations. Many mystics have started as exceptional intellectuals and then have gone on from there. Take a look at Bose who worked with Einstein and his relationship to Tagore for example or Bohm and J. Krishnamurti.

Nothing personal, I respect your perspective and I was much more outspoken in this regard until the age of 26 when I "had to eat crow" so to speak....On we go....

# Thread: Vit. D in salmon roe
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vit-d-in-salmon-roe.10291/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 1

### cmegan — 2014-03-23T15:58:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vit-d-in-salmon-roe.10291/post-117357>

Hi!

Does anyone have a reliable source about the amount of vit. D in salmon roe? I've been eating a lot of it lately for the DHA, but read on the Weston Price website that 1 Tablespoon of salmon roe has 17,000 units of Vit. D! This sounds too good to be true! I can't seem to find any other information about this on the internet. WP doesn't specify which type of Vit. D it is high in...

I am eating a few tablespoons per day, I am wondering if I should be cutting back on my D3/K2 drops! Not just in relation to fear of overdosing, but also in terms of cutting back on costs of supplements since I've been spending more money on roe!

Thanks!

# Thread: Ancient clam gardens
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ancient-clam-gardens.10292/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-03-23T16:15:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ancient-clam-gardens.10292/post-117360>

> A three-year study of ancient clam gardens in the Pacific Northwest has led researchers, including three from Simon Fraser University, to make a discovery that could benefit coastal communities' food production. _PLOS ONE_, a peer-reviewed science journal, has just published their study.

[http://www.eurekalert.org/pub\_releases/2014-03/sfu-acg032014.php](http://www.eurekalert.org/pub_releases/2014-03/sfu-acg032014.php)

### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-23T16:46:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ancient-clam-gardens.10292/post-117371>

You should look into the story about NYC and Long Island. 120 yrs ago they had the biggest oyster beds in the world. And we destroyed it in 50 yrs. This is why a city in Long Island had the name Oyster Bay. There is a great bar in Grand Central Station too Called the Oyster Bar. I do not think they even serve them any longer.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-23T17:57:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ancient-clam-gardens.10292/post-117383>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=117371)
>
> You should look into the story about NYC and Long Island. 120 yrs ago they had the biggest oyster beds in the world. And we destroyed it in 50 yrs. This is why a city in Long Island had the name Oyster Bay. There is a great bar in Grand Central Station too Called the Oyster Bar. I do not think they even serve them any longer.

So I see:

> Originally much of the west shore of [Upper New York Bay](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Upper_New_York_Bay) consisted of large tidal flats which hosted vast [oyster banks](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oyster), a major source of food for the [Lenape](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lenape) \[Delaware\] population who lived in the area prior to the arrival of Dutch settlers. There were several islands which were not completely submerged at high tide, although most of them were submerged. Three of them (later to be known as [Liberty](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Liberty_Island), [Black Tom](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Black_Tom_explosion#Black_Tom_Island) and Ellis) were given the name Oyster Islands by the settlers of [New Netherland](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/New_Netherland), the first European colony in the [Mid-Atlantic](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mid-Atlantic_states) states. The oyster beds would remain a major source of food for nearly three centuries.[\[14\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ellis_Island#cite_note-14)[\[15\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ellis_Island#cite_note-15)[\[16\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ellis_Island#cite_note-16)[Landfilling](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Land_reclamation) to build the railyards of the [Lehigh Valley Railroad](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lehigh_Valley_Railroad) and the [Central Railroad of New Jersey](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Central_Railroad_of_New_Jersey) would eventually obliterate the beds, engulf one island and bring the shoreline much closer to the others.[\[17\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ellis_Island#cite_note-supreme.justia.com-17)

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ellis\_Island#Ownership](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ellis_Island#Ownership)

With hindsight one could see the discovery of the New World as an ecological disaster. The proximate cause was the railroad, but the ultimate cause the incursion of a civilisation that was moving towards that technology.

Not to glamourise pre-contact cultures in the Americas. Powhatan's people were not the "willowy vegans" of Walt Disney imaginings, as one commentator caustically observed when the Disney Pocahontas came out. What Powhatan most wanted off Europeans was cannon for prosecuting tribal wars, as the film critic correctly observed. What he didn't add, but could have, is that anyone who annoyed Powhatan was liable to find himself being flayed alive with sharp sea-shells.

But Native Americans lacked the power, the technological means, to really trash their own environment. Modern society's problem is that it has that power and is too short-sighted to see that it's short-term decisions come back on it.

### Josh — 2014-03-23T18:01:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ancient-clam-gardens.10292/post-117386>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=117371)
>
> You should look into the story about NYC and Long Island. 120 yrs ago they had the biggest oyster beds in the world. And we destroyed it in 50 yrs. This is why a city in Long Island had the name Oyster Bay. There is a great bar in Grand Central Station too Called the Oyster Bar. I do not think they even serve them any longer.

I grew up on a beach in New Rochelle Harbor and there were no oysters, only muscles, clams-mostly Cohogs and snails. A few people would still find oysters in the Sound at that time.

[http://www.oysterguide.com/maps/new-york-and-connecticut/](http://www.oysterguide.com/maps/new-york-and-connecticut/)

# Thread: Humans Drive Evolution of Conch Size
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/humans-drive-evolution-of-conch-size.10293/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-03-23T16:17:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/humans-drive-evolution-of-conch-size.10293/post-117361>

Another Epi-paleo story from the Smithsonian!

> The first humans to pluck a Caribbean fighting conch from the shallow lagoons of Panama’s Bocas del Toro were in for a good meal. Smithsonian scientists found that 7,000 years ago, this common marine shellfish contained 66 percent more meat than its descendants do today. Because of persistent harvesting of the largest conchs, it became advantageous for the animal to mature at a smaller size, resulting in evolutionary change. ...

[http://newsdesk.si.edu/releases/humans-drive-evolution-conch-size](http://newsdesk.si.edu/releases/humans-drive-evolution-conch-size)

### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-23T16:44:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/humans-drive-evolution-of-conch-size.10293/post-117370>

We do a lot to alter circadian biology to ourselves and to things around us.

# Thread: Can't buy access to premium materials because of faulty handling of non-US payments. Please fix it!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cant-buy-access-to-premium-materials-because-of-faulty-handling-of-non-us-payments-please-fix-it.10294/>
**Subforum:** Feedback/Suggestions
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Miguel Toribio-Mateas — 2014-03-23T19:39:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cant-buy-access-to-premium-materials-because-of-faulty-handling-of-non-us-payments-please-fix-it.10294/post-117415>

Hi Jack,

Firstly congratulations on you work. I'm hooked!

Secondly, I couldn't buy access to the 90-Day Reset programme last week. The form kept failing. I had to contact your customer support, who kindly created another link for me to use. This gave me the same problem. Apparently it was something to do with the form not recognising my billing address. I only have one billing address registered for all my cards. I used 4 different cards and they all failed.

Last Thursday I got a call from my bank saying that 4 times a payment had been taken from my account, and then refunded. It was you guys. My bank says that it is due to a faulty handling of the payment, which was coming from an UK bank, possibly by using the only option available for non-US, non-Australian customers: "My state is not listed".

To cut a long story short, I missed the early bird and now I can't even buy access at full price. Your customer support has stopped answering my emails. So, basically, I'm stuck. There must be a way to solve this? Have you had any other UK customers with the same issue?

Look forward to being able to buy access to your premium materials!

Thank you!

Miguel
